create-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a private card with steps for morning and evening.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student folds a card into morning and evening sections. They write self-care steps in each that fit their own needs. They keep the card private in their folder. Guide checks each section has at least two steps without reading specifics aloud.

create-3.objective: Build a class self-care guide that pairs each common change with a self-care step.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group contributes finished guide entries for two assigned changes.
create-3.sampleTask: Groups each take two body changes and write a guide entry for each: the change and the self-care step it calls for. Guide reviews entries before they are posted. Entries are combined into one class self-care guide. Each group reads its entries.

create-4.objective: Produce a short illustrated routine showing self-care steps across one day.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student turns in a four-panel routine covering morning and evening self-care.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student plans a four-panel illustrated routine of self-care across a day. Panels must show two morning steps and two evening steps. Students draft, then finish the panels. Routines stay in folders or are shared only if the student chooses.

2.1.5.PGD.3.standardStatement: Explain the physical, social, and emotional changes that occur during puberty and adolescence and why the onset and progression of puberty can vary.
2.1.5.PGD.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name one physical, one social, and one emotional change that happens during puberty.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one change in each of the three categories on a slip.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide draws three columns labeled physical, social, emotional. Each student writes one change under each column on a slip. Slips are posted in the matching column. Guide reads the range aloud and fills any gaps.

understand-1.objective: Match each change to whether it is a physical, social, or emotional change.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student sorts nine change cards into three labeled categories.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student nine change cards and three category labels. Students place each card under physical, social, or emotional. Pairs compare and fix any swaps. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain why puberty can start and move at different times for different people.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences explaining why timing varies from person to person.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that puberty starts and moves at its own pace for each person. Students write two sentences in their own words explaining why one classmate's timing can differ from another's. Pairs trade and underline the clearest reason. Guide reads two aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how physical, social, and emotional changes connect during adolescence.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence showing how a change in one area touches another.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students read three short scenes where a physical change leads to a social or emotional one. They write one sentence showing how the areas connect. They test it on a new scene. Sentences are refined in pairs.

understand-4.objective: Connect one change to a reason it is a normal part of growing up.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names one change and one reason it is a normal step.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student picks one change from the board they are comfortable discussing. They write one reason it is a normal part of growing up. They add one thing that helps a person handle it. Notes go on a private card.

apply-1.objective: Label each of five described changes as physical, social, or emotional.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the category for each of five described changes.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide reads five described changes aloud, such as feeling more private or growing taller. After each, students write the category. Students hold up answers and Guide notes the spread. Close calls are discussed briefly.

apply-2.objective: Use a timeline to show that two people can reach the same change at different ages.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student marks two figures reaching one change at different points on a timeline.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide hands out a blank age timeline from 8 to 16. Students mark two figures reaching the same change at two different ages. They write one sentence saying why both are normal. Pairs compare timelines.

apply-3.objective: Carry out a matching game pairing a change with a healthy response to it.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each pair correctly pairs change cards with healthy-response cards.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives pairs change cards and healthy-response cards. Pairs match each change with a healthy response, such as a mood swing paired with naming the feeling. Pairs check against a key. Guide clarifies any tricky pair.

apply-4.objective: Apply the idea of varied timing to a new scenario about two classmates.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a respectful explanation for a new timing scenario.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student reads a new scenario where two classmates are at different stages. They write a short, respectful explanation of why both are normal. They mark one kind thing a classmate could do. Responses go to the Guide.

analyze-1.objective: Sort changes into mostly-physical, mostly-social, and mostly-emotional, noting overlaps.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each pair places ten change cards into three groups and flags two overlaps.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair ten change cards and three trays: mostly physical, mostly social, mostly emotional. Pairs sort and flag two cards that overlap categories. Pairs compare with another group. Guide highlights the overlaps.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two students at different puberty stages and identify what is the same underneath.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names two surface differences and one thing both share.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide hands out two profiles of students at different stages. Students list two surface differences and one thing both students share, such as needing support. They underline the shared part. Pairs compare answers.

analyze-3.objective: Examine a worry about puberty timing and find the misunderstanding behind it.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the misunderstanding and writes a correct fact.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read a worry a student might have, such as starting later than friends. They name the misunderstanding behind the worry and write one correct fact that addresses it. They suggest one supportive thing to say. Worries are traded and facts compared in pairs.

analyze-4.objective: Trace how one emotional change during puberty can affect friendships if not understood.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each pair builds a three-step chain from an emotional change to a friendship effect.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Pairs get a starter card naming an emotional change, such as stronger mood swings. They build a three-step chain showing how it could affect a friendship without understanding. They mark where understanding the change would break the chain. Chains are posted and read by two other pairs.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a statement about puberty timing is accurate and supportive.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates a statement accurate or not and gives one reason.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide gives students several statements about puberty timing, some accurate and some not. Students rate each accurate or not and write one reason for the hardest one. They rewrite one inaccurate statement to be correct and kind. Ratings are shared in small groups.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend the claim that there is no single normal age for puberty against a common myth.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short argument with two facts that counter the myth.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students read a common myth, such as everyone should start at the same age. Each student writes a short argument that there is no single normal age, using two facts. They name one supportive message that follows from it. Arguments are read in trios and the trio picks the strongest.

create-1.objective: Write one reassuring fact about puberty timing a peer would find helpful.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces one written fact that is accurate and kind.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide assigns each student one area: physical, social, or emotional change. Each writes one reassuring, accurate fact about timing. The Guide reviews each fact before posting. Approved facts go on a 'this is normal' wall.

create-2.objective: Design a personal note-to-self about one change and how to handle it well.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a private note naming one change and one coping step.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a private note-to-self naming one change they expect and one healthy way to handle it. They add one trusted person they could ask about it. They keep the note in their folder. Guide checks each note has a change and a step without reading specifics aloud.

create-3.objective: Build a class myth-versus-fact board about puberty changes and timing.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group contributes two finished myth-and-fact pairs to the board.
create-3.sampleTask: Groups each write two common myths about puberty and the matching facts. The Guide reviews all pairs before posting. Pairs are arranged into one class myth-versus-fact board. Each group reads its pairs to the class.

create-4.objective: Produce a short comic showing a character handling a puberty change with support.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student turns in a four-panel comic with a change, a feeling, and a support step.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student plans a four-panel comic where a character notices a change, has a feeling about it, gets support, and feels steadier. Students draft, then ink the page. The Guide checks each comic is respectful before posting. Comics chosen by their authors are posted for a gallery walk.

2.1.5.PGD.4.standardStatement: Explain common human sexual development and the role of hormones (e.g., romantic and sexual feelings, masturbation, mood swings, timing of pubertal onset).
2.1.5.PGD.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the hormones that drive puberty and one change each one signals in the body.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the word hormone and lists two body changes hormones trigger on an index card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide states that chemical messengers called hormones tell the body to begin puberty. Each student writes the word hormone at the top of an index card and lists two changes hormones trigger, such as growth or mood shifts. Cards are collected and read aloud without names. Guide fills any change the group missed.

understand-1.objective: Match each puberty term to its plain-language meaning.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student draws a line from each of six terms to its correct definition on a worksheet.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a worksheet with six terms on the left, including hormone, mood swing, and pubertal onset, and six meanings on the right. Students draw a line from each term to its meaning. Pairs compare and fix any mismatch. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain in your own words why hormones can cause mood swings during adolescence.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking hormone levels to changing moods.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that rising and shifting hormone levels can make feelings change quickly. Each student writes two sentences in plain language explaining why a person might feel calm one hour and irritable the next during puberty. Pairs trade and underline the clearest cause. Guide reads two aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how romantic and sexual feelings are a normal part of growing up.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence stating that new feelings during puberty are common and normal.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide reads three short, age-appropriate scenes where a young person notices a new crush or strong feeling. Students write one sentence summarizing that these feelings are a normal part of development. They test the sentence against a fourth scene. Sentences are refined in pairs and one is read aloud.

understand-4.objective: Explain why the timing of pubertal onset varies from person to person and why that variation is healthy.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph explaining two reasons timing differs and why no single timing is right.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews that puberty starts at different ages for different people because of genes and body differences. Each student writes a short paragraph giving two reasons timing varies and stating that an early or late start is each healthy. Pairs swap and check that both reasons appear. Guide collects paragraphs as an exit check.

apply-1.objective: Sort everyday statements into accurate and inaccurate piles about hormones and puberty.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places eight statement cards into a true pile and a myth pile.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair eight statement cards mixing facts and common myths about hormones, mood, and timing. Students sort each card into a true pile or a myth pile. Pairs compare with a neighboring pair and defend any disagreement. Guide reveals the key and pairs correct their piles.

apply-2.objective: Use a feelings vocabulary list to label the emotion in three short adolescence scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student writes the matching feeling word beside each of three scenarios.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide hands out a list of feeling words and three short scenarios about mood swings during puberty. Each student labels the feeling shown in each scene using a word from the list. Pairs compare labels and discuss any scene with more than one fair answer. Guide reads the scenarios and confirms reasonable labels.

apply-3.objective: Apply a calm-down strategy to a mood-swing situation and write the steps you would take.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-step plan for handling a strong mood in the moment.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide models one strategy for steadying a strong mood, such as slow breathing or a short break. Each student picks a mood-swing situation from a list and writes a three-step plan using a strategy. Pairs read each other's plans and check that each step is something a fifth grader could actually do. Guide collects plans.

apply-4.objective: Demonstrate how to ask a trusted adult a question about a body change using a sentence frame.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes one respectful question for a trusted adult using the frame.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide gives a sentence frame for asking a question, such as I noticed and I want to understand. Each student writes one real question about a body change or feeling they could ask a trusted adult. Students place questions in an anonymous box. Guide reads several aloud and models a calm, factual answer to each.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which statements in a paragraph are facts about hormones and which are opinions.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student underlines facts in one color and opinions in another in a short paragraph.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a short paragraph that mixes hormone facts with opinions about puberty. Each student underlines facts in blue and opinions in red. Pairs compare and discuss any sentence they marked differently. Guide displays the paragraph and the class agrees on each mark.

analyze-2.objective: Compare how two students experience the same body change differently and explain why.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart noting differences between two cases.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide presents two short profiles of students who start puberty at different ages. Each student fills a two-column chart listing how each profile feels and what each notices. Below the chart, students write one sentence explaining why the same standard produces different experiences. Pairs compare charts and Guide collects them.

analyze-3.objective: Break a mood-swing scenario into the trigger, the feeling, and the response.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student labels the trigger, feeling, and response in a scenario on a graphic organizer.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a scenario where a small event leads to a big feeling and a reaction. Each student maps the scenario into three boxes labeled trigger, feeling, and response. Students add one healthier response in a fourth box. Pairs trade organizers and check that each box matches the scene.

analyze-4.objective: Examine three sources about puberty and decide which is most trustworthy and why.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student ranks three sources and writes one reason for the top choice.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides three short source descriptions: a doctor's health page, a social media post, and a rumor from a friend. Each student ranks the three from most to least trustworthy and writes one reason for the top pick. Pairs compare rankings and defend their reasoning. Guide leads a class vote and names the marks of a reliable source.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a peer's advice about handling mood swings is healthy and support the judgment.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student marks advice as helpful or harmful and writes one supporting reason.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide posts four pieces of advice a young person might hear about handling strong moods. Each student rates each as helpful or harmful and writes one reason for each rating. Pairs compare and resolve any split rating. Guide reviews each piece and the class agrees which advice is healthy.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique a sample puberty fact sheet for accuracy and clarity and recommend two fixes.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student marks one inaccurate or unclear line and writes two specific fixes.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample fact sheet about hormones and puberty that contains one wrong claim and one confusing line. Each student finds and marks both, then writes two specific fixes that would make the sheet accurate and clear. Pairs compare fixes and pick the stronger pair. Guide collects the marked sheets.

create-1.objective: List five accurate facts about hormones and puberty for a class reference card.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five checked facts on a reference card.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks each student to write five accurate facts about hormones and puberty drawn from the lesson. Students check each fact against the class notes before writing it. Pairs trade cards and verify all five are accurate. Guide collects cards to compile a class reference set.

create-2.objective: Design a short comic strip showing a character handling a mood swing in a healthy way.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a three-panel comic ending in a healthy response.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews one healthy strategy for strong moods. Each student draws a three-panel comic showing a trigger, a strong feeling, and a healthy response. A caption names the strategy used. Pairs read each other's comics and confirm the ending is healthy and realistic.

create-3.objective: Write a short advice letter to a younger student explaining that puberty timing varies and that is normal.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph letter with one fact and one reassurance.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide sets up a letter to an imaginary fourth grader who worries about starting puberty later than friends. Each student writes a short letter with at least one accurate fact about timing and one honest reassurance. Pairs swap letters and check for accuracy and kindness. Guide reads two aloud without names.

create-4.objective: Build a one-page class guide naming trusted facts and trusted adults for puberty questions.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a one-page guide with three facts and three sources of help.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups of three combine the lesson's facts into a one-page guide for the class. The guide lists three accurate facts about hormones and puberty and three trusted adults a student could ask. Groups check each fact against class notes. Guide posts the finished guides and the class picks the clearest layout.

2.1.5.PGD.5.standardStatement: Identify trusted adults, including family members, caregivers, school staff, and health care professionals, whom students can talk to about relationships and ask questions about puberty and adolescent health.
2.1.5.PGD.5.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name four trusted adults a student could ask about puberty and relationships.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four trusted adults on a hand outline, one per finger.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide traces a hand on the board and explains that each finger can name a trusted adult. Each student traces a hand and writes one trusted adult on each of four fingers, drawing from family, caregivers, school staff, and health professionals. Hands are posted on a class wall. Guide reads categories aloud and fills any type the group missed.

understand-1.objective: Sort a list of adults into the four trusted-adult categories.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student places eight adult cards under family, caregiver, school staff, or health professional.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair eight cards naming adults such as school nurse, aunt, doctor, and counselor, plus four category labels. Students sort each card under the right category. Pairs compare with a neighbor and discuss any card that fits more than one. Guide reveals fair placements and pairs adjust.

understand-2.objective: Explain what makes an adult trustworthy to talk to about a personal question.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes three qualities that make an adult trustworthy.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide asks what makes someone safe to ask a hard question. Each student writes three qualities, such as listens without teasing, keeps it private, and gives accurate answers. Pairs trade and circle the quality they both named. Guide builds a class list from the shared answers.

understand-3.objective: Summarize why asking a trusted adult is better than relying on rumors from peers.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences comparing a trusted adult to a rumor as a source.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide reads a short scene where a student gets wrong information from a friend. Each student writes two sentences summarizing why a trusted adult is a safer source than a rumor. Pairs swap and underline the clearer comparison. Guide reads two aloud and names the risk of rumors.

understand-4.objective: Explain how different trusted adults can help with different kinds of questions.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student matches three question types to the adult best suited to answer each.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives three question types, such as a body change, a friendship problem, and a health worry. Each student writes which trusted adult fits each question and one sentence explaining why. Pairs compare and discuss any different match. Guide confirms that several adults can help and notes a health professional for medical questions.

apply-1.objective: Choose the right trusted adult for each of three situations from a class list.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one trusted adult next to each of three situations.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide posts a class list of trusted adults and hands out three situations. Each student writes which adult they would go to for each situation and a quick reason. Pairs compare choices and explain any difference. Guide reads each situation and confirms reasonable choices.

apply-2.objective: Use a sentence frame to start a conversation with a trusted adult about a worry.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student writes one opening line using the frame.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide models a sentence frame for opening a hard talk, such as Can I ask you about something. Each student writes one real opening line for a worry they could raise with a trusted adult. Students practice saying the line quietly to a partner. Guide invites two volunteers to model a calm opening.

apply-3.objective: Role-play asking a trusted adult a puberty question and have the partner respond kindly.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each pair performs a short role-play with a clear question and a respectful answer.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide pairs students and assigns one as the student asker and one as the trusted adult. The asker uses a real but safe question; the adult responds with calm, accurate words. Pairs switch roles and try a second question. Guide watches and names one strong opening line from the room.

apply-4.objective: Build a personal trusted-adults contact card a student can keep.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student fills a card with three named adults and how to reach each.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a small card with three rows. Students write three trusted adults by name or role and how they would reach each one. Students keep the card in a notebook. Guide checks that each card lists at least one adult at home and one at school.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which adult in a scenario is acting as a trusted source and which is not.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student labels two adults in a scenario as trusted or not trusted with a reason.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a scenario with two adults, one who listens and answers honestly and one who teases. Each student labels each adult trusted or not trusted and writes one reason for each label. Pairs compare and discuss. Guide confirms the marks and names the behavior that breaks trust.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two ways of asking the same question and decide which is more likely to get help.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the stronger of two openings and writes why it works better.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide shows two openings for the same question, one vague and one clear and respectful. Each student picks the stronger opening and writes one reason it is more likely to get a helpful answer. Pairs compare picks. Guide reads both openings aloud and the class names what made the better one work.

analyze-3.objective: Break a help-seeking situation into the worry, the chosen adult, and the next step.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps a situation into three labeled boxes on an organizer.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a situation where a student has a question about a body change. Each student fills three boxes labeled worry, chosen trusted adult, and next step. Students add one backup adult in a fourth box. Pairs trade organizers and check that the chosen adult fits the worry.

analyze-4.objective: Examine a list of information sources and explain why a trusted adult outranks the others.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student ranks four sources and writes one reason a trusted adult is at the top.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide lists four sources: a trusted adult, a search result, a classmate, and a video. Each student ranks them from most to least reliable for a puberty question and writes one reason the trusted adult ranks first. Pairs compare rankings. Guide leads a discussion on what makes a source dependable.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a student in a scenario chose a good trusted adult and support the call.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates the choice as strong or weak and writes one supporting reason.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide reads three short scenarios where a student picks an adult to ask. Each student rates each choice strong or weak and writes one reason. Pairs compare ratings and resolve any split. Guide reviews each scenario and the class names what made a choice strong.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique a sample help-seeking plan and recommend two improvements.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student marks one weak part of a plan and writes two specific improvements.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample plan a student wrote for asking an adult a question, with one vague step and one missing backup. Each student marks both weak spots and writes two specific improvements. Pairs compare improvements and pick the stronger set. Guide collects the marked plans.

create-1.objective: List five trusted adults and their roles for a personal reference page.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five adults with one role note each.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks each student to list five trusted adults and a short note on what each one is good to ask about. Students draw from home, school, and health settings. Pairs trade pages and confirm the list covers more than one setting. Guide collects pages for students to keep.

create-2.objective: Design a poster that helps classmates name trusted adults to ask.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster naming at least four trusted adults with a clear title.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews the four categories of trusted adults. Each student designs a poster with a clear title and at least four named adults or roles a classmate could ask about puberty and relationships. Pairs check that each poster is clear and accurate. Guide posts the strongest few in the room.

create-3.objective: Write a short script for asking a trusted adult a real question and getting an answer.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a six-line dialogue with a clear question and a helpful reply.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide sets the scene of a student approaching a trusted adult. Each student writes a six-line dialogue: a respectful opening, a clear question, and a calm, accurate answer. Pairs read the scripts aloud and check the question is clear and the answer is helpful. Guide reads one strong script to the class.

create-4.objective: Build a class help map showing which trusted adults handle which questions.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a labeled map linking three question types to trusted adults.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups of three build a help map on chart paper. The map links three common question types to the trusted adults best suited to each. Groups add one home adult and one school adult to every link. Guide posts the maps and the class compares which is easiest to follow.

2.1.5.PP.1.standardStatement: Explain the relationship between sexual intercourse and human reproduction.
2.1.5.PP.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the two cells that join to begin human reproduction.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes sperm and egg and the word that names their joining on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide states that human reproduction begins when a sperm cell joins an egg cell, a step called fertilization. Each student writes the two cell names and the word fertilization on an index card. Cards are collected and read aloud without names. Guide confirms the correct terms.

understand-1.objective: Match each reproduction term to its plain-language meaning.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student links five terms to their meanings on a worksheet.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a worksheet with five terms, including egg, sperm, fertilization, and uterus, and five meanings. Each student draws a line from each term to its meaning. Pairs compare and fix any mismatch. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain in your own words how a sperm and an egg joining can lead to a pregnancy.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences describing fertilization leading to a developing baby.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that when a sperm joins an egg, the fertilized cell can grow in the uterus into a baby. Each student writes two plain-language sentences explaining this link. Pairs trade and underline the clearer sentence. Guide reads two accurate examples aloud.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the order of steps from fertilization to the start of a pregnancy.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student numbers three steps in the correct order on a strip.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide provides three step cards: sperm joins egg, the fertilized cell settles in the uterus, and the cell begins to grow. Each student numbers the steps in order. Pairs compare and discuss any difference. Guide displays the correct sequence and students check their numbers.

understand-4.objective: Explain why reproduction needs both an egg cell and a sperm cell.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph stating why both cells are required.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews that each cell carries part of the information needed to start a new person. Each student writes a short paragraph explaining why reproduction needs both an egg and a sperm. Pairs swap and check the paragraph names both cells and their roles. Guide collects paragraphs as an exit check.

apply-1.objective: Sort statements about reproduction into accurate and inaccurate piles.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places six statement cards into a true pile and a myth pile.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair six cards mixing accurate statements and common myths about how reproduction works. Students sort each into a true pile or a myth pile. Pairs compare with a neighbor and defend any disagreement. Guide reveals the key and pairs correct their piles.

apply-2.objective: Label a simple reproduction diagram with the correct terms.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student writes four correct labels on a blank diagram.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide hands out a simple, age-appropriate diagram showing where fertilization and early growth happen. Each student labels four parts using a word bank. Pairs compare labels and fix any swap. Guide projects the key and students self-correct.

apply-3.objective: Use the steps of reproduction to complete a cause-and-effect sentence chain.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student finishes three because sentences linking each step to the next.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a chain of three sentence starters, each beginning with Because. Each student completes the chain so it runs from fertilization to a growing baby. Pairs read each other's chains and check each link makes sense. Guide reads one accurate chain aloud.

apply-4.objective: Answer three classmate questions about reproduction using accurate vocabulary.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence accurate answer to each of three anonymous questions.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide reads three anonymous student questions about reproduction collected in advance. Each student writes a one-sentence accurate answer to each using lesson vocabulary. Pairs compare answers for accuracy. Guide reads a model answer to each question and corrects any error.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which sentences in a paragraph are facts and which are unsupported claims.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student underlines facts in one color and unsupported claims in another.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a short paragraph mixing accurate reproduction facts with unsupported claims. Each student underlines facts in blue and unsupported claims in red. Pairs compare and discuss any sentence marked differently. Guide displays the paragraph and the class agrees on each mark.

analyze-2.objective: Compare a plant and a human in how reproduction needs two cells to join.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart of likenesses and differences.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide reminds students that plants also join cells to reproduce. Each student fills a two-column chart comparing how a plant and a human each need two cells to join, noting one likeness and one difference. Pairs compare charts. Guide names the shared idea that reproduction joins two cells.

analyze-3.objective: Break the reproduction process into stages and label what happens at each.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student fills a flow map with three stages and a note for each.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a blank flow map with three boxes. Each student labels the stages from cells joining to early growth and writes one note under each box. Students mark where a pregnancy begins. Pairs trade maps and check the order and notes.

analyze-4.objective: Examine three sources about reproduction and decide which is most reliable.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student ranks three sources and writes one reason for the top choice.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides three source descriptions: a health textbook, a social media clip, and a playground rumor. Each student ranks them from most to least reliable and writes one reason for the top pick. Pairs compare rankings and defend reasoning. Guide names the marks of a reliable health source.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample explanation of reproduction is accurate and support the judgment.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student marks an explanation accurate or inaccurate and gives one reason.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide posts three short explanations of how reproduction works, two accurate and one with an error. Each student rates each accurate or inaccurate and writes one reason. Pairs compare and resolve any split. Guide reviews each and the class names the error in the weak one.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique a peer's reproduction summary for accuracy and recommend two fixes.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student marks one inaccurate or unclear line and writes two specific fixes.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide pairs students to swap short summaries of the reproduction process written earlier. Each student finds one inaccurate or unclear line and writes two specific fixes. Pairs discuss the fixes and agree on the strongest one. Guide collects the marked summaries.

create-1.objective: List five accurate facts about human reproduction for a class reference card.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five checked facts on a reference card.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks each student to write five accurate facts about reproduction drawn from the lesson. Students check each fact against the class notes before writing it. Pairs trade cards and verify all five are accurate. Guide compiles a class reference set.

create-2.objective: Design a labeled diagram that shows how fertilization leads to early growth.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a diagram with four correct labels and one caption.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews the steps from cells joining to early growth. Each student draws and labels a simple diagram with four parts and writes one caption explaining the main idea. Pairs check labels for accuracy. Guide posts the clearest diagrams.

create-3.objective: Write a clear paragraph explaining the relationship between intercourse and reproduction for a younger reader.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph using accurate, age-appropriate vocabulary.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide sets up writing for a younger student who asked how babies begin. Each student writes a short, accurate, age-appropriate paragraph linking the joining of cells to reproduction. Pairs swap and check for accuracy and clear words. Guide reads one model paragraph aloud.

create-4.objective: Build a class fact sheet on reproduction with accurate vocabulary and a diagram.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a one-page sheet with four facts and one labeled diagram.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups of three combine lesson content into a one-page fact sheet. The sheet lists four accurate facts and includes one labeled diagram of how reproduction begins. Groups check each fact against class notes. Guide posts the sheets and the class picks the clearest layout.

2.1.5.PP.2.standardStatement: Explain the range of ways pregnancy can occur (e.g., IVF, surrogacy).
2.1.5.PP.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name three ways a pregnancy can occur.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes three ways pregnancy can occur on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide states that a pregnancy can begin in more than one way, including without medical help, through IVF, and with a surrogate. Each student writes three ways on an index card. Cards are read aloud without names. Guide confirms the correct terms.

understand-1.objective: Match each pregnancy term to its plain-language meaning.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student links four terms to their meanings on a worksheet.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a worksheet with four terms, including IVF and surrogacy, and four meanings. Each student draws a line from each term to its meaning. Pairs compare and fix any mismatch. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain in your own words what IVF means.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences describing IVF in plain language.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that IVF stands for in vitro fertilization, where doctors join an egg and sperm outside the body and then place the fertilized cell in the uterus. Each student writes two plain-language sentences explaining IVF. Pairs trade and underline the clearer sentence. Guide reads two accurate examples aloud.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how surrogacy lets a pregnancy happen for a family that cannot carry one.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences explaining the role of a surrogate.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide explains that in surrogacy, one person carries a pregnancy for another family. Each student writes two sentences summarizing how surrogacy works and why a family might use it. Pairs swap and underline the clearer sentence. Guide reads two accurate examples aloud.

understand-4.objective: Explain why families may use different paths to have a child.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph naming two reasons families use different paths.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews that families form in many ways and may need medical help to have a child. Each student writes a short paragraph giving two reasons a family might choose IVF, surrogacy, or another path. Pairs swap and check both reasons appear. Guide collects paragraphs as an exit check.

apply-1.objective: Sort short scenarios by which pathway to pregnancy each one describes.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places six scenario cards under the matching pathway label.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair six short scenario cards and three pathway labels. Students place each scenario under the pathway it describes. Pairs compare with a neighbor and discuss any card that is unclear. Guide reveals fair placements and pairs adjust.

apply-2.objective: Use accurate vocabulary to answer three classmate questions about how pregnancy can occur.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence accurate answer to each of three questions.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide reads three anonymous student questions about pathways to pregnancy collected in advance. Each student writes a one-sentence accurate answer to each using lesson vocabulary. Pairs compare answers for accuracy. Guide reads a model answer to each and corrects any error.

apply-3.objective: Place the steps of IVF in order on a sequence strip.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student numbers four IVF steps in the correct order.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives four step cards describing IVF in plain language. Each student numbers the steps from joining the cells to placing the fertilized cell in the uterus. Pairs compare and discuss any difference. Guide displays the correct order and students check their numbers.

apply-4.objective: Match three different families to a pathway that could help each.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes one fitting pathway and a reason for each family case.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide gives three short, respectful family cases. Each student writes which pathway could help each family and one reason. Pairs compare matches and explain any difference. Guide confirms that more than one pathway can fit and that families choose what is right for them.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which statements about pathways to pregnancy are facts and which are opinions.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student underlines facts in one color and opinions in another.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a short paragraph mixing facts about IVF and surrogacy with opinions. Each student underlines facts in blue and opinions in red. Pairs compare and discuss any sentence marked differently. Guide displays the paragraph and the class agrees on each mark.

analyze-2.objective: Compare IVF and surrogacy by what each one does and who carries the pregnancy.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart of likenesses and differences.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide sets up a two-column chart for IVF and surrogacy. Each student notes one likeness and two differences, including who carries the pregnancy in each. Pairs compare charts and fix any error. Guide confirms the key differences.

analyze-3.objective: Break a family-building story into the goal, the pathway, and the people involved.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student fills a three-box organizer for one story.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a short, respectful story of a family using a pathway to have a child. Each student fills three boxes labeled goal, pathway, and people involved. Students add one fact they learned in a fourth box. Pairs trade organizers and check each box fits the story.

analyze-4.objective: Examine three sources about pathways to pregnancy and decide which is most reliable.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student ranks three sources and writes one reason for the top choice.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides three source descriptions: a clinic's health page, a social media post, and a friend's secondhand story. Each student ranks them from most to least reliable and writes one reason for the top pick. Pairs compare rankings. Guide names the marks of a reliable health source.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample explanation of a pathway to pregnancy is accurate and support the call.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student marks an explanation accurate or inaccurate and gives one reason.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide posts three short explanations of IVF or surrogacy, two accurate and one with an error. Each student rates each accurate or inaccurate and writes one reason. Pairs compare and resolve any split. Guide reviews each and the class names the error in the weak one.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique a sample fact sheet on pathways to pregnancy and recommend two improvements.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student marks one inaccurate or unclear line and writes two specific improvements.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample fact sheet on IVF and surrogacy with one wrong claim and one confusing line. Each student marks both and writes two specific improvements. Pairs compare improvements and pick the stronger set. Guide collects the marked sheets.

create-1.objective: List five accurate facts about the ways pregnancy can occur for a reference card.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five checked facts on a reference card.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks each student to write five accurate facts about pathways to pregnancy drawn from the lesson. Students check each fact against class notes before writing it. Pairs trade cards and verify all five are accurate. Guide compiles a class reference set.

create-2.objective: Design a simple infographic showing three pathways to pregnancy.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces an infographic naming three pathways with one accurate note each.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews the three pathways covered. Each student designs a simple infographic with three sections, one per pathway, each with a short accurate note. Pairs check the notes for accuracy and clear words. Guide posts the clearest infographics.

create-3.objective: Write a short explanation of one pathway to pregnancy for a younger reader.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph using accurate, age-appropriate vocabulary.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide sets up writing for a younger student who asked how families can have a baby. Each student picks one pathway and writes a short, accurate, age-appropriate paragraph explaining it. Pairs swap and check for accuracy and clear words. Guide reads one model paragraph aloud.

create-4.objective: Build a class chart that maps each pathway to pregnancy to a plain-language description.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a chart linking three pathways to clear descriptions.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups of three build a chart on chart paper linking three pathways to pregnancy to a clear, accurate description of each. Groups check each description against class notes. Guide posts the charts and the class picks the clearest one.

2.1.5.SSH.1.standardStatement: Describe gender-role stereotypes and their potential impact on self and others.
2.1.5.SSH.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Define gender-role stereotype and give one example.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a definition and one example on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide states that a gender-role stereotype is a fixed idea that a person must act a certain way because of their gender. Each student writes the definition and one example, such as only boys can play a sport, on an index card. Cards are read aloud without names. Guide confirms accurate examples.

understand-1.objective: Sort statements into stereotype and not-a-stereotype piles.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student places eight statement cards into two labeled piles.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair eight statement cards and two labels: stereotype and not a stereotype. Students sort each card. Pairs compare with a neighbor and discuss any disagreement. Guide reveals the key and pairs correct their piles.

understand-2.objective: Explain in your own words why a gender-role stereotype is not always true.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences giving a counterexample to one stereotype.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide names one common stereotype. Each student writes two sentences explaining why it is not always true and gives one real counterexample. Pairs trade and underline the clearest counterexample. Guide reads two aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how a stereotype can affect how a person feels about themselves.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence describing a feeling a stereotype can cause.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide reads a short scene where a student is told they cannot do something because of their gender. Each student writes one sentence summarizing how the comment could make the student feel. Pairs swap and compare. Guide reads two aloud and names the impact on self.

understand-4.objective: Explain how a stereotype can affect others, not only the person it targets.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph naming an effect on a bystander or a group.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews that stereotypes can shape how a whole group is treated. Each student writes a short paragraph explaining one way a stereotype can affect people beyond its target, such as limiting who joins an activity. Pairs swap and check the effect is clear. Guide collects paragraphs as an exit check.

apply-1.objective: Spot the stereotype in each of three short ads or scenes.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student circles the stereotype and names it in each of three examples.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out three short ad or scene descriptions. Each student circles the part that shows a stereotype and writes what the stereotype is. Pairs compare and discuss. Guide reviews each example and the class agrees on the stereotype shown.

apply-2.objective: Rewrite a stereotyped statement so it is fair and open.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student rewrites three statements to remove the stereotype.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives three stereotyped statements. Each student rewrites each one so it no longer limits people by gender. Pairs trade and check that each rewrite is fair and clear. Guide reads one strong rewrite for each statement.

apply-3.objective: Use a response frame to push back on a stereotype heard in a scenario.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes one respectful response using the frame.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide models a response frame, such as Actually, anyone can. Each student reads a scenario where a peer states a stereotype and writes one respectful response using the frame. Students practice saying it to a partner. Guide invites two volunteers to model a calm response.

apply-4.objective: Demonstrate a fair choice in a role-play where a stereotype could block someone.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each pair performs a short role-play where a character is included despite a stereotype.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide sets up a scene where a group is choosing teams and a stereotype could leave someone out. Pairs role-play a version where a character speaks up and includes everyone fairly. Pairs switch roles and try a second scene. Guide names one strong inclusive line from the room.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the stereotype, the target, and the possible impact in a short story.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student labels three parts of a story on an organizer.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a short story containing a gender-role stereotype. Each student labels the stereotype, who it targets, and one possible impact on a three-part organizer. Pairs trade and check each label fits the story. Guide reviews one story with the class.

analyze-2.objective: Compare how two characters are treated differently because of a stereotype.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart of how each character is treated.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a scene with two characters treated differently due to a stereotype. Each student fills a two-column chart noting how each is treated and one effect on each. Below the chart, students write one sentence naming the unfair pattern. Pairs compare charts and Guide collects them.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how a stereotype spreads from one comment to a wider effect.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student fills a flow map showing three steps of spread.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a scenario where one stereotyped comment leads others to repeat it. Each student maps three steps showing how the idea spreads and grows. Students mark where it could be stopped. Pairs trade maps and check the steps connect.

analyze-4.objective: Examine messages from media and decide which reinforce stereotypes and which break them.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student sorts four message cards into reinforces and breaks columns with a reason.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide gives four short media message descriptions. Each student sorts them into reinforces a stereotype or breaks a stereotype and writes one reason per card. Pairs compare and discuss any split. Guide reviews each and the class agrees on the sort.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a character's response to a stereotype was fair and effective and support the call.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates a response strong or weak and writes one supporting reason.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide posts three responses characters gave when they heard a stereotype. Each student rates each strong or weak and writes one reason. Pairs compare and resolve any split. Guide reviews each response and the class names what made the strong ones work.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique a classroom poster meant to reduce stereotypes and recommend two improvements.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student marks one weak part of the poster and writes two specific improvements.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide shows a sample anti-stereotype poster with one unclear message and one weak example. Each student marks both and writes two specific improvements. Pairs compare improvements and pick the stronger set. Guide collects the marked critiques.

create-1.objective: List five true statements that break common gender-role stereotypes.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five fair statements on a card.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks each student to write five true statements that show people are not limited by gender, such as anyone can be a nurse or a coach. Pairs trade cards and check each statement is fair and accurate. Guide compiles a class list.

create-2.objective: Design a poster that challenges one gender-role stereotype.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster with a clear message and one fair example.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews how a stereotype limits people. Each student designs a poster that names one stereotype and challenges it with a clear message and a fair example. Pairs check the poster is clear and respectful. Guide posts the strongest few.

create-3.objective: Write a short story where a character overcomes a gender-role stereotype.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a story showing a stereotype, a response, and a fair outcome.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide sets up a story prompt about a character who faces a stereotype. Each student writes a short story showing the stereotype, how the character responds, and a fair outcome. Pairs swap and check all three parts appear. Guide reads one strong story aloud.

create-4.objective: Build a class pledge that names how the group will treat people fairly across genders.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group contributes two pledge lines that the class combines into one pledge.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups of three each write two pledge lines about treating everyone fairly regardless of gender. Groups read their lines aloud and the class combines them into one class pledge on chart paper. The class checks each line is fair and specific. Guide posts the pledge in the room.

2.1.5.SSH.2.standardStatement: Differentiate between sexual orientation and gender identity.
2.1.5.SSH.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: State a plain-language definition of sexual orientation and of gender identity.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one short definition for each term on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a plain-language definition of each term: gender identity is a person's own sense of their gender, and sexual orientation is who a person is romantically drawn to. Each student writes both definitions on an index card. Cards are read aloud without names. Guide confirms accurate wording.

understand-1.objective: Match each term to its correct meaning on a worksheet.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student links the two terms to their meanings and two examples to the right term.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a worksheet with the two terms and four cards: two meanings and two short examples. Each student links each card to sexual orientation or gender identity. Pairs compare and fix any mismatch. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain in your own words how sexual orientation and gender identity are different.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences stating the key difference.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that gender identity is about who a person is, while sexual orientation is about who a person is drawn to. Each student writes two sentences in plain language stating the difference. Pairs trade and underline the clearer sentence. Guide reads two accurate examples aloud.

understand-3.objective: Summarize why both terms are part of how people understand themselves.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence explaining that both are normal parts of identity.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide reviews that everyone has a gender identity and that people have different orientations. Each student writes one sentence summarizing that both are normal parts of who a person is. Pairs swap and compare. Guide reads two aloud and confirms the idea.

understand-4.objective: Explain why mixing up the two terms can lead to a misunderstanding.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph showing one misunderstanding that comes from mixing the terms.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives an example where someone confuses the two terms. Each student writes a short paragraph explaining the misunderstanding and how using the right term fixes it. Pairs swap and check the paragraph names both terms correctly. Guide collects paragraphs as an exit check.

apply-1.objective: Sort example statements into the sexual orientation pile or the gender identity pile.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places six statement cards under the correct term.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair six statement cards and two labels. Students place each card under sexual orientation or gender identity. Pairs compare with a neighbor and discuss any card that is unclear. Guide reveals the key and pairs correct their piles.

apply-2.objective: Label which term a short scenario is about.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student writes the correct term beside each of three scenarios.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide hands out three short, respectful scenarios. Each student writes whether each scenario is about sexual orientation or gender identity. Pairs compare labels and discuss any difference. Guide reads each scenario and confirms the correct term.

apply-3.objective: Use the correct term to complete three fill-in sentences.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student fills three blanks with the right term.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives three sentences each missing one of the two terms. Each student fills each blank with the correct term. Pairs trade and check the choices. Guide reads the complete sentences aloud and the class confirms.

apply-4.objective: Answer three classmate questions about the two terms using accurate vocabulary.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence accurate answer to each of three anonymous questions.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide reads three anonymous student questions about the two terms collected in advance. Each student writes a one-sentence accurate, respectful answer to each. Pairs compare answers for accuracy. Guide reads a model answer to each and corrects any error.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which sentences in a paragraph use the terms correctly and which mix them up.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks each sentence correct or mixed up.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a short paragraph that uses the two terms, some correctly and some swapped. Each student marks each sentence correct or mixed up. Pairs compare and discuss any difference. Guide displays the paragraph and the class agrees on each mark.

analyze-2.objective: Compare the two terms in a chart by what each describes and one example of each.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart for the two terms.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide sets up a two-column chart for sexual orientation and gender identity. Each student notes what each term describes and gives one respectful example of each. Pairs compare charts and fix any error. Guide confirms the key differences.

analyze-3.objective: Break a scenario into which term applies and the evidence that shows it.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the term and the clue that points to it for one scenario.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a short scenario. Each student writes which term the scenario is about and the specific clue in the text that shows it. Pairs trade and check the clue matches the term. Guide reviews one scenario with the class.

analyze-4.objective: Examine three sources about these terms and decide which gives accurate, respectful information.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student ranks three sources and writes one reason for the top choice.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides three source descriptions: a health education page, a social media comment, and an overheard remark. Each student ranks them from most to least reliable and writes one reason for the top pick. Pairs compare rankings. Guide names the marks of accurate, respectful information.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample explanation keeps the two terms straight and support the call.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student marks an explanation accurate or mixed up and gives one reason.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide posts three short explanations of the two terms, two accurate and one that swaps them. Each student rates each accurate or mixed up and writes one reason. Pairs compare and resolve any split. Guide reviews each and names the error in the weak one.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique a peer's definition of the two terms and recommend two fixes.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student marks one unclear or mixed-up line and writes two specific fixes.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide pairs students to swap definitions they wrote earlier. Each student finds one unclear or mixed-up line and writes two specific fixes that make the definition accurate and respectful. Pairs discuss the fixes and agree on the strongest. Guide collects the marked definitions.

create-1.objective: List four accurate facts that keep sexual orientation and gender identity distinct.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four checked facts on a card.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks each student to write four accurate facts that clearly separate the two terms. Students check each fact against class notes before writing it. Pairs trade cards and verify accuracy. Guide compiles a class reference set.

create-2.objective: Design a two-column reference card explaining each term with an example.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a card with a clear definition and one example per term.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews both terms. Each student designs a two-column reference card, one column per term, with a plain definition and one respectful example each. Pairs check the card for accuracy and clear words. Guide posts the clearest cards.

create-3.objective: Write a short explanation for a younger reader that keeps the two terms separate.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph defining both terms with one example each.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide sets up writing for a younger student who is confused about the two terms. Each student writes a short, accurate, respectful paragraph defining both and giving one example of each. Pairs swap and check for accuracy and clear words. Guide reads one model paragraph aloud.

create-4.objective: Build a class anchor chart that shows the difference between the two terms.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a chart with a definition and one example for each term.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups of three build an anchor chart with two sections, one per term, each with a plain definition and one respectful example. Groups check each section against class notes. Guide posts the charts and the class picks the clearest one to keep up.

2.1.5.SSH.3.standardStatement: Demonstrate ways to promote dignity and respect for all people (e.g. sexual orientation, gender identity and expression, race, ethnicity, socio-economic status, differing ability, immigration status, family configuration).
2.1.5.SSH.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name three ways to show respect to people who are different from you.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes three respectful actions on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide states that everyone deserves dignity and respect. Each student writes three specific respectful actions, such as using a person's correct name, listening, and including others. Cards are read aloud without names. Guide builds a class list from the actions.

understand-1.objective: Sort actions into respectful and disrespectful piles.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student places eight action cards into two labeled piles.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair eight action cards and two labels: respectful and disrespectful. Students sort each card. Pairs compare with a neighbor and discuss any disagreement. Guide reveals the key and pairs correct their piles.

understand-2.objective: Explain in your own words what dignity means and why every person has it.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences defining dignity and stating it belongs to everyone.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that dignity is the worth every person has just by being a person. Each student writes two sentences defining dignity and stating that it belongs to everyone regardless of differences. Pairs trade and underline the clearer sentence. Guide reads two aloud.

understand-3.objective: Summarize why respecting differences makes a classroom safer for everyone.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence linking respect to a safer classroom.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide reads a short scene where respect helps a new student feel welcome. Each student writes one sentence summarizing how respecting differences makes the classroom safer for everyone. Pairs swap and compare. Guide reads two aloud and names the benefit.

understand-4.objective: Explain how one respectful action can affect a whole group, not just one person.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph naming a ripple effect of one respectful act.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews that respect can spread. Each student writes a short paragraph describing how one respectful action, such as including someone left out, can change how a whole group acts. Pairs swap and check the ripple is clear. Guide collects paragraphs as an exit check.

apply-1.objective: Choose a respectful action for each of three situations involving difference.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one respectful action for each of three situations.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out three situations where a person could be treated unfairly because of a difference. Each student writes one respectful action they could take in each. Pairs compare actions and explain any difference. Guide reads each situation and confirms strong actions.

apply-2.objective: Use a respectful phrase to respond to a put-down in a scenario.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student writes one respectful response to a put-down using a phrase bank.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a phrase bank of respectful responses. Each student reads a scenario with a put-down and writes one response that stands up for the person with dignity. Students practice saying it to a partner. Guide invites two volunteers to model a calm response.

apply-3.objective: Demonstrate including a left-out classmate in a role-play.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each pair performs a short role-play that includes the left-out character with respect.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide sets up a scene where one character is being left out because of a difference. Pairs role-play a version where another character includes them respectfully. Pairs switch roles and try a second scene. Guide names one strong inclusive line from the room.

apply-4.objective: Apply a set of class respect rules to decide the right move in three quick cases.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student names which rule applies and the action for each case.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide posts three or four class respect rules and hands out three quick cases. Each student writes which rule applies to each case and the respectful action it calls for. Pairs compare and discuss. Guide reviews each case and confirms the matching rule.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the disrespectful action and its target in a short story.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the disrespectful action and names who it affects.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a short story where a character is treated unfairly because of a difference. Each student marks the disrespectful action and names who it affects. Pairs trade and check the marks. Guide reviews one story with the class.

analyze-2.objective: Compare a respectful and a disrespectful response to the same situation.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart of the two responses and their effects.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a situation with two possible responses, one respectful and one not. Each student fills a two-column chart noting each response and its effect on the person. Below the chart, students write one sentence on which response builds dignity. Pairs compare charts.

analyze-3.objective: Break an inclusion situation into the problem, the respectful action, and the result.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student fills a three-box organizer for one situation.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a situation where someone is excluded. Each student fills three boxes labeled problem, respectful action, and result. Students add one backup action in a fourth box. Pairs trade organizers and check each box fits the situation.

analyze-4.objective: Examine four classroom moments and sort which build dignity and which break it.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student sorts four moment cards into builds and breaks columns with a reason.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide gives four short classroom moment cards. Each student sorts them into builds dignity or breaks dignity and writes one reason per card. Pairs compare and discuss any split. Guide reviews each and the class agrees on the sort.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a character's action promoted dignity and support the call.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates an action as respectful or not and writes one supporting reason.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide posts three character actions from short scenes. Each student rates each as respectful or not and writes one reason. Pairs compare and resolve any split. Guide reviews each action and the class names what made the respectful ones work.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique a draft class respect pledge and recommend two improvements.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student marks one weak line and writes two specific improvements.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide shows a draft class respect pledge with one vague line and one missing group. Each student marks both and writes two specific improvements that make the pledge clear and inclusive. Pairs compare improvements and pick the stronger set. Guide collects the critiques.

create-1.objective: List five specific respectful actions for a class kindness board.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five specific actions on cards for the board.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks each student to write five specific respectful actions, each naming what a student actually does. Students avoid vague words like be nice. Pairs trade and check each action is specific. Guide posts the cards on a class kindness board.

create-2.objective: Design a poster that promotes dignity and respect for all people.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster with a clear message and two respectful actions.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews what dignity means. Each student designs a poster with a clear message about respecting all people and two specific respectful actions. Pairs check the poster is clear and inclusive. Guide posts the strongest few.

create-3.objective: Write a short skit that shows characters promoting dignity in a hard moment.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair writes a skit with a problem and a respectful resolution.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide sets up a skit prompt where a character faces unfair treatment. Each pair writes a short skit showing the problem and a respectful resolution. Pairs swap scripts and check the resolution builds dignity. Guide invites one pair to perform.

create-4.objective: Build a class respect agreement that names how the group treats all people.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group contributes two specific lines that the class combines into one agreement.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups of three each write two specific lines for a class respect agreement that name respectful actions toward people of all backgrounds. Groups read their lines and the class combines them into one agreement on chart paper. The class checks each line is specific and inclusive. Guide posts it in the room.

2.1.5.SSH.4.standardStatement: Describe how families can share common values, offer emotional support, and set boundaries and limits.
2.1.5.SSH.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name one example each of a family value, a way families give support, and a family boundary.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one example under each of three headings on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide explains the three terms: values, emotional support, and boundaries. Each student writes one example under each heading on a card, such as honesty, a hug after a hard day, and a bedtime. Cards are read aloud without names. Guide fills any heading the group left thin.

understand-1.objective: Sort examples into values, support, and boundaries.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student places nine example cards under three labels.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair nine example cards and three labels: value, emotional support, and boundary. Students place each card under the right label. Pairs compare with a neighbor and discuss any card that fits more than one. Guide reveals fair placements and pairs adjust.

understand-2.objective: Explain in your own words why families set boundaries and limits.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences giving a reason families set limits.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that boundaries help keep family members safe and respected. Each student writes two sentences in plain language explaining why a family sets limits, such as a screen-time rule. Pairs trade and underline the clearer reason. Guide reads two aloud.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how emotional support helps a family member through a hard time.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence describing support given in a scene.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide reads a short scene where a family member is upset and another offers support. Each student writes one sentence summarizing the support and its effect. Pairs swap and compare. Guide reads two aloud and names the kind of support shown.

understand-4.objective: Explain how shared values can guide the choices a family makes.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph linking one value to one family choice.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews that values are beliefs a family holds. Each student writes a short paragraph naming one value and one family choice that value would guide, such as honesty guiding telling the truth. Pairs swap and check the link is clear. Guide collects paragraphs as an exit check.

apply-1.objective: Match three family situations to the value, support, or boundary they show.
