analyze-3.observableCheck: Each group names the persuasion technique and the accurate correction.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a misleading STI or HIV claim, such as a cure scam. Groups identify the technique that makes it look convincing, such as fake testimonials, and write the accurate correction from a vetted source. Groups post findings on a shared chart.

analyze-4.objective: Trace how a teen would verify an online STI claim step by step.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student maps the verification steps and marks where a false claim would fail the check.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students map the steps to verify an online STI claim, such as checking the author, the date, and a second accurate source. Each marks the step where a sample false claim would fail. They write one sentence on the most useful check. Pairs compare maps.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample STI fact sheet is medically accurate and justify the verdict.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student marks each claim accurate or not against a vetted source.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample STI fact sheet with some accurate and some false claims. Each student checks each claim against the vetted source and names the error that would most harm a reader. Pairs compare and settle disagreements with the source. The class decides whether the sheet is safe to share.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which single source you would tell a friend to use first for STI information.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each group presents one source with reasons tied to accuracy and access.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Groups choose the one source they would send a friend to first for STI and HIV information. They defend it with reasons tied to accuracy, currency, and ease of access. Each group presents and answers one challenge. The Guide records the reasoning behind the top choice.

create-1.objective: Assemble a class chart pairing each vetted source with the kind of question it answers.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student posts at least two verified source-question pairs.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide supplies source strips and question-type strips. Each student matches at least two pairs, checks them against the vetted list, and posts them on the class chart. The class reviews for any mismatched pair.

create-2.objective: Design a teen-friendly card that lists accurate STI sources and one prevention step.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a card naming at least two vetted sources and one accurate prevention fact.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a card listing at least two medically accurate sources and one accurate prevention fact, such as how testing works. They verify each entry against the vetted source. Students quiz a partner using the card, then cards form a class set.

create-3.objective: Produce a short PSA script directing teens to medically accurate STI information.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group writes a PSA script that names an accurate source and one action step.
create-3.sampleTask: Each group writes a 60-second PSA script telling teens where to find accurate STI and HIV information and ending with one action step, such as getting tested. The script must name a vetted source. Groups read scripts aloud while the class checks accuracy.

create-4.objective: Develop a guide that routes a teen's STI question to the right accurate source and service.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a flowchart routing three questions to a vetted source and a local service.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a flowchart taking a teen from a question to a medically accurate source and an approved local service if care is needed. They cover at least three questions and verify each path. Groups trade guides and trace one path to test it. The Guide reviews for accuracy before posting.

2.1.8.CHSS.6.standardStatement: Develop an advocacy plan regarding a health issue and share this information in an appropriate setting.
2.1.8.CHSS.6.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the parts of an advocacy plan: issue, audience, message, action, and setting.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists all five parts on a card after one review.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide reviews the five parts of an advocacy plan once, then each student writes them on a card without looking: issue, audience, message, action, and setting. The class assembles the full list and the Guide clarifies any part students could not name. The card stays in their folder for the unit.

understand-1.objective: Describe what each part of an advocacy plan does.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student matches the five plan parts to their purpose on a worksheet.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a worksheet pairing the five plan parts with scrambled descriptions of what each does. Each student matches them and writes one sentence on why the message must fit the audience. Pairs check answers and the Guide reviews.

understand-2.objective: Explain why an advocacy message must change with the audience.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes how one message would differ for peers versus for adults.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide gives one health issue and two audiences, peers and a school board. Each student writes how the message would change for each audience and why. Pairs compare and mark the biggest difference. The Guide reads a few aloud.

understand-3.objective: Group sample advocacy actions by how directly they reach the audience.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts sample actions from indirect to direct.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students sort sample advocacy actions, such as a poster, a petition, and a presentation, by how directly they reach the chosen audience. They label the ends of the scale and write one sentence on when a direct action is worth the effort. Pairs compare sorts.

understand-4.objective: Summarize what makes a setting appropriate for sharing an advocacy message.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short summary naming two features of an appropriate setting.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide provides examples of appropriate and poor settings for sharing a health message. Each student writes a three-sentence summary naming two features that make a setting appropriate, such as the audience being present and the timing fitting. Pairs check summaries against the examples.

apply-1.objective: Match a health issue to an appropriate audience and setting from options.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student pairs each of five issues with a fitting audience and setting.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out five health issues and option lists of audiences and settings. Each student pairs each issue with a fitting audience and setting. Students compare with a partner, then the Guide reviews any split answers.

apply-2.objective: Use the plan parts to draft a one-sentence advocacy message for a chosen issue.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a clear message naming the issue and the action they want.
apply-2.sampleTask: Each student picks a health issue and drafts a one-sentence advocacy message that names the issue and the action they want the audience to take. They check it against the five-part list. Pairs trade messages and mark whether the action is clear.

apply-3.objective: Produce a complete one-page advocacy plan covering all five parts.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page plan filling in issue, audience, message, action, and setting.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student builds a one-page advocacy plan for a health issue they care about, filling in all five parts. They make the message and action fit the chosen audience and setting. Students swap plans and check that every part is present and consistent.

apply-4.objective: Apply the plan to choose the best channel when the first setting is unavailable.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group names a backup setting and channel that still reaches the audience.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide gives a plan whose chosen setting falls through, such as an assembly being canceled. Groups name a backup setting and channel that still reaches the audience and explain why it works. Groups present and the class compares backup choices.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which part of a weak advocacy plan is missing or mismatched.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the flawed part in each of four sample plans.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives four sample plans, each with one weak or missing part, such as a message that does not fit the audience. Each student names the flawed part and writes a one-line fix. Pairs compare, then the Guide reviews the flaws.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two advocacy actions for the same issue to determine which would reach the audience better.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair completes a comparison chart and circles the stronger action with a reason.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Each pair gets one issue and two possible actions, such as a flyer versus a short presentation. They fill a comparison chart on reach, effort, and fit, then circle the stronger action and write one reason. Pairs defend the choice to another pair.

analyze-3.objective: Examine why a real advocacy message failed to move its audience.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each group names the breakdown and the part of the plan responsible.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a short case of an advocacy message that did not work, such as one aimed at the wrong audience. Groups identify why it failed and which of the five parts caused the breakdown. They write one change that would fix it. Groups post findings on a shared chart.

analyze-4.objective: Trace how a strong advocacy plan moves an audience from awareness to action.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student maps the steps from message to audience action and marks the key turning point.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students map how a strong plan takes an audience from first hearing the message to taking the action, naming each step. Each marks the turning point where the audience decides to act. They write one sentence on what makes that point work. Pairs compare maps.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a classmate's advocacy plan is ready to share and justify the verdict.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates a peer plan against a rubric and names the deciding weakness or strength.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students exchange draft advocacy plans. Using a rubric for clarity, audience fit, and feasibility, each rates a peer's plan and names the one part that most affects readiness. Pairs discuss and the reviewer writes one specific improvement. The author notes whether they agree.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which health issue would have the greatest impact if the class advocated on it.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each group presents one issue ranked with reasons tied to reach and need.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Groups choose the one health issue the class should advocate on for the most impact and defend it with reasons tied to how many students it affects and how fixable it is. Each group presents and answers one challenge. The class votes and the Guide records the reasoning behind the top choice.

create-1.objective: Assemble a class display of advocacy plan parts from supplied example pieces.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student places at least two example pieces under the correct plan part.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide supplies example pieces, such as sample messages, audiences, and actions. Each student selects at least two and places them under the correct plan part on a class display. The class reviews the display for any piece in the wrong spot.

create-2.objective: Design an advocacy poster that delivers a clear message and action for one issue.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster naming the issue, message, and a specific action.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a poster for a chosen health issue that states the message and asks the audience to take a specific action. They check that the message fits the intended setting. Students display posters and a partner checks that the action is clear.

create-3.objective: Produce a 90-second advocacy pitch and deliver it to the class.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student delivers a pitch that names the issue, message, and requested action.
create-3.sampleTask: Each student writes and delivers a 90-second advocacy pitch on their chosen issue, naming the message and the action they want. They rehearse once with a partner. The class scores each pitch on whether the action was clear using a quick checklist.

create-4.objective: Develop a full advocacy plan and the materials to deliver it in an appropriate setting.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a complete plan plus one finished delivery material such as a slide or flyer.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student develops a complete advocacy plan covering all five parts and builds one delivery material, such as a flyer, slide, or script, matched to the chosen setting. They check that the material carries the message and action. Students present the plan and material to a small group, who confirm every part is consistent.

2.1.8.CHSS.7.standardStatement: Collaborate with other students to develop a strategy to address health issues related to climate change.
2.1.8.CHSS.7.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List three local health issues that get worse during extreme heat, poor air quality, or flooding.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student names three climate-linked health issues on a sticky note posted to the class wall.
remember-1.sampleTask: Each student writes three health issues that climate events make worse in their town, one per sticky note, and posts them on a labeled wall chart with columns for heat, air, and water. The Guide reads the wall aloud and removes duplicates. Students copy the combined list into their notebook. The class keeps this list as the source for later cells.
materials: wall chart with three labeled columns

understand-1.objective: Explain in one sentence how one climate event raises a specific health risk for a named group.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a 'because' sentence linking a climate event to a health risk for a group such as elders or asthmatics.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student picks one issue from the class wall and writes a sentence in the form 'Heat waves raise heat stroke risk for outdoor workers because ...'. Pairs swap sentences and underline the cause and the group. The Guide collects two strong examples and reads them to the class. Students fix any sentence that names no group.

understand-2.objective: Sort a mixed set of climate health facts into causes and effects on a two-column organizer.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each pair places at least eight fact cards correctly in cause and effect columns.
understand-2.sampleTask: Pairs receive twelve fact cards about climate and health, such as 'wildfire smoke' and 'more emergency room visits for breathing trouble'. They place each card under cause or effect on a two-column mat and draw arrows linking matched pairs. The Guide checks three mats and asks each pair to defend one arrow. Pairs write the three clearest cause-effect pairs into their notebook.

understand-3.objective: Summarize a short article on a climate health problem and restate the main risk in your own words.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-sentence summary that names the problem, who it harms, and one driver.
understand-3.sampleTask: Each student reads a one-page article on a climate health problem such as longer pollen seasons. They write a three-sentence summary naming the problem, the group harmed, and one driver, without copying full lines from the text. Pairs check each other for copied phrasing and mark it. Students revise any copied line into their own words.

understand-4.objective: Trace how one climate change driver leads through two steps to a community health outcome.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student draws a three-box chain linking a driver, an intermediate change, and a health outcome with labeled arrows.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student builds a three-box flow on paper, for example 'hotter summers' to 'more ground-level ozone' to 'more asthma attacks', and labels each arrow with the word 'causes'. Students explain their chain to a partner in under a minute. The Guide picks two chains to display and asks the class to spot any missing middle step. Students add a missing step where a partner flagged a jump.

apply-1.objective: Match each listed climate health risk to one prevention action a school could take.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student pairs at least five risks with a plausible school prevention action on a matching sheet.
apply-1.sampleTask: Each student receives a sheet listing climate health risks in one column and blank lines for actions. They write one school action per risk, such as pairing 'heat stress at recess' with 'shade tents and water breaks'. Pairs compare and keep the action that costs less to start. The Guide collects the strongest action for each risk on the board.

apply-2.objective: Carry out a short survey of classmates to find which climate health issue worries them most.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each group reports a tallied count for each issue from at least ten classmates.
apply-2.sampleTask: Groups of three write three survey questions, ask ten or more classmates which climate health issue worries them most, and tally answers on a chart. They convert the top tally into a single sentence such as 'Most students chose air quality'. The Guide checks that each chart shows a clear count. Groups post their tally chart on the wall.

apply-3.objective: Apply a problem-and-action template to draft a first strategy step for one chosen climate health issue.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each group fills a template naming the issue, the target group, and one concrete action with a doer.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each group picks one issue from the class data and completes a template with four fields: the issue, who it harms most, one action, and who does the action. They keep the action specific, such as 'the green team adds two water stations by the field'. The Guide reviews each template and circles any vague action. Groups rewrite circled actions to name an exact doer and place.

apply-4.objective: Use a decision grid to pick one action from several based on cost, reach, and speed.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group marks scores in three columns and circles the action with the best total.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each group lists three candidate actions in a grid and rates each from one to three on cost, reach, and speed. They add the scores and circle the highest total, then write one sentence defending the pick. The Guide spot-checks two grids and asks why one low-scoring action lost. Groups attach the grid to their strategy draft.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which part of a sample student strategy is the weakest link and name why.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks one weak step in a sample strategy and writes a one-line reason.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Each student reads a sample strategy that addresses school flooding and marks the step most likely to fail, such as an action with no named doer. They write one line explaining the weakness. Pairs compare marks and settle disagreements by pointing to the text. The Guide records the most common weak step on the board.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two proposed strategies and explain which better fits the group the issue harms most.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes a short verdict naming which strategy fits the target group and one reason.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs read two strategies for reducing heat illness, one adding shade and one shifting recess times. They build a quick T-chart of who each helps and write a verdict naming the better fit for the target group. The Guide asks two pairs to defend their verdict aloud. Pairs revise the verdict if the discussion changes their reason.

analyze-3.objective: Break the group's draft strategy into steps and flag any step that depends on a missing resource.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each group lists ordered steps and tags every step needing a resource the school lacks.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Each group rewrites its draft strategy as numbered steps and writes the resource each step needs in the margin. They tag any resource the school does not have with a star. The Guide reviews tags and asks how each group could replace one starred resource. Groups note one workaround beside each star.

analyze-4.objective: Examine survey and article evidence to judge whether the chosen issue is the right priority for the school.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each group writes a claim about priority backed by one survey figure and one article fact.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Each group pulls one number from its class survey and one fact from an article and writes a claim such as 'Air quality is our top priority because 14 of 20 students chose it and smoke days rose last year'. They mark which piece is opinion and which is evidence. The Guide checks that the claim cites both sources. Groups fix any claim that leans on opinion alone.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a peer group's strategy against a three-part rubric and recommend keep, change, or cut for each step.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each reviewer records a keep, change, or cut verdict with a reason for every step.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Groups trade strategies and score each step against a rubric covering clear doer, real reach, and low cost. For each step the reviewer writes keep, change, or cut plus a one-line reason. The Guide reads two verdicts and asks the author group to respond. Reviewers hand the marked rubric back to the authors.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend the group's final strategy against one strong objection raised by a classmate.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers a 60-second rebuttal that answers the objection with evidence, not opinion.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Each group hears one prepared objection to its strategy, such as 'the action is too expensive', and gives a 60-second rebuttal citing its cost grid or survey. A classmate scores whether the rebuttal used evidence. The Guide notes any group that fell back on opinion. Groups write one sentence strengthening the weak part of their answer.

create-1.objective: Assemble the group's chosen steps into a single labeled one-page strategy poster.
create-1.observableCheck: Each group produces a poster showing the issue, target group, ordered steps, and doers.
create-1.sampleTask: Each group lays out its strategy on one poster with a title, the issue, the target group, numbered steps, and a doer beside each step. They add a one-line goal at the top. The Guide checks that every step shows a doer. Groups hang the poster in the strategy gallery.

create-2.objective: Design a short action plan that schedules the strategy steps across four weeks with owners.
create-2.observableCheck: Each group fills a four-week calendar assigning each step a week and an owner.
create-2.sampleTask: Each group transfers its strategy steps onto a four-week calendar, placing each step in a week and naming an owner. They mark which step must finish before the next can start. The Guide checks for steps with no owner. Groups adjust the order so no step waits on an unfinished one.

create-3.objective: Produce a one-minute pitch script that asks a specific school decision-maker to approve the strategy.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group writes a script naming the decision-maker, the ask, and the expected health benefit.
create-3.sampleTask: Each group writes a one-minute script aimed at a named person such as the principal, opening with the health problem and closing with a clear ask. They include one figure from their data. The Guide times a read of two scripts and flags any over a minute. Groups trim wording until the script fits the minute.

create-4.objective: Build a combined class strategy that merges the strongest step from each group into one plan.
create-4.observableCheck: The class produces one master plan listing each kept step, its source group, and its owner.
create-4.sampleTask: Each group nominates its strongest step, and the class debates which steps to merge into one master plan without repeats. A scribe records each kept step, the group it came from, and an owner on chart paper. The Guide settles ties by a quick show of hands. The class posts the master plan as the term climate health strategy.

2.1.8.CHSS.8.standardStatement: Analyze difficult situations that might lead to feelings sadness, anxiety and or depression and identify individuals, agencies or places in the community where assistance may be available.
2.1.8.CHSS.8.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List four community sources of help for someone feeling sad, anxious, or depressed.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four named sources such as school counselor or a crisis line on an index card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Each student writes four places or people in the community where someone could get help for low mood on an index card, one per line. The Guide builds a master list on the board from the cards and removes repeats. Students copy any source they missed into their notebook. The class keeps this list posted for the rest of the lessons.

understand-1.objective: Explain in your own words the difference between everyday sadness and lasting depression.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences distinguishing a passing mood from a lasting pattern.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student reads a short fact box on mood and writes two sentences, one describing a passing sad feeling and one describing a pattern that lasts for weeks. Pairs swap and underline the time clue in each sentence. The Guide reads two clear pairs aloud. Students revise any sentence that gives no time clue.

understand-2.objective: Match each community help source to the kind of situation it best fits.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student links at least five sources to fitting situations on a matching sheet.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student matches help sources to situations on a two-column sheet, such as pairing 'school counselor' with 'trouble coping during the school day'. Pairs compare and discuss any source that fits more than one situation. The Guide asks two students to defend a tricky match. Students mark sources that fit several situations with a star.

understand-3.objective: Summarize a short scenario and name the feeling the person in it is likely experiencing.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-sentence summary naming the situation and the likely feeling.
understand-3.sampleTask: Each student reads a one-paragraph scenario about a teen facing a hard change and writes two sentences naming the situation and the feeling it could trigger. Pairs check that the named feeling matches the clues in the text. The Guide collects two strong summaries. Students fix any summary where the feeling does not match the clues.

understand-4.objective: Describe how one hard situation can build into stronger feelings over time if no help arrives.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student draws a three-stage timeline showing a situation, a feeling, and a worsening point.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student draws a three-stage timeline for a scenario, marking the trigger, the early feeling, and a point where feelings worsen without support. They label where reaching out could change the path. Pairs compare timelines and discuss the turning point. Students add an arrow showing where help could break the chain.

apply-1.objective: Choose the right first contact for each of several sample situations.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student names one fitting contact for at least four sample situations.
apply-1.sampleTask: Each student reads four short situations and writes the best first contact for each, drawing from the class help list. Pairs compare picks and resolve gaps by pointing to what each source does. The Guide records the most common picks for two situations. Students correct any pick that does not match the situation.

apply-2.objective: Role-play asking a trusted adult for help using a clear opening line.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student delivers a practiced opening line that names the feeling and the ask.
apply-2.sampleTask: In pairs, students take turns playing a teen and a trusted adult, opening with a line such as 'I have felt down for weeks and I want to talk to someone'. The listener notes whether the opener named both a feeling and an ask. The Guide circulates and models one strong opener. Students rewrite their opener if it skipped the ask.

apply-3.objective: Build a personal help card listing two adults and two community sources with how to reach each.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student completes a card with four contacts and a reach method for each.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student fills a wallet-size card with two trusted adults and two community sources, writing how to reach each, such as a room number or a phone line. They keep entries specific, not 'a friend'. The Guide checks that each contact has a real reach method. Students replace any vague contact with a specific one.

apply-4.objective: Use a quick decision flow to pick the right level of help for a rising-concern scenario.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student follows a flowchart and lands on a defensible help level for the scenario.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student walks a printed flowchart that asks about how long feelings have lasted and whether safety is a concern, then lands on a help level from talk-to-an-adult to call-a-crisis-line. They write the path they took. Pairs compare endpoints for the same scenario. The Guide reviews any mismatched endpoints and clarifies the safety branch.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which clues in a scenario signal that a feeling may be more than a passing mood.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student underlines at least three warning clues in a scenario text.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Each student reads a scenario and underlines clues such as 'for weeks', 'stopped seeing friends', or 'cannot sleep'. They list the clues in the margin. Pairs compare underlines and add any missed clue. The Guide records the agreed clue list on the board.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two community sources and explain which fits a given situation and why.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes a verdict naming the better-fit source with one reason tied to the situation.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs read a situation and compare two sources, such as a school counselor and a community clinic, listing what each offers. They write a verdict naming the better fit and one reason linked to the situation. The Guide asks two pairs to defend their choice. Pairs revise the reason if it does not connect to the situation.

analyze-3.objective: Break a hard situation into its triggers and sort which are within the teen's control.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student lists triggers and labels each as in or out of the teen's control.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Each student lists the triggers in a scenario and labels each as something the teen can change or cannot change. They circle one out-of-control trigger and note who could help with it. Pairs compare labels and debate any disagreement. Students adjust a label after the discussion if the evidence shifts.

analyze-4.objective: Examine a help-seeking story to judge what worked and what blocked the person from getting help.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student names one factor that helped and one barrier, each tied to a line in the story.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Each student reads a short story of a teen who eventually got help and marks one moment that helped and one barrier that delayed it, citing the line. They write a sentence on each. Pairs compare the barriers they found. The Guide lists common barriers and asks how the class help list could lower one.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a sample help plan against a rubric for specificity, fit, and safety.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a sample plan on three rubric rows and writes one fix for the lowest row.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Each student scores a sample help plan against a rubric covering specific contacts, fit to the situation, and a safety step. They mark the lowest-scoring row and write one concrete fix. Pairs compare scores and settle gaps by citing the rubric. The Guide reads two fixes that target the safety row.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend a recommended help source for a tough scenario against a classmate's challenge.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student answers one challenge with a reason tied to what the source actually does.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Each student presents a recommended source for a scenario, then a classmate raises one challenge such as 'that source costs money'. The presenter answers with a reason grounded in the source's role or a backup option. The Guide notes any answer that ignored the challenge. Students write one sentence improving their weakest answer.

create-1.objective: Assemble a class resource poster listing community help sources with how to reach each.
create-1.observableCheck: The class produces one poster where every source shows a reach method.
create-1.sampleTask: Groups each contribute three vetted sources, and the class arranges them on one poster grouped by school, local agency, and crisis line. A scribe writes a reach method beside each. The Guide checks that no source lacks a method. The class hangs the poster by the classroom door.

create-2.objective: Design a short skit that models a teen recognizing a warning sign and reaching out.
create-2.observableCheck: Each group performs a skit that shows a warning sign and a clear help-seeking step.
create-2.sampleTask: Each group plans a 90-second skit where a character notices a lasting low mood and reaches out to a named source. They write a four-line script and assign roles. The Guide reviews scripts to confirm the help step is clear and respectful. Groups rehearse once before performing for the class.

create-3.objective: Produce a one-page guide for a younger student on what to do when a friend seems down.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group writes a guide with at least three steps and one community contact.
create-3.sampleTask: Each group writes a one-page guide aimed at a fifth grader, listing three steps such as listen, encourage talking to an adult, and share a contact. They keep wording simple and kind. The Guide checks reading level and that one community contact appears. Groups swap guides and mark any step a younger reader might not understand.

create-4.objective: Build a decision tool that routes any of three situations to a fitting community source.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a flowchart that routes all three test situations to defensible endpoints.
create-4.sampleTask: Each group designs a flowchart with yes-or-no questions that routes a worried teen to a fitting source, then tests it against three given situations. They adjust branches until all three land somewhere defensible. The Guide runs one extra situation through a group's chart to test it. Groups post the chart beside the class resource poster.

2.1.8.EH.1.standardStatement: Compare and contrast stress management strategies that are used to address various types of stress-induced situations (e.g., academics, family, personal relationships, finances, celebrations, violence).
2.1.8.EH.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List six stress management strategies and one situation each can address.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student records six strategies with a paired situation on a chart.
remember-1.sampleTask: Each student fills a six-row chart with a stress strategy in one column and a fitting situation in the other, such as 'deep breathing' for 'before a test'. The Guide builds a shared list on the board from student charts. Students add any strategy they missed. The class keeps the strategy list posted.

understand-1.objective: Explain how one strategy lowers stress in a specific situation.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'because' sentence linking a strategy to its effect in a situation.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student writes a sentence such as 'Making a study plan lowers test stress because it breaks the work into clear steps'. Pairs swap and underline the cause. The Guide reads two clear examples. Students revise any sentence that states no effect.

understand-2.objective: Sort strategies into ones that calm the body and ones that solve the problem.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student places at least eight strategies into the correct of two columns.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student sorts strategy cards into 'calm the body' such as breathing and 'solve the problem' such as making a plan, on a two-column mat. Pairs compare and discuss any card that could fit both. The Guide asks two students to defend a placement. Students mark dual-fit cards with a star.

understand-3.objective: Summarize a short scenario and name the stressor type it shows.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-sentence summary naming the situation and its stressor type.
understand-3.sampleTask: Each student reads a scenario and writes two sentences naming what happened and whether the stressor is academic, family, relationship, financial, celebration, or safety related. Pairs check that the labeled type fits the text. The Guide collects two strong summaries. Students fix any mislabeled type.

understand-4.objective: Describe how the same stressor could call for different strategies at different moments.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names two strategies for one stressor and explains when each fits.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student picks one stressor, such as a family argument, and writes two strategies plus the moment each fits, for example breathing in the heat of it and a talk later when calm. Pairs compare timing choices. The Guide highlights one example where timing changed the strategy. Students add a timing note to any strategy that lacked one.

apply-1.objective: Choose a fitting strategy for each of several stress situations.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student names one fitting strategy for at least five situations.
apply-1.sampleTask: Each student reads five situations and writes one fitting strategy for each, drawing from the class list. Pairs compare and resolve gaps by checking the situation type. The Guide records common picks for two situations. Students correct a pick that does not match the situation.

apply-2.objective: Practice one calming strategy and record its effect on a one-to-ten stress rating.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student logs a before and after stress rating around a practiced strategy.
apply-2.sampleTask: Each student rates current stress from one to ten, practices a guided strategy such as paced breathing for two minutes, then rates again. They write one line on what changed. Pairs share ratings and note who felt a shift. The Guide charts class before-and-after averages on the board.

apply-3.objective: Build a two-strategy plan for a personal upcoming stress situation.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a plan naming the situation, two strategies, and when to use each.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student names one real upcoming stressor and writes a plan with two strategies and the moment to use each. They keep the strategies specific to the situation type. The Guide checks that each strategy fits the named stressor. Students swap a strategy that does not fit.

apply-4.objective: Use a fit grid to pick the best strategy for a situation based on speed, place, and effort.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student scores candidates in three columns and circles the best fit.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student lists three strategies for one situation in a grid and rates each on speed, where it can be done, and effort. They circle the best total and write a one-line reason. Pairs compare circled picks. The Guide asks why a low-scoring strategy lost.

analyze-1.objective: Identify why a chosen strategy would fail in a mismatched situation.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names a mismatch and explains the failure in one sentence.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Each student reads a pairing such as 'use a long workout to calm down right before a test' and writes why it fails, for example no time and no place. Pairs compare reasons. The Guide lists common mismatch reasons on the board. Students revise any reason that names no specific clash.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two strategies for the same stressor and explain which fits better and why.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes a verdict naming the better strategy with one situation-tied reason.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs read one stressor and compare two strategies on a T-chart of strengths and limits. They write a verdict naming the better fit and one reason tied to the situation. The Guide asks two pairs to defend their verdict. Pairs revise the reason if the discussion shifts it.

analyze-3.objective: Break a multi-stressor scenario into its parts and assign a strategy to each part.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student lists each stressor in a scenario and pairs it with a fitting strategy.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Each student reads a scenario carrying two or three stressors, such as a money worry plus a friendship rift, lists each, and writes a fitting strategy beside it. Pairs compare which stressor they tackled first. The Guide asks how to order the strategies. Students number their strategies in a sensible order.

analyze-4.objective: Examine class rating data to judge which strategy lowered stress most across the room.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim about the most effective strategy backed by a rating figure.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Each student reads the posted before-and-after class data and writes a claim naming the strategy with the largest average drop, citing the numbers. They note one limit of the data, such as a small sample. Pairs compare claims. The Guide records the agreed top strategy and the noted limit.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a peer's stress plan against a rubric for fit, specificity, and timing.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each reviewer scores three rubric rows and writes one fix for the weakest row.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students trade plans and score each against a rubric covering fit to the stressor, specific strategies, and clear timing. The reviewer marks the weakest row and writes one fix. Pairs return marked rubrics and discuss one disagreement. The Guide reads two fixes aimed at timing.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend a strategy choice for a hard scenario against a classmate's objection.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student answers one objection with a reason tied to the situation, not a slogan.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Each student presents a strategy for a tough scenario, then a classmate objects, for example 'that will not work during a real argument'. The presenter answers with a reason grounded in the situation or names a backup. The Guide flags any slogan answer. Students write one sentence strengthening their answer.

create-1.objective: Assemble a labeled class strategy menu grouped by stressor type.
create-1.observableCheck: The class produces one menu where each stressor type lists at least two strategies.
create-1.sampleTask: Groups each contribute strategies for one stressor type, and the class arranges them on a single menu poster grouped by type. A scribe checks each type has at least two strategies. The Guide removes repeats. The class posts the menu for daily reference.

create-2.objective: Design a one-week stress plan that schedules a strategy against each known stress point.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a seven-day plan pairing each stress point with a strategy.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student maps known stress points across a week, such as a Monday quiz and a Friday game, and assigns a fitting strategy and time to each. They mark one day to check how it went. The Guide reviews for empty stress points. Students fill any stress point that lacks a strategy.

create-3.objective: Produce a short how-to card teaching one strategy step by step to a peer.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a card with ordered steps a peer could follow without help.
create-3.sampleTask: Each student writes a how-to card for one strategy, such as box breathing, with numbered steps and a note on when to use it. A partner tests the card by following only the written steps. The Guide checks that the steps stand alone. Students fix any step a partner could not follow.

create-4.objective: Build a decision guide that points a stressed person to a strategy based on three questions.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a guide that routes three test situations to fitting strategies.
create-4.sampleTask: Each group designs a guide with three questions, such as how much time is there and is the body or the problem the issue, that points to a strategy. They test it on three situations and adjust branches. The Guide runs one fresh situation through the guide. Groups post the finished guide beside the strategy menu.

2.1.8.EH.2.standardStatement: Analyze how personal attributes, resiliency, and protective factors support mental and emotional health.
2.1.8.EH.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List five protective factors that support mental and emotional health.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student records five protective factors on a card, such as a trusted adult or a hobby.
remember-1.sampleTask: Each student writes five protective factors on an index card, drawing from a word bank that includes supportive relationships, routines, and skills. The Guide builds a class list on the board from the cards. Students add any factor they missed. The class keeps the list posted.

understand-1.objective: Explain in one sentence how one protective factor supports emotional health.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'because' sentence linking a factor to an emotional health benefit.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student writes a sentence such as 'A steady sleep routine supports mood because it keeps energy and focus stable'. Pairs swap and underline the benefit. The Guide reads two clear examples. Students revise any sentence that names no benefit.

understand-2.objective: Sort a mixed set of traits into personal attributes and outside protective factors.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student places at least eight items into the correct of two columns.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student sorts cards into 'personal attributes' such as patience and 'outside factors' such as a mentor, on a two-column mat. Pairs compare and discuss any card that could be both. The Guide asks two students to defend a placement. Students star cards that bridge both columns.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how resiliency helps a person recover after a setback.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-sentence summary defining resiliency and giving one example.
understand-3.sampleTask: Each student reads a short text on resiliency and writes three sentences defining it, naming one way it shows up, and giving one example from the text or their life. Pairs check that the example fits the definition. The Guide collects two strong summaries. Students fix any example that does not show recovery.

understand-4.objective: Describe how several protective factors can work together to support one person.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student draws a web linking a person to three factors with a note on each link.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student draws a web with a person at the center and three protective factors around it, labeling each link with how it helps. They mark which factor they could strengthen. Pairs explain their webs to each other. Students add one note where a partner found a missing link.

apply-1.objective: Identify which protective factors are present in a sample student's life from a profile.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least four factors found in a profile with the line that shows each.
apply-1.sampleTask: Each student reads a one-page profile of a fictional teen and lists the protective factors it shows, citing the line for each. Pairs compare lists and add any missed factor. The Guide records the agreed list on the board. Students correct any factor not backed by a line.

apply-2.objective: Map your own protective factors onto a personal asset chart.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a chart naming personal attributes, relationships, and routines they rely on.
apply-2.sampleTask: Each student fills an asset chart with three columns for personal attributes, supportive people, and helpful routines, writing at least two real entries in each. They keep entries specific, not 'family'. The Guide checks that each column has entries. Students replace any vague entry with a specific one. This chart stays private unless the student chooses to share.

apply-3.objective: Apply a strengthening plan to one protective factor the student wants to build.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a plan naming the factor, one action, and when to do it.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student picks one factor to strengthen, such as a steady sleep routine, and writes a plan with one concrete action and a time to start. They name how they will know it worked. The Guide checks that the action is doable this week. Students trim any action that is too large to start.

apply-4.objective: Use a quick screen to decide which protective factor to build first based on need and ease.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student scores factors on need and ease and circles the first to build.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student lists three factors to build, rates each on how much it is needed and how easy it is to start, and circles the best first pick. They write a one-line reason. Pairs compare picks. The Guide asks why a high-need but hard factor was or was not chosen.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which factor in a profile is doing the most to support the person's health.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the strongest factor in a profile and cites the supporting line.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Each student rereads a profile and marks the factor that seems to carry the most weight, citing the line that shows it. They write one sentence on why. Pairs compare and debate any disagreement using the text. The Guide records the most-named factor.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two profiles and explain how differing protective factors lead to different outcomes.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes a contrast naming one factor difference and its effect on each teen.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs read two profiles of teens facing a similar setback and build a T-chart of the factors each has. They write a sentence naming one factor one teen has that the other lacks and how it shaped the outcome. The Guide asks two pairs to defend the link. Pairs revise if the link is not in the text.

analyze-3.objective: Break a setback story into the moments where a protective factor changed the path.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student marks at least two points where a factor altered the story's direction.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Each student reads a story of a teen recovering from a setback and marks each point where a protective factor changed the direction, noting the factor. They list these turning points. Pairs compare the points they found. The Guide asks how the story would differ if one factor were missing.

analyze-4.objective: Examine class asset data to judge which protective factors are most and least common.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim about a common and a rare factor backed by a tally.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Using an anonymous class tally of which factor types students listed, each student writes a claim naming the most and least common factor, citing the counts. They note one reason a factor might be rare. Pairs compare claims. The Guide records the agreed pattern and asks what support a rare factor might need.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a peer's strengthening plan against a rubric for fit, doability, and a clear check.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each reviewer scores three rubric rows and writes one fix for the weakest row.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students trade plans and score each against a rubric covering fit to a real need, a doable action, and a clear way to check progress. The reviewer marks the weakest row and writes one fix. Pairs return rubrics and discuss one disagreement. The Guide reads two fixes aimed at the check row.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend a claim about which protective factor matters most for a given teen against a challenge.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student answers one challenge with evidence from the profile, not a guess.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Each student claims which factor matters most for a profiled teen, then a classmate challenges the pick. The presenter answers with a line from the profile or revises the claim. The Guide flags any answer with no textual support. Students write one sentence improving their weakest point.

create-1.objective: Assemble a class poster of protective factors grouped by self, people, and routines.
create-1.observableCheck: The class produces one poster with each group holding at least three factors.
create-1.sampleTask: Groups each contribute factors for one group, and the class arranges them on a single poster under self, people, and routines. A scribe checks each group has at least three. The Guide removes repeats. The class posts the factor map on the wall.

create-2.objective: Design a personal resiliency toolkit page listing factors and how to reach each in a hard moment.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a page naming factors and one access step for each.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student builds a one-page toolkit listing their top protective factors and a step to reach each in a hard moment, such as which adult to text. They keep entries specific. The Guide checks that each factor has an access step. Students add a step to any factor that lacked one. The page stays private unless the student shares it.

create-3.objective: Produce a short advice piece for a younger student on building one protective factor.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a piece with three steps a younger reader could follow.
create-3.sampleTask: Each student writes a half-page advice piece for a younger student on building one factor, such as making a steady friend, with three simple steps. A partner checks the reading level and the steps. The Guide confirms the advice is realistic. Students simplify any step a younger reader could not follow.

create-4.objective: Build a class action that strengthens one shared protective factor for the whole group.
create-4.observableCheck: The class produces one plan naming a shared factor, an action, and owners.
create-4.sampleTask: Each group proposes one action that would strengthen a shared protective factor, such as a weekly check-in circle, and the class picks one to run. A scribe records the action, the steps, and owners on chart paper. The Guide settles the choice with a vote. The class posts the plan and sets a start date.

2.1.8.PGD.1.standardStatement: Explain how appropriate health care can promote personal health.
2.1.8.PGD.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List five types of health care and one purpose each serves.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student records five care types with a purpose, such as a dental check to prevent decay.
remember-1.sampleTask: Each student fills a five-row chart with a care type in one column and its purpose in the other, drawing from a word bank that includes checkups, dental visits, vision exams, vaccines, and screenings. The Guide builds a class list on the board. Students add any type they missed. The class keeps the list posted.

understand-1.objective: Explain in one sentence how one type of care promotes health.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'because' sentence linking a care type to a health benefit.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student writes a sentence such as 'A yearly checkup promotes health because it catches problems before they grow'. Pairs swap and underline the benefit. The Guide reads two clear examples. Students revise any sentence that names no benefit.

understand-2.objective: Sort care actions into prevention and treatment.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student places at least eight care actions into the correct of two columns.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student sorts cards into 'prevention' such as a vaccine and 'treatment' such as a cast for a break, on a two-column mat. Pairs compare and discuss any card that could be both. The Guide asks two students to defend a placement. Students star cards that fit both.

understand-3.objective: Summarize a short text on how regular checkups support long-term health.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-sentence summary naming the care, the benefit, and one example.
understand-3.sampleTask: Each student reads a one-page text on routine care and writes three sentences naming the care type, the long-term benefit, and one example from the text. Pairs check for copied lines and mark them. The Guide collects two strong summaries. Students reword any copied line.

understand-4.objective: Describe how skipping one type of care can lead through steps to a worse health outcome.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student draws a three-box chain from a skipped care action to a health outcome.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student draws a three-box chain such as 'skip dental visits' to 'cavity grows' to 'tooth pain and missed school', labeling each arrow. They mark where care could break the chain. Pairs compare chains and check for missing steps. Students add any step a partner flagged.

apply-1.objective: Match each health need to the right type of care or provider.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student pairs at least five needs with a fitting care type on a matching sheet.
apply-1.sampleTask: Each student matches needs to care on a sheet, such as pairing 'blurry vision in class' with 'an eye exam'. Pairs compare and resolve gaps by checking the need. The Guide records common picks for two needs. Students correct any pick that does not fit the need.

apply-2.objective: Carry out a short interview to find what routine care a family member or trusted adult uses.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student records at least three care types from an interview on a log.
apply-2.sampleTask: Each student interviews a trusted adult with three set questions about which routine care they use and why, and logs the answers. They keep names off the log for privacy. The Guide checks that each log shows at least three care types. Students bring the log to the next lesson as data.

apply-3.objective: Build a personal care calendar marking when key checkups and screenings are due.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a calendar listing at least four care events with rough timing.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student fills a year calendar with key care events, such as a dental visit every six months and a yearly checkup, placing each in a rough month. They mark which is overdue. The Guide checks that each event has timing. Students adjust any event with no timing. The calendar stays private unless shared.

apply-4.objective: Use a priority grid to decide which overdue care to schedule first based on urgency and ease.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student scores overdue items on urgency and ease and circles the first to schedule.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student lists overdue or upcoming care, rates each on urgency and ease of getting it, and circles the first to book. They write a one-line reason. Pairs compare picks. The Guide asks why an urgent but hard item was or was not chosen.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which care choice in a sample profile did the most to keep the person healthy.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the most useful care choice in a profile and cites the line.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Each student reads a profile of a teen's care history and marks the choice that did the most for their health, citing the line. They write one sentence on why. Pairs compare and debate using the text. The Guide records the most-named choice.

analyze-2.objective: Compare a person who gets routine care with one who only seeks care when sick and explain the difference.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes a contrast naming one outcome difference tied to the care pattern.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs read two short profiles, one with routine care and one with only sick visits, and build a T-chart of outcomes. They write a sentence naming one outcome difference and tying it to the care pattern. The Guide asks two pairs to defend the link. Pairs revise if the link is not in the text.

analyze-3.objective: Break a care decision into the barriers a person faced and sort which are fixable.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student lists barriers in a scenario and labels each as fixable or hard to fix.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Each student reads a scenario where a teen delayed care, lists the barriers such as cost or transport, and labels each fixable or hard to fix. They circle one fixable barrier and note a step to address it. Pairs compare labels. Students adjust a label after discussion.

analyze-4.objective: Examine class interview data to judge which routine care is most and least used.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim about a common and a rare care type backed by a tally.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Using a class tally of care types from the interviews, each student writes a claim naming the most and least used care, citing counts. They note one reason a care type might be skipped. Pairs compare claims. The Guide records the pattern and asks what could raise use of the rare type.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a sample care plan against a rubric for coverage, timing, and fit to needs.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores three rubric rows and writes one fix for the weakest row.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Each student scores a sample care plan against a rubric covering whether it covers key care, gives clear timing, and fits the person's needs. They mark the weakest row and write one fix. Pairs compare scores and settle gaps by citing the rubric. The Guide reads two fixes aimed at timing.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend a recommendation for which care a profiled teen should get first against a challenge.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student answers one challenge with a reason tied to the profile, not a guess.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Each student recommends the first care a profiled teen should seek, then a classmate challenges it, for example 'something else is more urgent'. The presenter answers with a line from the profile or revises. The Guide flags any answer with no support. Students write one sentence improving their weakest point.

create-1.objective: Assemble a class poster of care types grouped by prevention, screening, and treatment.
create-1.observableCheck: The class produces one poster with each group holding at least three care types.
create-1.sampleTask: Groups each contribute care types for one group, and the class arranges them on a single poster under prevention, screening, and treatment. A scribe checks each group has at least three. The Guide removes repeats. The class posts the care map.

create-2.objective: Design a one-page checkup readiness sheet that lists what to bring and ask at a visit.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a sheet with at least three items to bring and three questions to ask.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student builds a one-page sheet for a checkup with a column of things to bring, such as a symptom list, and a column of questions to ask the provider. The Guide checks that each column has at least three entries. Students add an entry to any short column. The sheet goes home for real use.

create-3.objective: Produce a short explainer for a younger student on why routine checkups matter.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes an explainer with one reason and one example a younger reader understands.
create-3.sampleTask: Each student writes a half-page explainer for a younger student giving one clear reason routine checkups matter and one simple example. A partner checks the reading level. The Guide confirms the reason is accurate. Students simplify any line a younger reader could not follow.

create-4.objective: Build a class guide that routes a health need to the right care or provider.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a guide that routes three test needs to fitting care.
create-4.sampleTask: Each group designs a guide with questions, such as is this a prevention need or a problem now, that routes a need to the right care. They test it on three needs and adjust branches. The Guide runs one fresh need through the guide. Groups post the guide beside the care map.

2.1.8.PGD.2.standardStatement: Analyze how genetics and family history can impact personal health.
2.1.8.PGD.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name three health conditions that can run in families.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists three inherited or family-linked conditions on an index card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name three health conditions a doctor might ask about when they ask for your family history.' Each student writes three on an index card from memory and prior reading. Cards go on a class chart sorted into 'physical' and 'mental health' columns. Guide reads a few aloud and confirms each is a real family-linked condition.

understand-1.objective: Match common conditions to the body system each one affects.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student completes an eight-pair matching worksheet linking a condition to its body system.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a worksheet with eight family-linked conditions in one column (such as type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, asthma) and body systems scrambled in the other. Students draw lines to match each condition to the system it affects. Pairs trade and check. Guide reviews answers on the board.

understand-2.objective: Explain the difference between a trait you inherit and a habit you learn from family.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts ten examples into 'inherited' and 'learned' and writes one sentence defining each term.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students get a sorting sheet of ten examples, such as eye color, eating fried food often, blood type, and going for evening walks. They sort each into 'inherited' or 'learned from family' and write a one-sentence definition of each category. Pairs compare sorts and resolve any they disagree on. Guide closes with a quick poll on the trickiest items.

understand-3.objective: Summarize why a doctor asks for family medical history at a checkup.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-sentence explanation in their own words and reads it to a partner.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide shows a short sample intake form with a 'family history' section. Students write three sentences explaining why a doctor wants this information and what the doctor can do with it. Partners read each other's answers and underline the clearest sentence. Guide collects to check understanding.

understand-4.objective: Describe how the same family risk can show up differently in two relatives.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph using a given family example to show two different outcomes.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives a one-paragraph scenario about a family with a history of heart disease where one parent develops it young and an aunt never does. Students write a paragraph describing how the same family risk led to different outcomes and name one reason why. Pairs trade and check that both outcomes are explained. Guide shares two strong paragraphs.

apply-1.objective: Fill in a blank three-generation family health template using a provided sample family.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student completes the template with each relative's conditions placed in the correct generation row.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a fictional family description and a blank three-generation template. Students place each relative and note any listed condition in the correct row. Guide circulates to confirm placement. Finished templates are checked against a key.

apply-2.objective: Use a provided risk chart to flag which conditions a sample patient should watch for.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student highlights at least two conditions on the sample chart and writes why each was flagged.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students receive a sample patient's filled family tree and a simple risk chart. They highlight conditions that appear in more than one relative and write a short note on why each is flagged. Pairs compare which conditions they flagged. Guide reviews the strongest reasoning aloud.

apply-3.objective: Produce a list of questions a teen could ask a relative to learn family health history.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes at least five interview questions that would surface useful health information.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students draft a set of interview questions a teen could ask a parent or grandparent to learn family health history without prying. They write at least five questions and mark which condition each one targets. Pairs role-play asking and answering three questions. Guide collects question sets for a class bank.

apply-4.objective: Apply family history facts to recommend two screening or lifestyle steps for a sample person.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two specific recommendations and ties each to a condition in the family tree.
apply-4.sampleTask: Using a sample patient's family tree, students recommend two health steps the person could take, such as a screening or a habit change, and connect each step to a specific family condition. They write the recommendation and a one-sentence reason. Pairs check that each step matches a real risk in the tree. Guide reviews a few.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which conditions in a family tree are most likely inherited versus shaped by shared habits.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student labels each condition on the tree as 'mostly genetic,' 'mostly habit,' or 'both' and defends two labels.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a detailed family tree with notes on diet, exercise, and conditions. Students label each condition as mostly genetic, mostly habit, or both, then write two sentences defending their hardest two calls. Pairs debate any disagreements. Guide names the cases where the answer is genuinely 'both' and why.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two sample families to determine which person carries more controllable health risk.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the higher controllable-risk person and supports the choice with two pieces of evidence from the trees.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students examine two family trees with habit and condition notes. They decide which person has more risk they can actually change through behavior and back the choice with two specifics from the trees. Pairs compare conclusions. Guide charts the class split and the strongest evidence on each side.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how one family condition could affect several areas of a person's daily life.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps one condition to at least three life areas it touches and explains each link.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students pick one condition from a sample family tree and build a simple map showing at least three areas of daily life it could affect, such as sleep, sports, and money. They write a sentence explaining each link. Pairs review whether each link is realistic. Guide displays two maps and questions the class on the connections.

analyze-4.objective: Distinguish what family history can and cannot predict about a person's future health.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-column chart separating accurate predictions from overreaches and justifies three entries.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide gives five claims about what a family history 'proves,' some accurate and some overstated. Students sort claims into 'family history can show this' and 'family history cannot prove this' and justify three placements in writing. Pairs argue any disputed claim. Guide closes by correcting the most common overreach.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample teen's health plan responds well to their family history.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates the plan against criteria and writes two reasons supporting the rating.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a teen's family history and a draft personal health plan. Using a short criteria list, they rate how well the plan responds to the real risks and write two reasons for the rating. Pairs compare ratings and resolve gaps. Guide collects ratings and discusses where the plan missed a risk.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique the limits of using family history to make decisions about a person's health.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short position naming one strength and two limits of family-history-based decisions.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: 'Should family history alone decide what tests a person gets?' Students write a short position that names one strength and at least two limits of relying on family history, such as missing data or stigma. Pairs trade and mark the strongest limit. Guide hosts a brief debate and surfaces opposing views.

create-1.objective: Assemble a labeled one-page family health tree handout for classroom display.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a tree with three generations labeled and a key for condition symbols.
create-1.sampleTask: Students build a clean one-page family health tree for a fictional family, with three generations, names, and a small key showing symbols for conditions. The handout must be readable from a few feet away. Pairs check each other's key for clarity. Guide posts finished trees on the wall.

create-2.objective: Design an interview guide a teen could use to gather real family health history.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a guide with grouped questions, an opening line, and a respectful closing.
create-2.sampleTask: Students create a usable interview guide for talking with a relative about family health. It groups questions by topic, opens with a respectful framing line, and closes with a thank-you. Pairs test the guide by running three questions on each other. Guide selects two guides for a shared class version.

create-3.objective: Develop a personal-action one-pager that turns a sample family history into three health goals.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-pager linking each of three goals to a specific family risk.
create-3.sampleTask: Using a provided family history, students design a one-page action plan with three concrete health goals, each tied to a named family risk and a first step. The page must fit on one side and use plain language. Pairs check that every goal connects to a real risk. Guide displays strong examples.

create-4.objective: Build a short awareness poster teaching peers why family health history matters.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster with a clear claim, two supporting facts, and one call to action.
create-4.sampleTask: Students design a poster aimed at peers explaining why knowing family health history matters and what to do about it. The poster needs a clear headline claim, two supporting facts, and one specific call to action, such as 'ask one relative this week.' Pairs review posters for accuracy and clarity. Guide hangs them in the hallway.

2.1.8.PGD.3.standardStatement: Describe the human reproductive systems, the external and internal body parts and their functions, and the natural variations that exist in human bodies.
2.1.8.PGD.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Label the main internal and external parts on a diagram of each reproductive system.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student fills in a blank labeled diagram with the correct medical term at each line.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out blank, medically accurate diagrams of both reproductive systems with a word bank. Students write the correct term at each labeled line. Pairs check against a key. Guide reviews the terms students missed most and clarifies pronunciation.

understand-1.objective: Match each reproductive part to the function it performs.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a matching sheet pairing ten parts with their functions.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students get a sheet listing ten reproductive parts and their functions scrambled. They match each part to its function. Pairs trade and check. Guide reviews on the board and corrects any function students confused.

understand-2.objective: Explain in plain language what 'natural variation' in human bodies means.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-sentence definition and gives one accurate example.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide presents the term 'natural variation' with two accurate, respectful examples. Students write a two-sentence definition in their own words and add one example of their own. Pairs read each other's and check that the example is accurate and respectful. Guide collects to confirm understanding and addresses misconceptions.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how the major parts of one reproductive system work together.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph tracing the parts in the order they function.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students choose one reproductive system and write a paragraph describing how its main parts work together, naming the parts in functional order. They use accurate medical terms. Pairs read and check that each named part appears in the right place. Guide shares one clear paragraph per system.

understand-4.objective: Describe why using accurate medical terms for body parts matters for health and safety.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student lists two reasons accurate terms matter and gives one health scenario for each.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide poses why doctors and nurses rely on exact body-part terms. Students write two reasons accurate terms matter, such as describing a symptom clearly, and pair each reason with a short health scenario. Pairs compare reasons. Guide charts the class reasons and connects them to talking with a provider.

apply-1.objective: Use a labeled diagram to point out where a named function happens in the body.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the correct location on a diagram for five given functions.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide reads five functions aloud, one at a time. For each, students mark the part of the diagram where that function happens. Pairs compare marks after each round. Guide projects the diagram and confirms the correct location.

apply-2.objective: Apply correct terminology to relabel a diagram that uses slang or wrong terms.
