apply-2.observableCheck: Each student replaces each incorrect label with the accurate medical term.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a diagram with several parts labeled using slang or errors. Students cross out each wrong label and write the accurate medical term beside it. Pairs check each correction. Guide reviews the most common fixes and why accuracy matters.

apply-3.objective: Use accurate terms to write a clear health question a teen could ask a provider.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes two questions that use correct terms and describe a concern clearly.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students write two health questions a teen could ask a doctor or nurse about their body, using accurate terms so the concern is clear. They mark which body part each question is about. Pairs read questions and check the term use. Guide collects questions for an anonymous class reference list.

apply-4.objective: Apply the idea of natural variation to respond accurately to a peer's misconception.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student rewrites a stated misconception into an accurate, respectful statement.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide provides three common misconceptions about bodies that imply there is only one 'normal.' Students rewrite each into an accurate, respectful statement that reflects natural variation. Pairs compare rewrites for accuracy and tone. Guide reviews the strongest corrections.

analyze-1.objective: Compare the two reproductive systems to identify parts with parallel functions.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student fills a two-column chart pairing parts that do similar jobs and notes the shared function.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students build a two-column chart matching parts of each system that perform parallel functions, noting the shared job. They use accurate terms throughout. Pairs check that each pairing is functionally correct. Guide reviews the parallels and corrects any forced matches.

analyze-2.objective: Examine a set of statements to separate medical fact from myth about bodies.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts eight statements into 'fact' and 'myth' and corrects three myths.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives eight statements about reproductive bodies, some accurate and some myths. Students sort each into fact or myth and rewrite three myths into accurate statements. Pairs debate any disputed item. Guide closes by correcting the myth students most often believed.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how a single body part contributes to a larger process across the system.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student diagrams the chain showing how one part's function feeds the next step.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students choose one part and build a simple flow diagram showing how its function connects to the next step in the system and the one after that. They label each arrow with what is happening. Pairs check that the chain is accurate. Guide displays one diagram and questions the class on the links.

analyze-4.objective: Distinguish statements that respect natural variation from those that wrongly imply one normal body.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student categorizes six statements and explains what makes three of them respectful or not.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides six statements about bodies. Students categorize each as respecting natural variation or implying a single 'normal,' then explain their reasoning for three of them. Pairs compare categorizations. Guide names the language patterns that signal a respectful, accurate statement.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample health pamphlet describes bodies accurately and respectfully.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates the pamphlet against criteria and cites two specific lines as evidence.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a short sample pamphlet about reproductive health. Using a criteria checklist for accuracy and respect for variation, they rate it and cite two specific lines supporting the rating. Pairs compare ratings. Guide collects ratings and discusses the lines students flagged.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique two diagrams to decide which teaches reproductive anatomy more clearly and accurately.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student selects the stronger diagram and defends the choice with three specific reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide gives two diagrams of the same system, one clearer and more accurate than the other. Students pick the stronger teaching tool and defend the choice with three specifics about labeling, accuracy, and clarity. Pairs argue any disagreement. Guide reveals the accuracy errors in the weaker diagram.

create-1.objective: Produce a labeled study card set covering the main parts and functions of one system.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a card set with each part on one side and its function on the back.
create-1.sampleTask: Students make a set of study cards for one reproductive system, writing each part on the front and its accurate function on the back. The set must cover all main parts. Pairs quiz each other with the cards. Guide checks two sets for accuracy.

create-2.objective: Design a one-page accurate glossary of reproductive terms written for a younger student.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a glossary defining at least eight terms in clear, age-appropriate language.
create-2.sampleTask: Students build a one-page glossary defining at least eight reproductive health terms in language a younger student could understand, while staying medically accurate. Pairs check each definition for accuracy and clarity. Guide compiles the best definitions into a shared class glossary.

create-3.objective: Develop a respectful infographic showing that human bodies have natural variation.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student produces an infographic with a clear claim, two accurate facts, and respectful visuals.
create-3.sampleTask: Students design an infographic teaching peers that natural variation in bodies is normal. It needs a clear headline claim, two accurate supporting facts, and visuals that stay respectful. Pairs review for accuracy and tone. Guide posts approved infographics in the room.

create-4.objective: Build a short teaching segment that explains one reproductive system to peers.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a script or storyboard with accurate terms and a check-for-understanding question.
create-4.sampleTask: Students create a short teaching segment, as a script or storyboard, explaining one reproductive system to peers using accurate terms in functional order. The segment must end with one question that checks whether the audience understood. Pairs rehearse and give feedback. Guide selects two to present to the class.

2.1.8.PGD.4.standardStatement: Analyze the relationship between healthy behaviors and personal health.
2.1.8.PGD.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List five daily behaviors that affect personal health.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five behaviors on a card and tags each as helping or harming health.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name five things you do most days that affect your health.' Students write five behaviors on a card and mark each with a plus or minus. Cards go on a class chart sorted by helps and harms. Guide reads several aloud and confirms the tags.

understand-1.objective: Match each healthy behavior to the part of health it most supports.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a matching sheet linking behaviors to physical, mental, or social health.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students get a sheet of behaviors and three health categories: physical, mental, and social. They match each behavior to the category it most supports, noting that some support more than one. Pairs compare and resolve differences. Guide reviews and points out behaviors that span categories.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one healthy behavior leads to a specific health benefit.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'because' chain connecting one behavior to one clear benefit.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students pick one healthy behavior and write a short 'this leads to this because' chain ending in a specific benefit, such as 'sleeping eight hours leads to better focus because the brain rests.' Pairs check that each step follows the last. Guide shares two clear chains.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how several small daily choices add up to overall health.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph explaining the cumulative effect of daily choices.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide shows a one-day timeline with several small choices marked. Students write a paragraph explaining how those small choices add up to affect overall health over time. Pairs read and underline the clearest sentence. Guide collects to check understanding.

understand-4.objective: Describe how the same behavior can affect physical and mental health at once.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names one behavior and explains its effect on both physical and mental health.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students choose one behavior, such as regular exercise, and write how it affects both physical and mental health, with one specific effect for each. Pairs check that both effects are realistic. Guide charts behaviors that clearly hit both kinds of health.

apply-1.objective: Track a sample person's day and tally their healthy and unhealthy choices.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student counts the choices on a sample log and records the totals correctly.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a one-day log for a fictional teen with choices listed by hour. Students tally healthy and unhealthy choices and record totals. Pairs compare counts. Guide projects the log and confirms the totals.

apply-2.objective: Use a sample schedule to swap one unhealthy choice for a healthier one.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student rewrites one slot in the schedule and names the health benefit of the swap.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students get a sample teen's daily schedule with a few weak choices. They pick one slot, swap in a healthier choice, and write the benefit of the change. Pairs check that the swap is realistic for a teen. Guide shares strong swaps.

apply-3.objective: Build a one-day healthy-choice plan for a sample teen with a stated goal.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a plan in which each choice supports the stated goal.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a sample teen and a goal, such as 'more energy for sports.' Students build a one-day plan of choices that support that goal, covering food, movement, sleep, and screens. Pairs check that each choice ties to the goal. Guide reviews two plans.

apply-4.objective: Apply health knowledge to advise a sample peer facing a specific choice.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes advice that names the healthier option and one realistic reason a teen would accept.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students read a short scenario where a peer faces a choice, such as staying up late on a phone before a test. They write advice naming the healthier option and one reason a teen would actually find convincing. Pairs trade and rate which reason lands best. Guide reads strong advice aloud.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which of a sample teen's behaviors most affect their stated health problem.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student ranks the behaviors by impact and defends the top two choices.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a profile of a teen with a health problem and a list of their daily behaviors. Students rank which behaviors most affect that problem and defend their top two in writing. Pairs compare rankings. Guide charts the class top picks and the reasoning.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two sample teens to determine whose daily habits better support long-term health.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the healthier pattern and supports the choice with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students examine two teens' daily habit logs. They decide whose habits better support long-term health and back the call with two specifics from the logs. Pairs compare conclusions. Guide charts the split and the strongest evidence on each side.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how one unhealthy habit can set off a chain of effects across a week.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps a habit through at least three downstream effects and explains each link.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students pick one unhealthy habit, such as skipping breakfast, and map at least three effects it could cause across a week, with a sentence explaining each link. Pairs check that each effect is realistic. Guide displays one map and questions the class on the chain.

analyze-4.objective: Distinguish health claims that are supported from those that overstate a behavior's effect.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student sorts six claims into 'supported' and 'overstated' and corrects three.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide gives six claims about behaviors and health, some accurate and some overstated, such as 'one workout fixes your health.' Students sort each and rewrite three overstated claims to be accurate. Pairs debate disputed claims. Guide corrects the most common overstatement.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample teen's health plan is realistic and likely to work.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates the plan against criteria and writes two reasons for the rating.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a sample teen's week-long health plan. Using a criteria list for realism and balance, they rate the plan and write two reasons supporting the rating. Pairs compare ratings. Guide discusses where the plan is too ambitious or too weak.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique the claim that willpower alone determines whether teens make healthy choices.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short position naming at least two factors beyond willpower.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide poses: 'Is making healthy choices just about willpower?' Students write a short position arguing for or against and naming at least two other factors, such as cost, time, or environment. Pairs trade and mark the strongest factor. Guide hosts a brief debate and surfaces both sides.

create-1.objective: Produce a labeled weekly habit tracker a teen could actually use.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a tracker with rows for behaviors and a clear marking system.
create-1.sampleTask: Students design a one-page weekly habit tracker with rows for chosen behaviors, columns for days, and a simple marking key. It must be usable without explanation. Pairs test each other's tracker by filling one day. Guide posts the clearest trackers.

create-2.objective: Design a 'healthy swap' menu that pairs common choices with better alternatives.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a menu with at least six swaps and a short reason for each.
create-2.sampleTask: Students build a 'healthy swap' menu listing at least six common teen choices and a realistic healthier alternative for each, with a one-line reason. The menu must be quick to read. Pairs check that swaps are realistic. Guide compiles a class swap menu.

create-3.objective: Develop a personal 30-day health goal plan with weekly steps.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a plan with one goal, four weekly milestones, and a way to track progress.
create-3.sampleTask: Students design a 30-day plan around one personal health goal, breaking it into four weekly milestones and naming how they will track progress. The plan must fit on one page and stay realistic. Pairs check that the milestones build toward the goal. Guide reviews strong plans.

create-4.objective: Build a short campaign that motivates peers to adopt one healthy behavior.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a campaign piece with a clear message, two facts, and a specific call to action.
create-4.sampleTask: Students design a short campaign, such as a poster or 30-second script, that motivates peers to adopt one healthy behavior. It needs a clear message, two accurate facts, and a specific call to action. Pairs review for accuracy and pull. Guide displays or screens the strongest pieces.

2.1.8.PP.1.standardStatement: Describe pregnancy testing, the signs of pregnancy, and pregnancy options, including parenting, abortion, and adoption.
2.1.8.PP.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List the common early signs of pregnancy and name the basic types of pregnancy tests.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the signs and test types on a card from a provided fact sheet.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a short fact sheet, then has students close it and list the common early signs of pregnancy and the two main types of pregnancy tests on a card. Pairs check against the fact sheet. Guide confirms the full list on the board.

understand-1.objective: Match each pregnancy option to an accurate one-line description.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a matching sheet pairing parenting, abortion, and adoption with accurate descriptions.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students get a matching sheet listing the three pregnancy options and several descriptions, some accurate and some not. They match each option to its accurate description. Pairs compare. Guide reviews the matches and corrects any inaccurate descriptions in neutral, factual terms.

understand-2.objective: Explain how a pregnancy test works in plain, accurate language.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-sentence explanation of what a test detects and when it is reliable.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews how a pregnancy test detects a hormone and why timing affects accuracy. Students write a two-sentence plain-language explanation of what the test detects and when it gives a reliable result. Pairs check accuracy. Guide collects to confirm understanding.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the three pregnancy options accurately and without bias.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes one neutral, accurate sentence describing each of the three options.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students write one neutral, factual sentence for each option: parenting, abortion, and adoption. The sentences must state what the option is without judging it. Pairs trade and flag any wording that sounds biased. Guide models neutral phrasing and shares strong examples.

understand-4.objective: Describe why accurate, judgment-free information matters when someone faces a pregnancy decision.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two reasons accurate, neutral information helps a person decide.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide poses why neutral, accurate information matters for someone facing a major decision. Students write two reasons, such as reducing fear or supporting an informed choice. Pairs compare. Guide charts the reasons and connects them to where teens can find reliable information.

apply-1.objective: Use a fact sheet to answer five factual questions about pregnancy signs and testing.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student answers all five questions correctly using the fact sheet.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a fact sheet and five factual questions about signs and testing. Students locate and write the answers. Pairs check against the sheet. Guide reviews the answers and clears up any confusion.

apply-2.objective: Use accurate sources to correct a set of common myths about pregnancy testing.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student rewrites three myths into accurate statements using the provided sources.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students get three common myths about pregnancy tests, such as 'a test is always right the next morning.' Using accurate sources, they rewrite each myth into a correct statement. Pairs check accuracy. Guide reviews the corrections and names the source for each.

apply-3.objective: Apply the three options to accurately answer a peer's factual questions in a scenario.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes accurate, neutral answers to three scripted peer questions.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide provides a scenario with three factual questions a peer might ask about the options. Students write accurate, neutral answers and note where a person could get more help. Pairs check that answers stay factual and judgment-free. Guide reads strong responses aloud.

apply-4.objective: Use a resource list to direct a sample person to accurate, confidential support.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student names two appropriate resources and explains what each provides.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students get a sample situation and a list of resources, including a clinic, a counselor, and a medical information line. They pick two appropriate resources and write what each provides. Pairs check that the choices fit the situation. Guide reviews and notes which resources are trustworthy.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which factors a person might weigh when considering the three options.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least four factors and groups them into practical and personal.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students list at least four factors a person might weigh among the options, such as support, health, finances, and values, then group them as practical or personal. Pairs compare lists. Guide charts the factors and notes that the weight of each is personal and varies.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two information sources to determine which gives more accurate, neutral pregnancy information.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the stronger source and cites two specifics that support the choice.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives two short sample sources on pregnancy options, one neutral and accurate and one biased or vague. Students pick the stronger source and cite two specifics, such as tone or accuracy. Pairs compare. Guide reveals the signs of a biased source.

analyze-3.objective: Trace the steps a person would take from a missed period to a confirmed result.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student builds an accurate ordered flowchart of the steps.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students build a flowchart showing the accurate steps from noticing a possible sign to confirming a result, such as taking a test and seeing a provider. Each step is labeled. Pairs check the order. Guide projects a correct flow and corrects any missteps.

analyze-4.objective: Distinguish factual statements about the options from opinion or judgment.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student sorts six statements into 'fact' and 'opinion' and explains three placements.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide gives six statements about the pregnancy options, some factual and some opinion or judgment. Students sort each into fact or opinion and explain three placements in writing. Pairs debate any disputed item. Guide names the language that marks an opinion.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample teen advice column answer is accurate, neutral, and helpful.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates the answer against criteria and cites two lines as evidence.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a sample advice column answer to a teen's pregnancy question. Using a criteria checklist for accuracy, neutrality, and helpfulness, they rate it and cite two lines as evidence. Pairs compare ratings. Guide discusses where the answer succeeded or fell short.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique a pregnancy information brochure for accuracy and whether it stays judgment-free.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short critique naming one strength and two specific problems.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide gives a sample brochure on pregnancy options. Students critique it for accuracy and neutral tone, naming one strength and two specific problems, such as a biased word or a missing fact. Pairs compare critiques. Guide reviews the problems students caught and any they missed.

create-1.objective: Produce a quick-reference card listing the early signs of pregnancy and basic test facts.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a card with accurate signs and test facts in a readable layout.
create-1.sampleTask: Students make a quick-reference card listing the early signs of pregnancy and basic facts about testing, drawn from accurate sources. The card must be readable at a glance. Pairs check accuracy. Guide collects the clearest cards for a class set.

create-2.objective: Design a neutral one-page handout describing all three pregnancy options factually.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a handout giving each option equal, accurate, judgment-free coverage.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a one-page handout describing parenting, abortion, and adoption with equal space and neutral, accurate language for each. The handout must avoid judgment. Pairs check for balance and accuracy. Guide reviews and compiles a class version.

create-3.objective: Develop a resource guide pointing teens to accurate, confidential pregnancy support.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a guide listing at least four resources with what each offers.
create-3.sampleTask: Students build a resource guide listing at least four accurate, confidential sources of pregnancy support, with a line on what each offers and how to reach it. The guide must be easy to scan. Pairs check the resources are appropriate. Guide vets the list before it is shared.

create-4.objective: Build a short FAQ that answers common teen questions about signs, testing, and options accurately.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student produces an FAQ with at least five questions and accurate, neutral answers.
create-4.sampleTask: Students create an FAQ with at least five common teen questions about pregnancy signs, testing, and options, each with an accurate, neutral answer and a pointer to a reliable resource. Pairs check accuracy and tone. Guide compiles a vetted class FAQ.

2.1.8.PP.2.standardStatement: Summarize the stages of pregnancy from fertilization to birth.
2.1.8.PP.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List the three trimesters and one key event in each.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the three trimesters in order with one event each on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide reviews the trimester structure, then has students close their notes and write the three trimesters in order with one key event in each on a card. Pairs check against a reference. Guide confirms the events on the board.

understand-1.objective: Match each major developmental event to the stage when it happens.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a matching sheet pairing events with the correct stage.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students get a matching sheet listing developmental events, such as the heartbeat forming or movement starting, and the stages scrambled. They match each event to its stage. Pairs compare. Guide reviews and corrects any out-of-order matches.

understand-2.objective: Explain in plain language what happens at fertilization.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-sentence accurate description of fertilization.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews fertilization with an accurate diagram. Students write a two-sentence plain-language description of what happens at fertilization. Pairs check accuracy. Guide collects to confirm understanding and corrects misconceptions.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the whole sequence from fertilization to birth in order.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph naming the stages in correct order.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph summarizing the sequence from fertilization to birth, naming each major stage in correct order. They use accurate terms. Pairs read and check the order. Guide shares one clear summary.

understand-4.objective: Describe why the early weeks of development are especially sensitive.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two reasons the early weeks matter for healthy development.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide explains that major systems form early. Students write two reasons the early weeks are especially sensitive, such as organ formation. Pairs compare. Guide charts the reasons and connects them to why prenatal care starts early.

apply-1.objective: Place a set of developmental events in correct order on a timeline.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student arranges all event cards in the correct sequence.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a set of event cards covering fertilization to birth. Students arrange them in correct order on a timeline strip. Pairs compare sequences. Guide projects the correct order and confirms.

apply-2.objective: Use a stage reference to label an unlabeled development diagram.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student labels each image with the correct stage and one event.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students get a row of unlabeled development images and a stage reference. They label each image with its stage and one event happening then. Pairs check labels. Guide reviews on the board.

apply-3.objective: Apply stage knowledge to answer questions about what is developing at a given point.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student answers three 'what is happening now' questions accurately for given weeks.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives three prompts naming a point in pregnancy, such as 'around 20 weeks.' Students write what is developing at each point using accurate terms. Pairs check answers against a reference. Guide reviews the strongest responses.

apply-4.objective: Use the stages to explain to a younger sibling how a baby develops over time.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short, accurate explanation in simple language.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students write a short explanation, as if for a younger sibling, of how a baby develops from fertilization to birth, keeping it accurate but simple. Pairs check that it stays accurate while being easy to follow. Guide reads two clear examples aloud.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which trimester shows the fastest change in a given body system.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the trimester for two systems and cites evidence from the reference.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students use a development reference to identify which trimester shows the fastest change for two systems, such as the nervous system, citing evidence. Pairs compare answers. Guide charts the class findings and clarifies any errors.

analyze-2.objective: Compare development at two points in pregnancy to describe what changed.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student lists three differences between the two points with accurate detail.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide names two points, such as 12 weeks and 28 weeks. Students list three accurate differences in development between the two points. Pairs compare lists. Guide reviews and adds any major differences students missed.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how one organ system develops across all three trimesters.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student builds a three-stage map showing the system's progress with a note per stage.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students choose one organ system and build a map showing its development across all three trimesters, with an accurate note at each stage. Pairs check the progression. Guide displays one map and questions the class on the sequence.

analyze-4.objective: Distinguish accurate statements about fetal development from common myths.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student sorts six statements into 'accurate' and 'myth' and corrects three myths.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide gives six statements about fetal development, some accurate and some myths. Students sort each and rewrite three myths into accurate statements. Pairs debate disputed items. Guide corrects the most common myth.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample development timeline is accurate and complete.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates the timeline against criteria and cites two errors or gaps.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students review a sample development timeline that contains a few errors. Using a criteria checklist for accuracy and completeness, they rate it and cite two errors or gaps. Pairs compare findings. Guide reveals all the planted errors.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique two student-made summaries to decide which explains the stages more accurately.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student selects the stronger summary and defends the choice with three reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide gives two summaries of pregnancy stages, one stronger than the other. Students pick the more accurate, clearer summary and defend the choice with three specifics. Pairs argue disagreements. Guide names the accuracy problems in the weaker summary.

create-1.objective: Produce a labeled timeline strip of pregnancy from fertilization to birth.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a timeline with stages in order and one accurate event per stage.
create-1.sampleTask: Students build a labeled timeline strip showing the stages from fertilization to birth, with one accurate event marked per stage. The strip must be readable left to right. Pairs check the order. Guide posts the clearest strips.

create-2.objective: Design a set of stage cards a peer could use to study fetal development.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces cards with a stage on the front and accurate events on the back.
create-2.sampleTask: Students make a set of study cards with a development stage on the front and the accurate key events on the back. The set must cover the full sequence. Pairs quiz each other. Guide checks two sets for accuracy.

create-3.objective: Develop a one-page illustrated guide summarizing pregnancy stages for peers.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a guide with each stage labeled, illustrated, and accurately described.
create-3.sampleTask: Students design a one-page illustrated guide to pregnancy stages, with each stage labeled, drawn simply, and accurately described in a sentence. The guide must read clearly top to bottom. Pairs check accuracy. Guide displays approved guides.

create-4.objective: Build a short narrated storyboard that walks through development from fertilization to birth.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a storyboard with ordered panels and accurate narration per panel.
create-4.sampleTask: Students create a storyboard, panel by panel, narrating development from fertilization to birth, with accurate captions in correct order. The storyboard must end at birth. Pairs review for accuracy and order. Guide selects two to present.

2.1.8.PP.3.standardStatement: Identify prenatal practices that support a healthy pregnancy and identify where to find medically accurate sources of information about prenatal care.
2.1.8.PP.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List five practices that support a healthy pregnancy.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five supportive prenatal practices on a card from a fact sheet.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a fact sheet, then has students close it and list five practices that support a healthy pregnancy, such as prenatal vitamins and regular checkups. Pairs check against the sheet. Guide confirms the list on the board.

understand-1.objective: Match each prenatal practice to the benefit it provides.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a matching sheet pairing practices with their benefits.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students get a matching sheet of prenatal practices and benefits scrambled. They match each practice to its benefit, such as folic acid to supporting early development. Pairs compare. Guide reviews and corrects any mismatches.

understand-2.objective: Explain why regular prenatal checkups matter during pregnancy.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-sentence explanation of what checkups do.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews what happens at a prenatal checkup. Students write a two-sentence explanation of why regular checkups matter, naming what they catch or track. Pairs check accuracy. Guide collects to confirm understanding.

understand-3.objective: Summarize what makes a source of prenatal information medically reliable.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes three traits of a reliable source in their own words.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide reviews signs of a reliable health source, such as medical authorship and a trusted organization. Students write three traits of a reliable prenatal information source. Pairs compare. Guide charts the agreed traits.

understand-4.objective: Describe why avoiding certain substances matters during pregnancy.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names two substances to avoid and explains the reason for each.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews substances that can harm development. Students name two to avoid during pregnancy and write the reason for each, using accurate, non-alarming language. Pairs check accuracy. Guide confirms the reasons and the reliable source behind them.

apply-1.objective: Use a fact sheet to answer five questions about supportive prenatal practices.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student answers all five questions correctly using the fact sheet.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a fact sheet and five questions about prenatal practices. Students locate and write the answers. Pairs check against the sheet. Guide reviews answers and clears up confusion.

apply-2.objective: Use source criteria to sort sample websites into reliable and unreliable.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts six sample sources and notes the deciding trait for each.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students get six sample source descriptions and the reliability criteria. They sort each into reliable or unreliable and note the trait that decided it. Pairs compare sorts. Guide reviews and flags the trickiest source.

apply-3.objective: Build a one-week supportive-practice checklist for a sample pregnant person.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a checklist where each item reflects a real prenatal practice.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a sample situation. Students build a one-week checklist of supportive prenatal practices, such as taking vitamins, light activity, and a checkup, with each item accurate. Pairs check that items are realistic. Guide reviews two checklists.

apply-4.objective: Apply source criteria to recommend where a sample person should look for prenatal information.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student names two reliable sources and explains why each is trustworthy.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students read a sample situation and a list of sources. They recommend two reliable sources for prenatal information and explain why each is trustworthy using the criteria. Pairs check the choices. Guide confirms which sources are credible.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which prenatal practices have the biggest effect on early development.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student ranks practices by early impact and defends the top two.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students rank a list of prenatal practices by their effect on early development and defend their top two in writing, citing the fact sheet. Pairs compare rankings. Guide charts the class top picks and the reasoning.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two sources to determine which gives more medically accurate prenatal guidance.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the stronger source and cites two specifics supporting the choice.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives two sample sources on prenatal care, one credible and one unreliable. Students pick the stronger source and cite two specifics, such as authorship or accuracy. Pairs compare. Guide reveals the warning signs in the weak source.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how skipping one supportive practice could affect a pregnancy over time.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps one skipped practice to at least three possible effects with a note each.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students pick one supportive practice and map at least three possible effects of skipping it over time, with an accurate note per link, drawn from reliable information. Pairs check that effects are realistic and not exaggerated. Guide reviews one map.

analyze-4.objective: Distinguish medically supported prenatal advice from myths or unverified claims.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student sorts six pieces of advice into 'supported' and 'unverified' and corrects three.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide gives six pieces of prenatal advice, some medically supported and some myths. Students sort each and rewrite three unverified claims into accurate ones, naming a reliable source. Pairs debate disputed items. Guide corrects the most common myth.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample prenatal-care website is medically reliable.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates the site against criteria and cites two specific signs as evidence.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students review a sample prenatal-care website description. Using the reliability criteria, they rate it and cite two specific signs, such as the author or the date, as evidence. Pairs compare ratings. Guide discusses the deciding signs.

evaluate-4.objective: Critique a prenatal advice post for accuracy and source quality.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a critique naming one accurate point and two problems with evidence or sourcing.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide gives a sample social media prenatal advice post. Students critique it, naming one accurate point and two problems with its evidence or sourcing. Pairs compare critiques. Guide reviews the problems students caught and the source that would correct them.

create-1.objective: Produce a quick-reference card of supportive prenatal practices.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a card listing accurate practices in a readable layout.
create-1.sampleTask: Students make a quick-reference card listing supportive prenatal practices drawn from a reliable source. The card must be readable at a glance. Pairs check accuracy. Guide collects the clearest cards for a class set.

create-2.objective: Design a checklist of questions to judge whether a health source is reliable.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a checklist with at least five usable source-evaluation questions.
create-2.sampleTask: Students build a checklist of at least five questions for judging whether a prenatal health source is reliable, such as 'who wrote it' and 'when was it updated.' The checklist must be quick to use. Pairs test it on one sample source. Guide compiles a class version.

create-3.objective: Develop a one-page guide pairing supportive practices with reliable sources.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a guide listing practices and a credible source for each topic.
create-3.sampleTask: Students design a one-page guide listing supportive prenatal practices and naming a credible source where someone could learn more about each topic. The guide must be easy to scan. Pairs check accuracy and source quality. Guide vets sources before sharing.

create-4.objective: Build a short campaign encouraging people to use medically accurate prenatal sources.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a campaign piece with a clear message, two facts, and a named reliable source.
create-4.sampleTask: Students design a short campaign, such as a poster or 30-second script, urging people to rely on medically accurate prenatal sources. It needs a clear message, two accurate facts, and at least one named reliable source. Pairs review for accuracy. Guide displays the strongest pieces.

2.1.8.PP.4.standardStatement: Predict challenges that may be faced by adolescent parents and their families.
2.1.8.PP.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List five common challenges an adolescent parent may face, such as finishing school, paying for childcare, sleep loss, and limited free time.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five challenges on an index card and reads two aloud.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'What are real challenges a teen parent deals with day to day?' Each student writes five on an index card from a word bank the Guide provides. Cards go up on a board sorted into money, school, time, and health columns. The class reads the board and removes duplicates.

understand-1.objective: Match each adolescent parenting challenge to the part of daily life it affects, such as school, money, or sleep.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a matching column with at least seven of eight pairs correct.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a worksheet with eight challenges in one column and eight areas of life in the other, scrambled. Students draw lines to connect each challenge to the area it affects most. The Guide reviews answers as a class and resolves the pairs students debate. Students fix any wrong lines in a second color.

understand-2.objective: Explain in writing why one named challenge, such as missing school, creates a second challenge for an adolescent parent.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-sentence cause-and-effect statement linking one challenge to another.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student picks one challenge from the board and writes how it leads to a second challenge, using the frame 'This causes that because ...'. For example, missing school lowers grades, which narrows job options later. Pairs trade papers and underline the cause and the effect in each other's writing. The Guide collects three to read aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how a single challenge ripples out from the adolescent parent to the wider family, such as grandparents who step in to help.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student draws a three-ring diagram showing the parent, the child, and the extended family for one challenge.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide models a ripple diagram on the board: the teen parent in the center ring, the baby in the next ring, and family members in the outer ring. Students pick one challenge and fill their own three-ring diagram showing who each ring feels the effect on. Students label one arrow between rings with a short phrase. The Guide displays two strong diagrams under the document camera.

understand-4.objective: Describe across several sources how the set of challenges adolescent parents face fits together over the first year of a child's life.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a labeled timeline with at least one challenge marked in each quarter of the first year.
understand-4.sampleTask: Groups read two short Guide-provided summaries about the first year of parenting. Each group builds a four-section timeline for months zero to twelve and places challenges where they tend to peak, such as sleep loss early and childcare cost when leave ends. Groups add a one-line note explaining each placement. The class compares two timelines and notes where they disagree.

apply-1.objective: Sort a stack of challenge cards into 'school,' 'money,' 'health,' and 'support' using a provided rule sheet.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each pair places all twelve cards into the four trays and matches the answer key on at least ten.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair twelve challenge cards and four labeled trays with a one-line rule for each category. Pairs read each card and drop it in the tray its rule fits. Pairs check their sort against an answer key the Guide reveals at the ten-minute mark. Each pair writes one card that was hard to place and why.

apply-2.objective: Use a weekly schedule template to slot a teen parent's school, work, childcare, and sleep into a seven-day grid.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student turns in a grid with no overlap and at least seven hours of sleep marked each night.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide hands out a blank weekly grid and a card listing one fictional teen parent's fixed commitments, such as classes from eight to two and a shift on two evenings. Students place each block on the grid without overlaps and shade the sleep hours. Students mark in red any day where something does not fit. The Guide collects grids and names one common conflict.

apply-3.objective: Apply a problem-and-solution chart to one predicted challenge, choosing a response a teen parent could realistically take.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student records one challenge, two possible responses, and the response they would pick with a reason.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student picks a challenge from the class board and fills a chart with the problem, two responses, and the trade-off of each. Students circle the response they would choose and write one sentence on why. Pairs read each other's charts and add one response the partner missed. The Guide posts three charts that show different reasonable choices.

apply-4.objective: Build a one-week support plan that connects a teen parent to two real resources for two different challenges.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each pair submits a plan naming two resources and the day and time the parent would use each.
apply-4.sampleTask: Pairs receive a card with one teen parent's situation and a list of local-style resources such as a school counselor, a clinic, and a relative. Pairs choose two resources for two challenges and place each on a day-and-time line. Pairs write one sentence on what could block the plan and a backup. The Guide reviews two plans and points out one realistic backup.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which of three listed challenges would hit an adolescent parent soonest after a baby arrives.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student ranks three challenges by how soon they appear and labels the earliest.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide displays three challenges, such as childcare cost, sleep loss, and saving for college. Students number them one to three by how soon each one shows up after birth and circle the earliest. Students write one sentence defending their top pick. The Guide takes a hand vote and names where students split.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two teen parents' situations to find which challenge they share and which one differs.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair fills a Venn diagram with at least two shared and two different challenges.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs read two short profiles of teen parents with different family setups, such as one living with grandparents and one living alone. Pairs sort challenges into a Venn diagram with shared challenges in the middle. Pairs write one sentence on why a difference in setup changes the challenge. The Guide displays one Venn and asks the class to add a missing item.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how one challenge for an adolescent parent leads to a chain of two or three further challenges for the family.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student builds a cause chain with at least three linked boxes and an explanation for each arrow.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide models a cause chain: dropping a class leads to fewer credits, which delays graduation, which delays full-time work. Each student picks a starting challenge and builds a chain of three boxes with a labeled arrow between each. Students write a phrase on each arrow saying why one leads to the next. Pairs check whether every arrow holds and flag one weak link.

analyze-4.objective: Investigate two sources on adolescent parenting and judge which predicted challenge they both treat as most serious.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each group cites a line from each source and names the challenge both rank highest.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups read two Guide-provided summaries that each discuss several challenges. Each group finds the challenge both sources stress most and copies one supporting line from each. Groups write two sentences on why both sources agree. The class compares which challenge groups landed on and discusses any group that chose differently.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge which of two support plans better addresses a teen parent's most urgent challenge and defend the choice with criteria.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student rates both plans against three criteria and writes a short paragraph naming the winner.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide gives students two finished support plans for the same teen parent and a rubric with three criteria, such as speed, cost, and reliability. Students score each plan on the rubric and total the points. Students write a paragraph naming the stronger plan and citing two rubric rows that decided it. Pairs trade and check that the paragraph matches the scores.

evaluate-4.objective: Assess across multiple cases whether schools generally do enough to predict and prevent challenges for adolescent parents, and back the verdict with evidence.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each group writes a claim with a verdict and supports it with evidence drawn from at least two cases.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Groups read three short cases of how different schools supported or failed teen parents. Each group writes a claim stating whether schools generally do enough, citing evidence from at least two cases. Groups add one recommendation that would close the gap they found. The class hears two opposing verdicts and notes which evidence was strongest.

create-1.objective: Label a prepared poster of adolescent parenting challenges with the correct category headings.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student places all four heading cards on the poster in the right zones.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide hands each student a poster showing twelve challenge icons grouped in four blank-headed zones. Students write the correct heading on each zone using a category list. Students add one icon of their own to the zone where it fits. The Guide checks headings at the table and corrects any misplaced one.

create-2.objective: Produce a one-page advice card for a friend who just became a teen parent, naming three challenges and one response to each.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student turns in a card listing three challenges paired with three concrete responses.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a folded advice card for a peer in this situation. Each card names three challenges and gives one specific response to each, such as 'ask the counselor about flexible deadlines.' Students add one resource the friend can contact. Pairs read each other's cards and mark whether each response is doable.

create-3.objective: Design a short skit that shows an adolescent parent meeting one predicted challenge and choosing a workable response.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group performs a two-minute skit that names the challenge and shows a clear response.
create-3.sampleTask: Groups script a two-minute skit in which a teen parent hits one challenge from the class board and works toward a response. The script must name the challenge out loud and end with a realistic step, not a magic fix. Groups rehearse once and perform for another group. The audience group writes one sentence on whether the response was realistic.

create-4.objective: Develop a school support proposal that predicts three challenges new teen parents face and proposes a concrete program for each.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a one-page proposal naming three challenges and a matching program with who runs it.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups write a proposal for school leaders that predicts three challenges teen parents at their school would face. For each challenge the proposal names one program, who would run it, and what it would cost in time or money. Groups pull at least one detail from a source the Guide provided. The class hears two proposals and votes on which is most realistic to start next year.

2.1.8.PP.5.standardStatement: Identify resources to assist with parenting.
2.1.8.PP.5.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name six resources that help parents, such as a pediatrician, a food assistance program, a parenting class, and a relative.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes six resources on a half-sheet and reads one the class has not heard yet.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Who or what can help a parent who needs support?' Each student lists six resources on a half-sheet using a word bank. The class builds a shared board sorted into health, money, learning, and people columns. The Guide removes duplicates and counts how many the class found.

understand-1.objective: Match each parenting resource to the kind of help it offers, such as health care, money, or knowledge.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student matches at least seven of eight resource-to-help pairs on a worksheet.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a worksheet listing eight resources and eight kinds of help, scrambled. Students draw lines connecting each resource to what it provides. The Guide reviews the answers and resolves any pair the class debates. Students correct wrong lines in a second color.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a parent would choose a free resource over a paid one for the same need.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-sentence comparison naming the trade-off between a free and a paid option.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide names one need, such as childcare, and shows one free option and one paid option. Each student writes two sentences on why a parent might pick the free one and what they might give up, such as fewer hours or a waitlist. Pairs trade and underline the trade-off in each other's writing. The Guide reads two examples aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how three resources fit together to support one parent's week.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph naming three resources and the order a parent would use them.
understand-3.sampleTask: Each student picks three resources from the board and writes a paragraph describing how a parent would use them across one week, such as a clinic on Monday, a food program on Wednesday, and a relative on the weekend. Students number the resources in order of use. Pairs check whether the order makes sense and suggest one change. The Guide displays two strong paragraphs.

understand-4.objective: Describe across two sources how parenting resources differ for families in a city versus a rural area.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each group lists two resources easier to reach in a city and two easier to reach in a rural area.
understand-4.sampleTask: Groups read two Guide-provided summaries, one about city resources and one about rural resources. Each group fills a two-column chart with resources that are easier to reach in each setting. Groups write one sentence on why the setting changes access, such as distance to a clinic. The class compares charts and notes one resource that is hard to reach in both.

apply-1.objective: Sort a stack of resource cards into 'health,' 'money,' 'learning,' and 'people' using a rule sheet.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each pair places all twelve cards and matches the key on at least ten.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair twelve resource cards, four labeled trays, and a one-line rule for each tray. Pairs read each card and drop it in the matching tray. Pairs check against an answer key revealed at the ten-minute mark. Each pair names one card that fit two trays and explains why.

apply-2.objective: Use a resource directory to find the right contact for a parent's stated need.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student records the resource name and contact step for three different needs.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide hands out a one-page mock directory and a card listing three parent needs, such as a sick child, low food, and trouble reading to a toddler. Students find one resource for each need and write the name and the first step to reach it. Students mark any need with no clear match. The Guide reviews and fills any gap the class found.

apply-3.objective: Apply a fit check to decide whether a given resource matches a parent's need, hours, and budget.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student scores one resource on three fit questions and writes a yes or no with a reason.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student gets one parent scenario and one resource description. Students answer three fit questions: does it meet the need, do the hours work, and is the cost workable. Students write a yes or no with one sentence of reasoning. Pairs trade scenarios and check whether the verdict matches the answers.

apply-4.objective: Build a resource plan that connects one parent to three resources for three different needs in one week.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each pair submits a plan naming three resources, the need each meets, and the day each is used.
apply-4.sampleTask: Pairs receive a parent profile with three needs and a resource list. Pairs choose one resource per need and place each on a day line. Pairs write one sentence on what could block a resource and a backup. The Guide reviews two plans and names one realistic backup.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which of three resources a parent should contact first in an urgent situation.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student ranks three resources by urgency and circles the first to call.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide displays an urgent scenario, such as a feverish infant at night, with three resources. Students number the resources by which to use first and circle the top choice. Students write one sentence defending the order. The Guide takes a hand vote and names where the class split.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two resources that meet the same need and find which fits a working parent better.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair fills a two-column chart with the strengths and limits of each resource.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs read two resource descriptions that meet one need, such as two childcare options with different hours and costs. Pairs fill a chart with strengths and limits of each. Pairs write one sentence naming which fits a parent who works evenings and why. The Guide displays one chart and asks the class to add a missing limit.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how missing one key resource forces a parent to lean harder on the others.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student builds a cause chain showing the effect of losing one resource on at least two others.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide models a chain: a closed clinic means longer trips, which costs more time off work, which strains the family budget. Each student picks one resource to remove and builds a three-box chain of effects. Students label each arrow with a phrase saying why one leads to the next. Pairs flag one weak link.

analyze-4.objective: Investigate two sources and judge which parenting resource they both treat as most essential.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each group cites one line from each source and names the resource both stress most.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups read two Guide-provided summaries about parenting supports. Each group finds the resource both stress most and copies one supporting line from each. Groups write two sentences on why both agree. The class compares which resource groups chose and discusses any outlier.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge which of two resource plans serves a parent's needs better and defend the pick with criteria.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores both plans on three criteria and writes a paragraph naming the winner.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide gives two finished resource plans for the same parent and a rubric with three criteria, such as coverage, cost, and reliability. Students score each plan and total the points. Students write a paragraph naming the stronger plan and citing two rubric rows that decided it. Pairs check that the paragraph matches the scores.

evaluate-4.objective: Assess across several cases whether a community gives parents enough usable resources, and support the verdict with evidence.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each group writes a claim with a verdict supported by evidence from at least two cases.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Groups read three short cases of parents trying to find help in the same community. Each group writes a claim on whether the resources are enough, citing evidence from at least two cases. Groups add one resource the community is missing. The class hears two opposing verdicts and weighs the evidence.

create-1.objective: Label a prepared resource map with the correct category for each marked location.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the right category on all marked points using a key.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide hands each student a simple map with marked resource points and a category key. Students label each point with its category, such as health or food. Students add one point of their own and label it. The Guide checks labels at the table and corrects any error.

create-2.objective: Produce a one-page resource flyer that a clinic could hand to a new parent.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student turns in a flyer naming at least four resources with one contact step each.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a flyer for new parents that lists at least four resources grouped by need. Each entry includes one step to reach it. Students add a short headline that tells parents what the flyer is for. Pairs read each other's flyers and mark whether each entry is clear enough to act on.

create-3.objective: Design a role-play where a parent calls a resource and gets the help they need.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair performs a two-minute call showing the parent stating the need and the resource responding.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs script a two-minute phone role-play: one plays a parent with a need, one plays a resource worker. The script must have the parent state the need clearly and the worker name a next step. Pairs rehearse once and perform for another pair. The audience writes one sentence on whether the call solved the need.

create-4.objective: Develop a resource guide for new parents at your school that organizes at least six resources by need and access.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a guide grouping six resources by need with a contact step for each.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a one-page resource guide for new parents at their school. The guide groups at least six resources by need and gives one contact step for each. Groups pull at least one resource from a Guide-provided source. The class reviews two guides and votes on which is easiest for a busy parent to use.

2.1.8.SSH.1.standardStatement: Differentiate between gender identity, gender expression and sexual orientation.
2.1.8.SSH.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: State the working definition of gender identity, gender expression, and sexual orientation from the class glossary.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes all three definitions from memory and checks them against the glossary.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts three terms and their class definitions, then covers the definitions. Each student writes what they remember for each term on a half-sheet. Students uncover the glossary and correct their own definitions in a second color. The Guide collects the half-sheets to see which term needs more review.

understand-1.objective: Match each of the three terms to a plain-language description of what it refers to.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student correctly matches all three terms to their descriptions on a card sort.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student three term cards and three description cards. Students pair each term with the description that fits, keeping the three concepts separate. The Guide reviews the pairs and clears up any mix-up between identity and expression. Students write one sentence stating the difference they found hardest.

understand-2.objective: Explain in writing how gender identity differs from gender expression using one neutral example.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences that separate identity from expression without merging them.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide models one neutral example: a person's sense of their own gender is their identity, while the clothes or haircut they choose is their expression. Each student writes two sentences explaining the difference in their own words with a new neutral example. Pairs trade and check that the two ideas stay separate. The Guide reads two clear examples aloud.

understand-3.objective: Summarize why mixing up the three terms can lead to misunderstanding a classmate.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph naming one misunderstanding that comes from confusing two of the terms.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a short, respectful scenario where someone assumes one term means another. Each student writes a paragraph naming the mix-up and the misunderstanding it caused. Students suggest one sentence the person could have said instead. Pairs check that the paragraph keeps the three terms distinct.

understand-4.objective: Describe across two sources how the three terms are defined consistently even when wording differs.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each group lists the shared idea behind each term as stated in both sources.
understand-4.sampleTask: Groups read two Guide-provided, age-appropriate sources that define the three terms. Each group fills a chart noting how each source words each term and the shared idea underneath. Groups write one sentence on why the core idea holds even when wording changes. The class compares charts and settles on one clear class definition per term.

apply-1.objective: Sort a set of statement cards into 'gender identity,' 'gender expression,' or 'sexual orientation' using the class glossary.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each pair places all nine cards and matches the key on at least seven.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair nine neutral statement cards and three labeled trays. Pairs read each card and place it in the tray it belongs to using the glossary. Pairs check against an answer key at the ten-minute mark. Each pair names one card that was tricky and explains the correct placement.

apply-2.objective: Use the three definitions to fill in the correct term in a set of cloze sentences.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a cloze worksheet with at least eight of ten blanks correct.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide hands out ten neutral sentences, each missing one of the three terms. Students fill each blank with the correct term using the glossary. Students mark any sentence where two terms could fit and explain why one is better. The Guide reviews answers and resolves the close calls.

apply-3.objective: Apply the three definitions to correct a paragraph that uses the terms inaccurately.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student finds and fixes at least three term errors in a sample paragraph.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a short paragraph that swaps the three terms incorrectly. Students underline each error and write the correct term above it. Students add one sentence explaining the most important fix. Pairs trade and confirm each correction matches the glossary.

apply-4.objective: Build a quick-reference card that keeps the three terms straight with one example each.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a card defining all three terms with one neutral example apiece.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students make a folded reference card with the three terms, a one-line definition, and one neutral example each. Cards must keep the three ideas in separate boxes. Students swap cards with a partner and check that no two boxes blur together. The Guide displays two clear cards under the document camera.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which of three statements confuses two of the terms.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student picks the flawed statement and names the two terms it confuses.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide displays three statements, two accurate and one that blends two terms. Students pick the flawed one and write which two terms it mixed. Students rewrite the flawed statement correctly. The Guide takes a hand vote and reviews the rewrite.

analyze-2.objective: Compare gender expression and sexual orientation to show they describe different things.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair fills a chart with what each term describes and one way they are independent.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs fill a two-column chart noting what gender expression describes and what sexual orientation describes. Pairs write one sentence on why a change in one does not set the other. The Guide displays one chart and asks the class to add a missing point. Students keep examples neutral and respectful.

analyze-3.objective: Examine a short scenario and separate which sentences refer to identity, which to expression, and which to orientation.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student tags each sentence in the scenario with the correct term and defends two tags.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a short, respectful scenario with five sentences that each touch one of the three concepts. Students tag each sentence with the matching term in the margin. Students write a defense for two of their tags. Pairs compare tags and resolve any sentence they tagged differently.

analyze-4.objective: Investigate two sources and judge whether they keep the three terms distinct or blur them.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each group cites one line from each source and rates how clearly each keeps the terms separate.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups read two Guide-provided sources. Each group finds one line in each that defines a term and rates on a three-point scale how clearly the source keeps the three ideas distinct. Groups write two sentences explaining the rating. The class compares ratings and discusses which source was clearest.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge which of two student-written definitions of a term is more accurate and defend the choice with the glossary.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student picks the stronger definition and cites the glossary point that decides it.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide gives two student-style definitions of the same term, one precise and one that drifts toward another concept. Students pick the stronger one and write the glossary point that makes it stronger. Students rewrite the weaker definition to fix it. Pairs check that the rewrite is now accurate.

evaluate-4.objective: Assess across several class examples whether the group uses the three terms accurately, and support the verdict with evidence.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each group writes a claim on the class's accuracy backed by evidence from at least two examples.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Groups review three anonymized example sentences pulled from earlier class work. Each group writes a claim on whether the class uses the terms accurately, citing evidence from at least two examples. Groups name the one term that causes the most confusion. The class hears two claims and agrees on one focus for the next lesson.

create-1.objective: Label a prepared diagram by placing each of the three terms next to its description.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student attaches all three term labels to the correct spots on the diagram.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide hands each student a diagram with three description boxes and three loose term labels. Students place each label by its matching description. Students add one neutral example under one box. The Guide checks the labels and corrects any swap.

create-2.objective: Produce a short explainer poster that teaches a younger student the difference between the three terms.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student turns in a poster defining all three terms in plain words with one neutral example each.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a poster aimed at a sixth grader that defines the three terms in simple language. Each term gets one neutral example and a small drawing. Students keep the three sections clearly separate. Pairs check that a younger reader could tell the terms apart.

create-3.objective: Design a short quiz of five items that tests whether a peer can tell the three terms apart.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces five items with an answer key that targets the differences among the terms.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs write a five-item quiz, such as match or multiple choice, that checks whether someone can distinguish the three terms. Each item must target a real difference, not a trick. Pairs make an answer key and trade quizzes with another pair to take. The taking pair flags any item that is unclear or inaccurate.

create-4.objective: Develop a short lesson plan that another class could use to learn the three terms, with a hook, definitions, and a check.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a one-page plan with a hook, the three definitions, and one check for understanding.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups write a one-page mini-lesson that teaches the three terms to another eighth-grade class. The plan needs a short hook, the three definitions with examples, and one check such as a quick sort. Groups pull definitions from the class glossary and one Guide-provided source. The class reviews two plans and picks one to refine.

2.1.8.SSH.2.standardStatement: Develop a plan for the school to promote dignity and respect for people of all genders, gender identities, gender expressions, and sexual orientations in the school community.
2.1.8.SSH.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List five concrete actions that show dignity and respect for classmates in everyday school settings.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five respectful actions on a sticky note and posts two to the board.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'What does respect look like in the hallway, the lunchroom, and the locker room?' Each student writes five concrete actions on a sticky note. Stickies go on a board sorted by setting. The class reads the board and stars actions that appear more than once.

understand-1.objective: Explain why a specific respectful action, such as using the name a classmate asks for, supports dignity.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking one action to the dignity it protects.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide names one respectful action and models why it matters. Each student picks a different action from the board and writes two sentences on the dignity it protects and how a person would feel without it. Pairs trade and underline the reason. The Guide reads two examples aloud.

understand-2.objective: Describe the difference between a school rule and a school culture of respect.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student gives one example of a rule and one example of a culture habit that goes beyond the rule.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that rules set a floor while culture is what people do without being told. Each student writes one school rule about respect and one daily habit that goes further. Pairs check that the habit is not just a restatement of the rule. The Guide collects three pairs to display.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how a respectful school climate affects students' willingness to take part and speak up.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph linking climate to one student behavior, such as joining a club.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a short scenario about a welcoming versus an unwelcoming class. Each student writes a paragraph on how climate changes whether students join in, ask for help, or stay quiet. Students name one behavior that climate directly affects. Pairs check that the link is specific.
