create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a four-minute routine with at least one move per skill, written with timings. They run it once against a clock and adjust to fit four minutes. A partner confirms all four skills appear. Students note any timing fix.

create-3.objective: Choreograph a partner sequence that weaves through shared space without contact.
create-3.observableCheck: Both partners complete the sequence with no collisions and matched timing.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs design a sequence that crosses paths through general space without touching, rehearsing until timing is clean. They perform for another pair who check for collisions and synchronized moves. Pairs note the spot they had to fix to stay clear.

create-4.objective: Design a body control game for a younger class with rules and a safety note.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a one-page game with rules, the skills trained, and a safety reminder.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student invents a game that trains body control for a younger class, writing rules, the skills it builds, and one safety reminder on one page. A partner plays a quick round and flags any unclear rule. Students revise the rules that caused confusion.

2.2.5.MSC.2.standardStatement: Explain and demonstrate movement sequences, individually and with others, in response to various tempos, rhythms, and musical styles.
2.2.5.MSC.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the parts of a movement sequence: beginning, middle, and end, plus tempo.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student labels the parts on a sequence diagram.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts a labeled sequence diagram with blanks. Each student fills in beginning, middle, end, and the term tempo on a quick card. Guide reveals answers and students self-check. Any miss gets corrected on the card.

understand-1.objective: Explain how tempo changes the way a movement looks.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student describes the same move at slow and fast tempo.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a slow track then a fast track for the same step. Each student writes how the step changed, naming size or sharpness. Pairs compare descriptions and agree on the clearest words. Guide collects two to share.

understand-2.objective: Describe how a rhythm guides when each move happens in a sequence.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student marks move spots on a four-beat count line.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide claps a four-beat rhythm. Each student marks on a count line where they would place three moves to fit the beats. They tap the line to test the fit. Pairs compare placements and adjust any move that lands off the beat.

understand-3.objective: Predict how a sequence will feel different in two musical styles before performing it.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names the expected change and checks it after trying both.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide names two styles, such as a march and a swing tune, for the same three moves. Each student predicts how the feel will differ and writes why. They perform to both, then confirm or revise the prediction. Pairs compare results.

understand-4.objective: Relate a movement sequence to the story or mood the music suggests.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names the mood and one move that matches it.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide plays a track with a clear mood. Each student names the mood and writes one move that fits it, such as low slow steps for a calm tune. Pairs check the move matches the mood and not just any movement.

apply-1.objective: Perform a taught three-move sequence on the beat.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student hits all three moves in time, checked by a partner.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide teaches a three-move sequence to a steady track. Each student performs it while a partner checks each move lands on the beat using a count. Students repeat until all three hit in time. Roles switch.

apply-2.objective: Adjust a sequence to match a faster tempo without losing the moves.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student keeps all moves intact as tempo increases, logged by a partner.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students perform a known sequence as Guide raises tempo across three tracks. A partner logs whether all moves stayed intact at each speed. Students name the adjustment, such as smaller steps, that kept the moves clean. Roles rotate.

apply-3.objective: Choose moves that fit a given musical style for a short sequence.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student selects three style-fitting moves and explains each pick.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide assigns a musical style. Each student picks three moves from a posted menu that fit the style and writes why each fits. Pairs trade picks and flag any move that clashes with the style. Students swap out the clashing move.

apply-4.objective: Lead a partner through a sequence using counts and verbal cues.
apply-4.observableCheck: The partner performs the full sequence on beat from the leader's cues.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student leads a partner through a four-move sequence using counts and spoken cues, no full demonstration. The partner follows on the track while a third checks timing. Roles rotate so all lead once. Leaders note one cue to sharpen.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which move in a sequence falls off the beat in a recorded run.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the off-beat move and the beat it missed.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a clip of a sequence with one move off the beat. Each student names the off move and the count where it slipped. Pairs compare and rewatch to confirm. Guide reveals the intended timing.

analyze-2.objective: Compare the same sequence in two styles to decide which fits the moves better.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the better style fit and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students watch a sequence performed to two styles. They pick which style fits the moves better and write two reasons, such as sharp moves matching a strong beat. Pairs compare verdicts and debate any difference using the clips.

analyze-3.objective: Diagnose why a group sequence falls out of sync and name the cause.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the sync breakdown and a fix.
analyze-3.sampleTask: In trios, two perform a learned sequence together while one observes for the moment they drift apart. The observer names the cause, such as a missed count or different start, and a fix. The pair retries the fix and the trio checks whether they stayed in sync.

analyze-4.objective: Break down a dance into its sections and map the tempo of each.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student labels each section with its tempo on a dance map.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide shows a dance with shifting tempos. Students divide it into sections on a map and label each section's tempo as slow, medium, or fast. They mark where tempo shifts. Pairs compare maps and resolve any disagreement by rewatching.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a recorded sequence matched its music and defend the rating.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores the match on a rubric and cites two rubric points.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide plays a student sequence with its track. Each student scores timing, style fit, and flow on a rubric and marks a verdict. They cite two rubric points. Guide surfaces any split for a short debate.

evaluate-4.objective: Recommend changes to a sequence to fit its music better and justify each.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student proposes two changes with a reason tied to tempo or style.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a written sequence and its assigned style. They propose two changes that would fit the music better and write a reason for each plus how to tell it worked. Pairs challenge any change that breaks the flow, and students revise.

create-1.objective: Assemble a three-move sequence card from a posted move bank.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a card listing three moves in order with counts.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick three moves from the bank and write them in order with a count for each on a sequence card. They number the moves. Guide checks a classmate could perform it from the card.

create-2.objective: Build a 90-second solo sequence set to a chosen tempo.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student's sequence has a clear beginning, middle, end, and fits the track.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a 90-second solo with a beginning, middle, and end set to a chosen track, written with counts. They run it once and adjust to fit the music. A partner confirms the three parts are clear. Students note any timing fix.

create-3.objective: Choreograph a partner sequence that stays in sync across a tempo change.
create-3.observableCheck: Both partners stay matched through the tempo shift.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs design a sequence that holds together when the tempo shifts midway, rehearsing the transition until matched. They perform for another pair who check sync through the shift. Pairs note the move they adjusted at the change.

create-4.objective: Design a group routine that teaches a musical style through movement.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a routine plan naming the style and the moves that show it.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups design a routine that shows a musical style's feel through chosen moves, writing the plan and the style features each move highlights. They rehearse and perform for the class. Viewers name the style from the movement alone. Groups note whether the style read clearly.

2.2.5.MSC.3.standardStatement: Demonstrate and perform movement skills with developmentally appropriate control in isolated settings (e.g., skill practice) and applied settings (e.g., games, sports, dance, recreational activities).
2.2.5.MSC.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name a movement skill and the two settings where it is used: isolated and applied.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one skill and an example of each setting.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide explains isolated practice versus applied game play. Each student writes one skill, such as dribbling, with an isolated example and an applied example on a quick card. Pairs check both settings are correct.

understand-1.objective: Explain why a skill is practiced in isolation before it is used in a game.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one reason isolated practice helps.
understand-1.sampleTask: After a short dribble drill, each student writes one reason practicing alone first helps before a game, such as building control without defenders. Pairs combine reasons and pick the strongest. Guide builds a class list.

understand-2.objective: Describe what changes about a skill when it moves from drill to game.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student names two things that get harder in a game.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students dribble in a clear lane, then dribble while a passive defender shadows them. Each writes two things that got harder, such as keeping eyes up or protecting the ball. Pairs compare lists and agree on the top two.

understand-3.objective: Predict how control will change between an isolated drill and a game setting.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names the predicted change and checks it after both settings.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide describes a passing drill then a small-sided game. Each student predicts how their passing control will change and writes why. They perform both, then confirm or revise the prediction. Pairs compare results.

understand-4.objective: Relate a drilled skill to the exact game moment it pays off.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names a game moment where the skill decides the outcome.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student picks a drilled skill and names the game moment it matters, such as a controlled stop before a shot. They write the skill, the moment, and why control matters there. Pairs check the link is specific.

apply-1.objective: Perform a skill with control in an isolated drill to a set target.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student hits the target count, tallied by a partner.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide sets a drill, such as ten controlled dribbles around a cone. Each student performs while a partner tallies clean reps. Students aim to reach the target count. Roles switch so both perform and tally.

apply-2.objective: Adjust a skill to keep control as a passive defender is added.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student keeps control with the defender present, logged across two tries.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students perform the skill with a passive defender shadowing. A partner logs lost-control moments on two tries. Students name one adjustment, such as a lower dribble, that helped. Roles rotate.

apply-3.objective: Choose when to use a skill during a small-sided game.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student uses the skill at a fitting moment, noted by an observer.
apply-3.sampleTask: In a three-on-three game, each student looks for the right moment to use a target skill, such as a pivot to protect the ball. A side observer notes one fitting use per player. Players reflect on whether they picked good moments and adjust for the next round.

apply-4.objective: Combine two skills in sequence during applied play.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student links the two skills smoothly at least once, noted by an observer.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide names a two-skill combo, such as dribble then pass. In a small-sided game, each student tries to link the two smoothly. An observer marks each clean combo. Players reflect on the smoothest link and what set it up.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which part of a skill breaks down in a recorded game clip.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the part that broke and the game pressure that caused it.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a clip of a skill failing under game pressure. Each student names the part that broke, such as the dribble rising too high, and the pressure that caused it. Pairs compare and rewatch to confirm.

analyze-2.objective: Compare control in the drill versus the game for one skill.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names where control was stronger and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Using their own drill tally and game observation, students compare where their control was stronger. They write two reasons for the gap, such as fewer choices in the drill. Pairs compare and mark any difference.

analyze-3.objective: Diagnose why a teammate's skill works in drills but fails in games.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the game factor and a practice fix.
analyze-3.sampleTask: In trios, students watch a teammate who dribbles cleanly in drills but loses it in a game. They name the game factor, such as not scanning for defenders, and a practice fix that adds that pressure. The teammate tries the fix in a quick game and the trio checks for improvement.

analyze-4.objective: Break a game performance into the skills used and rate control for each.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student lists skills used and a control rating with evidence for each.
analyze-4.sampleTask: After a small-sided game, each student lists the skills they used and rates control for each on a three-point scale, citing one moment as evidence. They mark the weakest skill. Pairs compare and check each rating has evidence.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a clip shows game-ready control and defend the rating.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores the clip on a rubric and cites two rubric points.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide plays a clip of a student in applied play. Each student scores control, decision, and consistency on a rubric and marks a verdict. They cite two rubric points. Guide surfaces any split rating.

evaluate-4.objective: Recommend a practice plan to move a skill from drill-ready to game-ready and justify it.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student proposes two practice changes with reasons tied to game pressure.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a peer's drill and game data. They recommend two practice changes that add game pressure, with a reason and a measure of success for each. Pairs challenge any change that skips the basics, and students revise.

create-1.objective: Lay out a two-station skill drill from a posted drill bank.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a labeled drill card naming the skill at each station.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick two drills from the bank and arrange them on a drill card, labeling the skill each builds and the rep goal. Guide checks a classmate could run it from the card.

create-2.objective: Build a practice-to-game progression for one skill in 5 minutes of play.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student's plan moves from isolated drill to applied game with a clear step between.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a progression that starts isolated, adds a passive defender, then enters a small game, written with timings to fit five minutes. They run it and adjust. A partner checks the steps build pressure in order. Students note any fix.

create-3.objective: Choreograph a partner drill that practices two skills under light pressure.
create-3.observableCheck: Both partners complete the drill linking both skills under the added pressure.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs design a drill that links two skills with one partner adding light defense, rehearsing until both can run it. They perform for another pair who check both skills appear under pressure. Pairs note the adjustment that made it work.

create-4.objective: Design a small-sided game that forces use of a target skill, with rules and a safety note.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a one-page game whose rules require the target skill plus a safety reminder.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student invents a small-sided game whose rules make a target skill necessary, such as requiring a pivot before a shot, written on one page with a safety reminder. A group plays a quick round and flags unclear rules. Students revise the rules that caused confusion.

2.2.5.MSC.4.standardStatement: Develop the necessary body control to improve stability and balance during movement and physical activity.
2.2.5.MSC.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name three cues that improve balance, such as wide base, low center, and eyes up.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes three balance cues on a quick card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts three balance cues. Each student writes all three on a quick card from memory. Guide reveals answers and students self-check. Any miss is corrected.

understand-1.objective: Explain how a wider base of support helps balance.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence linking foot width to steadiness.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students stand with feet together, then shoulder width, and feel the difference when nudged lightly by a partner. Each writes one sentence on why a wider base helps. Pairs compare and pick the clearest wording.

understand-2.objective: Describe what 'center of gravity' means using a body example.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student points to and names where their center sits during a pose.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide defines center of gravity simply. Each student holds a balance pose and writes where their center sits and how lowering it helped, such as bending the knees. Pairs check the explanation uses a real body part.

understand-3.objective: Predict which of two stances will hold balance longer before testing.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names the steadier stance and checks the prediction after a timed hold.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide shows a narrow stance and a low wide stance. Each student predicts which holds longer against a gentle push and writes why. Pairs test both with a timed hold and a light nudge, then confirm or revise the prediction.

understand-4.objective: Relate balance control to a real activity, such as landing a jump.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names the activity moment and the balance cue that helps it.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student picks an activity moment that needs balance, such as landing a jump, and names the cue that helps, such as bent knees on landing. They write the moment, the cue, and why it matters. Pairs check the link is specific.

apply-1.objective: Hold a single-leg balance for a set time using taught cues.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student holds the pose for the target seconds, timed by a partner.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide sets a single-leg hold goal, such as fifteen seconds, with cues to use. Each student holds while a partner times and notes which cue helped. Students aim to reach the goal. Roles switch.

apply-2.objective: Adjust stance to stay stable while a partner adds a gentle nudge.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student stays balanced through nudges, logged across two tries.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students hold a stance while a partner gives light, fair nudges. A partner logs steadiness on two tries. Students name the adjustment, such as widening the base, that helped. Roles rotate. Nudges stay gentle and agreed.

apply-3.objective: Choose a stable position to receive a pass without being pushed off balance.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student sets a stable base before receiving, noted by an observer.
apply-3.sampleTask: In a passing drill with a light defender, each student sets a stable base before catching. An observer notes whether the base was set in time. Players reflect on whether their stance held against the defender and adjust.

apply-4.objective: Land a jump with control and hold the landing for two seconds.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student lands and holds without a wobble, judged by a partner.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student performs a two-foot jump and holds the landing for two seconds with bent knees. A partner judges whether the landing held without a step or wobble across three jumps. Students name the cue that steadied the best landing.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the moment balance is lost in a recorded movement.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the lost-balance moment and names the cause.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a clip of a student wobbling on a landing. Each student marks the moment balance went and names the cause, such as a straight-leg landing. Pairs compare and rewatch to confirm.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two landing styles to decide which is more stable.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the steadier style and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students watch two landing clips, one stiff and one with bent knees. They pick the steadier and write two reasons, such as a lower center and wider feet. Pairs compare and mark any difference.

analyze-3.objective: Diagnose why a partner falls out of a balance pose and propose the cause.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the breakdown and the cue tied to it.
analyze-3.sampleTask: In trios, one holds a balance pose while two observe for the moment it breaks. Observers name the cause, such as eyes dropping or a stiff knee, on a diagnosis card. The performer tries the matching cue and the trio checks for a longer hold.

analyze-4.objective: Break a balance course into stations and rank them by stability demand.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student ranks the stations and justifies the top demand.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students complete a four-station balance course, then rank the stations from most to least demanding on stability. They justify the top rank with one reason. Pairs compare rankings and resolve differences by revisiting a station.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a clip shows controlled balance and defend the rating.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores the clip on a rubric and cites two rubric points.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide plays a balance clip. Each student scores base, center, and steadiness on a rubric and marks a verdict. They cite two rubric points. Guide surfaces any split rating.

evaluate-4.objective: Recommend changes to a routine to build balance over two weeks and justify each.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student proposes two changes with reasons tied to stability.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a balance routine and propose two changes that would build stability over two weeks, such as longer holds or an uneven surface. They write a reason and a success measure for each. Pairs challenge any unsafe change, and students revise.

create-1.objective: Lay out a three-pose balance sequence from a posted pose bank.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a card listing three poses with hold times.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick three poses from the bank and write them in order with a hold time for each on a card. They number the poses. Guide checks a classmate could perform it from the card.

create-2.objective: Build a 3-minute balance circuit that grows in difficulty.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student's circuit increases challenge across stations and fits the time.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a three-minute circuit where balance demand rises station to station, written with timings. They run it once and adjust. A partner confirms difficulty climbs in order. Students note any fix.

create-3.objective: Choreograph a partner balance routine where partners support each other.
create-3.observableCheck: Both partners hold the shared poses without a fall.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs design a routine with two poses where they brace each other for balance, rehearsing until steady. They perform for another pair who check both held without falling. Pairs note the adjustment that steadied the hardest pose.

create-4.objective: Design a balance game for a younger class with rules and a safety note.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a one-page game with rules, the balance skill, and a safety reminder.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student invents a balance game for a younger class, writing rules, the balance skill it builds, and one safety reminder on one page. A partner plays a quick round and flags any unclear rule. Students revise the confusing rules.

2.2.5.MSC.5.standardStatement: Correct movement skills and analyze concepts in response to external feedback and self-evaluation with understanding and demonstrating how the change improves performance.
2.2.5.MSC.5.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the two feedback sources for fixing a skill: external feedback and self-evaluation.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes both sources with one example of each.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide defines external feedback as input from others and self-evaluation as judging your own move. Each student writes both terms with an example, such as a coach tip and a video check. Pairs confirm the examples fit.

understand-1.objective: Explain how a single cue can change a skill for the better.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one cue and the change it produces.
understand-1.sampleTask: After a throwing drill, each student writes one cue they received, such as 'step toward the target,' and the change it made. Pairs compare and check the change is observable, not vague.

understand-2.objective: Describe how to turn feedback into a specific action.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student rewrites a vague tip as a concrete action step.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide gives vague tips, such as 'do better.' Each student rewrites two into concrete actions, such as 'bend knees before the throw.' Pairs trade and check each rewrite names a doable move. Guide shares two strong rewrites.

understand-3.objective: Predict how a proposed correction will change performance before trying it.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names the expected change and checks it after the trial.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide names a correction for a skill, such as a follow-through on a throw. Each student predicts how distance or accuracy will change and writes why. They try the correction and confirm or revise the prediction. Pairs compare.

understand-4.objective: Relate a self-spotted error to the concept behind the fix.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names the error and the movement concept that explains the fix.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student names an error they noticed in their own skill and the concept behind the fix, such as 'follow-through adds force.' They write the error, the fix, and the concept. Pairs check the concept actually explains the fix.

apply-1.objective: Apply one given cue to a skill and perform the corrected version.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student performs the skill with the cue visibly applied, checked by a partner.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student one cue for a target skill. They perform the corrected version while a partner checks the cue shows in the move. Students repeat until the cue is visible each rep. Roles switch.

apply-2.objective: Adjust a skill across reps based on a partner's feedback.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student shows the adjustment in later reps, logged by the partner.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students perform a skill while a partner gives one piece of feedback after each set. The partner logs whether the adjustment appeared in later reps. Students name the feedback that helped most. Roles rotate.

apply-3.objective: Use a video replay of yourself to choose one correction to apply.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student names one correction from the replay and performs it.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students watch a short replay of their own skill on a tablet, pick one correction, and perform the fixed version. A partner confirms the chosen fix appeared. Students write the before and after in one line each.

apply-4.objective: Coach a partner by giving one targeted correction that improves their skill.
apply-4.observableCheck: The partner's skill visibly improves after the correction, confirmed by both.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student watches a partner's skill, gives one targeted correction, and watches the next attempts for improvement. The partner reports whether the fix helped. Roles rotate so all coach once. The coach notes whether the cue was clear.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the single error in a recorded skill that limits performance most.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the limiting error and the part of the skill it sits in.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a clip of a skill with several small flaws. Each student names the one error that limits performance most and where it sits in the move. Pairs compare and rewatch to confirm.

analyze-2.objective: Compare a before and after clip to decide whether the correction worked.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student judges whether performance improved and cites two changes.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students watch a before and after clip of a corrected skill. They judge whether it improved and cite two visible changes, such as a longer follow-through. Pairs compare verdicts and debate any difference.

analyze-3.objective: Diagnose which feedback would most help a struggling teammate and why.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student picks one piece of feedback and ties it to the root cause.
analyze-3.sampleTask: In trios, students watch a teammate's skill and brainstorm three possible cues. They pick the one feedback that targets the root cause, not a symptom, and write why. The teammate tries it and the trio checks for improvement.

analyze-4.objective: Break a skill into phases and locate which phase the feedback should target.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student labels the skill phases and marks the target phase with a reason.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students label a skill's phases, such as prep, action, and follow-through, on a diagram. Using a teammate's performance, they mark which phase the feedback should target and write why. Pairs compare and resolve any difference.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a peer applied feedback well and defend the rating.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores the correction process on a rubric and cites two rubric points.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide shows a clip of a student receiving and applying feedback. Each student scores how well the feedback was used on a rubric and marks a verdict. They cite two rubric points. Guide surfaces any split rating.

evaluate-4.objective: Recommend a feedback plan for a teammate to fix a skill over a week and justify it.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student proposes two feedback steps with reasons tied to the error.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a teammate's skill data and recommend a week-long plan with two feedback steps, each with a reason and a way to check progress. Pairs challenge any step that targets the wrong phase, and students revise.

create-1.objective: Make a correction checklist card for one skill from a posted cue bank.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a card listing the key cues in order for one skill.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick the key cues for one skill from the bank and write them in order on a checklist card. They number the cues. Guide checks a classmate could use it to self-correct.

create-2.objective: Build a 5-minute self-correction routine using video and a checklist.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student's routine pairs a recorded attempt with a checklist review and a retry.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a five-minute routine: perform, record, review against a checklist, then retry the fix, written with timings. They run it once and adjust. A partner confirms the routine includes a review step. Students note any fix.

create-3.objective: Design a peer-coaching protocol that gives one clear correction per round.
create-3.observableCheck: Both partners run the protocol and each receives one usable correction.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs design a coaching protocol: watch, name one fix, retry, confirm, written as steps. They run it on a real skill and check each gave one usable correction. They perform the protocol for another pair who check the steps are clear. Pairs note any step they tightened.

create-4.objective: Design a self-evaluation tool a younger class could use to fix one skill.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a one-page tool with picture cues and a check box per cue.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student builds a self-evaluation tool for a younger class on one skill, using picture cues and a check box for each, on one page. A partner pretends to be a younger student and uses it, flagging any unclear cue. Students revise the confusing cues.

2.2.5.MSC.6.standardStatement: Execute appropriate behaviors and etiquette while participating as a player and viewing as an observer during physical activity, games, and other events, contributes to a safe environment.
2.2.5.MSC.6.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List four good-sport behaviors for players and observers.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four behaviors on a recall card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide prompts students to recall good-sport behaviors covered in class. Each writes four, such as shaking hands or cheering fairly, on a recall card. Stickies go on a class chart split into player and observer columns.

understand-1.objective: Explain how one etiquette behavior keeps activity safe.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student links a behavior to a specific safety outcome.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student picks one etiquette behavior, such as calling for a ball before catching, and writes how it prevents a specific injury or conflict. Pairs check the safety outcome is concrete, not 'it is nice.'

understand-2.objective: Describe the difference between a player's and an observer's responsibilities.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts six behaviors into player and observer roles.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a strip of six behaviors. Students sort each into a player or observer column on a T-chart and write one that fits both. Pairs compare charts and resolve any disagreement.

understand-3.objective: Predict how a game changes when one player ignores etiquette.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names the likely effect on safety and play.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide describes a scene where a player argues every call. Each student predicts the effect on the game's safety and flow and writes why. Pairs compare predictions and agree on the most likely effect.

understand-4.objective: Relate sideline behavior to the safety of players on the field.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names an observer action that protects players.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student names one observer behavior, such as staying behind the line, and writes how it protects players on the field. Pairs check the link names a real safety risk the behavior reduces.

apply-1.objective: Show two good-sport behaviors during a small-sided game.
apply-1.observableCheck: An observer marks both behaviors when each player performs them.
apply-1.sampleTask: In a small-sided game, each student aims to show at least two good-sport behaviors, such as encouraging a teammate and accepting a call. A side observer marks each behavior seen. Players reflect on which they showed and missed.

apply-2.objective: Adjust behavior after a reminder to follow game etiquette.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student corrects the flagged behavior in later play, logged by an observer.
apply-2.sampleTask: During a game, an observer gives a quiet reminder when an etiquette slip happens. The observer logs whether the player corrected it afterward. Players reflect on the reminder that helped. Roles rotate across rounds.

apply-3.objective: Choose the right response to a disputed call during play.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student names and performs a fair response in a staged dispute.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide stages a disputed call in a quick game. Each student responds in a fair way, such as a redo or accepting the official's call. An observer notes the response. Players reflect on whether their choice kept the game safe and fair.

apply-4.objective: Officiate a peer game while modeling fair, calm decisions.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student makes clear calls and manages a dispute without escalation.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students take turns officiating a short peer game, making clear calls and handling one staged dispute calmly. Players rate whether the official stayed fair and calm. The official notes one call they would explain more clearly next time.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the etiquette breakdown in a recorded game clip.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the breakdown and the safety risk it created.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a clip with a clear etiquette slip, such as crowding an official. Each student names the breakdown and the safety risk it created. Pairs compare and rewatch to confirm.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two players' sideline conduct to decide which kept the game safer.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the safer conduct and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students watch two sideline clips. They pick which conduct kept the game safer and cite two reasons, such as staying behind the line and cheering fairly. Pairs compare and debate any difference.

analyze-3.objective: Diagnose why a small conflict grew during a game and name the turning point.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the moment the conflict escalated and a behavior that would have stopped it.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a scene where a small disagreement grows. In trios, students trace the moment it escalated and name the behavior that would have stopped it. They write the turning point and the fix on a card. Trios share one each.

analyze-4.objective: Break down an event into roles and map the etiquette each role owes the others.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student maps players, observers, and officials to the behaviors each owes.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students map a game event into three roles: players, observers, officials. For each role they list the etiquette it owes the others on a role map. They mark one behavior shared by all roles. Pairs compare maps and resolve differences.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a clip shows a safe, respectful environment and defend the rating.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores conduct on a rubric and cites two rubric points.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide plays a game clip. Each student scores player conduct, observer conduct, and safety on a rubric and marks a verdict. They cite two rubric points. Guide surfaces any split rating.

evaluate-4.objective: Recommend changes to a class game's rules to improve etiquette and justify each.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student proposes two rule changes with reasons tied to safety or fairness.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review the class game rules and propose two changes that would improve etiquette, such as a mandatory handshake or a calm-call rule. They write a reason and a way to check it works for each. Pairs challenge any change that slows play too much, and students revise.

create-1.objective: Make a good-sport behavior card for players and observers from a posted bank.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a card listing three player and three observer behaviors.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick three player and three observer behaviors from the bank and write them on a two-column card. Guide checks a classmate could post it as a reminder.

create-2.objective: Build a sportsmanship pledge for the class with five specific commitments.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student's pledge lists five concrete, checkable behaviors.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a class sportsmanship pledge with five concrete commitments, such as 'I shake hands after every game.' They keep each line checkable, not vague. A partner reviews and flags any line that cannot be observed. Students rewrite the vague lines.

create-3.objective: Design a role-play that teaches how to handle a disputed call.
create-3.observableCheck: The pair performs a dispute and a fair resolution that classmates can name.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs script and rehearse a short role-play showing a disputed call and a fair resolution. They perform it for the class, who name the fair behavior shown. Pairs note whether the resolution read clearly and adjust.

create-4.objective: Design an etiquette guide for a younger class's first game day.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a one-page guide with rules, pictures, and a safety reminder.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student creates a one-page etiquette guide for a younger class's first game day, using simple rules, pictures, and one safety reminder. A partner pretends to be a younger student and reads it, flagging any unclear rule. Students revise the confusing parts.

2.2.5.MSC.7.standardStatement: Apply specific rules, strategies, and procedures for specific physical activity, games, and sports in a safe active environment.
2.2.5.MSC.7.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List the basic rules and one strategy for a chosen game.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes three rules and one strategy on a recall card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide names a familiar game, such as small-sided soccer. Each student writes three rules and one strategy from memory on a recall card. Pairs compare and fill any gap.

understand-1.objective: Explain why one rule exists in a chosen game.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student links a rule to the problem it prevents.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student picks one rule and writes the problem it prevents, such as offside stopping cherry-picking. Pairs check the explanation names a real game problem, not just 'it is the rule.'

understand-2.objective: Describe how a strategy helps a team reach the game's goal.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student explains one strategy and the advantage it creates.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students learn a spacing strategy, then each writes how spreading out creates passing lanes. Pairs compare and agree on the clearest explanation. Guide shares two to the class.

understand-3.objective: Predict how breaking a procedure would affect safety or flow.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names the likely effect of skipping a procedure.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide names a procedure, such as waiting for the whistle to enter play. Each student predicts what happens if it is skipped and writes why. Pairs compare and agree on the most likely effect.

understand-4.objective: Relate a strategy to a specific game moment where it wins an advantage.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names the moment and the strategy that fits it.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student names a game moment, such as a fast break, and the strategy that wins there, such as a quick outlet pass. They write the moment, the strategy, and why it works. Pairs check the link is specific.

apply-1.objective: Follow the rules and procedures correctly through one round of play.
apply-1.observableCheck: An observer logs zero rule or procedure breaks for the player in the round.
apply-1.sampleTask: In a small-sided game, each student plays one round aiming to follow every rule and procedure. A side observer logs any break. Players reflect on which rule was hardest to keep. Roles rotate.

apply-2.objective: Adjust positioning to apply a team strategy during live play.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student moves to the strategy's spot at the right time, noted by an observer.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide assigns a spacing strategy. In live play, each student moves to the right spot to keep the spacing. An observer notes timely moves. Players reflect on whether they held their spot. Roles rotate.

apply-3.objective: Choose the right strategy for the game situation in the moment.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student picks a fitting strategy at a decision point, noted by an observer.
apply-3.sampleTask: In a game with built-in decision points, each student picks a fitting strategy, such as pressing when up a player. An observer notes the choice and the situation. Players reflect on whether the choice matched the moment.

apply-4.objective: Run a set play that combines a rule, a strategy, and a procedure.
apply-4.observableCheck: The team executes the set play within the rules at least once, noted by an observer.
apply-4.sampleTask: Teams learn a simple set play, such as a throw-in routine, that uses a rule, a strategy, and a starting procedure. They run it in a game and an observer marks a clean execution. Teams reflect on the part that broke down and retry.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the rule or strategy error in a recorded play.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the error and the rule or strategy it broke.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a clip with a clear strategy or rule error, such as bunching on offense. Each student names the error and what it broke. Pairs compare and rewatch to confirm.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two strategies to decide which fits a given game situation.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the better strategy and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students study a situation, such as defending a lead. They pick the better of two strategies and write two reasons. Pairs compare verdicts and debate any difference using the situation details.

analyze-3.objective: Diagnose why a team's strategy is failing and name the cause.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the cause and a specific fix.
analyze-3.sampleTask: In trios, students watch a team whose press keeps getting beaten. They name the cause, such as gaps between defenders, and a specific fix. The team tries the fix in a quick round and the trio checks for improvement.

analyze-4.objective: Break a game into phases and map the strategy each phase calls for.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student labels game phases and the strategy each needs.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students divide a game into phases, such as attack, transition, and defense, on a map. For each they write the strategy it calls for. They mark a phase where teams often slip. Pairs compare and resolve differences.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a team applied its strategy well and defend the rating.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores strategy use on a rubric and cites two rubric points.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide plays a clip of team play. Each student scores rule-following, strategy use, and spacing on a rubric and marks a verdict. They cite two rubric points. Guide surfaces any split rating.

evaluate-4.objective: Recommend a strategy change to fix a team's weakness and justify it.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student proposes two changes with reasons tied to the weakness.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a team's game notes and recommend two strategy changes with a reason and a success measure for each. Pairs challenge any change that ignores the safety rules, and students revise.

create-1.objective: Make a play card for one set play from a posted strategy bank.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a card diagramming the play with positions and the rule it uses.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick a set play from the bank and diagram it on a card, marking positions, the strategy, and the rule it relies on. Guide checks a team could run it from the card.

create-2.objective: Build a simple game plan for a small-sided match with two strategies.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student's plan names two strategies and when to use each.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a game plan for a small-sided match naming two strategies and the situations to use each, such as press when ahead. They check the plan follows the safety rules. A partner reviews for clarity. Students adjust any vague trigger.

create-3.objective: Design a set play that a team can run within the game's rules.
create-3.observableCheck: The team runs the designed play within the rules during a scrimmage.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs design a set play that follows the rules, diagram the movement, and teach it to two teammates. The four run it in a scrimmage and check it stayed legal and worked. The pair notes the step they adjusted.

create-4.objective: Design a modified game with new rules and a strategy guide for a younger class.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a one-page game with rules, a strategy tip, and a safety note.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student modifies a known game for a younger class, writing the new rules, one strategy tip, and a safety reminder on one page. A group plays a quick round and flags unclear rules. Students revise the confusing rules.

2.2.5.N.1.standardStatement: Explain how healthy eating provides energy, helps to maintain healthy weight, lowers risk of disease, and keeps body systems functioning effectively.
2.2.5.N.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the four benefits of healthy eating from the standard: energy, healthy weight, lower disease risk, and body function.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists all four benefits on a recall card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the four benefits with one missing. Each student lists all four from memory on a recall card. Guide reveals the answers and students self-check. Any miss is corrected.

understand-1.objective: Explain how food gives the body energy in plain terms.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence explanation using the word energy.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide explains that food is fuel the body breaks down for energy. Each student writes a one-sentence version in their own words and names one food and an activity it could fuel. Pairs check the sentence makes sense.

understand-2.objective: Describe how one food choice connects to one body benefit.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student links a specific food to a specific benefit.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student picks a food, such as oranges, and writes the benefit it supports, such as helping the body fight illness. Pairs check the link is specific, not 'it is healthy.' Guide collects three strong links.

understand-3.objective: Predict how skipping balanced meals would affect energy during the school day.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names the likely effect and gives a reason.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide describes a day with only sugary snacks. Each student predicts the effect on afternoon energy and writes why. Pairs compare predictions and agree on the most likely pattern, such as a crash after a spike.

understand-4.objective: Relate a body system to the nutrient that helps it work.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student matches one body system to a supporting nutrient.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students match three body systems to a nutrient that supports each, such as bones and calcium. They write one match in a full sentence. Pairs check the match is accurate using a nutrient chart.

apply-1.objective: Sort a set of foods by the main benefit each provides.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places ten foods into benefit groups correctly.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives ten food cards and four benefit bins. Each student sorts the cards into the bin for the benefit each food best supports. Pairs compare sorts and resolve any difference using a nutrient chart.

apply-2.objective: Use a nutrition label to decide whether a snack supports energy and health.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student reads two labels and marks which better supports the benefit.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students get two snack labels. They read sugar, fiber, and serving size, then mark which snack better supports steady energy and write why. Pairs compare and check the reasoning uses label numbers.

apply-3.objective: Choose a balanced lunch from a menu that hits three benefits.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student selects foods covering three named benefits and labels each.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide provides a menu. Each student builds a lunch that supports at least three benefits and labels which food meets which benefit. A partner checks all three benefits are covered. Students swap any food that doubles up.

apply-4.objective: Plan a day of meals that supports energy across the school day.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student's day plan spaces meals and names the energy role of each.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student plans breakfast, lunch, and a snack to keep energy steady through the school day, naming the role of each meal. A partner checks the plan spaces food sensibly and avoids a sugar-only meal. Students adjust any gap.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which food in a meal contributes most to a stated benefit.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the top contributor and cites the nutrient.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide shows a meal photo. Each student names which food contributes most to lower disease risk and cites the nutrient, such as fiber from beans. Pairs compare and check the nutrient with a chart.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two breakfasts to decide which better supports morning energy.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the better breakfast and cites two nutrient reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students compare a sugary cereal breakfast and a whole-grain plus fruit breakfast. They pick the one that better supports steady energy and cite two reasons, such as fiber slowing sugar release. Pairs compare verdicts.

analyze-3.objective: Diagnose why a sample diet leaves a person tired and name the gap.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the missing benefit and the food group that would fill it.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a one-day food log heavy on sweets and light on protein and produce. In trios, students name why the person feels tired and the food group missing. They write the gap and a food to add. Trios share one each.

analyze-4.objective: Break a day's eating into benefits met and missed across body systems.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student maps each meal to the benefits it covers and flags the gaps.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students take a sample daily menu and map each meal to the benefits it covers on a chart. They flag which benefit or system is underserved. Pairs compare maps and resolve any difference using a nutrient chart.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample meal plan supports all four benefits and defend the call.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores the plan on a rubric and cites two rubric points.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample meal plan. Each student scores it on a rubric covering energy, weight, disease risk, and body function, then marks a verdict. They cite two rubric points. Guide surfaces any split.

evaluate-4.objective: Recommend changes to a peer's daily menu to better support health and justify each.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student proposes two swaps with reasons tied to a benefit.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a peer's daily menu and recommend two food swaps, each with a reason tied to a benefit and a note on what improves. Pairs challenge any swap that ignores cost or taste, and students revise.

create-1.objective: Make a benefit-to-food matching card from a posted food bank.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a card pairing each of four benefits with a food.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick a food from the bank for each of the four benefits and write the pairs on a card. Guide checks each pairing is accurate and could serve as a reminder.

create-2.objective: Build a one-day menu that supports all four eating benefits.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student's menu labels which meal supports each benefit.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a one-day menu and labels which meal supports each of the four benefits. They check no benefit is missing. A partner reviews and flags any gap. Students fill the gap with one food.

create-3.objective: Design a poster teaching peers how healthy eating fuels the body.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student's poster shows each benefit with a food example and a short why.
create-3.sampleTask: Each student makes a poster with the four benefits, a food example for each, and a one-line reason. They check every benefit appears. A partner reviews for accuracy and clarity. Students fix any unclear or inaccurate line.

create-4.objective: Design a healthy-eating campaign for the school cafeteria with a clear message and three actions.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a one-page campaign with a message, three actions, and a benefit each supports.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student plans a cafeteria campaign with a slogan and three specific actions, such as a fruit-first line, each tied to a benefit. They keep it to one page. A partner role-plays a student and judges whether the actions are doable. Students revise the weak action.

2.2.5.N.2.standardStatement: Create a healthy meal based on nutritional content, value, calories, and cost.
2.2.5.N.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the four factors a healthy meal balances: nutrition, value, calories, and cost.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists all four factors on a recall card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the four factors with one blank. Each student lists all four from memory on a recall card. Guide reveals the answers and students self-check. Any miss is corrected.

understand-1.objective: Explain what 'nutritional value' means using a food example.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence definition with a food example.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide defines nutritional value as how much nourishment a food gives for its calories. Each student writes a one-sentence version and names a high-value and a low-value food. Pairs check the examples fit the definition.

understand-2.objective: Describe how cost can affect which healthy foods a family chooses.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student gives one example of a cheaper healthy swap.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student writes one example of a cheaper food that keeps nutrition, such as dried beans instead of canned. Pairs compare swaps and check each keeps the nutrition while cutting cost.

understand-3.objective: Predict how adding a vegetable changes a meal's calories and value.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names the likely change to calories and value with a reason.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide shows a plain meal. Each student predicts how adding a serving of vegetables changes calories and nutritional value and writes why. Pairs compare predictions and agree on the most likely effect.

understand-4.objective: Relate a meal's calorie level to the activity it should fuel.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student matches a meal to a fitting activity level.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students match three meals to activity levels, such as a bigger meal before a long bike ride. Each writes one match as a full sentence with a reason. Pairs check the match is sensible.

apply-1.objective: Use a price and nutrition chart to total a meal's cost and calories.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student computes the correct totals for a given meal.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a meal of four foods with a price and calorie chart. Each student totals the cost and the calories and records both. Pairs compare totals and find any math slip together.

apply-2.objective: Adjust a meal to lower its cost while keeping its nutrition.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student makes one swap that cuts cost and notes the nutrition kept.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students start with a set meal and its cost. They make one swap that lowers the cost while keeping the nutrition, then recompute the cost. They note what nutrition was preserved. Pairs check the swap held the nutrition.

apply-3.objective: Choose foods to build a meal under a set budget and calorie target.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student's meal meets both the budget and calorie target.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide sets a budget and a calorie target. Each student picks foods from a priced menu to build a meal that meets both. They show their totals. A partner checks both limits are met. Students adjust if a limit is missed.

apply-4.objective: Build a balanced meal that meets nutrition, calorie, and cost limits at once.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student's meal satisfies all three limits with totals shown.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student builds a meal that hits a nutrition checklist, stays under a calorie cap, and stays under a budget, showing all totals. A partner verifies each limit. Students swap a food to fix any limit that fails. They note the trade-off they made.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which food in a meal drives the cost up the most.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the costliest item and its price share.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a meal with prices. Each student finds the food that takes the biggest share of the cost and names it with its price. Pairs compare and confirm with the price list.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two meals to decide which gives better nutrition for the cost.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the better value meal and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students compare two meals with similar prices but different nutrition. They pick the better value and cite two reasons, such as more protein for the same cost. Pairs compare verdicts and debate any difference.

analyze-3.objective: Diagnose why a meal is over budget and find the swap that fixes it.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the cost driver and a swap that brings it under budget.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a meal that is over budget. In trios, students find the cost driver and propose a swap that keeps nutrition and brings the meal under budget. They recompute to confirm. Trios share one fix each.

analyze-4.objective: Break a meal into its four factors and rate it on each.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student rates the meal on all four factors with evidence.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students take a sample meal and rate it on nutrition, value, calories, and cost using a three-point scale, citing one piece of evidence per factor. They mark the weakest factor. Pairs compare ratings and check each has evidence.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a designed meal balances all four factors and defend the call.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores the meal on a rubric and cites two rubric points.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a peer-designed meal. Each student scores it on a rubric covering nutrition, value, calories, and cost, then marks a verdict. They cite two rubric points. Guide surfaces any split rating.

evaluate-4.objective: Recommend the best of three meals for a family on a budget and justify it.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student names the best meal and cites evidence across the four factors.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review three meals with prices and nutrition. They recommend the best for a family on a budget and justify it with evidence across the four factors. Pairs challenge the pick using the data, and students defend or revise.

create-1.objective: Assemble a balanced meal card from a posted food bank.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a card naming the foods and the factor each helps.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick foods from the bank to build a meal and write each food with the factor it helps, such as cost or nutrition, on a card. Guide checks the meal covers all four factors at a basic level.

create-2.objective: Build a healthy meal that meets a calorie target and stays under a budget.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student's meal hits the target and budget with totals shown.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a meal that meets a calorie target and a budget, showing the totals and the nutrition it provides. A partner verifies both limits and the nutrition. Students adjust one food to fix any miss. They note the trade-off.

create-3.objective: Design a week of one balanced dinner per day within a family budget.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student's week meets nutrition each day and a total weekly budget.
create-3.sampleTask: Each student plans seven dinners that each meet a nutrition checklist while the week stays under a total budget, with daily and weekly totals. A partner checks the weekly budget holds and no day skips a food group. Students swap to fix any overage.

create-4.objective: Design a budget meal guide for families with three recipes and a cost breakdown.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a one-page guide with three meals, costs, and the nutrition each gives.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student creates a one-page meal guide with three low-cost balanced meals, each with a cost breakdown and the nutrition it provides. A partner role-plays a parent and checks the costs and clarity. Students revise the meal that is unclear or over budget.

2.2.5.N.3.standardStatement: Develop a plan to attain a personal nutrition health goal that addresses strengths, needs, and culture.
2.2.5.N.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the three parts a nutrition goal plan must address: strengths, needs, and culture.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists all three parts on a recall card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the three plan parts with one blank. Each student lists all three from memory on a recall card. Guide reveals the answers and students self-check. Any miss is corrected.

understand-1.objective: Explain what makes a nutrition goal specific rather than vague.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student rewrites a vague goal as a specific one.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide shows a vague goal, such as 'eat better.' Each student rewrites it as a specific goal, such as 'eat a fruit at breakfast five days a week.' Pairs trade and check the rewrite is measurable.

understand-2.objective: Describe how a personal strength can support a nutrition goal.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student names one strength and how it helps a goal.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student names a personal strength, such as liking to cook, and writes how it would help a nutrition goal. Pairs compare and check the strength connects to a real goal action.

understand-3.objective: Predict a barrier that could block a nutrition goal and how it would show up.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names a likely barrier and when it would hit.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a sample goal. Each student predicts one barrier, such as no time for breakfast, and when in the week it would hit. Pairs compare and agree on the most likely barrier.

understand-4.objective: Relate a cultural food tradition to a healthy goal that honors it.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names a cultural food and a goal that keeps it.
understand-4.sampleTask: Each student names a food from their family or culture and writes a nutrition goal that keeps that food while staying healthy, such as adding vegetables to a family dish. Pairs check the goal honors the tradition.

apply-1.objective: Fill in a goal template with a specific, measurable nutrition goal.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student completes the template with a goal that names what, how much, and how often.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a goal template. Each student writes a specific goal naming the food, the amount, and the frequency. A partner checks all three blanks are filled clearly. Students sharpen any vague blank.

apply-2.objective: Adjust a draft goal to fit a personal strength and a real need.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student revises the goal to use a strength and address a need.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students take a draft goal and revise it so it uses a named strength and addresses a real need, such as more energy for sports. They mark the change. A partner checks both the strength and the need now appear. Students fix any that is missing.

apply-3.objective: Choose two actions that move a goal forward and fit the student's culture.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student lists two doable actions, at least one tied to their culture.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student picks two weekly actions that advance their goal, with at least one that fits a family or cultural food. They write each action so it is doable at home. A partner checks the actions are realistic and culturally fitting. Students swap any action that is not doable.

apply-4.objective: Build a one-week action plan that tracks progress toward the goal.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student's plan lists daily actions and a way to check progress.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student builds a seven-day plan with a daily action toward the goal and a simple tracker, such as a check box. They name how they will know the goal moved. A partner checks the plan is doable and trackable. Students fix any day that is unrealistic.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which part of a sample plan is missing: strength, need, or culture.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the missing part and points to the evidence.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample plan missing one part. Each student names the missing part and points to where it should appear. Pairs compare and confirm against the plan checklist.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two goals to decide which is more realistic for a fifth grader.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the more realistic goal and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students compare two goals, one steep and one steady. They pick the more realistic for a fifth grader and cite two reasons, such as the smaller change being easier to keep. Pairs compare verdicts.

analyze-3.objective: Diagnose why a sample plan stalled and name the cause.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the cause and a specific adjustment.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a plan that stalled after week one. In trios, students find the cause, such as a goal too big or a missed barrier, and name an adjustment. They write the cause and the fix. Trios share one each.

analyze-4.objective: Break a plan into its parts and check each against the student's life.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student maps each plan part to a real detail from the student's routine.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students take a sample student profile and map each plan part, strength, need, and culture, to a real detail from that student's routine. They flag any part that does not fit the life. Pairs compare maps and resolve differences.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a plan addresses strengths, needs, and culture and defend the call.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores the plan on a rubric and cites two rubric points.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a peer plan. Each student scores it on a rubric covering specific goal, strengths, needs, and culture, then marks a verdict. They cite two rubric points. Guide surfaces any split rating.

evaluate-4.objective: Recommend changes to make a peer's plan more achievable and justify each.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student proposes two changes with reasons tied to a plan part.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a peer's plan and recommend two changes that make it more achievable, each tied to a strength, need, or cultural fit, with a way to check progress. Pairs challenge any change that drops the cultural part, and students revise.

create-1.objective: Assemble a goal card naming the goal, one strength, and one cultural food.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a card with all three elements filled in.
create-1.sampleTask: Students fill a goal card with a specific goal, one strength that helps, and one cultural food it honors. Guide checks all three are present and specific. Students sharpen any vague element.

create-2.objective: Build a personal nutrition plan that ties the goal to strengths, needs, and culture.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student's plan shows all three parts feeding a single specific goal.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a plan with a specific goal and a short section for strengths, needs, and culture, each connected to a weekly action. A partner checks all three parts link to the goal. Students fix any part that floats free of the goal.

create-3.objective: Design a two-week plan with a tracker and a checkpoint to adjust the goal.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student's plan includes a tracker and a mid-point review with an adjustment rule.
create-3.sampleTask: Each student builds a two-week plan with daily actions, a tracker, and a checkpoint at day seven to review and adjust. They write a rule for what to change if progress stalls. A partner checks the checkpoint and rule are clear. Students sharpen any vague rule.

create-4.objective: Design a family nutrition plan that honors a shared cultural meal and a health goal.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a one-page plan with a goal, the cultural meal kept, and three family actions.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student creates a one-page plan for a family nutrition goal that keeps a shared cultural meal while improving health, with three actions the family can take together. A partner role-plays a family member and checks the actions are realistic and the meal is honored. Students revise the weak action.

2.2.5.PF.1.standardStatement: Identify the physical, social, emotional, and intellectual benefits of regular physical activity that effect personal health.
2.2.5.PF.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List four benefits of regular physical activity, one each for the body, friendships, feelings, and thinking.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one benefit under each of four labeled headers on a quarter-sheet.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide names the four benefit areas: physical, social, emotional, intellectual. Each student gets a quarter-sheet with the four headers and writes one benefit of moving the body under each, such as stronger heart, more friends, better mood, sharper focus. Students read their physical benefit aloud in a quick go-around. Guide adds any area a student left blank.

understand-1.objective: Match each benefit of physical activity to the correct one of the four areas it belongs to.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student draws a line from eight benefit cards to the correct body, social, emotional, or intellectual column.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a sorting sheet with four columns and eight benefit strips, such as builds stronger muscles, makes new teammates, lowers stress, and helps memory. Students place each strip in the column it fits. Pairs compare and fix any strip in the wrong column. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one type of activity produces a benefit in more than one of the four areas.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking one activity to at least two different benefit areas.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide picks one activity, such as a team relay race. Each student writes two sentences explaining how that one activity helps in two areas at once, such as building leg strength and making friends. Pairs trade papers and underline the two areas named. Guide reads two examples aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Generalize why a person who moves every week tends to feel and think better than one who rarely moves.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-sentence explanation that connects regular activity to two named benefits using the word because.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: why does moving most days help the whole person, not just the body? Each student writes three sentences using the word because to connect a weekly habit to at least two benefit areas. Students swap with a partner and check that a reason follows each claim. Guide selects two strong responses and reads them aloud.

understand-4.objective: Connect a physical activity benefit to a goal from another part of school life, such as schoolwork or class friendships.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence tying a fitness benefit to a non-PE goal they care about.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide explains that the benefits of moving reach beyond the gym. Each student names one goal from another part of life, such as staying focused in math or having more friends at lunch, and writes one sentence on how physical activity helps that goal. Students post their sentence on a shared wall chart under the matching area. Guide reads the chart aloud and names any area with few entries.

apply-1.objective: Use a benefit checklist to mark which of the four areas one named activity supports.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student checks at least two boxes on a benefit checklist for an assigned activity.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide assigns each student one activity from a list, such as jumping rope or a walking game. Students use a four-box checklist to mark every area that activity helps and add one word of proof in each checked box. Pairs with the same activity compare checks. Guide collects and names the activity that hit the most areas.

apply-2.objective: Choose two activities for a short routine so that all four benefit areas are covered.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student fills a two-row plan naming two activities and labeling which areas each covers so all four appear.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a planning sheet with space for two activities.
