Students pick two activities and, for each, mark the benefit areas it covers, choosing the pair so all four areas show up across the two. Students check their plan against the rule that every area must appear once. Guide reviews three plans and points out any area left uncovered.

apply-3.objective: Test whether a chosen activity raises heart rate by measuring pulse before and after one minute of movement.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student records two pulse counts and writes whether the activity raised the heart rate.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide teaches students to find and count their pulse for fifteen seconds. Students record a resting count, move briskly for one minute, then record a second count, writing both on a data card. Each student writes one sentence stating whether the activity raised the heart rate and by how much. Guide collects cards and names the activity with the biggest average jump.

apply-4.objective: Design a one-week move plan that picks activities to cover all four benefit areas across the week.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a seven-day grid naming a daily activity and tagging the benefit area each one serves.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide explains that a good week of moving touches the body, friendships, feelings, and thinking. Each student builds a seven-day grid, choosing one activity per day and tagging the area it serves so all four appear by week end. Students mark the two days they think will be hardest to keep and add a backup activity. Guide reviews plans and flags any week missing an area.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which benefit area is missing from a sample weekly activity plan.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student circles the missing area on a sample plan and names it on the line below.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample weekly plan that covers only three of the four benefit areas. Each student reviews the plan, circles the area that never appears, and writes its name on the line below. Pairs compare which area they found. Guide reveals the gap and asks two students to suggest an activity that would fill it.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two activities and explain which one covers more of the four benefit areas.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column comparison and writes one sentence naming the broader activity.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each student two activity cards, such as solo stretching and a team game. Students fill a two-column chart marking the areas each one covers, then write one sentence naming which activity reaches more areas and why. Pairs trade charts and check the counts. Guide tallies the class vote and explains any split decision.

analyze-3.objective: Examine a classmate's move plan and explain, with evidence, whether it gives a balanced set of benefits.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short note citing two specific entries from the plan to support a balanced or unbalanced verdict.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide pairs students and has them swap weekly move plans. Each student studies the partner's plan and writes a short note that names two specific days as evidence for whether the plan is balanced across the four areas. Partners read each other's notes and respond to one point. Guide reads one strong evidence-based note aloud.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how heredity, activity, and sleep each shape the benefits a person gets from moving.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student completes a three-part web showing one way each factor changes the benefit a person feels.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide draws a three-spoke web with heredity, activity level, and sleep at the spokes. Each student copies the web and writes one way each factor changes how much benefit a person feels from exercise, such as more sleep meaning a better mood after a game. Students compare webs in trios and add one idea they did not have. Guide collects webs and names a factor the class linked most often.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample activity plan is worth keeping and back the judgment with evidence from the four benefit areas.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a keep-or-change verdict supported by at least two named benefit areas.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample plan from an imagined student. Each student writes a verdict on whether the plan is worth keeping and supports it with at least two of the four benefit areas as evidence. Students trade verdicts and mark whether the evidence backs the verdict. Guide reads two opposing verdicts and asks the class which evidence was stronger.

evaluate-4.objective: Rank three activity plans by how well each covers all four benefit areas and defend the top choice.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a ranked list of three plans with a written defense of the first-place plan.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide gives each trio three sample weekly plans. Each student ranks the three by how fully they cover the four benefit areas and writes two sentences defending the top pick using evidence from the plans. Trios compare rankings and try to reach one shared order. Guide records each trio's top choice and names the most common winner.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm a list of activities that could fill each of the four benefit areas.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least one activity under each of four benefit headers.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide sets a two-minute timer per area. Students fill a four-box sheet, listing as many activities as they can that fit each benefit area, aiming for at least one per box. Pairs combine lists and circle the activity that fits more than one area. Guide builds a class master list on the board.

create-2.objective: Plan a balanced class fitness day that schedules activities to hit all four benefit areas.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a timed schedule naming activities and tagging the area each one serves.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that the class will plan one fitness day with four short blocks. Each student writes a timed schedule, naming an activity for each block and tagging the benefit area it serves so all four appear. Students check that no area is doubled before another is covered. Guide collects schedules and reads two that balanced the day well.

create-3.objective: Produce a one-page move plan for a younger student that covers all four benefit areas and explains each choice.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student creates a one-page plan with four activities, each labeled with its area and one reason.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide assigns each student to design a move plan for an imagined second grader. Each student writes a one-page plan naming four activities, one per benefit area, and adds one sentence on why each one fits a young child. Students swap plans and check that all four areas appear with reasons. Guide reads one plan aloud and names its clearest reason.

create-4.objective: Build a family move challenge that combines activities across all four benefit areas and tracks them for a week.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a family challenge card with a weekly tracker and at least one activity per benefit area.
create-4.sampleTask: Guide explains the class will design challenges families can run at home. Each student makes a challenge card naming activities that together cover all four benefit areas and adds a seven-day tracker for checking off each day. Students test their card by filling in two pretend days to confirm it works. Guide collects cards and names two ready to send home.

2.2.5.PF.2.standardStatement: Accept and respect others of all skill levels and abilities during participation.
2.2.5.PF.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List three respectful actions a player can show toward a teammate of any skill level.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes three respectful actions on a sticky note.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide names that respect during play means treating every skill level fairly. Each student writes three respectful actions, such as cheering a teammate, passing to everyone, and waiting your turn, on a sticky note. Students post notes on a shared chart. Guide reads several aloud and groups repeats.

understand-1.objective: Match each play situation to the respectful response it calls for.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student draws a line from six situations to the matching respectful response.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a worksheet with six play situations on the left, such as a teammate misses a catch, and six responses on the right. Students draw a line from each situation to the respectful response. Pairs compare and fix mismatches. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain why respecting all skill levels makes a game better for everyone.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking respect to a better game for the whole group.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: why does a team play better when everyone is respected? Each student writes two sentences explaining how respect changes the game for both strong and new players. Pairs trade and underline the clearest reason. Guide reads two aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Generalize how a single disrespectful act can spread and change a whole team's play.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-sentence chain showing how one unkind act leads to a worse game.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide explains that one action can set off a chain. Each student writes a three-sentence chain starting from one disrespectful act and showing how it changes how others play and feel. Students swap and check that each step follows the one before. Guide reads one chain aloud and names the turning point.

understand-4.objective: Connect respect during play to respect needed in another setting, such as group work in class.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence showing how a play respect rule also fits a classroom group task.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide explains that respect on the field and respect at a group table share rules. Each student picks one respect rule from play and writes one sentence on how the same rule helps a class group project. Students post sentences on a two-column chart, play and classroom. Guide reads the chart and names a rule that fit both.

apply-1.objective: Use a respect checklist to mark respectful moves during a short partner activity.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student checks at least three respect actions observed during one partner round.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide pairs students for a short toss-and-catch round and gives each a respect checklist. While one tosses, the other watches and checks the respect actions the partner shows, such as encouraging words and fair turns. Partners switch roles and repeat. Guide collects checklists and names the most common respectful action.

apply-2.objective: Choose the respectful response that fits a tricky play situation from several options.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student circles the best response on three scenario cards and writes why for one.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each student three scenario cards, each with a tricky moment and three possible responses. Students circle the most respectful response on each card and write one sentence explaining their pick on one card. Pairs compare choices and discuss any difference. Guide reveals strong choices and explains a tricky one.

apply-3.objective: Run a small-sided game using a team rule that includes every skill level, then report how it worked.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each group plays one round under an inclusion rule and writes whether everyone got involved.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives each group an inclusion rule, such as everyone must touch the ball before a goal. Groups play one short round under the rule, then each student writes whether the rule got every player involved and names one moment of respect. Groups share one finding with the class. Guide names the rule that spread play the widest.

apply-4.objective: Select inclusion rules from several options to build a game that fits players of mixed skill.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a rule set of two or three chosen rules and a one-line reason for each.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide offers a menu of inclusion rules. Each group selects two or three rules and writes one line on how each keeps mixed-skill players involved. Groups test the rule set in a two-minute trial and adjust one rule. Guide reviews rule sets and names one that balanced challenge and inclusion.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the disrespectful moment in a described play scene.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student underlines the disrespectful action in a short written scene.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a short written play scene that contains one disrespectful action. Each student reads it and underlines the moment where respect breaks down. Pairs compare underlines. Guide reveals the moment and asks one student to suggest a respectful redo.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two players' choices and explain which showed more respect for skill differences.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column note and names the more respectful player.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide describes two players handling the same mixed-skill moment differently. Each student fills a two-column note listing each player's actions, then writes one sentence naming who showed more respect and why. Pairs trade and check the reason. Guide tallies the class judgment and explains the difference.

analyze-3.objective: Examine a team's play and explain, with evidence, whether all skill levels were included.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a note citing two specific moments as evidence of inclusion or exclusion.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide has half the class play a short game while the other half observes with a watch sheet. Each observer writes a note naming two specific moments as evidence for whether every skill level was included. Observers share notes with the players they watched. Guide reads one evidence-based note aloud.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how rules, attitudes, and team setup each affect whether lower-skill players get included.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student completes a three-part web with one factor effect each.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide draws a three-spoke web labeled rules, attitudes, team setup. Each student copies it and writes one way each factor changes whether a newer player gets the ball, such as a rule requiring all passes. Trios compare and add one idea. Guide names the factor the class found strongest.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a team's inclusion rule worked and support the judgment with what was observed.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a worked-or-failed verdict backed by two observed details.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide replays an inclusion rule from an earlier round. Each student writes a verdict on whether the rule worked and supports it with two details observed during play. Students trade verdicts and mark whether the evidence fits. Guide reads two opposing verdicts and asks which evidence held up.

evaluate-4.objective: Rank three inclusion rules by how well each respects all skill levels and defend the top pick.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a ranked list of three rules with a written defense of first place.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide lists three inclusion rules tried during the unit. Each student ranks them by how well each respects every skill level and writes two sentences defending the top pick with evidence from play. Trios compare rankings and try for one shared order. Guide records top picks and names the class favorite.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm respectful actions that fit before, during, and after a game.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least one respectful action in each of three time blocks.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide sets a timer for each time block: before, during, after. Students list as many respectful actions as they can per block, aiming for at least one each. Pairs combine lists and circle one that fits all three blocks. Guide builds a class master list on the board.

create-2.objective: Plan a respect routine the class can run at the start of every game.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-step start routine naming a respectful action at each step.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide explains the class wants a short respect routine to open games. Each student writes a three-step routine, naming one respectful action per step, such as a team cheer, a fair-teams check, and a goal for everyone to touch the ball. Students test their routine by walking through it with a partner. Guide collects routines and reads two that flowed well.

create-3.objective: Produce an inclusion rule for a mixed-skill game and explain how it keeps everyone involved.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes one new rule with two sentences on how it includes all skill levels.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide assigns each student to invent one new inclusion rule for a class game. Each student writes the rule and two sentences on how it keeps strong and new players both involved. Students swap rules and predict whether each would work. Guide picks two rules for the class to test next session.

create-4.objective: Build a respect agreement poster the whole class can sign and follow during games.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a poster with at least four agreed respect rules and space for signatures.
create-4.sampleTask: Guide explains groups will draft a class respect agreement. Each group writes at least four respect rules that cover all skill levels and leaves a signature line at the bottom. Groups trade posters and suggest one rule to strengthen. Guide collects posters and the class votes on which rules to adopt.

2.2.5.PF.3.standardStatement: Participate in moderate to vigorous age-appropriate physical fitness activities and build the skills that address each component of health-related fitness (e.g., endurance, strength, speed, agility, flexibility, balance).
2.2.5.PF.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the six components of health-related fitness from the standard.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists endurance, strength, speed, agility, flexibility, and balance on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide reads the six fitness components aloud once. Each student writes all six on an index card from memory, then checks against the posted list and fixes any miss. Students quiz a partner by naming three and having the partner supply the rest. Guide names the component most often forgotten.

understand-1.objective: Match each fitness component to an activity that builds it.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student draws a line from six components to a matching activity.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a worksheet with the six components on the left and six activities on the right, such as stretching for flexibility. Students draw a line from each component to a matching activity. Pairs compare and fix mismatches. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one activity can build two fitness components at once.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences naming an activity and the two components it builds.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide names an activity such as an obstacle course. Each student writes two sentences explaining which two components the activity builds, such as agility and speed, and how. Pairs trade and underline the two components named. Guide reads two aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Generalize why a fitness plan that trains all six components beats one that trains only one.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-sentence explanation using the word because.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: why train all six components instead of just running every day? Each student writes three sentences using because to explain why a mix beats a single focus. Students swap and check that a reason follows each claim. Guide reads two strong responses aloud.

understand-4.objective: Connect a fitness component to a real task outside the gym that depends on it.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence linking a component to a daily task that needs it.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide explains the components show up in daily life. Each student picks one component and writes one sentence naming a real task that needs it, such as balance for carrying a full tray. Students post sentences on a wall chart by component. Guide reads the chart and names a component used most in daily life.

apply-1.objective: Perform a station exercise correctly using the posted form cues.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student completes one station and a partner checks two form cues were met.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide sets one station with posted form cues, such as a plank with a flat back and tight middle. Each student performs the station while a partner uses the cue card to check two cues. Partners switch roles. Guide circulates and corrects one common form error.

apply-2.objective: Move through a circuit, selecting the correct effort level at each of several component stations.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student records a count or time at each station on a circuit card.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide sets a circuit with one station per component and a thirty-second work window each. Each student rotates through, performing each station and recording a count or hold time on a circuit card. Students mark the station that felt hardest. Guide collects cards and names the station with the widest range of results.

apply-3.objective: Test which of two activities better builds a chosen component by measuring before and after.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each pair records a baseline and a result and writes which activity built the component more.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide assigns each pair one component, such as flexibility, and two activities to compare. Pairs measure a baseline, do one activity, measure again, then repeat with the second activity, recording all four numbers. Each pair writes one sentence naming which activity built the component more. Guide collects results and names the class winner per component.

apply-4.objective: Design a circuit that trains all six components and assigns a sensible work time to each.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a six-station plan naming an activity and a work time per component.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide explains a balanced circuit hits every component. Each student plans six stations, one per component, naming an activity and a work time for each. Students check that no component is left out and total time fits the class period. Guide reviews plans and flags any missing component.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which fitness component a described exercise mainly builds.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student labels each of five exercises with its main component.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sheet with five described exercises. Each student writes the main component each one builds beside it. Pairs compare labels and discuss any split. Guide reveals the answers and explains one tricky case.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two circuits and explain which one covers more of the six components.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a coverage chart and names the broader circuit.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each student two sample circuits. Students fill a chart marking which components each circuit covers, then write one sentence naming the broader circuit and the gap in the other. Pairs trade and check the counts. Guide tallies the class judgment.

analyze-3.objective: Examine a classmate's circuit and explain, with evidence, whether it is balanced across components.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a note citing two stations as evidence for a balanced or unbalanced verdict.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide pairs students to swap circuit plans. Each student studies the partner's plan and writes a note naming two stations as evidence for whether the circuit is balanced across the six components. Partners read each other's notes and respond to one point. Guide reads one strong note aloud.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how rest, effort, and form each affect the results a person gets from a circuit.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student completes a three-part web with one effect per factor.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide draws a three-spoke web labeled rest, effort, form. Each student copies it and writes one way each factor changes circuit results, such as poor form reducing strength gains. Trios compare and add one idea. Guide names the factor the class linked most often.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample circuit is ready to run and support the verdict with component evidence.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a ready-or-fix verdict backed by two named components.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample circuit with one weak spot. Each student writes a verdict on whether it is ready to run and supports it with evidence about which components are covered or missing. Students trade verdicts and check the evidence. Guide reads two opposing verdicts and names the stronger case.

evaluate-4.objective: Rank three circuits by balance across the six components and defend the top choice.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a ranked list of three circuits with a written defense of first place.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide gives each trio three sample circuits. Each student ranks them by how fully each covers the six components and writes two sentences defending the top pick with evidence. Trios compare and try for one shared order. Guide records top picks and names the class favorite.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm activities that build each of the six fitness components.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least one activity under each of the six components.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide sets a short timer per component. Students fill a six-box sheet, listing activities that build each component, aiming for at least one per box. Pairs combine lists and circle one activity that builds two components. Guide builds a class master list on the board.

create-2.objective: Plan a class warm-up that touches at least three fitness components.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a warm-up sequence naming three moves and the component each builds.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide explains a good warm-up wakes up several components. Each student writes a three-move warm-up sequence, tagging the component each move builds. Students test the sequence by walking through it with a partner. Guide collects sequences and reads two that flowed well.

create-3.objective: Produce a personal circuit that targets the two components the student most wants to build.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student creates a circuit naming four stations and marking which two target components.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide has each student pick the two components they most want to build. Each student designs a four-station circuit and marks which stations target the two chosen components and explains each choice in one line. Students swap and check the two targets show up. Guide reads one circuit aloud and names its clearest target.

create-4.objective: Build a six-station team challenge that trains every component and tracks results for the class.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a station challenge with a tracker covering all six components.
create-4.sampleTask: Guide explains groups will build a team challenge for the whole class. Each group designs six stations, one per component, and a tracker for recording each student's result. Groups test two stations to confirm they work. Guide collects challenges and names two ready to run next session.

2.2.5.PF.4.standardStatement: Develop a short term and/or a long-term health-related fitness goal (e.g., cardiorespiratory endurance 'heart & lungs', muscular strength, muscular endurance, flexibility, body composition, nutrition) to evaluate personal health.
2.2.5.PF.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the parts of a fitness goal: what, how much, and by when.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the three parts of a fitness goal on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide states that a strong fitness goal names what, how much, and by when. Each student writes the three parts on a card and adds an example for each, such as push-ups, ten, by Friday. Students check a partner's card has all three. Guide names the part most often left off.

understand-1.objective: Match each sample goal to the fitness area it targets.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student draws a line from six goals to the matching fitness area.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a worksheet with six sample goals on the left and six areas on the right, such as cardiorespiratory endurance. Students draw a line from each goal to its area. Pairs compare and fix mismatches. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a fitness goal needs a deadline to be useful.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences on how a by-when date changes a goal.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide shows two goals, one with a date and one without. Each student writes two sentences explaining why the dated goal is easier to act on. Pairs trade and underline the clearest reason. Guide reads two aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Generalize why a measurable goal helps a person know whether they improved.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-sentence explanation using a number example.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: why must a fitness goal use a number? Each student writes three sentences explaining how a number lets a person check progress, using one example. Students swap and check the example fits the claim. Guide reads two strong responses aloud.

understand-4.objective: Connect goal setting in fitness to goal setting in another area, such as reading.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence showing how the same goal parts fit a non-fitness goal.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide explains the what, how much, by when frame works beyond fitness. Each student writes one sentence applying the frame to a goal in another area, such as books read by month end. Students post sentences on a two-column chart. Guide reads the chart and names a shared rule.

apply-1.objective: Use the goal frame to fill in a blank short-term fitness goal.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a goal template with what, how much, and by when filled in.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a goal template with three blanks. Students fill what, how much, and by when for a short-term fitness goal they choose. Pairs check each other's template has all three parts. Guide collects templates and reads two complete examples.

apply-2.objective: Select a baseline test and use the result to set a realistic goal number.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student records a baseline and writes a goal number based on it.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide leads a quick baseline test, such as a one-minute count of an exercise. Each student records the baseline, then sets a goal number that is a sensible step up and writes a by-when date. Pairs compare whether each goal is a reasonable step. Guide names the most common baseline and a fair next step.

apply-3.objective: Track progress toward a goal over a short window and adjust the goal if needed.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student logs at least three data points and writes whether the goal needs adjusting.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a three-day mini tracker for one goal. Students log a count each session, then write one sentence on whether the goal is on track or needs a new number. Pairs review each other's logs for clear data. Guide names a goal that needed adjusting and why.

apply-4.objective: Design a four-week goal plan with a baseline, a target, and weekly checkpoints.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a plan naming a baseline, a target number, and four weekly checks.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide explains a longer goal needs checkpoints. Each student builds a four-week plan with a baseline, a target number, a by-when date, and a check for each week. Students mark the week they expect to struggle and add a backup step. Guide reviews plans and flags any missing checkpoint.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the missing part in an incomplete fitness goal.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student circles and names the missing part on five sample goals.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out five sample goals, each missing one part. Each student circles the gap and writes which part is missing: what, how much, or by when. Pairs compare. Guide reveals the gaps and asks a student to repair one goal.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two goals and explain which one is easier to measure.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column note and names the more measurable goal.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide shows two goals, one vague and one specific. Each student fills a two-column note listing what each goal does and does not let you measure, then names the stronger goal. Pairs trade and check the reason. Guide tallies the class judgment.

analyze-3.objective: Examine a classmate's goal plan and explain, with evidence, whether the target is realistic.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a note citing the baseline and target as evidence for the verdict.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide pairs students to swap goal plans. Each student studies the partner's baseline and target and writes a note on whether the jump is realistic, naming both numbers as evidence. Partners read the notes and respond to one point. Guide reads one evidence-based note aloud.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how effort, rest, and consistency each affect whether a goal is met.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student completes a three-part web with one effect per factor.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide draws a three-spoke web labeled effort, rest, consistency. Each student copies it and writes one way each factor changes whether a goal is reached. Trios compare and add one idea. Guide names the factor the class found strongest.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample goal is worth keeping and support the verdict with goal-frame evidence.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a keep-or-fix verdict backed by two parts of the goal frame.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample goal with one weak part. Each student writes a verdict on whether it is worth keeping and supports it with evidence from the goal frame parts. Students trade verdicts and check the evidence. Guide reads two opposing verdicts and names the stronger case.

evaluate-4.objective: Rank three goals by how clear and realistic each is and defend the top choice.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a ranked list of three goals with a written defense of first place.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide gives each trio three sample goals. Each student ranks them by clarity and realism and writes two sentences defending the top pick with evidence. Trios compare and try for one shared order. Guide records top picks and names the class favorite.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm possible fitness goals across several fitness areas.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least one possible goal in three different fitness areas.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide sets a short timer per area. Students list possible goals across areas such as endurance, strength, and flexibility, aiming for at least one per area. Pairs combine lists and circle the most doable. Guide builds a class master list on the board.

create-2.objective: Plan a personal short-term goal with a baseline, a target, and a check date.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a complete goal naming all three frame parts plus a baseline.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide explains each student will set one real short-term goal. Each student writes the goal with what, how much, by when, and the baseline they will start from. Students check a partner's goal is complete and realistic. Guide collects goals and reads two strong examples.

create-3.objective: Produce a goal tracker that shows checkpoints and space to record real results.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student creates a tracker with dated checkpoints and a result row.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide has each student build a tracker for their goal. Each student makes a tracker with dated checkpoints and a row to record each result, plus a spot to mark on-track or behind. Students swap and check the tracker is easy to use. Guide reads one tracker aloud and names a clear feature.

create-4.objective: Build a goal-setting guide a younger student could use to set and track a fitness goal.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a one-page guide with the goal frame, an example, and a blank tracker.
create-4.sampleTask: Guide explains groups will write a goal guide for younger students. Each group makes a one-page guide showing the goal frame, one worked example, and a blank tracker to copy. Groups trade guides and test whether they could follow it. Guide collects guides and names two ready to share.

2.2.5.PF.5.standardStatement: Determine how different factors influence personal fitness and other healthy lifestyle choices (e.g., heredity, physical activity, nutrition, sleep, technology).
2.2.5.PF.5.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List the five factors that influence personal fitness named in the standard.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes heredity, physical activity, nutrition, sleep, and technology on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide reads the five factors once. Each student writes all five on an index card from memory, then checks against the posted list and fixes any miss. Students quiz a partner on two and have them supply the rest. Guide names the factor most often forgotten.

understand-1.objective: Match each factor to an example of how it shows up in daily life.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student draws a line from five factors to a matching daily example.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a worksheet with the five factors on the left and five daily examples on the right, such as screen time for technology. Students draw a line from each factor to its example. Pairs compare and fix mismatches. Guide reveals the key and students self-correct.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one factor, such as sleep, can change how a person feels and moves the next day.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking one factor to a next-day effect.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide names one factor, such as sleep. Each student writes two sentences explaining how that factor changes energy or movement the next day. Pairs trade and underline the cause and the effect. Guide reads two aloud without names.

understand-3.objective: Generalize why a person cannot control every fitness factor equally.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-sentence explanation sorting factors into ones you can and cannot change.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: which factors can a person change and which are set? Each student writes three sentences explaining why heredity differs from sleep or nutrition in how much control a person has. Students swap and check the sort is correct. Guide reads two strong responses aloud.

understand-4.objective: Connect a fitness factor to how it shows up in another part of a person's day, such as school focus.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence linking a factor to a non-fitness outcome.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide explains these factors reach beyond fitness. Each student picks one factor and writes one sentence on how it also affects something else, such as sleep affecting focus in class. Students post sentences on a wall chart by factor. Guide reads the chart and names a factor with wide reach.

apply-1.objective: Use a daily checklist to record one habit for each of the five factors for one day.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student fills a five-row checklist with one real habit per factor.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives each student a five-row checklist, one row per factor. Students record one real habit from the past day in each row, such as hours slept. Pairs compare which factor was easiest to fill. Guide collects checklists and names the factor with the most variety.

apply-2.objective: Choose one factor to improve and select a fitting action from several options.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student names a target factor and circles one matching action with a reason.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide offers an action menu for each factor. Each student picks one factor to improve, circles one fitting action from the menu, and writes one sentence on why it fits. Pairs compare choices. Guide reads two strong factor-action pairs.

apply-3.objective: Test how changing one factor for several days affects a simple fitness measure.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student logs the changed factor and a fitness measure across at least three days.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide has each student pick one factor to adjust, such as adding ten minutes of activity. Each student logs the change and a simple measure, such as energy rating, across three days. Each student writes one sentence on what they noticed. Guide collects logs and names a pattern several students saw.

apply-4.objective: Design a weekly plan that improves two factors and tracks both.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a seven-day plan naming actions for two factors and a tracker for each.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide explains a good plan can target two factors at once. Each student builds a seven-day plan with daily actions for two chosen factors and a tracker for each. Students mark the day they expect to struggle and add a backup. Guide reviews plans and flags any factor without a tracker.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which factor is shaping the outcome in a short scenario.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student labels each of five scenarios with the factor at work.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out five short scenarios, each driven by one factor. Each student writes the factor at work beside each scenario. Pairs compare labels. Guide reveals the answers and explains one tricky case.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two daily routines and explain which one better supports fitness across the factors.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a factor chart for both routines and names the stronger one.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each student two sample daily routines. Students fill a chart rating each factor in both routines, then write one sentence naming the stronger routine and its weakest factor. Pairs trade and check the ratings. Guide tallies the class judgment.

analyze-3.objective: Examine a sample week and explain, with evidence, which factor is holding fitness back.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a note citing two days as evidence for the weak factor.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample week log. Each student studies it and writes a note naming the factor holding fitness back, citing two days as evidence. Pairs read each other's notes and respond to one point. Guide reads one evidence-based note aloud.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how technology can both help and hurt fitness across the other factors.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart with at least two helps and two hurts.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide draws a two-column chart, helps and hurts, for technology. Each student fills at least two of each, showing how screens or apps touch sleep, activity, or nutrition. Trios compare and add one idea. Guide names the most common help and hurt.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a sample routine sets a person up for good fitness and support it with factor evidence.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a strong-or-weak verdict backed by two named factors.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample daily routine. Each student writes a verdict on whether it supports fitness and backs it with evidence from two factors. Students trade verdicts and check the evidence. Guide reads two opposing verdicts and names the stronger case.

evaluate-4.objective: Rank three routines by how well each balances the five factors and defend the top choice.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a ranked list of three routines with a written defense of first place.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide gives each trio three sample routines. Each student ranks them by how well each balances the five factors and writes two sentences defending the top pick with evidence. Trios compare and try for one shared order. Guide records top picks and names the class favorite.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm one healthy action for each of the five factors.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least one action under each of the five factors.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide sets a short timer per factor. Students fill a five-box sheet with healthy actions for each factor, aiming for at least one per box. Pairs combine lists and circle the easiest to start today. Guide builds a class master list on the board.

create-2.objective: Plan a one-day routine that includes a healthy choice for each of the five factors.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-day plan with a choice tagged to each factor.
create-2.sampleTask: Guide explains a balanced day touches every factor. Each student writes a one-day routine with a choice for each of the five factors tagged in the margin. Students check all five appear. Guide collects plans and reads two that balanced the day well.

create-3.objective: Produce a personal habit-change plan targeting the one factor the student most wants to improve.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student creates a plan naming the target factor, a daily action, and a tracker.
create-3.sampleTask: Guide has each student pick the one factor they most want to improve. Each student writes a plan naming a daily action and a simple tracker for the week. Students swap and predict whether the plan would work. Guide reads one plan aloud and names its clearest action.

create-4.objective: Build a family habits poster that gives one practical tip for each of the five factors.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group produces a poster with a clear tip under each of the five factors.
create-4.sampleTask: Guide explains groups will design posters families can hang at home. Each group writes one practical tip for each factor, kept short enough to follow. Groups trade posters and suggest one tip to sharpen. Guide collects posters and names two ready to send home.

2.2.8.LF.1.standardStatement: Develop and build an effective movement and physical fitness vocabulary for self, peers, and family members that can enhance wellness.
2.2.8.LF.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name ten movement and fitness terms covered in this unit, with the muscle group or activity each one names.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student fills a ten-row term sheet and reads three rows aloud.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts ten fitness terms on the board (aerobic, anaerobic, flexibility, endurance, repetition, set, range of motion, heart rate, warm-up, cooldown). Each student writes each term and one short phrase naming what it points to. Students check three rows against a partner's sheet. Guide collects the sheets as the unit baseline.

understand-1.objective: Match each fitness term to a plain-language definition a younger sibling would understand.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a term-to-definition matching grid with no more than one error.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a grid with ten terms in one column and ten scrambled plain-language definitions in the other. Students draw lines to pair them. Pairs trade grids and mark mismatches. Guide reviews the two terms most students missed and rewrites those definitions on the board.

understand-2.objective: Explain in writing why a fitness term means more than its everyday use, using endurance and flexibility as the two examples.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences that separate the gym meaning from the everyday meaning of the term.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students pick endurance and flexibility. For each, they write one sentence using the everyday meaning and one sentence using the fitness meaning. Pairs read both sentences aloud and decide whether the fitness meaning is clear. Guide collects two strong examples and reads them to the class.

understand-3.objective: Group the unit's terms into categories that show how the vocabulary is organized.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a three-column sort with every term placed and a heading on each column.
understand-3.sampleTask: Each pair gets the ten terms on cut cards. They sort the cards into three groups and write a heading for each group, such as kinds of fitness, parts of a workout, and ways to measure effort. Pairs explain one placement choice to another pair. Guide photographs two sorts that used different headings.

understand-4.objective: Summarize how a shared fitness vocabulary helps a family talk about wellness, using examples from the unit terms.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph naming at least four unit terms in context.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph describing a family deciding how to get more active, using at least four unit terms correctly. The paragraph must show one moment where the right word prevents confusion. Pairs underline each term used and confirm it fits. Guide collects paragraphs and flags any term used loosely.

apply-1.objective: Use the correct fitness term to label each station during a circuit walkthrough.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places the right term card at each of six stations.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide sets up six labeled stations in the gym. Students walk the circuit and place a term card at each station naming the fitness component it trains. Guide reveals the answer key one station at a time. Students fix any misplaced card and note which station they missed.

apply-2.objective: Apply the unit vocabulary to describe a personal workout in writing using the terms in sequence.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a workout description that uses warm-up, repetition, set, and cooldown in order.
apply-2.sampleTask: Each student describes a fifteen-minute workout they could do at home, written as numbered steps. The description must use warm-up, repetition, set, and cooldown in the right order. Pairs read each other's steps and check the sequence. Guide collects two that sequenced the terms cleanly.

apply-3.objective: Teach two unit terms to a peer who has not learned them, using a demonstration.
apply-3.observableCheck: The partner repeats the term and performs the movement it names without prompting.
apply-3.sampleTask: Pairs split into teacher and learner. The teacher explains and demonstrates two assigned terms, then the learner performs each movement and says the term. They switch roles with two new terms. Guide circulates and notes one pair whose demonstration made the term clear.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled family wellness glossary that defines eight terms for use at home.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page glossary with eight terms, definitions, and one home example each.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student selects eight unit terms and builds a one-page glossary for their family. Each entry gives the term, a plain definition, and one example of using it at home. Students design it to be read by a family member who knows no fitness terms. Guide reviews three glossaries for clarity and accuracy.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which terms in a sample fitness flyer are used correctly and which are misused.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks each flagged term on the flyer as correct or misused.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a one-page fitness flyer with eight underlined terms, three of them misused. Students mark each underlined term correct or misused and write the right word for any misuse. Pairs compare marks. Guide reveals the three planted errors and the corrections.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two students' workout descriptions to find where vocabulary use diverges.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair lists three differences in how the two descriptions used the same terms.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs receive two anonymized workout descriptions of the same activity. They list three places where the writers used a fitness term differently or where one was more precise. Pairs decide which description a beginner would follow more easily. Guide collects the lists and names the most common divergence.

analyze-3.objective: Break a confusing fitness instruction into the terms it should have used to be clear.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student rewrites a vague instruction using at least three precise unit terms.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide posts three vague instructions, such as do it a bunch of times then take it easy. Each student rewrites one using precise terms like repetition, set, and cooldown. Pairs check whether the rewrite removes the guesswork. Guide reads two rewrites and asks the class which terms did the work.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how vocabulary choice changes when explaining fitness to a peer versus a younger family member.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a two-column chart contrasting word choices for the two audiences.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students pick one fitness idea, such as building endurance. They write how they would explain it to a classmate and how they would explain it to a seven-year-old sibling, in two columns. They mark which terms they kept, swapped, or dropped and why. Guide collects charts and names one swap that improved clarity.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a peer's family glossary is accurate and usable, citing specific entries.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a verdict on a peer glossary with two cited entries supporting it.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students swap the glossaries from Apply-4. Using a three-point rubric for accuracy, clarity, and usefulness at home, each student scores a peer's glossary and writes a verdict naming two specific entries that earned or lost points. The reviewer suggests one fix. Guide spot-checks three reviews against the rubric.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which five terms a family most needs to know first, ranking them with reasons.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student presents a ranked list of five terms with a one-sentence reason for the top choice.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Each student ranks five unit terms by how useful they are for a family starting to get active, most useful first. They write one sentence defending the top term and one defending the bottom. Small groups compare rankings and try to reach a shared top three. Guide records where groups disagreed and why.

create-1.objective: Produce a set of flash cards covering ten unit terms for home study.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in ten cards with the term on one side and a definition plus example on the other.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student makes ten flash cards, term on the front, plain definition and one example on the back. Cards must be readable by a family member. Students quiz a partner using the finished set. Guide collects sets and checks that definitions match the unit meanings.

create-2.objective: Design a short family fitness challenge that uses correct vocabulary in its instructions.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student submits a challenge card with steps written in at least four unit terms.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a one-week home fitness challenge for a family, written on a single card. The instructions must use at least four unit terms correctly so a family member could follow them. Pairs test whether the steps are clear enough to do without asking questions. Guide collects two challenge cards that read cleanly.

create-3.objective: Create an illustrated wall poster that teaches the unit vocabulary to the whole class.
create-3.observableCheck: Each small group produces a poster with five terms, each shown with a drawing and a definition.
create-3.sampleTask: Small groups build a poster teaching five unit terms, each with a drawing of the movement and a short definition. The poster must work for a student who missed the unit. Groups present the poster and answer one question from another group. Guide hangs the posters and has the class vote on the clearest term illustration.

create-4.objective: Develop a peer-teaching plan that uses the vocabulary to lead a family member through one workout.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a step-by-step teaching plan naming the term taught at each step.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student writes a plan to teach a family member one short workout, marking which term they introduce at each step and how they check the person understood it. The plan must move from naming a term to using it in the movement. Pairs rehearse one step of the plan aloud. Guide reviews three plans for whether the vocabulary builds in a sensible order.

2.2.8.LF.2.standardStatement: Explain the importance of assuming responsibility for personal health behaviors through physical activity throughout one’s lifetime.
2.2.8.LF.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List five personal health behaviors tied to physical activity that a person controls day to day.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five behaviors on an index card and reads two aloud.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide asks: name behaviors a person decides on themselves that involve movement, such as walking instead of riding, stretching before bed, or joining a weekend game. Each student writes five on an index card. Cards go in a pile and Guide reads several aloud. The class builds a shared list on the board.

understand-1.objective: Explain what assuming responsibility for a health behavior means in plain words.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence definition and gives one example.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students write one sentence defining what it means to take responsibility for a health behavior, then give one example from their own week. Pairs read both and decide if the example fits the definition. Guide collects three clear pairings and reads them to the class. The class agrees on a shared definition to post.

understand-2.objective: Describe why physical activity habits matter across a whole lifetime, not just during school years.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two reasons a habit started now could matter at age sixty.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: why would a habit you build at thirteen still matter at sixty? Each student writes two reasons. Pairs combine into a stronger pair of reasons. Guide charts the reasons by category, such as health, money, and independence, and names which category came up most.

understand-3.objective: Explain how one daily choice connects to a long-term health outcome using a cause-and-effect chain.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student draws a three-link chain from a daily choice to a long-term outcome.
understand-3.sampleTask: Each student picks one daily choice, such as walking to school, and draws a three-box chain showing how it could lead to a long-term outcome. Each arrow needs a short label naming the link. Pairs check that each arrow actually follows from the box before it. Guide collects two chains with the clearest links.

understand-4.objective: Summarize the case for personal responsibility in health behavior in a short paragraph.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph that states the claim and supports it with two reasons.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph answering why each person should take charge of their own activity habits. The paragraph must state a claim and back it with two reasons from class discussion. Pairs underline the claim and the two reasons in each other's writing. Guide flags any paragraph missing a clear claim.

apply-1.objective: Apply the idea of responsibility by logging personal activity choices for one day.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student fills a one-day log with at least four activity choices and who decided each.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a one-day activity log. Students record at least four moments where movement was a choice and mark whether they decided or someone else did. The next day, students bring the log and count their own decisions. Guide tallies how many decisions the class made for themselves.

apply-2.objective: Use a goal-setting template to turn one responsibility into a concrete weekly plan.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a weekly plan with a specific action, days, and a way to check it.
apply-2.sampleTask: Each student picks one activity behavior to own and fills a goal template with the action, the days they will do it, and how they will know they did it. The plan must be specific enough that a parent could check it. Pairs test each other's plan for the check step. Guide collects three plans with measurable checks.

apply-3.objective: Explain to a peer how taking responsibility for one habit would change their week.
apply-3.observableCheck: The listening partner restates the plan and names the one change it requires.
apply-3.sampleTask: Pairs take turns explaining the weekly plan from Apply-2. The listener restates the plan and names the single biggest change it requires in the speaker's week. They switch roles. Guide circulates and notes one pair whose restatement was accurate.

apply-4.objective: Build a one-page commitment card a student could post at home to track a chosen habit.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student turns in a card with the habit, daily checkboxes, and a reason to keep going.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student designs a commitment card for one activity habit, with daily checkboxes for a week and a written reason that would keep them going on a hard day. The card must be something they would actually post. Pairs swap cards and check the reason is personal, not generic. Guide reviews three cards for usability.

analyze-1.objective: Distinguish, in a set of statements, which describe taking responsibility and which describe blaming circumstances.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student sorts eight statements into two labeled columns.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out eight statements about activity, such as I did not have time and I moved my workout to the morning. Students sort them into takes responsibility and blames circumstances. Pairs compare sorts and argue any disputed card. Guide reveals the intended sort and discusses the two trickiest statements.

analyze-2.objective: Compare a responsible weekly plan with a vague intention to find what makes one workable.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair lists three features the workable plan has that the vague one lacks.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs get two sample plans for the same goal, one specific and one vague. They list three features the workable plan has that the vague one lacks, such as named days or a check step. Pairs decide which plan would survive a busy week. Guide collects the lists and names the feature cited most.

analyze-3.objective: Trace which barrier most often breaks a student's activity plan and where in the week it hits.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student marks on a week grid where their plan is most likely to break and names the barrier.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students map their weekly plan onto a seven-day grid and mark the day and time the plan is most likely to fail. They name the barrier, such as homework load or no equipment. Pairs suggest one adjustment for the weak spot. Guide charts the most common barrier across the class.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how personal responsibility and outside support each contribute to keeping an activity habit.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-part chart separating what the person controls from what support provides.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students fill a chart with two parts, what the person controls and what support from family or coaches provides, for keeping one activity habit. They mark which part carries more weight for them. Pairs compare and note where support could cover a personal weak spot. Guide names one example where support and responsibility worked together.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a peer's weekly plan is realistic and owned by the student, citing parts of the plan.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a verdict on a peer plan with two cited parts supporting it.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students swap the plans from Apply-2 and score them on a rubric for specific, realistic, and self-owned. Each reviewer writes a verdict and cites two parts of the plan that earned or lost points. The reviewer names one change that would raise the weakest score. Guide checks three reviews against the rubric.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend a position on whether schools or individuals carry more of the duty for lifelong activity.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student states a position and backs it with two reasons in a short written stance.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: who carries more duty for lifelong activity, schools or individuals? Each student writes a stance with a clear position and two reasons. Students form two sides and trade their strongest reason. Guide records the reason from each side that the other side found hardest to answer.

create-1.objective: Produce a personal pledge listing three activity behaviors the student will own this term.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a signed pledge naming three specific behaviors.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student writes a short pledge naming three activity behaviors they will take responsibility for this term and signs it. Each behavior must be specific enough to check. Pairs read each other's pledge and confirm the behaviors are not vague. Guide files the pledges to revisit later in the term.

create-2.objective: Design a habit tracker that a peer could use for a month to hold themselves accountable.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student submits a tracker with daily marks, a weekly check, and a restart rule.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a one-month habit tracker for an activity behavior, including daily marks, a weekly check, and a rule for restarting after a missed day. The restart rule is the part many trackers skip. Pairs test whether the restart rule is clear. Guide collects two trackers that handle a missed day well.

create-3.objective: Create a short public service announcement script arguing for owning your activity habits for life.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a thirty-second script with a claim and one memorable line.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs write a thirty-second public service announcement script that argues for taking charge of your own activity for life. The script needs a clear claim and one line a listener would remember. Pairs read the script aloud to time it. Guide selects two scripts to be read to the class and notes the strongest lines.

create-4.objective: Develop a lifelong activity roadmap that maps habits a person could keep at three life stages.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a roadmap naming a realistic activity for teen, adult, and older-adult stages.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student builds a roadmap with three life stages and one realistic activity for each, with a note on what makes it doable at that stage. The roadmap must show how a habit could carry forward or change shape over time. Pairs check that each stage's activity fits that stage. Guide reviews three roadmaps for realism.

2.2.8.LF.3.standardStatement: Explore by leading self and others to experience and participate in different cultures' physical fitness activities.
2.2.8.LF.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name six physical activities and the cultures or regions each one comes from.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student fills a six-row sheet pairing an activity with its origin.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide shows short clips or photos of six activities from different cultures, such as capoeira, sepak takraw, and tinikling. Students write each activity and the culture or region it comes from on a six-row sheet. Pairs check three rows against each other. Guide posts the answer key and corrects any mismatches.

understand-1.objective: Describe the basic movements of one cultural activity in your own words.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-step description a classmate could try from.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student picks one activity from Remember-1 and writes a three-step description of its basic movement. The steps must be clear enough for a classmate to attempt. Pairs read each other's steps and try the first step. Guide collects two descriptions that a partner could follow.

understand-2.objective: Explain what one cultural activity reflects about where it comes from.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence linking a feature of the activity to its culture or setting.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students pick one activity and write one sentence connecting a feature of it to its origin, such as how tinikling uses bamboo poles common in the Philippines. Pairs read both sentences and check the link is real, not guessed. Guide reads two strong links and notes where students avoided stereotypes.

understand-3.objective: Group several cultural activities by a shared movement type they all use.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each pair sorts six activities into movement-type groups with a heading on each.
understand-3.sampleTask: Pairs receive cards for the six activities and sort them by movement type, such as balance, footwork speed, or whole-body rhythm. Each group gets a heading. Pairs explain one placement to another pair. Guide photographs two sorts that used different groupings and discusses the overlap.

understand-4.objective: Summarize why trying activities from other cultures broadens a person's fitness experience.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph with two reasons drawn from the unit.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph answering why a person benefits from trying activities outside their own culture. The paragraph needs two reasons grounded in the unit, not general praise. Pairs underline the two reasons. Guide flags any paragraph that stayed vague.

apply-1.objective: Perform the basic movement of one cultural activity following a peer's instructions.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student completes the basic movement when guided through it by a partner.
apply-1.sampleTask: Pairs use the three-step descriptions from Understand-1. One partner reads the steps while the other performs the basic movement, then they switch. Each tries to complete the movement without seeing a demonstration first. Guide circulates and notes one pair who got the movement from words alone.

apply-2.objective: Lead a small group through a short warm-up drawn from one cultural activity.
apply-2.observableCheck: The group follows the leader's cues and completes the warm-up together.
apply-2.sampleTask: Each student prepares a two-minute warm-up based on one cultural activity and leads a group of three through it. The leader gives clear cues and adjusts pace for the group. Group members give one piece of feedback after. Guide watches and names one leader whose cues kept the group together.

apply-3.objective: Teach a peer one cultural activity well enough for them to do it unprompted.
apply-3.observableCheck: The learner performs the activity once without further cues from the teacher.
apply-3.sampleTask: Pairs split into teacher and learner. The teacher introduces one cultural activity, demonstrates, and coaches the learner until the learner can do it once on their own. They switch roles with a new activity. Guide circulates and notes a pair whose teaching led to independent practice.

apply-4.objective: Build a station card so another group can run a cultural activity without the author present.
apply-4.observableCheck: A different group runs the activity using only the card.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student writes a station card for one cultural activity with the origin, the movements, and the safety notes. A different group runs the activity using only the card. The author watches without speaking and notes any step the card missed. Guide collects cards that another group ran successfully.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which features two cultural activities share and which set them apart.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student completes a Venn diagram for two assigned activities.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide assigns each pair two cultural activities. Students complete a Venn diagram marking shared features and distinct ones, such as equipment, number of players, and movement type. Pairs compare diagrams with another pair. Guide names one shared feature most pairs missed.

analyze-2.objective: Compare how two cultures train a similar skill, such as balance, through different activities.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes two specific differences in how the two activities build the skill.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs pick two activities that build the same skill and write two specific differences in how each trains it. They decide which approach a beginner would find easier to start. Pairs share their call with another pair. Guide charts the differences the class found most interesting.

analyze-3.objective: Break a cultural activity into the movement skills a beginner must learn first.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student orders three to four sub-skills from first to learn to last.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students pick one cultural activity and break it into three or four sub-skills, then order them from what a beginner learns first to last. They write one sentence on why the first skill comes first. Pairs check the order makes sense. Guide collects two breakdowns with sound ordering.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze what made one peer-led activity session work or stall, citing specific moments.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two observations naming a moment that helped or hurt the session.
analyze-4.sampleTask: After the Apply-2 warm-ups, students write two observations about a session they joined, each naming a specific moment that helped or hurt the group's success. They mark whether the issue was the cues, the pacing, or the movement itself. Pairs compare observations. Guide names one pattern across sessions.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a peer's station card respects the activity's culture and gives safe, clear steps.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a verdict on a peer card citing two specific entries.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students swap the station cards from Apply-4 and score them on a rubric for accuracy, respect for the culture, and safety. Each reviewer writes a verdict citing two entries that earned or lost points. The reviewer suggests one fix to the weakest part. Guide checks three reviews against the rubric.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which cultural activity the class should add to its regular rotation, with reasons.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student presents a pick with two reasons and addresses one drawback.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Each student picks one cultural activity for the class to keep doing and writes two reasons plus a response to one likely drawback, such as equipment cost. Small groups compare picks and try to agree on one. Guide records each group's choice and the drawback they found hardest to answer.

create-1.objective: Produce a labeled card listing the origin and basic moves of one cultural activity.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a card with origin, three moves, and one image or sketch.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student makes a reference card for one cultural activity with the origin, three basic moves, and a small sketch or image. The card must let a newcomer recognize the activity. Pairs check the moves match the activity. Guide collects the cards into a class deck.

create-2.objective: Design a short fitness routine that blends moves from two different cultural activities.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student submits a routine naming the two source activities and the moves taken from each.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a three-minute routine that combines moves from two cultural activities, naming which move came from which. The blend must keep each move recognizable. Pairs try one section of the routine. Guide collects two routines that combined the sources cleanly.

create-3.objective: Create a class culture-and-movement showcase plan that lets every group lead one activity.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group submits a showcase slot with the activity, the lead, and the time needed.
create-3.sampleTask: Small groups plan one slot of a class showcase, naming the cultural activity, who leads, the space and equipment needed, and the time. The plan must fit a shared class schedule. Groups present the slot and adjust for overlaps with others. Guide assembles the slots into a full showcase schedule.

create-4.objective: Develop a leadership plan for guiding a mixed-skill group through an unfamiliar cultural activity.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a plan that names how they will introduce, demonstrate, and adjust for skill levels.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student writes a plan to lead a mixed-skill group through a cultural activity new to them, covering how they introduce it, demonstrate it, and adjust for students who struggle or race ahead. The plan must include one adjustment for each skill level. Pairs rehearse the introduction step. Guide reviews three plans for whether the adjustments are concrete.

2.2.8.LF.4.standardStatement: Identify and recognize factors that generate positive emotions from participating in movement and physical fitness activities.
2.2.8.LF.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List five things about a physical activity that can make a person feel good while doing it.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five factors on a card and shares one with the class.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide asks students to recall a time movement felt good and name what caused it, such as being with friends, hitting a goal, or moving to music. Each student writes five factors on a card. Cards go on the board grouped by theme. The class reads the most common factors aloud.

understand-1.objective: Explain how one named factor produces a positive feeling during activity.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a because sentence linking the factor to the feeling.
