understand-1.sampleTask: Each student picks one factor from Remember-1 and writes a sentence in the form this feels good because. Pairs read both sentences and decide if the link is believable. Guide collects three clear links and reads them. The class sorts whether the cause is social, physical, or about progress.

understand-2.objective: Describe the difference between enjoying an activity and being good at it.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student gives one example where the two come apart.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: can you enjoy something you are not good at? Each student writes one example where enjoyment and skill come apart, from their own experience. Pairs compare examples. Guide charts how many found enjoyment without high skill and discusses what that means for trying new activities.

understand-3.objective: Group the class's positive-feeling factors into categories that reveal a pattern.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each pair sorts the factor cards into named categories.
understand-3.sampleTask: Pairs take the factor cards from Remember-1 and sort them into categories such as social, sense of progress, physical sensation, and fun. Each pair names its categories. Pairs compare with another pair and discuss any card that fit two groups. Guide names the category the class generated most.

understand-4.objective: Summarize why positive feelings matter for sticking with physical activity over time.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph connecting enjoyment to staying active.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph explaining why how an activity feels affects whether a person keeps doing it. The paragraph must use at least two factors from the class list. Pairs underline the factors used. Guide flags any paragraph that did not connect feeling to staying active.

apply-1.objective: Rate personal enjoyment of three activities right after doing each one.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student records a rating and one factor for each of three activities.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide runs three short activity stations. After each, students record a one-to-five enjoyment rating and name the main factor behind it on a tracking sheet. After all three, students mark which factor mattered most across the day. Guide tallies the highest-rated station and the factors behind it.

apply-2.objective: Apply known positive factors to redesign a drill the student usually dislikes.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student rewrites one drill, naming the factor each change adds.
apply-2.sampleTask: Each student names a drill they dislike and rewrites it to add two positive factors, such as a partner or a clear progress marker. Each change is labeled with the factor it adds. Pairs check the changes would actually help. Guide collects two redesigns that made a disliked drill more appealing.

apply-3.objective: Explain to a peer how to make a familiar activity feel better using one factor.
apply-3.observableCheck: The partner names the factor and the change the speaker suggested.
apply-3.sampleTask: Pairs take turns. One suggests how to make a familiar activity feel better using one positive factor and explains why it would work. The listener restates the factor and the change. They switch. Guide circulates and notes one pair whose suggestion was specific.

apply-4.objective: Build a personal feel-good activity menu listing five activities and the factor each provides.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student turns in a menu of five activities each paired with its main positive factor.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student builds a one-page menu of five activities they could turn to, each labeled with the positive factor it reliably provides, such as music for energy or a friend for fun. The menu must work for a low-motivation day. Pairs check the factors are honest, not aspirational. Guide reviews three menus for usefulness.

analyze-1.objective: Identify, in two activity descriptions, which positive factors each one offers.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the factors present in each of two descriptions.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out two activity descriptions. Students mark which positive factors each one offers, drawing from the class category list. Pairs compare their marks. Guide reveals the factors built into each description and discusses any students missed.

analyze-2.objective: Compare which factors drive enjoyment for two different students for the same activity.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair lists where their enjoyment factors for one activity overlap and differ.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs pick one activity both have done and list which factors drive their enjoyment, then mark overlaps and differences. They discuss why the same activity can feel different to two people. Pairs share one surprising difference with another pair. Guide names the most common point of difference.

analyze-3.objective: Trace why enjoyment of an activity dropped over time by examining which factor disappeared.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the lost factor and the point where enjoyment fell.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students recall an activity they once liked but stopped enjoying and trace what changed, naming the factor that disappeared, such as friends leaving or progress stalling. They mark roughly when the drop happened. Pairs suggest one way the factor could have been restored. Guide charts the most common lost factor.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how the same factor, such as competition, can raise enjoyment for some and lower it for others.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student gives one case where a factor helped and one where it hurt.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide names a double-edged factor like competition or visible progress tracking. Each student writes one case where it raised enjoyment and one where it lowered enjoyment. Pairs compare and decide what tips it one way or the other. Guide collects two analyses that pinpointed the tipping condition.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a peer's feel-good menu would actually help on a low-motivation day, citing entries.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a verdict on a peer menu citing two specific entries.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students swap the menus from Apply-4 and score them on a rubric for honest factors, variety, and usefulness on a hard day. Each reviewer writes a verdict citing two entries that earned or lost points. The reviewer suggests one swap. Guide checks three reviews against the rubric.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which single factor matters most for keeping teenagers active, ranking it against others.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student presents a top factor with two reasons and a ranked list behind it.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Each student ranks the class's positive factors by how much they keep teenagers active and defends the top pick with two reasons. Small groups compare rankings and try to agree on a top three. Guide records each group's top factor and the reasoning that swayed the group.

create-1.objective: Produce a poster of five positive factors with a quick example of each.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a poster naming five factors each with one example.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student makes a small poster listing five positive factors, each with a one-line example a classmate would recognize. The poster must read at a glance. Pairs check the examples fit the factors. Guide posts the clearest ones around the gym.

create-2.objective: Design a class warm-up that deliberately builds in three positive factors.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student submits a warm-up plan labeling the three factors and where each appears.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a five-minute warm-up that builds in three positive factors, labeling where each appears, such as music for energy or a partner for fun. The warm-up must still get the body ready to move. Pairs run one section to test it. Guide collects two warm-ups that wove the factors in naturally.

create-3.objective: Create a class enjoyment campaign that promotes one activity by highlighting its positive factors.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group produces a campaign piece naming the activity and at least two factors.
create-3.sampleTask: Small groups build a short campaign, such as a poster and a thirty-second pitch, to get classmates to try one activity, leading with two positive factors it offers. The campaign must be honest about what the activity provides. Groups deliver the pitch to the class. Guide has the class note which campaign would get them to try the activity.

create-4.objective: Develop a personal plan to add one positive factor to a required activity the student finds dull.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a plan naming the activity, the added factor, and how they will know it helped.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student picks a required activity they find dull and writes a plan to add one positive factor, with steps and a way to tell if enjoyment rose. The plan must keep the activity's purpose intact. Pairs check the success measure is observable. Guide reviews three plans for whether the added factor is realistic.

2.2.8.LF.5.standardStatement: Engages in a variety of physical activities (e.g., aerobic-fitness, strengthen, endurance-fitness activities) using technology and cross-training, and lifetime activities.
2.2.8.LF.5.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name two activities each for aerobic fitness, strength, and endurance, plus one tech tool that tracks effort.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student fills a category sheet with two activities per type and one tracking tool.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the three fitness categories and the term cross-training. Students write two activities under each category and name one technology tool, such as a step counter or heart-rate app, that tracks effort. Pairs check three entries. Guide builds a shared class chart from the sheets.

understand-1.objective: Explain what cross-training means and why it mixes activity types.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence definition with one example of two mixed activities.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student writes a one-sentence definition of cross-training and names two activities a person might mix and why. Pairs read both and check the example actually mixes types. Guide collects three clear examples and reads them. The class agrees on a posted definition.

understand-2.objective: Describe how a tracking tool turns effort into information a person can use.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student names one number a tool reports and what decision it could inform.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students pick one tracking tool and write what number it reports, such as steps or heart rate, and one decision that number could inform, such as slowing down or adding a day. Pairs compare and check the decision follows from the number. Guide charts the tools and the decisions they support.

understand-3.objective: Sort activities by which fitness type they mainly build.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each pair places ten activity cards under aerobic, strength, or endurance with reasons for split cases.
understand-3.sampleTask: Pairs sort ten activity cards under aerobic, strength, or endurance. For any card that fits more than one, they write which they chose and why. Pairs compare with another pair and argue split cases. Guide reveals that some activities are mixed and names them.

understand-4.objective: Summarize why a varied program beats doing one activity for general fitness.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph giving two reasons variety helps.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph explaining why mixing activity types serves general fitness better than repeating one. The paragraph needs two reasons, such as balanced muscle use or lower boredom. Pairs underline the reasons. Guide flags any paragraph that only described variety without explaining its benefit.

apply-1.objective: Use a tracking tool to record effort across one varied activity session.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student logs a reading from each of three activity types in one session.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide runs a session with an aerobic, a strength, and an endurance station. Students use a tracking tool or a manual count to record one effort reading at each station on a log. After, students mark which station pushed effort highest. Guide collects logs and notes the spread of readings.

apply-2.objective: Build a one-week cross-training plan that includes all three fitness types.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student turns in a seven-day plan with each day labeled by fitness type.
apply-2.sampleTask: Each student fills a seven-day grid with activities, labeling each day's main fitness type and including at least one of each type. The plan must include one rest or light day. Pairs check the plan is balanced and realistic. Guide collects three plans that mixed all three types sensibly.

apply-3.objective: Explain to a peer how to use one tech tool to guide a workout.
apply-3.observableCheck: The partner restates how to read the tool and adjust effort from it.
apply-3.sampleTask: Pairs take turns explaining how to use one tracking tool to guide a workout, including what to watch and when to ease off or push. The listener restates the steps. They switch with a different tool. Guide circulates and notes one clear explanation.

apply-4.objective: Build a station-rotation card that runs a small group through a cross-training circuit.
apply-4.observableCheck: A group completes the circuit using only the card.
apply-4.sampleTask: Each student writes a circuit card with four stations, each labeled by fitness type, with the time and the tracking step at each. A small group runs the circuit using only the card. The author watches and notes any unclear step. Guide collects cards a group could run unaided.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which fitness types a sample weekly plan covers and which it neglects.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks each day's type and names the missing or overloaded type.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample seven-day plan. Students label each day's fitness type and name which type is missing or overloaded. Pairs compare. Guide reveals the intended gap and discusses how to fill it.

analyze-2.objective: Compare a plan that uses tracking technology with one that does not, for what each can adjust.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each pair lists two adjustments the tracked plan supports that the untracked one cannot.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Pairs get two versions of a plan, one with tracking and one without. They list two adjustments the tracked plan makes possible that the untracked plan cannot, such as matching effort to a target heart rate. Pairs decide whether the tracking is worth the trouble here. Guide names the most useful adjustment the class found.

analyze-3.objective: Break a single sport into the fitness types its training should include for cross-training.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps one sport to the aerobic, strength, and endurance work it needs.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students pick one sport and map what aerobic, strength, and endurance work would support it, naming one activity for each. They mark which type that sport tends to neglect. Pairs check the map fits the sport. Guide collects two maps that caught a real training gap.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how tracking data from one week reveals where a program is unbalanced.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student reads a sample data set and names the imbalance it shows.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide hands out a week of sample tracking data, such as daily steps and active minutes by type. Students read the data and name where the program is unbalanced and one change the data supports. Pairs compare readings. Guide confirms the imbalance built into the data set.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a peer's cross-training plan is balanced and doable, citing specific days.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a verdict on a peer plan citing two specific days.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students swap the plans from Apply-2 and score them on a rubric for balance across types, realism, and inclusion of recovery. Each reviewer writes a verdict citing two days that earned or lost points. The reviewer suggests one swap. Guide checks three reviews against the rubric.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend whether tracking technology helps or distracts a teenager building a fitness habit.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student states a position with two reasons and answers one counterpoint.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: does tracking technology help or distract a teenager building a habit? Each student writes a stance with two reasons and a response to one counterpoint. Students form two sides and trade their strongest reason. Guide records the reason each side found hardest to answer.

create-1.objective: Produce a reference card listing one activity for each fitness type and one tracking tool.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student turns in a card with three activities by type and one tool with what it measures.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student makes a reference card naming one activity per fitness type and one tracking tool with what it measures. The card must let a newcomer build a basic varied workout. Pairs check the activities match their types. Guide collects the cards into a class set.

create-2.objective: Design a four-week cross-training program that progresses across the month.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student submits a four-week plan showing how load or variety increases week to week.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a four-week program covering all three fitness types, showing how it gets harder or more varied each week. The progression must be gradual, not a sudden jump. Pairs check week four is reachable from week one. Guide collects two programs with sound progression.

create-3.objective: Create a class cross-training challenge that uses a shared tracking method.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group submits a challenge with the activities, the tracking method, and how a winner is judged.
create-3.sampleTask: Small groups design a one-week class challenge that covers all three fitness types and uses one shared tracking method everyone can access. The group sets a fair way to judge progress, not just raw numbers. Groups present the challenge. Guide has the class pick one challenge to run and discusses its fairness.

create-4.objective: Develop a personal lifetime-activity plan that names cross-training a student could keep past school.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a plan naming activities, a tracking method, and how it would survive a busy adult week.
create-4.sampleTask: Each student builds a plan for activities they could keep into adulthood, covering all three fitness types and a tracking method, with a note on how it survives a busy week. The plan must use activities that do not depend on a school gym. Pairs check the plan is realistic outside school. Guide reviews three plans for lifetime fit.

2.2.8.LF.6.standardStatement: Develop a strategy to overcome barriers that allows for a visit in the community that promotes physical activities.
2.2.8.LF.6.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name three community places nearby where a person can be physically active.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists three named places on an index card before discussion starts.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name three real places within a short trip of our school where someone could walk, play, swim, or move.' Each student writes three on an index card. Cards go on a class map of the area, pinned near the place named. The Guide reads back the full list so every student leaves with more options than they came in with.

understand-1.objective: Describe what counts as a barrier that keeps someone from visiting a community activity place.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence definition of barrier and gives one example.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a half-sheet with the sentence stem 'A barrier is something that...'. Students finish the sentence and add one example from their own week. Pairs trade sheets and check that the example actually blocks a visit rather than just makes it less fun. The Guide collects three strong examples to read aloud.

understand-2.objective: Explain how a named barrier blocks access to a specific activity place.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'because' sentence linking one barrier to one place from the class map.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student picks one place from the class map and one barrier and writes 'I cannot visit [place] because [barrier]'. Then they add one sentence saying who else the same barrier would block. Pairs read each other's sentences and mark whether the barrier is real for many people or only for one. The Guide charts the barriers that showed up most.

understand-3.objective: Sort barriers into types such as cost, distance, time, and safety.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student places eight barrier cards into the correct type column on a sorting mat.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide hands each pair eight barrier cards and a four-column sorting mat labeled cost, distance, time, and safety. Pairs place each card and write one card of their own for any column that feels thin. Pairs compare mats with a neighbor pair and resolve disagreements. The Guide names the column with the most cards as the one the class will target.

understand-4.objective: Connect the type of barrier a person faces to the kind of help that would remove it.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student matches four barrier types to four support options on a worksheet.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students get a worksheet with four barrier types on the left and six possible supports on the right, such as a bus pass, a buddy, a free community day, and a daylight schedule. Students draw lines from each barrier type to the support that fits and cross out the two extras. They write one sentence explaining the hardest match. Whole-class review settles the matches.

apply-1.objective: Select the correct support for a stated barrier in five short scenarios.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the matching support beside each of five scenarios.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a scenario strip with five short situations, each naming a person and a barrier. Students write next to each the one support that would let that person make the visit. Pairs compare answers and flag any scenario with two reasonable answers. The Guide reads the trickiest scenario and takes two student answers.

apply-2.objective: Use a planning template to lay out the steps of one community activity visit.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student fills every row of a visit-planning template for one chosen place.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students pick one place from the class map and complete a planning template with rows for getting there, cost, who comes along, what to bring, and the day and time. Each row must name a specific answer, not 'somehow'. Pairs swap templates and circle any row that is still vague. Students fix circled rows before the Guide collects.

apply-3.objective: Apply a barrier-removal strategy to a peer's stalled visit plan and revise it.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student adds at least two concrete fixes to a partner's plan in writing.
apply-3.sampleTask: Pairs trade the planning templates from Apply-2. Each student finds the two weakest rows on the partner's plan and writes a specific fix in the margin, such as a named bus route or a free Saturday hour. Partners talk through the fixes and decide which to keep. Each student rewrites one fixed row in clean form.

apply-4.objective: Build a step-by-step strategy that carries a visit plan through more than one barrier at once.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a numbered action sequence that names a fix for each barrier in their plan.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students take their revised plan and write a numbered strategy, one step per line, that handles every barrier they listed in order. Each step names who does it and when. Students test the sequence by reading it to a partner who marks any step that depends on a missing earlier step. Students reorder steps to close the gaps.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which single barrier blocks the most students in the class.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student reads the class barrier tally and names the top barrier in one sentence.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide displays the tally of barriers collected across the lesson. Students study the counts and write the one barrier with the highest count, plus the runner-up. They add one sentence on whether the top barrier is the same for grade 5 students they know. Pairs compare conclusions and the Guide confirms the count.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two activity places to determine which has fewer barriers for most students.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column comparison and circles the lower-barrier place.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students pick two places from the class map and fill a two-column chart listing cost, distance, time, and safety for each. They circle the place with fewer or smaller barriers and write one sentence defending the choice. Pairs challenge each other's circles with one counterpoint. The Guide takes two defenses to the class.

analyze-3.objective: Trace why a sample visit plan failed and pinpoint the barrier the strategy missed.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student marks the failure point on a sample plan and names the missed barrier.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a sample plan that broke down partway through a visit. Students read it, mark the exact step where it failed, and write which barrier the planner did not handle. They add the one fix that would have kept the plan alive. Pairs compare failure points and the Guide reveals what actually went wrong.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how cost, distance, time, and safety interact in one student's real situation.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page map showing how their four barriers connect and reinforce each other.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students draw a one-page web with their chosen activity place in the center and the four barrier types around it. They draw arrows showing where one barrier makes another worse, such as distance raising cost. Each arrow gets a short label. Pairs read each other's webs and add one arrow the partner missed. The Guide collects the webs as the analysis artifact.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge which of three barrier-removal strategies is most likely to work for a given student and defend the pick.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student ranks three strategies and writes a paragraph defending the top choice against the others.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a profile of a student with named barriers and three competing strategies to fix them. Students rank the three and write a paragraph saying why the top one beats the other two for this person. They name one situation where their pick would fail. Pairs trade paragraphs and score each other against a two-point rubric for evidence and a named weakness.

evaluate-4.objective: Assess a full class plan to promote community activity and recommend which strategy to fund first.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a recommendation memo naming one strategy to fund first with two reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review the class's collected strategies as if they had a small budget that covers only one. Each writes a short memo to the Guide naming the one strategy to fund first, with two reasons tied to the barrier tally. They name who benefits most and who is left out. The Guide sorts memos by recommendation and reports the class split.

create-1.objective: Draft a single poster slogan that invites peers to a named community activity place.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one slogan under ten words naming the place and the action.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick one place from the class map and write a single slogan, under ten words, that names the place and tells peers what to do there. They write it large on a quarter sheet. Pairs vote on which of two slogans is clearer. The Guide posts the top slogans near the class map.

create-2.objective: Produce a one-page visit plan that combines a place, a strategy, and a schedule.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student turns in a one-page plan with place, barrier strategy, and a dated schedule filled in.
create-2.sampleTask: Students build a clean one-page plan that pulls together their chosen place, the barrier strategy they wrote, and a specific day and time. Each section gets at least two full sentences. Pairs check that the schedule and the strategy do not contradict each other. Students fix any clash before turning in.

create-3.objective: Design a small campaign that gets a group of peers to visit one activity place together.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group produces a campaign sheet naming the message, the channel, and the visit date.
create-3.sampleTask: Groups of three design a short campaign to get classmates to one activity place on one date. The campaign sheet must name the message, how they will spread it, and the exact visit day and time. Groups present the sheet in sixty seconds to another group, who asks one hard question about barriers. Groups revise the weakest part before the Guide collects.

create-4.objective: Construct a class proposal that removes the top barrier so most students can reach community activity.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a proposal naming the barrier, the fix, the cost, and who carries it out.
create-4.sampleTask: Working from the class barrier tally, groups write a proposal that attacks the single top barrier for the whole class. The proposal names the barrier, the concrete fix, a rough cost, and the person or group who would run it. Groups read proposals to the class and the class scores each on whether it would actually move the most students. The Guide keeps the highest-scoring proposal as the class artifact.

2.2.8.LF.7.standardStatement: Evaluate personal attributes as they relate to career options in physical activity and health professions.
2.2.8.LF.7.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List five careers in physical activity or health professions.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes five named careers on a card before any discussion.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name five jobs where someone works with movement, fitness, or health.' Students write five on a card. Cards go on a board sorted into physical-activity jobs and health-profession jobs. The Guide reads the combined list so students see careers they had not named.

understand-1.objective: Describe what a personal attribute is and give two examples about yourself.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a definition of personal attribute and lists two of their own.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives the stem 'A personal attribute is...'. Students finish it and list two attributes they have, such as patience or strong balance. Pairs check that each listed item is an attribute, not a hobby or a wish. The Guide reads three clear examples aloud and corrects one common mix-up.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a specific career calls for a specific attribute.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'this job needs this trait because' sentence for two careers.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student picks two careers from the class board and writes 'a [career] needs [attribute] because [reason]' for each. The reason must name a real task the worker does. Pairs read each other's sentences and mark whether the reason names a task or just repeats the trait. The Guide charts the attributes that came up most.

understand-3.objective: Group careers by the cluster of attributes each one demands.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts ten career cards into attribute clusters on a mat.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide hands pairs ten career cards and a mat with clusters such as people-facing, detail-and-precision, and physical-stamina. Pairs place each career and write one card for any cluster that looks thin. Pairs compare with a neighbor pair and resolve splits. The Guide names which cluster held the most careers.

understand-4.objective: Connect a student's own attributes to the cluster of careers that fits them.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes which career cluster fits their attributes and names one career in it.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students list three of their own attributes, then mark which cluster from Understand-3 those attributes point toward. They name one career in that cluster and write one sentence on the fit. Pairs trade and check that the named career actually sits in the chosen cluster. The Guide collects the strongest fits.

apply-1.objective: Match a listed attribute to the career that most needs it across five items.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the matching career beside each of five attributes.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a strip listing five attributes. Students write next to each the one career from a provided word bank that needs it most. Pairs compare and flag any attribute that fits two careers. The Guide reads the most-debated item and takes two answers.

apply-2.objective: Use a self-rating scale to score your own attributes against one chosen career.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a rating sheet scoring four attributes one through four for one career.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students pick one career and rate themselves one through four on four attributes that career needs. Each rating gets a one-line example that backs the score. Pairs swap sheets and challenge any score with no example. Students add a missing example before turning in.

apply-3.objective: Apply the rating results to choose which of two careers fits you better right now.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student picks one of two careers and cites two ratings that drove the choice.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students run the Apply-2 rating for a second career, then pick the better current fit. They write two sentences, each naming a specific rating that pushed the decision. Pairs check that the cited ratings actually favor the chosen career. The Guide takes two contrasting choices to the class.

apply-4.objective: Build an attribute-growth plan that raises your weakest score for a target career.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-step plan naming actions to grow one low-rated attribute.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students take their lowest rating from Apply-2 and write a three-step plan to raise it, with each step naming a specific action and when to do it. The plan ends with how they would know the attribute grew. Pairs test the plan by marking any step that is a wish rather than an action. Students rewrite weak steps.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which attribute shows up across the most health and activity careers.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student reads the class attribute chart and names the most-shared attribute.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide displays the chart of attributes collected across careers. Students name the attribute that appears in the most careers and the one that appears least. They add a sentence on why the top one is so common. Pairs compare and the Guide confirms the count.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two careers to determine which demands more of the same attribute.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart and circles the higher-demand career.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students pick one attribute and two careers, then fill a two-column chart describing how each career uses that attribute. They circle the career that demands more of it and write one defending sentence. Pairs challenge the circle with a counterpoint. The Guide takes two defenses.

analyze-3.objective: Examine a worker profile to find the gap between the person's attributes and the job's demands.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student marks one attribute gap on a profile and names its effect on the job.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide hands out a profile of a worker whose attributes only partly fit their job. Students mark the attribute the worker is short on and write how that gap would show up at work. They add one way the worker could close the gap. Pairs compare gaps and the Guide reveals the intended gap.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how several of your attributes combine to open or close a career path.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page web linking three attributes to the careers they open or block.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students draw a one-page web with three of their attributes in the center and careers around the edge. Arrows show which attributes open which careers and which attributes would hold one back. Each arrow gets a short label. Pairs add one arrow the partner missed. The Guide collects the webs.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a peer's career choice fits their stated attributes and defend the verdict.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a verdict with two pieces of evidence from the peer's attribute list.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Pairs trade their Understand-4 fits. Each student judges whether the partner's named career truly fits the partner's attributes and writes a verdict backed by two specific attributes. They name one attribute the partner would still need to build. Partners discuss and each scores the other against a two-point rubric for evidence and a named gap.

evaluate-4.objective: Assess your own readiness for a target career and recommend your single best next step.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a readiness memo naming one next step with two reasons drawn from their ratings.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review their full set of ratings and plans for a target career and write a short memo on how ready they are now. The memo names the one next step that matters most, with two reasons tied to specific ratings. They name the attribute that would change the verdict if it grew. The Guide sorts memos by next step and reports common choices.

create-1.objective: Draft a one-line career goal statement that names a career and one matching attribute.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence naming a target career and one attribute that fits it.
create-1.sampleTask: Students write a single goal sentence in the form 'I am aiming at [career] because I have [attribute]'. They write it large on a quarter sheet. Pairs check that the attribute genuinely fits the career. The Guide posts the goal statements on the careers board.

create-2.objective: Produce a one-page profile pairing your attributes with a chosen career and its demands.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student turns in a profile with attributes, career demands, and the matches marked.
create-2.sampleTask: Students build a one-page profile with their key attributes on one side, the chosen career's demands on the other, and lines drawn between matches. Each match gets a one-line note. Pairs check that every drawn line connects a real attribute to a real demand. Students fix stray lines before turning in.

create-3.objective: Design a short interview plan to learn what attributes a real worker in a target career uses.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student writes five interview questions aimed at attributes the career demands.
create-3.sampleTask: Students pick a target career and write five interview questions for a worker in it, each aimed at a specific attribute, not at salary or hours. Pairs swap question sets and cut any question that does not target an attribute. Students replace cut questions. The Guide collects the sharpest sets to model a real interview.

create-4.objective: Construct a two-year attribute-building roadmap toward a target career.
create-4.observableCheck: Each student submits a roadmap with dated milestones tied to specific attributes.
create-4.sampleTask: Students build a roadmap that names a target career and lays out milestones across the next two years, each tied to growing a specific attribute the career needs. Each milestone names an action and roughly when it happens. Pairs read each other's roadmaps and flag any milestone with no attribute attached. Students fix flagged milestones before the Guide collects the roadmap.

2.2.8.MSC.1.standardStatement: Explain and demonstrate the transition of movement skills from isolated settings (e.g., skill practice) into applied settings (e.g., games, sports, dance, recreational activities).
2.2.8.MSC.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the parts of one movement skill in the order they happen.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the steps of one skill in sequence on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide names one skill the class has practiced, such as a chest pass or an overhand throw. Each student writes the steps in order on a card, from set-up to follow-through. Cards go up and the Guide reads back a correct sequence. Students fix any step they had out of order.

understand-1.objective: Describe the difference between an isolated drill and an applied game setting.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence for each setting and one way they differ.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a half-sheet with two boxes, isolated and applied. Students write what a skill looks like in each box, then write one sentence on the main difference, such as a defender being present. Pairs check that each box names a real condition. The Guide reads two clear contrasts aloud.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a skill changes when a defender or a partner enters the setting.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'because' sentence naming what the added person forces the mover to do.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student picks one skill and writes 'this skill changes in a game because [the defender or partner forces ...]'. The reason must name a specific adjustment, such as faster decisions or shielding the ball. Pairs read each other's sentences and mark whether the adjustment is named. The Guide charts the adjustments students found.

understand-3.objective: Classify movement situations as isolated practice or applied play.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts eight situation cards into the two settings on a mat.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide hands pairs eight situation cards describing a mover doing a skill. Pairs place each card under isolated or applied and write one card of their own for the thinner column. Pairs compare with a neighbor pair and resolve splits. The Guide names which situations were hardest to place and why.

understand-4.objective: Connect a drill cue to the game moment where that cue pays off.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student matches four practice cues to four game moments on a worksheet.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students get a worksheet with four practice cues on the left, such as 'eyes up', and six game moments on the right. They draw lines from each cue to the moment where it matters and cross out the two extras. They write one sentence on the hardest match. Whole-class review settles the matches.

apply-1.objective: Demonstrate one isolated skill on cue to a clear form standard.
apply-1.observableCheck: The Guide observes each student perform the skill and checks three form points.
apply-1.sampleTask: At a station, each student performs one skill, such as a dribble or a bump pass, ten times while a partner checks three posted form points and tallies clean reps. The performer then names which form point slipped most. Partners swap roles. The Guide circulates and confirms two tallies per pair.

apply-2.objective: Demonstrate the same skill inside a small-sided game while keeping the form points.
apply-2.observableCheck: A partner tallies how many game reps held the three form points.
apply-2.sampleTask: Pairs join a three-on-three small-sided game for five minutes. One partner plays while the other watches one named skill and tallies reps that held the form points from Apply-1. They switch so each plays and observes. Each student writes which form point was hardest to keep once a defender appeared. The Guide keeps the games short and rotating.

apply-3.objective: Apply a verbal cue mid-game to fix a slipping skill in real time.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student names the cue they used and reports whether the next rep improved.
apply-3.sampleTask: In the next small-sided round, each player picks one cue word to say to themselves when their skill slips, drawn from the Understand-4 matches. After the round they write the cue they used and one rep where it helped. Partners confirm they heard or saw the cue applied. The Guide collects the cue words that worked.

apply-4.objective: Carry a skill through three settings of rising pressure without losing the form points.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student records form-point success across solo, partner, and game settings on one sheet.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students run the same skill through three stations: alone, with a passive partner, and in live play. A peer records form-point success at each station on one tracking sheet. Students mark the setting where form first broke down. Pairs discuss what changed between the last clean setting and the first broken one. The Guide collects the tracking sheets.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the exact point in a game clip where a skill broke down.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the breakdown moment on a clip timeline and names the skill.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a short game clip twice. Students mark on a simple timeline strip the moment a skill broke down and write which skill it was. They add one sentence on what the player faced at that moment. Pairs compare marks and the Guide replays the moment to confirm.

analyze-2.objective: Compare a player's drill form to their game form to find what changed.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart listing form in drill versus form in game.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Using the Apply tracking sheets, students fill a two-column chart for one skill, drill form on the left and game form on the right. They circle the one form point that changed most under pressure. They write one sentence on why that point is the first to go. Pairs compare circles and the Guide takes two.

analyze-3.objective: Trace which game condition caused a skill to transfer poorly.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the condition and links it to the specific form breakdown.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students study a peer's game-rep tally and pick the condition that hurt the skill most, such as time pressure or a tight defender. They write how that condition caused the specific breakdown they saw. They add the one practice change that would prepare for it. Pairs compare conditions and the Guide charts them.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how force, timing, and decision-making interact when a skill moves into a game.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page diagram linking the three factors to one skill's game performance.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students draw a one-page diagram with one skill in the center and force, timing, and decision-making around it. Arrows show where one factor changes another in live play, such as a rushed decision throwing off timing. Each arrow gets a label. Pairs add one missed arrow. The Guide collects the diagrams.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge which of two practice designs better prepares a skill for game transfer and defend the choice.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student picks one design and writes two reasons tied to game conditions.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide describes two practice plans for the same skill, one isolated and repetitive, one with built-in pressure. Students pick the one that transfers better and write two reasons that name real game conditions. They name one thing the losing design still does well. Pairs trade and score each other on a two-point rubric for evidence and fairness.

evaluate-4.objective: Assess a player's full transfer record and recommend the next practice focus.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a recommendation naming one practice focus with two reasons from the record.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a peer's tracking sheets and analysis from the lesson and write a short recommendation for what that player should practice next. The recommendation names one focus and two reasons drawn from where the skill broke down. They name the game condition the focus targets. The Guide pairs students to deliver recommendations and collects them.

create-1.objective: Draft one coaching cue under six words that helps a skill survive game pressure.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one short cue tied to a named skill.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick one skill and write a single cue, under six words, that a teammate could say to hold form in a game. They write it large on a quarter sheet. Pairs test whether the cue is short enough to say mid-play. The Guide posts the cues by skill.

create-2.objective: Produce a small-sided game rule tweak that forces one target skill to appear more often.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes one rule tweak and predicts how it raises the skill's frequency.
create-2.sampleTask: Students invent one rule tweak for a small-sided game that makes a target skill happen more, such as requiring three passes before a shot. They write the rule and one sentence predicting its effect. Pairs check that the rule actually forces the skill, not just slows play. Students refine the rule before turning in.

create-3.objective: Design a three-station progression that moves one skill from isolated to applied.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group produces a station card naming the skill demand and the added pressure at each step.
create-3.sampleTask: Groups of three design a three-station progression for one skill, where each station adds pressure: alone, with a partner, then in live play. The station card names what the mover does and the pressure added at each step. Groups walk another group through the cards in two minutes and take one question. Groups fix the weakest station before the Guide collects.

create-4.objective: Construct a full practice-to-game plan for one skill that names cues, drills, and a game test.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a plan with a drill, a pressure step, a cue, and a game-based check.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a one-page plan that takes one skill from drill to game. The plan names the isolated drill, the pressure step, the in-game cue, and how they would test transfer in real play. Groups present the plan and the class scores whether the game test truly checks transfer. The Guide keeps the highest-scoring plan as the class artifact.

2.2.8.MSC.2.standardStatement: Demonstrate control of motion in relationship between force, flow, time, and space in interactive dynamic environments.
2.2.8.MSC.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the four movement concepts force, flow, time, and space.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes all four concepts on a card from memory.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name the four ways we describe how a body moves.' Students write the four on a card. Cards go up and the Guide reads each concept with a one-line meaning. Students fix any they missed or mislabeled.

understand-1.objective: Describe what each of the four concepts means using a movement example.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-line meaning and one example for each concept.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a four-box half-sheet, one box per concept. Students write a short meaning and one movement example in each box, such as a hard kick for force. Pairs check that each example fits its concept and not a neighbor concept. The Guide reads one clean example per box.

understand-2.objective: Explain how changing force changes the result of a movement.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'because' sentence linking a force change to a movement result.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student writes 'when I add force to [a movement], the result changes because ...'. The reason names a concrete result, such as the ball traveling farther or control dropping. Pairs read and mark whether the result is concrete. The Guide charts how force traded against control in students' examples.

understand-3.objective: Classify sample movements by which concept they most depend on.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts eight movement cards under the four concepts on a mat.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide hands pairs eight movement cards and a four-column mat. Pairs place each movement under the concept it most depends on and write one card for the thinnest column. Pairs compare with a neighbor pair and resolve splits. The Guide names a movement that depends on two concepts at once.

understand-4.objective: Connect a change in space or time to the adjustment a mover must make.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student matches four space or time changes to four required adjustments on a worksheet.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students get a worksheet with four changes, such as a shrinking playing area, on the left and six adjustments on the right. They draw lines to the adjustment each change forces and cross out the extras. They write one sentence on the hardest match. Whole-class review settles it.

apply-1.objective: Demonstrate controlled force in a target task such as a soft toss to a near goal.
apply-1.observableCheck: A partner tallies how many tosses land in the target zone.
apply-1.sampleTask: At a station, each student makes ten controlled tosses or strikes at a near target, aiming for soft, accurate landings rather than power. A partner tallies how many land in the target zone. The thrower names whether they used too much or too little force. Partners switch and the Guide confirms two tallies.

apply-2.objective: Demonstrate control of flow by linking two movements without a pause.
apply-2.observableCheck: A partner confirms whether the two movements connected smoothly.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students pick two movements, such as a catch and a quick pass, and perform them linked five times with no pause between. A partner watches and marks each rep as smooth or broken. The performer names what caused any break. Partners switch and the Guide spot-checks pairs.

apply-3.objective: Apply a deliberate change in timing to beat a moving partner to a spot.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student reports whether the timing change won the spot and how.
apply-3.sampleTask: In a one-on-one move-to-space drill, each student tries to reach an open cone before a partner by changing their timing, speeding up or delaying a start. After three rounds they write whether the timing change worked and which spot it won. Partners confirm the result. The Guide collects the timing tactics that succeeded.

apply-4.objective: Control force, flow, time, and space together while moving through a dynamic obstacle course.
apply-4.observableCheck: A peer scores the run on all four concepts using a four-row rubric.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students run a short obstacle course that demands a hard throw, a smooth transition, a timed move past a mover, and tight use of space. A peer scores the run on a four-row rubric, one row per concept. The runner names the concept that cost them most. Pairs discuss one fix and rerun once. The Guide collects rubrics.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which concept a player controlled worst in a short clip.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the weakest concept and the moment it showed.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a short movement clip twice. Students name which of the four concepts the mover controlled worst and the moment it showed. They add one sentence on the cost of that loss. Pairs compare and the Guide replays the moment.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two performers on the same task to find who controlled space better.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart and circles the better space-controller.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students watch or recall two performers on the same task and fill a two-column chart on how each used space. They circle the better one and write one defending sentence naming a specific moment. Pairs challenge the circle. The Guide takes two defenses.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how a loss of flow led to a loss of control later in a movement.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps the chain from the flow break to the control loss.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students study a flow checklist from Apply-2 that shows a break and write the chain: the break, what it forced next, and the control loss that followed. They add the one fix at the break point. Pairs compare chains and the Guide charts common break points.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how all four concepts interact in one dynamic game moment.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page diagram linking the four concepts to one game moment.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students pick one game moment and draw a one-page diagram with the four concepts around it. Arrows show where one concept forced a change in another, such as tight space cutting available time. Each arrow gets a label. Pairs add one missed arrow. The Guide collects the diagrams.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge which of two movers showed better overall control in a dynamic setting and defend it.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student picks one mover and cites evidence from at least two of the four concepts.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide shows or describes two movers handling the same dynamic task. Students pick the one with better overall control and defend it with evidence from at least two concepts. They name the one concept where the loser was actually stronger. Pairs trade and score each other on a two-point rubric for evidence and fairness.

evaluate-4.objective: Assess your own four-concept control record and recommend the concept to train first.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a recommendation naming one concept with two reasons from their scores.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review their Apply-4 rubric and analysis and write a short recommendation for the one concept to train first. The recommendation gives two reasons drawn from specific scores. They name the dynamic setting where that concept hurt most. The Guide sorts recommendations by concept and reports the class pattern.

create-1.objective: Draft one movement challenge that targets a single concept.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one challenge naming the concept it trains.
create-1.sampleTask: Students write one short movement challenge, such as 'reach the far cone using the least force', and name the concept it trains. They write it on a quarter sheet. Pairs check that the challenge truly targets the named concept. The Guide posts challenges by concept.

create-2.objective: Produce a partner drill that forces a deliberate change in timing or space.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a drill with rules that require the timing or space change.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a partner drill whose rules make a mover change timing or use space differently, such as a tag rule that rewards delayed starts. They write the rules and name which concept the rule trains. Pairs test that the rule forces the change. Students refine before turning in.

create-3.objective: Design a dynamic obstacle course that demands all four concepts in sequence.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group produces a course map labeling which concept each station trains.
create-3.sampleTask: Groups of three design an obstacle course where four stations each demand a different concept. The course map labels each station with its concept and the task. Groups walk another group through the course in two minutes and take one question. Groups fix the weakest station before the Guide collects the maps.

create-4.objective: Construct a small-game design where controlling all four concepts decides who wins.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits game rules that tie scoring to control of force, flow, time, and space.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a small game whose rules reward control of all four concepts, such as points for soft accurate passes and for reaching space first. The rules sheet names how each concept earns or loses points. Groups teach the game to another group in three minutes and play one round. The class scores which game best rewards control, and the Guide keeps the top design.

2.2.8.MSC.3.standardStatement: Create and demonstrate planned movement sequences, individually and with others, based on tempo, beat, rhythm, music, and physical activities (e.g., creative, cultural, social, fitness aerobics, dance, yoga).
2.2.8.MSC.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the terms tempo, beat, and rhythm and clap one example of each.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the three terms and claps a beat on the Guide's cue.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the three terms and gives a one-line meaning for each. Students write the terms on a card, then clap a steady beat together and a simple rhythm on the Guide's count. The Guide checks that the room holds the beat. Students mark which term they still confuse.

understand-1.objective: Describe how tempo and beat differ using a movement example.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence distinguishing tempo from beat with an example.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a half-sheet asking students to explain how tempo and beat differ. Students write one sentence and one movement example, such as the same step done fast or slow. Pairs check that the example shows tempo, not just a different move. The Guide reads two clear contrasts.

understand-2.objective: Explain why matching movement to the beat makes a sequence look planned.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'because' sentence linking on-beat movement to a planned look.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student writes 'movement on the beat looks planned because ...', naming a concrete effect such as the group moving together. Pairs read and mark whether the effect is concrete. The Guide charts the effects students named and links them to the idea of timing.

understand-3.objective: Classify movement styles by the rhythm pattern each one uses.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts six style cards by rhythm feel on a mat.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide hands pairs six style cards, such as aerobics, a cultural folk step, and yoga flow, and a mat with rhythm headers like steady, flowing, and sharp. Pairs place each style and write one note on why. Pairs compare with a neighbor pair and resolve splits. The Guide names a style that fits two rhythm feels.

understand-4.objective: Connect a piece of music to the movement style that fits its rhythm.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student matches four short tracks to four movement styles on a worksheet.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide plays four short clips. Students write next to each clip which of six listed styles fits its rhythm and cross out the two extras. They write one sentence on the clue that decided the hardest match. Whole-class review confirms the matches.

apply-1.objective: Demonstrate a four-count movement on the beat of a steady track.
apply-1.observableCheck: A partner confirms whether each count landed on the beat.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a steady track. Each student performs a simple four-count move, such as step-touch, trying to land each count on the beat. A partner watches and marks each count as on or off. The performer names which count drifted. Partners switch and the Guide keeps the track running.

apply-2.objective: Demonstrate a planned eight-count sequence solo using two different moves.
apply-2.observableCheck: A partner checks that the sequence holds eight counts and uses two moves.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students build and perform an eight-count solo sequence using at least two distinct moves on the beat. A partner counts aloud and marks whether all eight counts were filled and both moves appeared. The performer names the count that was hardest to hold. Partners switch. The Guide spot-checks two pairs.

apply-3.objective: Apply tempo changes to the same sequence to give it a slow and a fast version.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student performs both versions and names what changed besides speed.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students take their eight-count sequence and perform it once slow and once at a faster tempo on the matching track. They write what got harder in the fast version besides speed, such as keeping moves clean. A partner confirms both versions stayed on beat. The Guide collects the notes on what tempo changed.

apply-4.objective: Combine individual sequences with a partner into one synced sequence.
apply-4.observableCheck: A second pair confirms whether the two performers stayed synced through the sequence.
apply-4.sampleTask: Pairs merge their eight-count solos into one synced sequence, choosing where to match and where to mirror. They rehearse and then perform for a second pair, who marks where the two fell out of sync. Performers name the transition that caused the slip and fix it. The Guide watches two performances.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the count where a recorded sequence fell off the beat.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the off-beat count on a count strip.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide plays a short sequence clip twice over a counted track. Students mark on a count strip the count where the mover fell off the beat. They write one sentence on what the mover did at that count. Pairs compare marks and the Guide replays to confirm.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two sequences to determine which uses rhythm more clearly.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart and circles the clearer sequence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students watch or recall two short sequences and fill a two-column chart on how each uses rhythm. They circle the clearer one and write one defending sentence naming a specific moment. Pairs challenge the circle. The Guide takes two defenses.

analyze-3.objective: Trace how one missed transition threw off the rest of a partner sequence.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps the chain from the missed transition to the later slip.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students study a sync checklist from Apply-4 that shows a break and map the chain: the missed transition, what it forced, and the later slip. They name the one fix at the transition. Pairs compare chains and the Guide charts common trouble transitions.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how tempo, beat, and partner timing interact in one synced sequence.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page diagram linking the three factors to the sequence's success.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students draw a one-page diagram with their synced sequence in the center and tempo, beat, and partner timing around it. Arrows show where one factor changed another, such as a faster tempo widening the partner gap. Each arrow gets a label. Pairs add one missed arrow. The Guide collects the diagrams.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge which of two sequences better fits its music and defend the verdict.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student picks one sequence and cites two specific timing or style reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide shows two sequences set to the same track. Students judge which fits the music better and defend it with two reasons about timing or style match. They name one thing the weaker sequence did well. Pairs trade and score each other on a two-point rubric for evidence and fairness.

evaluate-4.objective: Assess your own synced sequence against a rubric and recommend the one change to rehearse next.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a recommendation naming one change with two reasons from the rubric.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students score their synced sequence on a posted rubric for beat, transitions, and partner sync, then write the one change to rehearse next. The recommendation gives two reasons tied to specific rubric rows. They name the moment that change would fix. The Guide sorts recommendations and reports common targets.

create-1.objective: Draft a four-count move that fits a given beat.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a four-count move in words and counts it out.
create-1.sampleTask: Students write a single four-count move in words, one action per count, that fits the class beat. They count it aloud once to a partner. Pairs check that each count has one clear action. The Guide collects strong four-counts to seed longer sequences.

create-2.objective: Produce an eight-count solo sequence notated so another student could perform it.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student turns in an eight-count notation a peer can read and try.
create-2.sampleTask: Students write an eight-count solo sequence using simple notation, one action per count, clear enough that a peer could perform it cold. A partner reads the notation and attempts the sequence without coaching. The writer fixes any count the partner could not follow. The Guide collects the cleaned notations.

create-3.objective: Design a partner sequence that blends two movement styles on one track.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a notated sequence that names where each style appears.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs design a sixteen-count sequence that blends two styles, such as aerobics and a folk step, on one track. The notation marks where each style appears and where the partners sync. The pair performs it for another pair, who names the smoothest blend point and the roughest. Pairs fix the rough point before the Guide collects.

create-4.objective: Construct a group routine for four people built around tempo and rhythm changes.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a routine plan marking formation, tempo changes, and counts.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups of four build a thirty-two-count routine that uses at least one tempo change and one formation change, tied to the music. The plan marks counts, formations, and where the tempo shifts. Groups perform for the class, which scores how clearly the routine used tempo and rhythm. The Guide keeps the highest-scoring routine plan as the class artifact.

2.2.8.MSC.4.standardStatement: Analyze, and correct movements and apply to refine movement skills.
2.2.8.MSC.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the form points the class uses to judge one movement skill.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists the posted form points for one skill on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide names one skill and posts its form points. Students copy the form points onto a card from memory first, then check against the posted list. Cards go up and students mark any point they missed. The Guide reads the full set so every student leaves with the checklist.

understand-1.objective: Describe what a movement error looks like for one form point.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence describing the error tied to a form point.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a half-sheet listing three form points. Students write what the error looks like when each point is missed, such as a dropped elbow on a throw. Pairs check that each description names what the body actually does. The Guide reads one clear error per point.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a named error reduces the success of a movement.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'because' sentence linking an error to a worse result.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student picks one error and writes 'this error hurts the skill because ...', naming a concrete result such as the throw losing accuracy. Pairs read and mark whether the result is concrete. The Guide charts the errors that hurt results most.

understand-3.objective: Classify observed errors as set-up, execution, or follow-through problems.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts eight error cards into the three phases on a mat.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide hands pairs eight error cards and a three-column mat labeled set-up, execution, and follow-through. Pairs place each error and write one card of their own for the thinnest column. Pairs compare with a neighbor pair and resolve splits. The Guide names the phase that held the most errors.

understand-4.objective: Connect a specific error to the correction cue that fixes it.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student matches four errors to four correction cues on a worksheet.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students get a worksheet with four errors on the left and six cues on the right, such as 'follow through to the target'. They draw lines from each error to its fix and cross out the extras. They write one sentence on the hardest match. Whole-class review settles it.

apply-1.objective: Spot one form error in a partner's live attempt and name it.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student names one real error their partner made during ten attempts.
apply-1.sampleTask: Pairs take turns performing one skill ten times while the other watches against the form-point chart. The watcher names the one clearest error they saw and points to which form point it broke. The performer says whether they felt it. Pairs switch and the Guide confirms two error calls.

apply-2.objective: Use a correction cue to fix a partner's named error on the next attempts.
apply-2.observableCheck: A partner tallies whether the error shrank over five cued attempts.
apply-2.sampleTask: Building on Apply-1, the watcher gives the matching cue from Understand-4. The performer does five more attempts with the cue while the watcher tallies whether the error shrank, stayed, or grew. The performer names what the cue made them change. Pairs switch and the Guide spot-checks tallies.

apply-3.objective: Apply self-correction by using a checklist to fix your own most common error.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student records error counts before and after self-cuing on one sheet.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students perform a skill five times, mark their most common error on a checklist, then perform five more while self-cuing the fix. They record error counts before and after on one sheet. They write one sentence on whether self-cuing worked. Pairs compare results and the Guide collects sheets.

apply-4.objective: Carry a corrected skill into a small-sided game and check whether the fix holds.
apply-4.observableCheck: A peer tallies whether the corrected form point held during game play.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students take their corrected skill into a short small-sided game while a peer tracks whether the fixed form point holds under game pressure. After play, the student names whether the old error returned and when. Pairs discuss one reason the fix did or did not hold. The Guide collects the game tallies.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the single error that appears most across a set of attempts.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student reads an error tally and names the most frequent error.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide displays a tally of errors collected across the class for one skill. Students name the most frequent error and the rarest. They write one sentence on why the top error is so common. Pairs compare and the Guide confirms the count.

analyze-2.objective: Compare a before clip to an after clip to determine whether the correction worked.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a two-column chart and states whether the error was fixed.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide shows a before and after clip of the same mover. Students fill a two-column chart noting the form point in each clip and circle whether the error was fixed, reduced, or unchanged. They write one sentence of evidence. Pairs compare verdicts and the Guide replays the key moment.

analyze-3.objective: Trace which earlier phase error caused a later breakdown in a movement.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the root-phase error and links it to the later breakdown.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students study a movement breakdown where a set-up error led to a follow-through failure. They name the root-phase error and map how it forced the later breakdown. They write the one fix that addresses the root, not the symptom. Pairs compare and the Guide charts root errors.

analyze-4.objective: Break down how several form points interact so that fixing one shifts another.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page diagram showing how form points affect each other.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Students draw a one-page diagram of one skill's form points with arrows showing where fixing one point changes another, such as a wider base steadying the throw. Each arrow gets a label. Pairs add one missed arrow. The Guide collects the diagrams as the analysis artifact.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge which of two corrections better fixed a movement and defend the choice.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student picks one correction and cites two pieces of evidence from the attempts.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide describes two corrections tried on the same error with their result tallies. Students pick the better correction and defend it with two pieces of evidence from the tallies. They name one case where the other correction might win. Pairs trade and score each other on a two-point rubric for evidence and fairness.

evaluate-4.objective: Assess your own correction record and recommend the next form point to target.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a recommendation naming one form point with two reasons from their data.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review their self-check and game tallies and write a short recommendation for the next form point to target. The recommendation gives two reasons drawn from specific counts. They name the setting where that error costs most. The Guide sorts recommendations by form point and reports the pattern.

create-1.objective: Draft one correction cue under six words for a common error.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one short cue tied to a named error.
create-1.sampleTask: Students pick one common error and write a single cue, under six words, that a teammate could say to fix it. They write it large on a quarter sheet. Pairs test whether the cue is short and clear enough to use mid-attempt. The Guide posts the cues by error.

create-2.objective: Produce a simple error-and-fix card for one skill that a peer could coach from.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student turns in a card pairing one error with its observable sign and its fix.
create-2.sampleTask: Students build a coaching card for one skill that names one error, the sign a watcher would see, and the cue that fixes it. A partner uses the card to coach a live attempt without extra help. The writer revises any part the partner could not use. The Guide collects the usable cards.

create-3.objective: Design a peer-feedback routine that catches and corrects errors during practice.
