remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt asking what signs might show a person has a substance use disorder. Students write four from the reading, such as needing more to feel the same effect or losing interest in usual activities. Cards go on a chart grouped into behavior, body, and mood signs. Guide reads the chart and adds any standard sign the class missed.

understand-1.objective: Describe in plain words what one sign of a substance disorder looks like.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student restates four signs in everyday language on a worksheet.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out a worksheet listing four clinical signs. Students rewrite each in everyday language describing what a person would notice. Pairs compare and confirm each restatement keeps the meaning.

understand-2.objective: Explain why one sign on its own does not mean a person has a disorder.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences explaining the difference between one sign and a pattern.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide explains that a disorder shows up as a pattern, not a single event. Students write two sentences explaining why one sign alone is not enough and what a pattern adds. Pairs check that the explanation names the idea of a pattern over time.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the main categories of signs: physical, behavioral, and emotional.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts nine signs into three categories on a chart.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a chart with three category headers. Students sort nine signs into physical, behavioral, and emotional. Whole-class review confirms each placement and adds one student example per category.

understand-4.objective: Explain how signs across categories together build a clearer picture than any one alone.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student connects three signs from different categories to one overall picture.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students pick three signs from different categories and write a short note explaining how, together, they paint a clearer picture of a possible disorder. Guide collects examples and reinforces that a fuller picture comes from patterns across categories. Class names why one category alone can mislead.

apply-1.objective: Identify signs of a disorder in a short case profile.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student underlines three signs in a profile and labels each category.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a short, age-appropriate case profile. Students underline three signs and label each as physical, behavioral, or emotional. Pairs compare underlines and point to the text for each.

apply-2.objective: Apply the sign categories to sort observations from a new case.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student tags a fresh case with at least one sign per category.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide presents a new case profile. Each student tags it with at least one physical, behavioral, and emotional sign, underlining the clue for each. Pairs compare tags and resolve differences using the text.

apply-3.objective: Use a sign checklist to summarize a case for a class discussion.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student completes a checklist and writes a two-sentence summary.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a sign checklist and a case profile. Students mark the signs present and write a two-sentence summary of the pattern they see. Pairs compare summaries and confirm the pattern is supported by the marked signs.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled summary chart of one case organized by sign category.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each pair produces a three-column chart with at least two signs per column.
apply-4.sampleTask: Pairs choose a case profile and build a three-column chart for physical, behavioral, and emotional signs, with at least two signs per column drawn from the text. Pairs post charts and the class checks each sign is supported by the profile.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which sign in a case is the strongest indicator of a disorder.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the strongest sign and cites the text clue.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a case with several signs. Students name the strongest indicator and cite the clue. Pairs compare and point to the text behind their choice.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two cases to determine which shows a clearer pattern of a disorder.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the clearer case with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read two short cases and decide which shows a clearer pattern, citing two details. Pairs discuss why a clearer pattern matters when deciding whether to seek help.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish signs of a disorder from ordinary teen behavior that is not a sign.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts behavior cards into sign and not-a-sign columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams behavior cards mixing real signs with ordinary teen behavior. Teams sort each into sign or not-a-sign and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a card that looks like a sign but may not be.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how signs in a detailed case developed over time into a pattern.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a timeline showing at least three signs and how the pattern grew.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a detailed case spanning several months. Teams build a timeline placing at least three signs in order and showing how the pattern grew clearer over time. Teams post timelines and the class marks the earliest point the pattern became hard to ignore.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue whether a case shows enough signs to suggest seeking help and defend the claim.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide presents one case. Students write a paragraph judging whether the signs suggest the person should seek help, giving two reasons and one reason for caution. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate a set of awareness materials and judge which most accurately lists signs.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three materials against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample awareness materials and a rubric scoring accuracy, clarity, and tone. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the wording that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any material that exaggerates or stigmatizes.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm changes a friend might notice when someone is struggling with substance use.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three observable changes to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'What changes might a friend notice in someone struggling?' Students name changes such as skipping practice or pulling away from friends. Guide records every idea on the board with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how noticing signs early could change what happens next.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if a friend noticed the signs early and spoke up, how would the situation change? The paragraph must name at least two changed outcomes. Pairs trade and check the changes follow from acting early.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page reference that groups common signs by category for peers.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a finished reference with at least two signs per category.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page reference grouping common signs into physical, behavioral, and emotional, with at least two signs per category. The reference must be accurate and written in plain language. Teams trade references and check each sign is correctly placed and clearly worded.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short awareness message that explains signs without stigma, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the message to a test audience and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups create a short awareness message that explains signs of a disorder in a caring, non-stigmatizing way. Groups deliver it to a test audience of classmates who rate accuracy and tone on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why before the final delivery.

2.3.8.DSDT.2.standardStatement: Compare and contrast the various services that are available for individuals affected by substance disorders in the community and at the state level.
2.3.8.DSDT.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name four services available to a person with a substance disorder, two local and two at the state level.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists four services on an index card, marking each local or state.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide displays the prompt: 'List four places or programs that help someone with a substance use problem.' Students write four on an index card and mark each as local or state. Cards go on a class board sorted into a local column and a state column.

understand-1.objective: Describe what one community service and one state service each do for a person with a substance disorder.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence per service naming who it serves and what it offers.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick one community service (such as a local counseling center) and one state service (such as a state helpline). Each student writes one sentence per service naming who it serves and what it offers. Pairs trade and check both sentences name a real form of help.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a person might choose a community service over a state service, or the reverse.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking a service feature to a person's need.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide gives short descriptions of a walk-in community clinic and a statewide phone line. Students write two sentences explaining when a person would pick each, linking a feature such as cost or distance to a need. Pairs share and the Guide records the clearest reasons on the board.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the range of services across community and state levels in a short paragraph.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a paragraph naming at least three services and what each provides.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students read a one-page list of community and state services. Each writes a paragraph summarizing the range, naming at least three services and what each provides. The paragraph must include at least one community service and one state service. Pairs check that both levels appear.

understand-4.objective: Explain how community and state services connect, so a person moves from one to another.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student draws a two-step path showing a referral from one service to another.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide explains that services often refer people to each other. Students draw a two-step path showing how a person could start at a local clinic and be referred to a state-funded program, labeling each step. Pairs compare paths and check each step names a real service and a reason for the referral.

apply-1.objective: Match four sample needs to the service that fits each best.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the matching service beside each of four need cards.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out four need cards, such as 'needs a free local meeting tonight' or 'wants a statewide number to call any hour.' Students write the matching service beside each card from the service list. Pairs compare and resolve any mismatch by pointing to the service feature that fits.

apply-2.objective: Use a service directory to find the right service for a described person.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair locates and records the service name, level, and contact type for one scenario.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair a short scenario about a person seeking help and a one-page service directory. Pairs locate a fitting service and record its name, whether it is community or state, and how to reach it. Pairs post their choice and the Guide checks the service matches the scenario.

apply-3.objective: Apply a two-column compare-and-contrast frame to one community service and one state service.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student fills a frame with at least two shared features and two differences.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students choose one community service and one state service from the directory. Using a compare-and-contrast frame, each student records at least two features the services share and two ways they differ, such as cost, hours, or who runs them. Pairs check that every entry is supported by the directory.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled resource card for a peer that lists three services by level and use.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a finished card naming three services, marking level and one-line use.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students design a pocket-size resource card a peer could keep. The card names three services, marks each as community or state, and gives a one-line use for each. Students trade cards and check each entry is accurate and clearly labeled by level.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the single most important difference between a chosen community and state service.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the key difference and cites the directory entry behind it.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives one community and one state service description. Students name the one difference that matters most for a person seeking help and cite the entry behind it. Pairs compare choices and point to the text that supports each.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two services to determine which better fits a specific person's situation.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the better-fit service with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read a short profile of a person and two service descriptions. Each decides which service fits better and gives two pieces of evidence from the descriptions. Pairs discuss why fit, not just availability, matters when someone reaches out for help.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish services that treat the disorder from services that provide support around it.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts service cards into treatment and support columns and defends two placements.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams service cards mixing treatment programs with support resources such as transportation or housing help. Teams sort each into treatment or support and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a card that could fit either column.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze gaps in a town's service list and identify a need that is not covered.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team marks one uncovered need and the evidence that the list misses it.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a sample town's full service list and a set of common needs. Teams check each need against the list and mark one need the town does not cover well. Teams post the gap and the evidence, and the class discusses how a state service might fill it.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which one service a town should add first and defend the choice.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment, two reasons, and one trade-off.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a town with three candidate services it could add. Students write a paragraph judging which to add first, giving two reasons and naming one trade-off. Volunteers share and the class weighs the strongest cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three service brochures and judge which most clearly helps a person take a next step.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three brochures against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample service brochures and a rubric scoring clarity, accuracy, and ease of next step. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the wording that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any brochure that promises more than a real service can offer.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm the kinds of help a person with a substance disorder might need beyond medical care.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three needs to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'Besides treatment, what does someone recovering from a substance problem need?' Students name needs such as a ride to appointments, a job, or a friend to check in. Guide records every idea on the board with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how a fuller set of services could change a person's chance of recovery.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if a town offered both treatment and support services together, how would a person's recovery change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes. Pairs trade and check the outcomes follow from the added services.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page community-and-state resource guide grouped by type of help.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a finished guide with at least two services per group and correct level labels.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page resource guide grouping services into treatment, counseling, and family support, with at least two services per group. Each entry is labeled community or state and written in plain language. Teams trade guides and check each service is correctly grouped and labeled.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short referral script that helps a peer reach the right service, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the script to a test listener and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups write a short script a peer could use to point a friend toward a fitting service, naming the service and the next step. Groups deliver the script to a test listener who rates clarity and accuracy on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why before a final read.

2.3.8.DSDT.3.standardStatement: Determine the impact that alcohol and drugs can have on an individual’s social, emotional, and physical well-being.
2.3.8.DSDT.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name two impacts of alcohol or drugs for each area: social, emotional, and physical.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes six impacts on a three-column chart before checking with a partner.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a blank three-column chart labeled social, emotional, and physical. Students write two impacts of alcohol or drugs in each column. Pairs compare charts and the Guide confirms a shared list on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe one physical impact of a substance in plain everyday terms.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a two-sentence description naming the body part and the effect.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick one physical impact, such as slower reaction time or liver strain. Each writes a two-sentence description naming the body part affected and the effect on daily life. Pairs trade and check the description names a real, accurate effect.

understand-2.objective: Explain how a substance can affect a person's mood or emotions.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a cause-and-effect sentence linking a substance to an emotional change.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews how substances change brain chemistry. Students write a cause-and-effect sentence linking a substance to an emotional change, such as a drug raising anxiety the next day. Pairs check that the sentence names a cause and a clear emotional effect.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how one substance can affect all three areas of well-being at once.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short paragraph naming a social, an emotional, and a physical effect.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students choose one substance and write a paragraph summarizing how it can touch social, emotional, and physical well-being. The paragraph must name one effect in each area. Pairs check all three areas appear and each effect is accurate.

understand-4.objective: Explain how one impact can lead to another across the three areas.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student draws a labeled chain linking at least three connected impacts.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide shows that impacts often chain, such as missed practice leading to lost friendships leading to low mood. Students draw a labeled chain of at least three connected impacts that crosses at least two areas. Pairs compare chains and check each link follows from the one before.

apply-1.objective: Sort eight described impacts into social, emotional, and physical categories.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places all eight impact cards into the correct column.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out eight impact cards describing real effects. Students place each in the social, emotional, or physical column on a sorting sheet. Pairs compare and resolve any disagreement by pointing to the wording on the card.

apply-2.objective: Use the three areas to map the impacts shown in a short case.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair lists at least one impact per area found in the case text.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair a short case about a teen using a substance. Pairs list at least one social, one emotional, and one physical impact they find in the text. Pairs post their map and the Guide checks each impact is supported by a line in the case.

apply-3.objective: Apply the impact chart to predict effects a substance could have on a described student.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes three predicted impacts, one per area, tied to the student's life.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students read a profile of a student with a busy life of sports, friends, and school. Each writes three predicted impacts, one social, one emotional, one physical, tied to that student's life. Pairs check each prediction connects to a real detail in the profile.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled diagram of one person showing impacts across all three areas.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a body-and-life diagram with at least two labeled impacts per area.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students draw a simple figure with a thought bubble and a friend circle around it. They label at least two physical impacts on the body, two emotional impacts in the bubble, and two social impacts in the circle. Students trade diagrams and check each label names an accurate impact in the right place.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which single impact in a case is most likely to spread to other areas.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the impact and cites the case clue.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a case listing several impacts. Students name the one impact most likely to spread to other areas and cite the clue. Pairs compare and point to the text behind their choice.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two cases to determine which shows wider impact across the three areas.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the wider-impact case with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read two short cases and decide which shows impact spread across more areas, citing two details. Pairs discuss why impact that crosses areas is harder to recover from than a single effect.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish a direct impact of a substance from a consequence that comes from a choice around it.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts impact cards into direct and indirect columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams cards mixing direct effects, such as slowed reflexes, with indirect consequences, such as a suspension for missing school. Teams sort each into direct or indirect and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a card that could fit either column.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how impacts in a detailed case built up over time across the three areas.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a timeline showing at least three impacts and how they accumulated.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a detailed case spanning several months. Teams build a timeline placing at least three impacts in order and showing how they accumulated across social, emotional, and physical areas. Teams post timelines and the class marks the point the impacts became hard to reverse.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which area of well-being a prevention message should focus on first, and defend it.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide asks which area, social, emotional, or physical, a prevention message for teens should lead with. Students write a paragraph naming their choice with two reasons drawn from the impact evidence. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three prevention posters and judge which most accurately shows real impacts.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three posters against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample prevention posters and a rubric scoring accuracy, clarity, and honesty. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the wording or image that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any poster that exaggerates or scares without facts.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm everyday signs that a substance is affecting someone's well-being.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three observable signs to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'What might you notice if a substance was hurting someone's life?' Students name signs such as falling grades, mood swings, or quitting a team. Guide records every idea on the board with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how removing a substance could change a person's well-being across areas.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if a person stopped using and got support, how would their social, emotional, and physical life change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes across different areas. Pairs trade and check the changes follow from stopping.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page guide that groups substance impacts by area for peers.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a finished guide with at least two impacts per area in plain language.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide grouping impacts into social, emotional, and physical, with at least two accurate impacts per area written in plain language. Teams trade guides and check each impact is correctly placed and clearly worded.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short message that explains real impacts honestly, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the message to a test audience and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups create a short message explaining the real impacts of a substance in an honest, non-scare way. Groups deliver it to a test audience of classmates who rate accuracy and tone on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why before final delivery.

2.3.8.DSDT.4.standardStatement: Examine how alcohol and drug disorders can impact the social, emotional, and physical lives of friends and family members.
2.3.8.DSDT.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name two ways a person's substance disorder can affect a friend or family member in each area: social, emotional, and physical.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes six effects on a three-column chart before checking with a partner.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a blank three-column chart labeled social, emotional, and physical. Students write two ways a loved one is affected in each column. Pairs compare and the Guide confirms a shared list on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe one emotional effect a substance disorder can have on a family member.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences naming the feeling and what causes it.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick one emotional effect on a family member, such as constant worry. Each writes two sentences naming the feeling and what in the situation causes it. Pairs trade and check the effect is realistic and clearly explained.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one person's substance use can change daily routines for the whole household.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a cause-and-effect sentence linking the use to a household change.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews how a disorder reshapes home life. Students write a cause-and-effect sentence linking one person's use to a change in the household, such as a sibling taking on extra chores. Pairs check the sentence names a cause and a clear household effect.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how a friend's well-being can be touched across all three areas.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming a social, an emotional, and a physical effect on a friend.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph summarizing how a close friend's social, emotional, and physical life can be affected when someone they care about has a substance disorder. The paragraph names one effect in each area. Pairs check all three areas appear and each effect is accurate.

understand-4.objective: Explain how the impact can ripple from the person to friends to family and back.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student draws a labeled ripple diagram with at least three connected effects.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide shows that impact ripples outward and back. Students draw a labeled ripple diagram showing at least three connected effects moving from the person to friends and family. Pairs compare and check each link follows from the one before.

apply-1.objective: Sort eight described effects on loved ones into social, emotional, and physical categories.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places all eight effect cards into the correct column.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out eight cards describing real effects on friends and family. Students place each in the social, emotional, or physical column on a sorting sheet. Pairs compare and resolve any disagreement by pointing to the card wording.

apply-2.objective: Use the three areas to map how a case affects the people around the person.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair lists at least one effect per area found in the case.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair a short case about a family living with a member's substance disorder. Pairs list at least one social, one emotional, and one physical effect on the people around that member. Pairs post their map and the Guide checks each effect is supported by the case.

apply-3.objective: Apply the effects chart to predict how a described teen's life changes when a parent has a disorder.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes three predicted effects, one per area, tied to the teen's life.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students read a profile of a teen whose parent has a substance disorder. Each writes three predicted effects on the teen, one social, one emotional, one physical, tied to details in the profile. Pairs check each prediction connects to a real detail.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled family map showing effects on each member across the three areas.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a map naming each member and at least one labeled effect per member.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students draw a simple map of a household and a friend. For each person they label at least one effect and mark whether it is social, emotional, or physical. Students trade maps and check each label names a realistic effect placed on the right person.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which family member in a case carries the heaviest impact.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the member and cites two clues from the case.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a case describing several family members. Students name the member carrying the heaviest impact and cite two clues. Pairs compare and point to the text behind each choice.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two cases to determine which shows greater impact on friends and family.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the greater-impact case with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read two short cases and decide which shows greater impact on the people around the person, citing two details. Pairs discuss why impact on loved ones can last even after the person gets help.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish an effect caused directly by the disorder from one a family member chooses in response.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts effect cards into caused and chosen-response columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams cards mixing direct effects, such as a missed family event, with chosen responses, such as a sibling joining a support group. Teams sort each and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a card that could fit either column.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how the impact on a family in a detailed case grew over time.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a timeline showing at least three effects on loved ones and how they grew.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a detailed case spanning a year. Teams build a timeline placing at least three effects on friends and family in order and showing how the impact grew. Teams post timelines and the class marks the point the family most needed outside support.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue what one form of support would help an affected family member most, and defend it.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a family member affected by a loved one's disorder. Students write a paragraph judging which one support, such as a support group or counseling, would help most, with two reasons. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three support resources for families and judge which best fits an affected teen.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three resources against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample support resources for families and a rubric scoring fit, clarity, and tone. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest for an affected teen and the feature that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any resource that blames the family.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm ways a friend can support someone whose family member has a substance disorder.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three supportive actions to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'How can you support a friend whose home is affected by a substance problem?' Students name actions such as listening, including them in plans, or pointing them to a counselor. Guide records every idea on the board with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how support for the family could change outcomes for everyone in the home.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if friends and family got support too, how would the whole home change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes for different people. Pairs trade and check the changes follow from the added support.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page guide for peers on how a substance disorder affects loved ones and how to help.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a finished guide grouping effects by area and listing two ways to help.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide grouping effects on loved ones into social, emotional, and physical, with at least one effect per area, and listing two supportive actions. The guide is written in plain, caring language. Teams trade guides and check each effect is correctly placed and each action is realistic.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short message that helps peers support an affected friend, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the message to a test audience and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups create a short message teaching peers how to support a friend whose family is affected by a substance disorder. Groups deliver it to a test audience of classmates who rate accuracy and tone on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why before final delivery.

2.3.8.DSDT.5.standardStatement: Compare and contrast the various services that are available for family members and others affected by substance disorders in the community and at the state level.
2.3.8.DSDT.5.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name four services for family members affected by a loved one's substance disorder, two local and two at the state level.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists four services on an index card, marking each local or state.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide displays the prompt: 'List four places or programs that help the family of someone with a substance problem.' Students write four on an index card and mark each local or state. Cards go on a class board sorted into local and state columns.

understand-1.objective: Describe what one family-support service does and who it serves.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences naming the service, who it serves, and what it offers.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick one family-support service, such as a peer support group for relatives. Each writes two sentences naming who it serves and what it offers. Pairs trade and check both sentences describe a real form of help for family members.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a family member might choose a community group over a state hotline, or the reverse.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking a service feature to a family need.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide gives descriptions of a local family support group and a statewide family helpline. Students write two sentences explaining when a family member would pick each, linking a feature such as privacy or in-person contact to a need. Pairs share and the Guide records the clearest reasons.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the range of family-support services across community and state levels.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming at least three services and what each provides.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students read a one-page list of community and state family-support services. Each writes a paragraph summarizing the range, naming at least three services and what each provides, with at least one at each level. Pairs check both levels appear.

understand-4.objective: Explain how a family member could move from one service to another through a referral.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student draws a two-step path showing a referral between two family-support services.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide explains that family services often refer people onward. Students draw a two-step path showing how a family member could start at a local group and be referred to a state program, labeling each step and the reason. Pairs compare and check each step names a real service.

apply-1.objective: Match four family needs to the family-support service that fits each best.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the matching service beside each of four need cards.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out four need cards, such as 'a sibling needs other teens who understand' or 'a parent wants a confidential number to call.' Students write the matching service beside each card. Pairs compare and resolve mismatches by pointing to the service feature that fits.

apply-2.objective: Use a directory to find the right family-support service for a described situation.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair records the service name, level, and contact type for one scenario.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair a scenario about a family seeking help and a one-page family-support directory. Pairs locate a fitting service and record its name, level, and how to reach it. Pairs post their choice and the Guide checks the service fits the scenario.

apply-3.objective: Apply a compare-and-contrast frame to one community and one state family-support service.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student fills a frame with at least two shared features and two differences.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students choose one community and one state family-support service from the directory. Using a compare-and-contrast frame, each records at least two shared features and two differences, such as cost, format, or who runs them. Pairs check every entry is supported by the directory.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled resource card for an affected family member listing three services by level.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a finished card naming three services, marking level and one-line use.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students design a pocket-size card a family member could keep. It names three services, marks each community or state, and gives a one-line use. Students trade cards and check each entry is accurate and clearly labeled.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the most important difference between a chosen community and state family-support service.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the key difference and cites the directory entry behind it.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives one community and one state family-support service. Students name the difference that matters most for a family seeking help and cite the entry. Pairs compare and point to the supporting text.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two family-support services to determine which better fits a specific family.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the better-fit service with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read a short family profile and two service descriptions. Each decides which service fits better and gives two pieces of evidence. Pairs discuss why fit matters when a whole family is affected.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish services that treat the affected person from services that support the family around them.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts service cards into person-focused and family-focused columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams cards mixing services for the person with services for the family. Teams sort each and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a card that could serve both.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze gaps in a town's family-support services and identify a need that is not covered.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team marks one uncovered family need and the evidence that the list misses it.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a sample town's family-support list and a set of common family needs. Teams check each need against the list and mark one the town does not cover well. Teams post the gap and evidence, and the class discusses how a state service might fill it.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which one family-support service a town should add first, and defend the choice.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment, two reasons, and one trade-off.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a town with three candidate family-support services. Students write a paragraph judging which to add first, giving two reasons and one trade-off. Volunteers share and the class weighs the strongest cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three family-support brochures and judge which most clearly guides a relative to a next step.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three brochures against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample family-support brochures and a rubric scoring clarity, accuracy, and ease of next step. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the wording that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any brochure that overpromises.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm the kinds of help a family of someone with a substance disorder might need.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three needs to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'What does a family living with a substance problem need?' Students name needs such as someone to talk to, childcare, or guidance on what to say. Guide records every idea with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how family-support services could change outcomes for an affected household.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if a family used both a local group and a state program, how would the household change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes. Pairs trade and check the changes follow from the added services.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page family-support resource guide grouped by type of help and level.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a finished guide with at least two services per group and correct level labels.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide grouping family-support services into peer support, counseling, and practical help, with at least two services per group. Each entry is labeled community or state in plain language. Teams trade guides and check grouping and labels.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short referral script that helps a peer point an affected friend's family to help, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the script to a test listener and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups write a short script a peer could use to point a friend's family toward a fitting service, naming the service and next step. Groups deliver it to a test listener who rates clarity and accuracy on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why.

2.3.8.HCDM.1.standardStatement: Justify how the use of universal precautions, sanitation and waste disposal, proper food handling and storage, and environmental controls can prevent diseases and health conditions.
2.3.8.HCDM.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the four prevention practices in this standard: universal precautions, sanitation and waste disposal, food handling and storage, and environmental controls.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists all four practices on a card before checking with a partner.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide displays the four prevention practices and removes them, then asks students to list all four from memory on an index card. Pairs compare lists and the Guide confirms the four on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe what one prevention practice involves in everyday terms.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences naming an action and the disease risk it lowers.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick one of the four practices, such as proper food storage. Each writes two sentences naming a real action and the disease risk it lowers. Pairs trade and check the action matches the practice and names a real risk.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one practice breaks the path a germ takes from source to person.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a cause-and-effect sentence showing where the practice stops the germ.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews how germs travel from a source to a person. Students write a cause-and-effect sentence showing how one practice, such as handwashing, stops a germ along that path. Pairs check the sentence names where the germ is blocked.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how the four practices together lower disease risk in a setting like a cafeteria.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming at least three practices and what each prevents there.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph about a school cafeteria, summarizing how at least three of the practices lower disease risk in that setting and what each prevents. Pairs check the paragraph names real actions tied to the cafeteria.

understand-4.objective: Explain why skipping one practice can undo the protection from the others.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences showing how one missed step lets disease through.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide poses a setting where three practices are followed but one is skipped. Students write two sentences explaining how the missed step lets disease through despite the others. Pairs compare and check the reasoning follows.

apply-1.objective: Match six described actions to the prevention practice each belongs to.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the matching practice beside each of six action cards.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out six action cards, such as 'wears gloves to handle a cut' or 'stores raw meat below ready food.' Students write the matching practice beside each. Pairs compare and resolve mismatches by pointing to the action wording.

apply-2.objective: Use a checklist to find prevention failures in a described kitchen or clinic.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair marks at least three failures and names the practice each violates.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair a scene description of a kitchen or clinic and a prevention checklist. Pairs mark at least three points where a practice is not followed and name the practice each violates. Pairs post findings and the Guide checks each against the scene.

apply-3.objective: Apply the four practices to design safe steps for one real task, such as serving lunch.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes an ordered step list covering at least three practices.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students choose a real task, such as serving lunch or cleaning up a spill. Each writes an ordered step list that builds in at least three of the four practices. Pairs check each step ties to a practice and the order makes sense.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled prevention poster for one practice that shows the right steps.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster naming the practice and showing at least three correct steps.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students design a poster for one practice, naming it and showing at least three correct steps in order with simple drawings. Students post and trade, checking each step is accurate and clearly shown.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which prevention practice matters most in a specific described setting.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the practice and cites the setting clue behind the choice.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a setting, such as a daycare. Students name the practice that matters most there and cite the clue. Pairs compare and point to the detail behind each choice.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two settings to determine which faces higher disease risk without prevention.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the higher-risk setting with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read two short setting descriptions and decide which faces higher disease risk if practices are skipped, citing two details. Pairs discuss why some settings need stricter prevention than others.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish a true universal precaution from a step that only looks protective.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts action cards into real-precaution and not-a-precaution columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams cards mixing real precautions with steps that look safe but are not, such as rinsing gloves to reuse them. Teams sort each and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a card that looks protective but is not.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how one prevention failure in a case led to a chain of disease spread.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a labeled chain from the failure to at least three downstream effects.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a case where one missed practice started an outbreak. Teams build a labeled chain from the failure to at least three downstream effects. Teams post chains and the class marks the point the spread could have been stopped.

evaluate-3.objective: Justify which prevention practice a school should invest in first, and defend it.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment, two reasons, and one trade-off.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a school choosing among the four practices to strengthen. Students write a paragraph justifying which to invest in first, with two reasons and one trade-off. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three sanitation plans and judge which best prevents disease in a setting.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three plans against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample sanitation plans for a cafeteria and a rubric scoring coverage, feasibility, and clarity. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the feature that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any plan that misses a key practice.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm everyday places where these prevention practices keep people from getting sick.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three places to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'Where do these practices quietly keep people healthy every day?' Students name places such as kitchens, pools, hospitals, and buses. Guide records every idea with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how adding one missing practice could change health in a described setting.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if a setting added one missing practice, how would health there change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes. Pairs trade and check the changes follow from the added practice.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page prevention guide grouping the four practices with example steps.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide with at least two example steps under each practice.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide grouping the four practices, each with at least two example steps in plain language. Teams trade guides and check each step is accurate and placed under the right practice.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short safety routine for one task and revise it after a trial.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group runs the routine in a mock trial and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups write a short safety routine for a real task, such as cleaning a shared surface, building in the relevant practices. Groups run a mock trial while classmates check each step on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why.

2.3.8.HCDM.2.standardStatement: Determine the role of genetics in being susceptible to disease and health conditions and identify the types of behavior that might reduce the risk factors.
2.3.8.HCDM.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name two health conditions with a known genetic risk and two behaviors that lower risk.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists two conditions and two behaviors on a card before checking with a partner.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide displays the prompt: 'Name two conditions that can run in families and two behaviors that lower the risk.' Students write them on a card. Pairs compare and the Guide confirms accurate examples on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe what it means for a condition to have a genetic risk factor.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences explaining genetic risk in plain terms.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students write two sentences explaining what it means that a condition can run in families, using the idea of inherited risk rather than certainty. Pairs trade and check the sentences say risk, not guarantee.

understand-2.objective: Explain how a behavior can lower the risk of a condition a person is genetically prone to.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a cause-and-effect sentence linking a behavior to lower risk.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews how genes and behavior both shape risk. Students write a cause-and-effect sentence linking a behavior, such as regular activity, to lower risk of a condition like heart disease. Pairs check the sentence names a behavior and a clear effect on risk.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how genes and behavior together shape one person's disease risk.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming one genetic factor and two behaviors that change risk.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph summarizing how genes and behavior together shape risk for one condition, naming one genetic factor and two behaviors that change the risk. Pairs check both genes and behavior appear and the claims are accurate.

understand-4.objective: Explain why two people with the same genetic risk can end up with different outcomes.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking the difference to behavior or environment.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide poses two people with the same family history but different habits. Students write two sentences explaining why their outcomes can differ, linking the difference to behavior or environment. Pairs compare and check the reasoning.

apply-1.objective: Sort eight factors into genetic risk and behavioral risk categories.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places all eight factor cards into the correct column.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out eight factor cards, such as 'family history of diabetes' or 'eats few vegetables.' Students place each in the genetic or behavioral column. Pairs compare and resolve any disagreement by pointing to the card wording.

apply-2.objective: Use a risk profile to identify which behaviors a described person could change.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair lists at least three changeable behaviors from the profile.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair a profile listing a person's family history and habits. Pairs list at least three behaviors the person could change to lower risk, leaving the genetic factors aside. Pairs post their list and the Guide checks each behavior is changeable and tied to risk.

apply-3.objective: Apply risk knowledge to write a behavior plan for a person with a given family history.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a plan with at least three behavior goals tied to the history.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students read a person's family history. Each writes a behavior plan with at least three goals that lower the risks tied to that history. Pairs check each goal connects to a real risk and is something the person controls.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled risk diagram separating fixed factors from factors a person can change.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a two-zone diagram with at least three items in each zone.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students draw a diagram with a fixed zone and a changeable zone. They place at least three genetic or fixed factors in one zone and three behaviors in the other for a chosen condition. Students trade diagrams and check each item is in the right zone.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which behavior in a profile would most lower the person's disease risk.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the behavior and cites the profile detail behind it.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a profile with several habits. Students name the one behavior change that would most lower risk and cite the detail. Pairs compare and point to the evidence.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two people to determine whose risk is shaped more by behavior than genes.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the person with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read two profiles and decide whose risk is driven more by behavior than by genes, citing two details. Pairs discuss why this matters for deciding what a person can change.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish a claim that genes cause a condition from a claim that genes raise its risk.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts statement cards into cause and risk-factor columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams statement cards, some overclaiming that genes cause a condition and some correctly naming raised risk. Teams sort each and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on an overclaiming card.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how behavior changed disease risk over time in a detailed case.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a timeline showing at least three behavior changes and their effect on risk.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a detailed case following a person with a family history over years. Teams build a timeline placing at least three behavior changes in order and noting the effect on risk at each. Teams post timelines and the class marks the change that mattered most.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which one behavior change a person with a strong family history should start first, and defend it.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a person with a strong family history of a condition. Students write a paragraph judging which behavior change to start first, with two reasons tied to the risk. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three health messages and judge which most accurately explains genes and behavior.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three messages against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample health messages and a rubric scoring accuracy, balance, and clarity. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the wording that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any message that says genes alone decide the outcome.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm habits teens can build now that lower future disease risk.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three habits to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'What habits started now could lower disease risk later?' Students name habits such as staying active, not vaping, and regular checkups. Guide records every idea with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how steady behavior changes could shift a person's risk despite family history.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if a person with a family history kept up healthy habits for years, how would their risk change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes. Pairs trade and check the changes follow from the habits.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page guide that pairs common genetic risks with behaviors that lower them.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide pairing at least three conditions with risk-lowering behaviors.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide pairing at least three conditions that carry genetic risk with behaviors that lower each, in plain language. Teams trade guides and check each pairing is accurate and the behavior is something a person controls.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short message urging a healthy habit for at-risk peers, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the message to a test audience and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups create a short message encouraging one risk-lowering habit without shaming anyone for their genes. Groups deliver it to a test audience who rate accuracy and tone on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why.

2.3.8.HCDM.3.standardStatement: Describe behaviors which may contribute to or prevent a person from being susceptible to disease and illness (e.g., cardiovascular, stroke, hepatitis, sexually transmitted infections (STIs), HIV/AIDS, breast cancer, HPV, testicular cancer).
2.3.8.HCDM.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name one behavior that raises risk and one that lowers risk for two of the listed conditions.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four behaviors on a card, marked raises or lowers, for two conditions.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide displays the prompt and a short condition list. Students pick two conditions and write one risk-raising and one risk-lowering behavior for each, marked on a card. Pairs compare and the Guide confirms accurate examples.

understand-1.objective: Describe how one behavior raises the risk of a specific condition.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences naming the behavior and the condition it affects.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick one risk-raising behavior, such as smoking. Each writes two sentences naming the behavior and a condition it raises risk for, such as stroke. Pairs trade and check the link is accurate.

understand-2.objective: Explain how a protective behavior lowers the risk of a specific condition.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a cause-and-effect sentence linking the behavior to lower risk.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews protective behaviors. Students write a cause-and-effect sentence linking a protective behavior, such as HPV vaccination, to lower risk of a condition. Pairs check the sentence names a behavior and a clear effect on risk.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the behaviors that raise and lower risk for one condition.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming at least two raising and two lowering behaviors.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students choose one condition and write a paragraph summarizing behaviors that raise and lower its risk, naming at least two of each. Pairs check the behaviors are accurate and matched to the condition.

understand-4.objective: Explain why one behavior can affect risk for more than one condition.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences naming the behavior and at least two conditions it touches.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide notes that some behaviors touch many conditions. Students write two sentences naming one behavior, such as not smoking, and at least two conditions it affects. Pairs compare and check both links are accurate.

apply-1.objective: Match eight behaviors to whether each raises or lowers disease risk.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student marks all eight behavior cards as raises or lowers.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out eight behavior cards. Students mark each as raising or lowering risk and name one condition it affects. Pairs compare and resolve disagreements by checking the fact sheet.

apply-2.objective: Use a fact sheet to identify the risk behaviors in a described person's routine.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair lists at least three risk behaviors and the condition each affects.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair a profile of a person's daily routine and a fact sheet. Pairs list at least three behaviors that raise risk and name the condition each affects. Pairs post the list and the Guide checks each against the profile.

apply-3.objective: Apply protective behaviors to revise a described person's routine to lower risk.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student rewrites the routine with at least three protective changes named.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students read a person's risky routine. Each rewrites it with at least three protective changes, naming the condition each change protects against. Pairs check each change is realistic and lowers a named risk.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled chart pairing behaviors with the conditions they raise or lower.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a chart with at least three behaviors, each linked to a condition and direction.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students build a chart pairing at least three behaviors with the conditions they affect and whether they raise or lower risk. Students trade charts and check each pairing is accurate and the direction is correct.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which behavior in a profile raises the most disease risk.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the behavior and cites the profile detail behind it.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a profile with several behaviors. Students name the one that raises the most risk and cite the detail. Pairs compare and point to the evidence.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two routines to determine which carries higher disease risk.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the higher-risk routine with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read two routines and decide which carries higher disease risk, citing two behaviors. Pairs discuss why small daily behaviors add up to large differences in risk.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish a behavior that prevents a disease from one that only treats it after.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts behavior cards into prevention and treatment columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams cards mixing prevention behaviors with treatment actions. Teams sort each into prevention or treatment and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a card that could look like either.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how behaviors over time shaped disease risk in a detailed case.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a timeline showing at least three behaviors and their effect on risk.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a detailed case following a person's behaviors over years. Teams build a timeline placing at least three behaviors in order and noting the effect on risk at each. Teams post timelines and the class marks the behavior that shifted risk most.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which one protective behavior gives teens the most benefit across conditions, and defend it.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide asks which single protective behavior helps teens most across the listed conditions. Students write a paragraph naming their choice with two reasons drawn from the evidence. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three health campaigns and judge which most accurately links behavior to disease risk.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three campaigns against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample health campaigns and a rubric scoring accuracy, clarity, and tone. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the wording that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any campaign that overstates or misstates risk.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm protective behaviors teens can start now to lower lifelong disease risk.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three behaviors to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'What can teens do now to lower disease risk for life?' Students name behaviors such as activity, vaccines, and avoiding tobacco. Guide records every idea with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how a set of protective behaviors could change a person's disease risk over a decade.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if a person kept up several protective behaviors for ten years, how would their disease risk change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes. Pairs trade and check the changes follow from the behaviors.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page guide pairing behaviors with the conditions they raise or lower.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide grouping at least four behaviors by effect with accurate condition links.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide grouping at least four behaviors into raises-risk and lowers-risk, each linked to a condition, in plain language. Teams trade guides and check each link is accurate and grouped correctly.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short message promoting one protective behavior, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the message to a test audience and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups create a short message promoting one protective behavior with accurate facts. Groups deliver it to a test audience who rate accuracy and tone on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why.

2.3.8.HCDM.4.standardStatement: Describe the signs, symptoms, and potential impacts of STIs (including HIV).
2.3.8.HCDM.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name three common signs or symptoms of STIs and one reason some show no symptoms.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists three signs and the no-symptom reason on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide displays the prompt using accurate medical terms. Students write three common signs or symptoms and note that some STIs show no symptoms at all. Pairs compare and the Guide confirms an accurate list on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe the difference between a sign and a symptom of an STI.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences giving one example of each.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students write two sentences explaining that a sign is something observed and a symptom is something felt, giving one accurate example of each. Pairs trade and check both examples fit the definitions.

understand-2.objective: Explain why many STIs can be present without obvious symptoms.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a cause-and-effect sentence linking no symptoms to delayed detection.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews that many STIs, including HIV, can be present without obvious symptoms. Students write a cause-and-effect sentence explaining how the lack of symptoms can delay detection and testing. Pairs check the sentence names cause and effect.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the potential health impacts of an untreated STI.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming at least three possible long-term impacts.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph summarizing potential impacts of an untreated STI, naming at least three accurate long-term effects such as ongoing infection or harm to future health. Pairs check the impacts are accurate and not exaggerated.

understand-4.objective: Explain why early testing changes the impact of an STI.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking early testing to better outcomes.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews that early testing and care change outcomes. Students write two sentences explaining how finding an STI early lowers its impact compared with finding it late. Pairs compare and check the reasoning.

apply-1.objective: Sort eight statements into accurate and inaccurate descriptions of STI signs and impacts.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student marks all eight statement cards accurate or inaccurate.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out eight statement cards about STI signs and impacts, some accurate and some myths. Students mark each accurate or inaccurate using the fact sheet. Pairs compare and resolve disagreements by checking the sheet.

apply-2.objective: Use a fact sheet to identify which described situations call for STI testing.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair marks at least three situations and names the reason testing fits.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair situation cards and a fact sheet. Pairs mark at least three situations where testing is the right next step and name the reason. Pairs post their choices and the Guide checks each against the sheet.

apply-3.objective: Apply accurate facts to correct three myths about STI signs and impacts.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student rewrites three myth statements into accurate ones.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students receive three common myths about STIs. Each rewrites all three into accurate statements using the fact sheet. Pairs check each correction is accurate and clearly worded.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled fact card on STI signs, symptoms, and impacts for a peer.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a card with at least two accurate points in each category.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students design a fact card with three sections: signs, symptoms, and impacts, each with at least two accurate points in plain language. Students trade cards and check each point is accurate and placed in the right section.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which fact about STIs is most important for prevention and explain why.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the fact and cites the reason it matters most.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a short fact set. Students name the fact most important for prevention, such as that many STIs show no symptoms, and explain why. Pairs compare and point to the reasoning.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two information sources to determine which describes STI impacts more accurately.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the more accurate source with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read two short sources about STI impacts, one accurate and one with errors. Each names the more accurate source and gives two pieces of evidence. Pairs discuss why accurate information matters for health decisions.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish an accurate STI fact from a stigmatizing or false claim.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts statement cards into fact and stigma-or-myth columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams cards mixing accurate facts with stigmatizing or false claims. Teams sort each and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a card that sounds factual but stigmatizes.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how delaying testing changed outcomes in a detailed case.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a timeline showing how impact grew while testing was delayed.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a detailed, non-graphic case where testing was delayed. Teams build a timeline showing how the potential impact grew over time and mark the point where earlier testing would have changed the outcome. Teams post timelines and compare.
