evaluate-3.objective: Argue what one message would most improve teen understanding of STIs, and defend it.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide asks what single accurate message would most improve teen understanding of STIs. Students write a paragraph naming their choice with two reasons. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three health resources and judge which most accurately and respectfully explains STIs.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three resources against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample health resources and a rubric scoring accuracy, clarity, and respect. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the wording that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any resource that is inaccurate or shaming.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm accurate questions a teen could ask a clinician about STI testing.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three questions to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'What could a teen ask a doctor or nurse about testing?' Students name questions such as what tests exist and whether results are private. Guide records every idea with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how routine testing could change STI impacts across a community.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if regular testing were common and stigma-free, how would STI impacts change for a community? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes. Pairs trade and check the changes follow from routine testing.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page accurate, respectful STI information sheet for peers.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a sheet with accurate sections on signs, impacts, and testing.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page information sheet with accurate, respectful sections on signs and symptoms, potential impacts, and where to get tested. Teams trade sheets and check each point is accurate and free of stigma.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short message encouraging testing without stigma, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the message to a test audience and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups create a short message encouraging STI testing in an accurate, non-stigmatizing way. Groups deliver it to a test audience who rate accuracy and tone on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why.

2.3.8.HCDM.6.standardStatement: Explain how the immune system fights disease.
2.3.8.HCDM.6.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name three parts of the immune system and what each does in one phrase.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student labels three parts on a diagram before checking with a partner.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a simple immune-system diagram with three blanks. Students label parts such as white blood cells, antibodies, and the skin barrier with a one-phrase job for each. Pairs compare and the Guide confirms the labels.

understand-1.objective: Describe what one part of the immune system does when a germ enters the body.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences naming the part and its action.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick one immune part, such as white blood cells. Each writes two sentences naming the part and what it does when a germ enters. Pairs trade and check the action is accurate.

understand-2.objective: Explain the order of steps the immune system takes to fight an infection.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes the steps in order showing cause and effect.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews the basic immune response. Students write the steps in order, from a germ entering to the body clearing it, showing how each step leads to the next. Pairs check the order and the cause-and-effect links.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how the immune system remembers a germ after the first infection.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph explaining immune memory and faster second response.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph summarizing how the immune system remembers a germ so it responds faster the second time, naming the role of memory cells. Pairs check the paragraph explains memory and a faster response.

understand-4.objective: Explain how a vaccine uses the immune system's memory to prevent disease.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking a vaccine to immune memory.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews how vaccines work. Students write two sentences explaining how a vaccine trains the immune system to remember a germ without causing the disease. Pairs compare and check the reasoning.

apply-1.objective: Order five immune-response steps into the correct sequence.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student arranges all five step cards in order.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out five step cards describing the immune response out of order. Students arrange them in the correct sequence on a strip. Pairs compare and resolve disagreements by checking the fact sheet.

apply-2.objective: Use the immune model to explain what happens in a described infection.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes a short account naming at least three immune actions in order.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair a scenario of a person catching a cold. Pairs write a short account naming at least three immune actions in order. Pairs post accounts and the Guide checks each action is accurate and in order.

apply-3.objective: Apply the immune model to predict how the body responds to a second exposure.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a prediction naming the role of memory cells.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students read about a person exposed to a germ a second time. Each writes a prediction of how the body responds faster, naming the role of memory cells. Pairs check the prediction uses immune memory correctly.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled flow diagram of the immune response to one germ.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a diagram with at least four labeled steps in order.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students draw a flow diagram tracing the immune response to one germ, with at least four labeled steps in order. Students trade diagrams and check each step is accurate and the arrows show the right order.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which immune part is most important in stopping a germ at the body's surface.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the part and cites the reason.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide describes a germ landing on the skin. Students name the immune part most important at that stage and cite the reason. Pairs compare and point to the reasoning.

analyze-2.objective: Compare a first and second exposure to the same germ to determine which response is faster.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the faster response with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read about a first and second exposure to the same germ. Each names which response is faster and gives two pieces of evidence about memory cells. Pairs discuss why this is the basis of immunity.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish an accurate statement about immunity from a common myth.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts statement cards into accurate and myth columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams cards mixing accurate immune facts with myths, such as that vaccines overload the system. Teams sort each and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a myth that sounds plausible.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how a weakened immune step in a case changed the body's ability to fight a germ.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a labeled chain from the weak step to its downstream effects.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a case where one immune step is weakened. Teams build a labeled chain showing how the weak step changes the body's response downstream. Teams post chains and the class marks where the response broke down.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which one habit best supports a strong immune system, and defend it.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide asks which single habit, such as sleep or vaccination, best supports immune function. Students write a paragraph naming their choice with two reasons drawn from the immune model. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three explanations of the immune system and judge which is most accurate and clear.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three explanations against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample explanations of immune defense and a rubric scoring accuracy, clarity, and completeness. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the feature that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any explanation with an error.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm everyday habits that help or hurt the immune system.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three habits to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'What helps or hurts your body's defenses?' Students name habits such as sleep, handwashing, and poor diet. Guide records every idea with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how a vaccine campaign could change disease spread in a school.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if most students were vaccinated against a germ, how would spread in the school change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes and uses immune memory. Pairs trade and check the reasoning.

create-3.objective: Construct a labeled model or comic that shows the immune system fighting a germ.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a model with at least four accurate, ordered steps.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams build a labeled model or comic showing the immune system fighting one germ, with at least four accurate steps in order. Teams trade models and check each step is accurate and clearly ordered.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short explainer that teaches a younger student how immunity works, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the explainer to a test listener and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups create a short explainer that teaches a younger student how the immune system fights disease, in plain words. Groups deliver it to a test listener who rates accuracy and clarity on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why.

2.3.8.HCDM.7.standardStatement: Explain how certain methods of disease prevention, treatment strategies, and appropriate medicine use promote health-enhancing behaviors.
2.3.8.HCDM.7.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name one example each of disease prevention, a treatment strategy, and appropriate medicine use.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one example in each of three boxes on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a card with three boxes labeled prevention, treatment, and medicine use. Students write one accurate example in each box. Pairs compare and the Guide confirms accurate examples on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe what appropriate medicine use means in plain terms.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences naming a rule of safe medicine use.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students write two sentences describing appropriate medicine use, naming a rule such as following the dose and finishing a prescription as directed. Pairs trade and check the rule is accurate.

understand-2.objective: Explain how following a treatment plan promotes a health-enhancing behavior.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a cause-and-effect sentence linking the plan to a healthy habit.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews how treatment plans build habits. Students write a cause-and-effect sentence linking following a plan, such as taking medicine on schedule, to a health-enhancing behavior. Pairs check the sentence names cause and effect.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how prevention, treatment, and medicine use work together for one condition.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming one example of each for the condition.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students choose one condition and write a paragraph summarizing how prevention, treatment, and appropriate medicine use work together, naming one accurate example of each. Pairs check all three appear and are accurate.

understand-4.objective: Explain why misusing medicine can undo the benefit of a treatment.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences showing how misuse harms the outcome.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide reviews risks of misuse. Students write two sentences explaining how skipping doses or sharing medicine can undo the benefit of treatment. Pairs compare and check the reasoning.

apply-1.objective: Sort eight actions into prevention, treatment, and medicine-use categories.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places all eight action cards into the correct column.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out eight action cards. Students place each into prevention, treatment, or medicine use on a sorting sheet. Pairs compare and resolve disagreements by checking the fact sheet.

apply-2.objective: Use a fact sheet to identify safe and unsafe medicine use in a described situation.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair marks at least three actions safe or unsafe with a reason.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair a scenario showing a person using medicine and a fact sheet. Pairs mark at least three actions safe or unsafe and give a reason for each. Pairs post findings and the Guide checks each against the sheet.

apply-3.objective: Apply safe-use rules to write directions for taking a sample medicine correctly.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes clear directions covering dose, timing, and one safety rule.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students read a sample medicine label. Each writes clear directions for a peer covering dose, timing, and one safety rule. Pairs check the directions match the label and include a safety rule.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled poster showing how prevention, treatment, and medicine use protect health.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster with at least one accurate example in each section.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students design a three-section poster on prevention, treatment, and appropriate medicine use, with at least one accurate example in each. Students trade posters and check each example fits its section.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which method in a case did the most to protect the person's health.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the method and cites the case clue.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a case using prevention, treatment, and medicine. Students name the method that did the most to protect health and cite the clue. Pairs compare and point to the evidence.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two patients to determine whose medicine use better supported recovery.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the patient with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read two short patient accounts and decide whose medicine use better supported recovery, citing two details. Pairs discuss why following directions changes outcomes.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish appropriate medicine use from misuse in a set of described actions.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts action cards into appropriate and misuse columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams cards mixing appropriate use with misuse, such as sharing a prescription. Teams sort each and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a card that looks fine but is misuse.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how a person's choices across prevention, treatment, and medicine use shaped their health over time.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a timeline showing at least three choices and their health effects.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a detailed case following a person's health choices over time. Teams build a timeline placing at least three choices in order and noting the health effect of each. Teams post timelines and the class marks the choice that mattered most.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which method does the most to promote health-enhancing behavior, and defend it.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide asks which method, prevention, treatment, or medicine use, does the most to promote healthy behavior. Students write a paragraph naming their choice with two reasons. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three medicine labels or instructions and judge which best supports safe use.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three labels against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample medicine labels or instruction sheets and a rubric scoring clarity, completeness, and safety. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the feature that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any label that could lead to misuse.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm health-enhancing habits that come from following prevention and treatment advice.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three habits to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'What good habits grow from following health advice?' Students name habits such as regular checkups, finishing prescriptions, and reading labels. Guide records every idea with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how good medicine habits could change a person's long-term health.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if a person always used medicine appropriately and followed prevention advice, how would their long-term health change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes. Pairs trade and check the reasoning.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page guide to safe medicine use and prevention habits for peers.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide with accurate sections on prevention, treatment, and medicine use.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide with accurate sections on prevention habits, following treatment, and safe medicine use, each with at least two plain-language tips. Teams trade guides and check each tip is accurate and placed correctly.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short message promoting safe medicine use, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the message to a test audience and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups create a short message promoting safe medicine use with accurate rules. Groups deliver it to a test audience who rate accuracy and clarity on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why.

2.3.8.HCDM.5.standardStatement: Compare and contrast behaviors, including abstinence, to determine the potential risk of pregnancy and/or STIs (including HIV) transmission.
2.3.8.HCDM.5.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name abstinence and two other behaviors, and state which carries the lowest risk of pregnancy and STIs.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists three behaviors and marks the lowest-risk one on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide displays the prompt using accurate medical terms. Students list abstinence and two other behaviors and mark which carries the lowest risk of pregnancy and STIs. Pairs compare and the Guide confirms that abstinence carries the lowest risk.

understand-1.objective: Describe what abstinence means and why it carries the lowest risk.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences defining abstinence and naming the risk it removes.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students write two sentences defining abstinence and explaining why it carries the lowest risk of pregnancy and STIs. Pairs trade and check the definition is accurate and the reason is clear.

understand-2.objective: Explain how risk of pregnancy and risk of STIs can differ between two behaviors.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences comparing the two kinds of risk for one behavior.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reviews that a behavior can carry different levels of pregnancy risk and STI risk. Students write two sentences comparing the two kinds of risk for one behavior. Pairs check both risks are named accurately.

understand-3.objective: Summarize how a set of behaviors ranks from lowest to highest risk.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph ordering at least three behaviors by risk with reasons.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students write a paragraph ordering at least three behaviors from lowest to highest risk of pregnancy and STIs, giving an accurate reason for the order. Pairs check the order and reasons match the fact sheet.

understand-4.objective: Explain why a behavior that lowers one risk may not lower the other.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences showing the gap between pregnancy and STI risk.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide poses a behavior that lowers pregnancy risk but not STI risk. Students write two sentences explaining why lowering one risk does not always lower the other. Pairs compare and check the reasoning.

apply-1.objective: Sort behavior cards by level of pregnancy and STI risk.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student places all behavior cards into low, medium, and high risk columns.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out behavior cards. Students place each into low, medium, or high risk columns on a sorting sheet using the fact sheet. Pairs compare and resolve disagreements by checking the sheet.

apply-2.objective: Use a fact sheet to rate the risk in a set of described situations.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each pair rates at least three situations and names the risk in each.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives each pair situation cards and a fact sheet. Pairs rate the pregnancy and STI risk in at least three situations and name the risk involved. Pairs post ratings and the Guide checks each against the sheet.

apply-3.objective: Apply accurate facts to correct three myths about risk and abstinence.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student rewrites three myth statements into accurate ones.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students receive three common myths about pregnancy and STI risk. Each rewrites all three into accurate statements using the fact sheet. Pairs check each correction is accurate and clearly worded.

apply-4.objective: Build a labeled risk ladder placing behaviors from lowest to highest risk.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a ladder with at least four behaviors placed accurately.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students build a risk ladder placing at least four behaviors from lowest to highest risk of pregnancy and STIs, with abstinence at the lowest rung. Students trade ladders and check each placement matches the fact sheet.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which behavior in a set most reduces both pregnancy and STI risk.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the behavior and cites the reason.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a set of behaviors. Students name the one that most reduces both pregnancy and STI risk and cite the reason. Pairs compare and point to the evidence.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two behaviors to determine which carries higher overall risk.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student names the higher-risk behavior with two pieces of evidence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students read descriptions of two behaviors and decide which carries higher overall risk of pregnancy and STIs, citing two facts. Pairs discuss why a behavior can be higher risk for one outcome and not the other.

analyze-3.objective: Distinguish an accurate risk claim from a myth about pregnancy or STI prevention.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each team sorts statement cards into accurate and myth columns and defends two.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives teams cards mixing accurate risk claims with myths. Teams sort each into accurate or myth and prepare to defend two placements. During share-out the Guide presses on a myth that sounds plausible.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how a decision in a non-graphic case changed the risk a person faced.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a labeled path showing how the decision changed risk.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide provides a non-graphic decision scenario. Teams build a labeled path showing how one decision changed the level of pregnancy and STI risk the person faced. Teams post paths and the class marks the point that most changed the risk.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue why abstinence is the most effective choice for avoiding both risks, and defend it with evidence.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a judgment and two evidence-based reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide asks students to make the case for abstinence as the most effective way to avoid both pregnancy and STIs. Students write a paragraph with a clear judgment and two evidence-based reasons. Volunteers share and the class weighs the cases.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate three prevention messages and judge which most accurately presents risk.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each team rates three messages against a rubric and reports the strongest with evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Teams receive three sample prevention messages and a rubric scoring accuracy, clarity, and respect. Teams rate each and write a note naming the strongest and the wording that earned the rating. Teams report out and the Guide flags any message that misstates risk.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm accurate questions a teen could ask a trusted adult or clinician about risk and prevention.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three questions to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks, 'What could a teen ask a trusted adult or clinician about staying safe?' Students name accurate questions. Guide records every idea with none rejected.

create-2.objective: Hypothesize how accurate risk knowledge could change decisions across a peer group.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-paragraph what-if naming at least two changed outcomes.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a what-if paragraph: if every teen had accurate risk knowledge, how would decisions across a peer group change? The paragraph names at least two changed outcomes. Pairs trade and check the reasoning.

create-3.objective: Construct a one-page accurate risk-comparison guide for peers.
create-3.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide ranking behaviors by risk with accurate notes.
create-3.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide ranking behaviors by risk of pregnancy and STIs, with abstinence as the lowest-risk choice and accurate notes for each. Teams trade guides and check each ranking and note is accurate.

create-4.objective: Develop and test a short accurate prevention message, then revise it.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group delivers the message to a test audience and submits one revision note.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups create a short message that presents risk accurately and respectfully and names abstinence as the most effective choice. Groups deliver it to a test audience who rate accuracy and tone on a feedback card. Each group revises once and notes what changed and why.

2.3.8.PS.1.standardStatement: Assess the degree of risk in a variety of situations, and identify strategies needed to reduce deliberate and non-deliberate injuries to self and others (e.g., digital safety, sexting, dating violence, domestic violence, gang violence, human trafficking, nonconsensual sexual encounters, other threats of violence).
2.3.8.PS.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name the difference between a deliberate injury and a non-deliberate injury and give one everyday example of each.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes both definitions and one example each on an index card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide shows plain-language definitions of deliberate and non-deliberate injury. Students copy each definition and write one everyday example of each on an index card. Students read examples aloud while the Guide sorts them into two columns on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe one factor that makes a situation higher risk than a similar safer one.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence description of a single risk factor.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a worked example: two similar situations and why one carries more risk. Students write a one-sentence description of a risk factor (being alone, no exit, a weapon or substance present) for a second pair. Partners read their sentences to each other and check that the factor is real.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one risk factor could lead to a specific injury, and name a strategy that breaks the chain.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a because-could-lead-to statement for two situations.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students take one digital and one in-person situation and write a cause-effect chain: the risk factor, the possible harm, and one strategy that breaks the chain. Pairs trade chains and check that each step is realistic. The Guide collects two strong chains to read aloud.

understand-3.objective: Explain why a chosen reduction strategy fits a high-risk situation, naming the risk factor it removes.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short explanation naming the strategy and the exact factor it lowers.
understand-3.sampleTask: Given a dating-violence or trafficking scenario summary, students choose a protective strategy (tell a trusted adult, leave with a plan, keep messages as evidence, set a code word) and explain why it fits, pointing to the specific risk factor it lowers. Pairs check that the strategy matches the factor named. The Guide collects one strong explanation to read aloud.

understand-4.objective: Generalize one personal-safety rule and show it working in an in-person, an online, and a money situation.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student states one rule and applies it correctly to all three contexts on one page.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students write one general rule (a private thing shared can be turned against you) and show it working in three places: an in-person threat, an online sextortion attempt, and a money scam. Three short applications on a single page. Pairs check that the same rule drives all three.

apply-1.objective: Apply a stop-name-exit rule to a single risky situation.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student walks one situation through the three steps in writing.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide teaches a three-step rule: stop, name the risk out loud or in your head, choose an exit. Students apply it to one given situation and write what each step looks like in that case. Two students share their exit step.

apply-2.objective: Select the correct help resource for each situation and complete the steps to reach it.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student fills a three-step action plan that ends at a named resource.
apply-2.sampleTask: Using a resource list (trusted adult, school counselor, hotline, 911), students take three situations and build a short action plan for each: first move, who to contact, what to say. They use the list to choose the right resource. Pairs check that each plan reaches a real source of help.

apply-3.objective: Plan a response to a situation where the safe choice and the easy choice pull in opposite directions.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a plan that names the conflict and a workable first move.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students get a scenario where a friend pressures them to cover for risky behavior. They plan a response that keeps both people safer, show their reasoning, and name one obstacle and how to get past it. Pairs read plans and flag any step that would raise the risk instead.

apply-4.objective: Devise a personal safety plan for a route or routine the student actually does.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page plan covering at least three risk points with a chosen strategy for each.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students map a real routine (walking home, riding the bus, being online after school) and mark three points where risk rises. For each point they choose a strategy from a menu and justify why that one over the others. The result is a one-page plan they could use this week.

analyze-1.objective: Identify from a teen-safety chart which situations the data marks as highest risk.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student reads three values off the chart correctly and names one pattern.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide provides a simple bar chart of teen injury or victimization by situation type. Students answer targeted questions: which situation is highest, which is lowest, and one pattern they notice. The Guide reviews answers so every student leaves with the correct reading.

analyze-2.objective: Categorize situations by type of risk and by who can help first.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts ten situation cards into a two-axis grid.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students sort situation cards into a grid: rows for risk type (digital, relationship, community), columns for first responder (self or peer, trusted adult, professional or 911). They pick two cards that landed in different cells and explain the difference. The Guide checks one full grid per pair.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two accounts of the same situation and draw a conclusion about which warning signs were missed.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student lists the missed warning signs and cites the line that shows each.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read two short first-person accounts (a teen and a bystander) of the lead-up to a harmful event. They compare what each noticed, conclude which warning signs were present early, and cite the sentence that shows each. Pairs compare their lists and settle any disagreement with the text.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several short sources to find the escalation pattern that all dangerous situations share.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team names the shared pattern and supports it with evidence from at least three sources.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Teams read three or four brief sources on how risky situations escalate (a dating-violence cycle graphic, a trafficking red-flags list, a gang-recruitment summary). They find the pattern common to all of them, isolation then control, and write it up with one piece of evidence from each source. Teams post their pattern statements for a gallery read.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which of two safety strategies better fits a scenario and defend the choice with evidence.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming the stronger strategy and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a scenario and two plausible strategies. Students pick the stronger one and write a short argument: two reasons it fits, one weakness of the other strategy, and the risk factor it best removes. Two students defend opposite picks so the class can weigh the reasons.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate the completeness of a sample safety plan against several risk sources and justify what to add.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student rates the plan and justifies at least two additions tied to sources.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students receive a sample peer safety plan plus the source packet from the analyze task. They judge whether the plan covers the patterns the sources name, then justify two specific additions, pointing to the source that shows each gap matters. The Guide collects the rated plans to check the justifications hold.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm situations in students' own lives where risk could rise without warning.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least three situations to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks where risk can sneak up: a new app, a new person, a ride home, a party. Students call out situations and the Guide records all of them on the board with no filtering. The list seeds the rest of the unit.

create-2.objective: Generate a conjecture about which warning sign shows up earliest in a risky situation.
create-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes one testable we-think-the-first-sign-is statement.
create-2.sampleTask: From the brainstorm, pairs pick one situation and write a conjecture about the earliest warning sign and why they think it comes first. They note what evidence would confirm or disprove it. Pairs post conjectures for the class to revisit after the analyze task.

create-3.objective: Develop a decision tree that routes any classmate from a risky moment to the right help.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a decision tree with at least four branches ending in a resource.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs build a one-page decision tree starting at the prompt something feels unsafe. Each yes or no question routes toward a strategy or a resource, with at least four endpoints. They test the tree on one brainstorm situation and fix any dead end. The Guide checks that every endpoint reaches real help.

create-4.objective: Design a one-page safety guide for incoming sixth graders that pulls from every source studied.
create-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide covering three risk types with one clear action for each.
create-4.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page safety guide for new sixth graders. It names three risk types from the unit, gives one clear action for each, and lists where to get help, drawing on the sources, the decision tree, and the plans built earlier. Teams swap guides and check each action is doable by a younger student.

2.3.8.PS.2.standardStatement: Define sexual consent and sexual agency.
2.3.8.PS.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Define sexual consent and sexual agency in plain words.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes both definitions on an exit card with one keyword each.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide presents plain-language definitions: consent is a clear, freely given yes, and agency is a person's right to make decisions about their own body. Students restate each in their own words on an index card and add one keyword that helps them remember it. A few keywords go on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe what asking for and giving consent looks and sounds like in an everyday non-romantic situation.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student lists two verbal and two nonverbal signs of a clear yes.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students brainstorm everyday consent moments such as borrowing a phone, a hug, or sharing food. They describe what asking and a clear yes look and sound like, listing two verbal and two nonverbal signals on a T-chart. Pairs compare charts and add one signal they missed.

understand-2.objective: Explain why silence, pressure, or impairment means consent is not present.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student explains for three cases why consent is missing.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students get three short cases (someone stays silent, someone is pressured, someone is impaired) and explain why each fails the definition of a freely given yes. Pairs compare reasons and agree on the clearest wording. The Guide collects one explanation per case to read aloud.

understand-3.objective: Explain how sexual agency gives each person the right to set and change boundaries, citing the definition.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a short explanation linking agency to one boundary example.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students read a boundary scenario and explain how agency gives each person the right to set a limit and to change it, citing the working definition. They name one respectful response to a changed limit. Pairs check that the explanation uses the definition, not just an opinion.

understand-4.objective: Generalize the consent rule and apply it to a digital, a physical, and a social situation.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student applies one consent rule across three contexts on one page.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students state one rule, that only a clear and freely given yes counts, and apply it to sharing a photo, physical touch, and posting about someone. They write three short applications on one page. Pairs check that the same rule drives all three and that none slips into a guess.

apply-1.objective: Apply an ask-listen-respect checklist to a single scenario.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student marks each checklist step for one scenario.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide teaches a three-step checklist: ask clearly, listen for a real yes or no, respect the answer. Students apply it to one everyday scenario and write what each step looks like there. Two students share the respect step.

apply-2.objective: Select the response that respects consent from several options and justify the pick.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student chooses and gives one reason for three items.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students work a short multiple-choice set where each item shows a situation and several responses. They pick the response that respects consent and agency and write one reason tied to the definition. Pairs compare picks and resolve any item where they differ.

apply-3.objective: Apply the consent definition to a non-routine scenario where words and body language conflict.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student names the safe action and explains why.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students get a scenario where a spoken yes does not match the other person's body language. They apply the rule that any unclear or pressured yes is not consent, decide the safe action, which is to stop and check in, and explain their reasoning. Pairs check that the action follows from the rule.

apply-4.objective: Devise a short script a peer can use to ask for, give, and decline consent across settings.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student writes usable lines for at least three settings.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students write a pocket script of lines for asking, for saying no, and for checking in, usable in physical, digital, and social settings. They test two lines with a partner and revise any that sound unclear or forced. The script is short enough to remember.

analyze-1.objective: Identify from a labeled diagram which examples show consent and which do not.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student labels each of six examples on the diagram.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a diagram with six brief examples arranged around a center term. Students label each one consent or not consent and note the single word that decided it. The Guide reviews answers so every student leaves with the correct labels.

analyze-2.objective: Categorize statements as respecting agency or overriding it.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts ten statement cards into two columns.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students sort statement cards, such as I changed my mind please stop versus you already said yes earlier, into respects agency and overrides agency. They pick two close calls and explain what tipped each one. The Guide checks one sorted set per pair.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two scenarios and draw a conclusion about where consent broke down, citing the moment.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the breakdown point and cites the line that shows it.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read two short scenarios and pinpoint the exact moment consent was assumed instead of asked. They conclude what should have happened and cite the line that shows the gap. Pairs compare the moments they marked and settle differences with the text.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several sources to find the features of consent that all of them share.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team lists the shared features with a quote from each source.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Teams read three short sources (a state definition, a school policy, a teen health page) and find the features all of them share, such as freely given, reversible, specific, and informed. They write the shared list with one quote from each source. Teams post their lists for a gallery read.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue whether a scenario meets the standard for consent and defend the judgment with the definition.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a clear judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a borderline scenario. Students judge whether consent is present, defend the call with two reasons from the definition, and name one fact that would change their answer. Two students who judged differently read their paragraphs so the class can weigh the reasons.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate a sample consent explanation written for younger teens and justify what to fix.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student rates the sample and justifies two changes tied to the definition.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a short sample explanation aimed at younger students, judge it against the multi-source definition, and justify two edits that make it more accurate or complete. They mark exactly where each edit goes. The Guide checks that each edit is tied to the definition, not to style alone.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm everyday moments where asking for consent matters.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds three moments to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm of daily consent moments such as photos, touch, sharing posts, and borrowing things. The Guide records every idea on the board with no filtering. The list seeds the visual and the guide built later in the unit.

create-2.objective: Generate a conjecture about which consent signal peers most often miss.
create-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes one we-think-peers-miss statement with a reason.
create-2.sampleTask: Pairs pick one signal from the T-chart and write a conjecture about why it is easy to miss, naming what evidence would confirm it. They post the conjecture and revisit it after the analyze task. The Guide reads two aloud to set up the next step.

create-3.objective: Develop a one-page visual that teaches the difference between consent and pressure.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a visual with at least three paired contrasts.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs design a one-page visual, a poster or infographic, contrasting consent and pressure with at least three paired examples and a one-line definition. They check that a younger student could read it without help. The Guide posts the strongest visuals in the room.

create-4.objective: Design a one-page consent guide for incoming students drawing on every source studied.
create-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide with the definition, signs of yes and no, and where to get help.
create-4.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide for new students that defines consent and agency, lists clear signs of yes and no, and names where to get help, pulling from the unit sources and visuals. Teams swap guides and check the definition matches the sources exactly. The Guide keeps a class set.

2.3.8.PS.3.standardStatement: Define interpersonal and sexual violence and describe their impacts on sexual health (e.g., sexual harassment, sexual assault, sexual abuse, incest, rape, domestic violence, coercion, dating violence).
2.3.8.PS.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Match the terms named in the standard to plain definitions of interpersonal and sexual violence.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student matches six terms to their definitions on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide presents plain, clinical definitions of interpersonal violence and sexual violence with a short glossary of the terms named in the standard. Students match six terms (harassment, abuse, coercion, dating violence, domestic violence, assault) to the correct definition. The Guide reviews matches so the glossary is accurate for everyone.

understand-1.objective: Describe who holds responsibility for an act of violence and who does not.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence placing responsibility on the person who chose to cause harm.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students read the definitions and write a one-sentence description making clear that responsibility for violence belongs to the person who commits it and never to the person harmed. Pairs check that the sentence states this plainly with no blame on the person harmed. The Guide reads two strong examples.

understand-2.objective: Explain how interpersonal or sexual violence can affect physical and emotional health.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student names two health impacts and explains the link.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students list two impacts on health (physical safety, ongoing stress, trouble sleeping, fear of certain places) and explain in general terms how the violence leads to each. Pairs compare lists and agree on the clearest wording. The Guide keeps the language non-graphic and focused on health.

understand-3.objective: Explain why naming a behavior correctly affects which help a person can get, citing the definitions.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student explains, for two cases, how the correct term points to the right resource.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students take two short cases and explain how naming the behavior accurately (harassment versus assault, teasing versus abuse) points to the right kind of help and report. They cite the definition that supports each naming. Pairs check that the resource fits the term chosen.

understand-4.objective: Generalize the marker that separates a healthy interaction from a harmful one and apply it across friendship, dating, and online contexts.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student states one marker and applies it correctly to all three contexts on one page.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students write one marker that separates healthy from harmful (respect for the other person's no) and show it working in a friendship, a dating situation, and an online exchange. Three short applications on one page. Pairs check that the same marker drives all three.

apply-1.objective: Apply a respect-safety-choice rule to one interaction.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student walks one interaction through the three checks in writing.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide teaches a three-check rule: is there respect, is everyone safe, is each person free to choose. Students apply it to one interaction and write what each check shows. Two students share which check failed in their case.

apply-2.objective: Select the correct term and the right resource for three short scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student names the term and the resource for each scenario.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three short, non-graphic scenarios and name the term that fits and the resource that helps (trusted adult, school counselor, hotline, 911). They write one reason the resource fits. Pairs check that the term matches the definition.

apply-3.objective: Apply the definitions to a non-routine scenario where harmful behavior is brushed off as a joke.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student names whether the behavior crosses a line and explains why.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students get a scenario where someone minimizes a harmful behavior by calling it a joke. They apply the definitions to decide whether it crosses into harassment or abuse and explain their reasoning. Pairs check that the call rests on the definition, not on whether anyone laughed.

apply-4.objective: Devise a get-help plan a peer could follow if they experienced or witnessed violence.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a plan with a first step, a person to tell, and a backup.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students build a short plan a peer could actually use: a first step, a trusted person to tell, what to say, and a backup if the first person is not available. They keep the steps simple enough to remember under stress. Pairs check that the plan reaches real help.

analyze-1.objective: Identify from a resource sheet which service matches which type of harm.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student links four harms to the correct service.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide provides a resource sheet listing services and what each handles. Students link four types of harm to the right service and note one detail that decided each match. The Guide reviews answers so the resource map is accurate.

analyze-2.objective: Categorize behaviors by type using the definitions.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts ten behavior cards into the correct categories.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students sort behavior cards into categories from the glossary using the definitions, not gut feeling. They pick two cards that were hard to place and explain what decided each. The Guide checks one sorted set per pair for accuracy.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two scenarios and draw a conclusion about which crosses from unhealthy to harmful, citing the line.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the crossing point and cites the line that shows it.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read two short scenarios and decide which one crosses from unhealthy into harmful. They conclude what marks the crossing and cite the line that shows it. Pairs compare the lines they marked and settle differences with the text.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several sources on impacts and find the through-line that all of them name.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team names the shared impact and supports it with evidence from each source.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Teams read three short sources on how violence affects health and find the impact that every source names, such as lasting stress or loss of a sense of safety. They write it up with one piece of evidence from each source. Teams post their through-line statements for a gallery read.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue whether a scenario should be reported and to whom, defending the call with the definitions.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a clear judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide presents a scenario. Students judge whether it should be reported and to whom, defend the call with two reasons from the definitions, and name one fact that would change their answer. Two students who judged differently read their paragraphs so the class can weigh the reasons.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate a sample help guide for accuracy and completeness against several sources and justify fixes.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student rates the guide and justifies two changes tied to sources.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a sample help guide written for teens, judge it against the source packet, and justify two changes that make it more accurate or complete. They mark exactly where each change goes. The Guide checks that each fix is tied to a source, not to style alone.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm signs that an interaction has crossed into harm.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds three signs to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks what signs show an interaction has crossed from unkind into harmful. Students call out signs and the Guide records all on the board with no filtering. The list seeds the resource map built later.

create-2.objective: Generate a conjecture about which warning sign peers dismiss most often.
create-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes one we-think-peers-dismiss statement with a reason.
create-2.sampleTask: Pairs pick one sign from the brainstorm and write a conjecture about why peers dismiss it, naming what evidence would confirm it. They post the conjecture to revisit after the analyze task. The Guide reads two aloud.

create-3.objective: Develop a one-page resource map that routes a peer to help by type of harm.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a map linking at least four harms to a resource.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs build a one-page resource map: a peer names what happened, and the map routes them to the right service for at least four types of harm. They test it on one scenario and fix any dead end. The Guide checks every route reaches real help.

create-4.objective: Design a respect-and-safety guide for younger students drawing on every source studied.
create-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a one-page guide naming healthy markers, warning signs, and where to get help.
create-4.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide for incoming students that names markers of a healthy interaction, warning signs of harm, and where to get help, drawing on the sources and the resource map. Teams swap guides and check each item is accurate and doable. The Guide keeps a class set.

2.3.8.PS.4.standardStatement: Describe strategies that sex traffickers/exploiters employ to recruit youth.
2.3.8.PS.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List recruitment red flags that exploiters use, from a vetted class list.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student names four red flags and adds one in their own words.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide presents a vetted red-flags list: false job or modeling offers, fast romance and expensive gifts, promises that seem too good, pressure to keep secrets, and moves to cut a young person off from family. Students copy four onto a card and write one more in their own words. A few go on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe what a recruitment red flag is and why it is a warning rather than a promise.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence description of a red flag in their own words.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students describe what a red flag is and why an exciting offer can still be a warning sign. They write one sentence in their own words and give a non-graphic example from the list. Pairs check that the description frames the flag as a reason to pause.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one tactic such as isolation, flattery, or secrecy sets up later control.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student explains for two tactics how the early move leads to control.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students take two tactics from the list and explain how each early move (showering with attention, pushing to keep secrets, pulling someone away from friends) sets up control later. Pairs compare explanations and agree on the clearest version. The Guide collects one per tactic.

understand-3.objective: Explain why an offer that seems too good often hides a cost, citing the red-flags list.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student explains one too-good offer and the hidden cost it points to.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students read a too-good-to-be-true offer and explain what hidden cost or control it likely hides, citing the red flag it matches. They name the safe next step, which is to check with a trusted adult before acting. Pairs check that the explanation rests on the list.

understand-4.objective: Generalize the lure-then-control pattern and show it in an online, an in-person, and a job-offer situation.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student applies the pattern correctly to all three situations on one page.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students state the pattern, attract first then control, and show it working in an online message, an in-person approach, and a fake job offer. Three short applications on one page. Pairs check that the same pattern drives all three.

apply-1.objective: Apply a pause-check-tell rule to one suspicious offer.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student walks one offer through the three steps in writing.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide teaches a three-step rule: pause before replying, check the offer against the red flags, tell a trusted adult. Students apply it to one suspicious offer and write what each step looks like. Two students share their tell step.

apply-2.objective: Select the safe response and the right adult for three recruitment scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student names a safe response and an adult to involve for each scenario.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three short scenarios and choose a safe response and the right adult to involve (parent or guardian, school counselor, trusted teacher, hotline). They write one reason each choice is safe. Pairs check that no response involves meeting or sharing more with the contact.

apply-3.objective: Apply the red-flags list to a non-routine online message that mixes kindness with pressure.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student marks the red flags in the message and names the safe action.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students read a sample online message that mixes compliments with pressure to keep a secret or meet up. They mark each line that matches a red flag and decide the safe action, which is to stop replying and tell an adult. Pairs compare the lines they marked.

apply-4.objective: Devise a protective checklist a peer could use before trusting a new online or job contact.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a checklist with at least five checks tied to red flags.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students build a short checklist a peer could run before trusting a new contact: verify with a known adult, never share location or private photos, watch for secrecy and rushing, and confirm any job through a real, known source. At least five checks, each tied to a red flag. Pairs test the checklist on one scenario.

analyze-1.objective: Identify in a sample message which lines are red flags.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks at least three red-flag lines correctly.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a short sample message. Students underline each line that matches a red flag and label which flag it is. The Guide reviews so every student leaves with the correct read.

analyze-2.objective: Categorize recruitment tactics by stage: attract, isolate, control.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts ten tactic cards into the three stages.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students sort tactic cards into three stages: attract, isolate, control. They pick two cards that could fit more than one stage and explain their choice. The Guide checks one sorted set per pair.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two recruitment stories and draw a conclusion about the shared first move, citing it.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the shared first move and cites the line in each story.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read two short, non-graphic recruitment stories and find the move both share at the start, often attention and false promises. They conclude why that first move works and cite the line in each story. Pairs compare and settle differences with the text.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several sources and find the tactics that every account shares.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team lists the shared tactics with evidence from each source.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Teams read three or four brief sources on recruitment (an awareness fact sheet, two short accounts, a red-flags graphic) and list the tactics common to all of them. They write the list with one piece of evidence from each source. Teams post their lists for a gallery read.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which red flag is the strongest early warning and defend the choice with evidence.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming the flag and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students pick the red flag they judge the strongest early warning and defend it with two reasons drawn from the sources, plus one weakness of relying on it alone. Two students defend different flags so the class can weigh the reasons. The Guide collects each paragraph to check the reasons hold.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate a sample awareness flyer against several sources and justify what to add.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student rates the flyer and justifies two additions tied to sources.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a sample awareness flyer, judge it against the source packet, and justify two additions that make it more complete or accurate. They mark where each addition goes. The Guide checks each addition is tied to a source.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm places and apps where a stranger could first make contact.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds three places or apps to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks where a stranger might first reach a young person. Students name places and apps and the Guide records all on the board. The list grounds the protective checklist built later.

create-2.objective: Generate a conjecture about which lure works on peers most and why.
create-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes one we-think-this-lure-works statement with a reason.
create-2.sampleTask: Pairs pick one lure from the list and write a conjecture about why it works on peers their age, naming what evidence would confirm it. They post the conjecture to revisit after the analyze task. The Guide reads two aloud.

create-3.objective: Develop a spot-the-red-flags poster from one source.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a poster showing at least four red flags with a one-line cue each.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs design a one-page poster that shows at least four red flags with a short cue a peer could remember. They use one source for accuracy and check a younger student could read it. The Guide posts the strongest ones.

create-4.objective: Design an awareness flyer for younger students drawing on every source studied.
create-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a flyer naming red flags, safe responses, and where to get help.
create-4.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page awareness flyer for incoming students that names red flags, gives safe responses, and lists where to get help, drawing on the sources and posters. Teams swap flyers and check each item is accurate and clear. The Guide keeps a class set.

2.3.8.PS.5.standardStatement: Determine the effectiveness of laws designed to keep children and adolescents healthy and safe (e.g., consent, child pornography, human trafficking, parental notification, drugs).
2.3.8.PS.5.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name what each law in a set of summaries is meant to protect.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student matches five law summaries to the protection each provides.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide provides plain-language summaries of laws that protect minors (consent age, child protection, anti-trafficking, parental notification, drug laws). Students match each summary to what it protects and copy one in their own words. The Guide reviews so the summaries are accurate for everyone.

understand-1.objective: Describe what one law is designed to prevent.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence description of what a chosen law prevents.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick one law summary and write a one-sentence description of the harm it is designed to prevent. They add one situation the law would cover. Pairs check the description matches the summary, not a guess.

understand-2.objective: Explain how a specific law protects a young person, using its summary.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student explains, for two laws, who is protected and how.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students take two law summaries and explain who each law protects and how, in plain words. Pairs compare explanations and fix any claim the summary does not support. The Guide collects one strong explanation per law.

understand-3.objective: Explain why a law alone does not stop harm without reporting and enforcement, citing the summary.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student explains one law and the action needed for it to work.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students explain why a protective law only helps when harm is reported and enforced, using one summary as the example. They name the action a young person or adult would need to take. Pairs check the explanation cites the summary.

understand-4.objective: Generalize how laws and personal strategies work together and apply it across three situations.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student shows a law plus a strategy working together in three situations on one page.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students state that laws set the rules and personal strategies keep a person safe in the moment, then show both working together in an online, a dating, and a substance situation. Three short applications on one page. Pairs check that each pairs a real law with a real strategy.

apply-1.objective: Apply one law summary to decide whether a situation breaks it.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student gives a yes or no with the summary line that supports it.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives one law summary and a clear situation. Students decide whether the situation breaks the law and point to the line in the summary that supports the call. Two students share their reasoning.

apply-2.objective: Select which law applies to each of three scenarios and name the protection.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student names the matching law and protection for each scenario.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios and select which law applies to each, naming the protection it provides. They write one reason for each match. Pairs check the matches against the summaries.

apply-3.objective: Apply a law summary to a non-routine case that sits at the edge of the rule.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student decides the case and names what fact would change the answer.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students get a case at the edge of a law, where one detail decides it. They apply the summary, decide, and name the single fact that would flip the answer. Pairs check the decision rests on the summary, not on opinion.

apply-4.objective: Devise a know-your-rights card a peer could carry, based on the summaries.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a card listing at least four protections in plain words.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students turn the summaries into a wallet-size know-your-rights card listing at least four protections and one place to get help, written so a peer could understand it fast. Pairs check each line matches a summary. The Guide collects the clearest cards.

analyze-1.objective: Identify from a summary table which law covers which harm.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student links four harms to the correct law.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide provides a summary table of laws and harms. Students link four harms to the law that covers each and note the column that decided it. The Guide reviews so the table read is accurate.

analyze-2.objective: Categorize protections by who enforces them.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts the laws by enforcer (school, police, courts, agencies).
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students sort the law summaries by who enforces each one: school, police, courts, or a state agency. They pick one law enforced by more than one body and explain. The Guide checks one sorted set per pair.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two laws and draw a conclusion about which better reaches online harm, citing the summaries.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the stronger law for online harm and cites the supporting line.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students compare two law summaries and conclude which better reaches harm that happens online. They cite the line in each summary that supports the conclusion. Pairs compare and settle differences with the summaries.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several sources to find where the laws leave gaps for teens.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team names one gap and supports it with evidence from the sources.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Teams read the law summaries plus two short sources on real teen risks and find one gap where a risk is not fully covered. They write the gap with evidence from the sources. Teams post their gap statements for a gallery read.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue how effective one law is at protecting teens, citing the summary and a scenario.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph rating the law with two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students pick one law and argue how well it protects teens, citing the summary and one scenario where it would or would not help. They give two reasons and one limit. Two students rate the same law differently so the class can weigh the reasons.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate the set of laws for completeness against real teen risks and justify one improvement.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student rates the set and justifies one improvement tied to a gap.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students judge whether the set of laws covers the real risks named in the sources, then justify one improvement that would close a gap, pointing to the evidence that shows the gap matters. They write the improvement as one clear change a lawmaker could make. The Guide checks the improvement targets a real gap, not a vague wish.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm situations where teens might not know a law protects them.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds three situations to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks where a teen might not realize a law is on their side. Students name situations and the Guide records all on the board. The list grounds the plain-language one-pager built later.

create-2.objective: Generate a conjecture about which protection teens know least.
create-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes one we-think-teens-know-least statement with a reason.
create-2.sampleTask: Pairs pick one protection and conjecture why teens know little about it, naming what evidence would confirm it. They post the conjecture to revisit after the analyze task. The Guide reads two aloud.

create-3.objective: Develop a plain-language one-pager that explains one law to peers.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a one-pager stating the law, who it protects, and how to use it.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs turn one law summary into a plain-language one-pager: what the law says, who it protects, and how a teen would use it. They check a peer could read it without help and that every claim matches the summary. The Guide posts the clearest ones.

create-4.objective: Design a know-your-rights guide for younger students drawing on all the summaries.
create-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide covering at least four protections and where to get help.
create-4.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page know-your-rights guide for incoming students covering at least four protections in plain words and where to get help, drawing on the summaries and one-pagers. Teams swap guides and check each line matches a summary. The Guide keeps a class set.

2.3.8.PS.6.standardStatement: Demonstrate strategies to use social media safely, legally, and respectfully (e.g., sexting, sextortion).
2.3.8.PS.6.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List strategies for using social media safely, legally, and respectfully, from a vetted class list.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student names four strategies and adds one in their own words.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide presents a vetted strategies list: set strong privacy settings, think before posting, never share private images, keep some things off social media, and block and report. Students copy four onto a card and write one more in their own words. A few go on the board.

understand-1.objective: Describe in plain words what a privacy setting does.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence description of one privacy setting.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick one privacy setting (who can see posts, who can message, location sharing) and describe in one sentence what it controls. They add who they would let in and who they would keep out. Pairs compare descriptions.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one post can spread beyond the people it was meant for.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student traces one post through at least three steps of spread.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students take one example post and trace how it could spread: screenshot, reshare, saved, sent on. They map at least three steps and name where control is lost. Pairs compare maps and add a step the other missed.

understand-3.objective: Explain why a private image, once sent, cannot be controlled, citing the strategy that prevents the risk.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student explains the loss of control and names the strategy that avoids it.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students explain why a private image cannot be pulled back once it is sent, and cite the strategy that prevents the risk, which is to never share it in the first place. They name one pressure that makes this hard and one way to resist it. Pairs check the explanation rests on the strategy.

understand-4.objective: Generalize a think-before-you-share rule and apply it to a photo, a comment, and a location.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student applies one rule correctly to all three on one page.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students state one rule, that anything posted can be saved and reshared, and apply it to sharing a photo, a comment, and a location. Three short applications on one page. Pairs check that the same rule drives all three.

apply-1.objective: Apply a privacy-settings checklist to one sample account.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student marks each checklist item for the sample account.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide provides a sample account profile and a privacy checklist. Students run the checklist, marking which settings are safe and which to change, and write the first change they would make. Two students share their first change.

apply-2.objective: Select the safe and respectful response for three posting scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student names a response and one reason for each scenario.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios (a friend tags an embarrassing photo, a stranger asks to chat, a group pressures a post) and select a safe, respectful response with one reason. Pairs check the response protects both safety and respect for others. The Guide reviews one response per scenario with the class.

apply-3.objective: Apply the strategies to a non-routine sextortion message and choose the safe action.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student names the safe steps in order for the message.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students read a sample sextortion message where someone threatens to share a private image unless the person sends more or pays. They apply the safe steps in order: stop, do not send anything more, do not pay, save the messages as evidence, and tell a trusted adult who can report it. Pairs check no step gives the sender more.

apply-4.objective: Devise a personal social-media safety setup the student could apply this week.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces a setup listing settings to change and habits to keep.
