apply-4.sampleTask: Students build a personal setup: three privacy settings to turn on, three habits to keep (think before posting, never share private images, report and block), and one person to ask for help. They write it as a checklist they could run this week. Pairs check each item is specific and doable.

analyze-1.objective: Identify in a sample profile which details are unsafe to share publicly.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks at least four unsafe details.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a sample public profile. Students mark each detail that is unsafe to share publicly (full address, school plus schedule, location tags, phone number) and note why. The Guide reviews so every student leaves with the correct read.

analyze-2.objective: Categorize posts by safe or risky and public or private.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts ten post cards into a two-axis grid.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students sort post cards into a grid: safe or risky on one axis, fine-for-public or keep-private on the other. They pick two close calls and explain what decided each. The Guide checks one sorted grid per pair.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two responses to a pressure-to-send message and draw a conclusion about which is safer, citing it.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the safer response and cites the line that makes it safer.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read two responses to a message pressuring someone to send a private photo. They conclude which response is safer and cite the exact words that make it so. Pairs compare and settle differences with the text.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several sources on sextortion and find the advice that all of them share.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team names the shared advice and supports it with evidence from each source.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Teams read three short sources on sextortion (a safety fact sheet, a reporting guide, a short account) and find the advice every source gives, often do not pay, save evidence, and tell an adult. They write it up with evidence from each source. Teams post their statements for a gallery read.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue which strategy best stops a sextortion attempt and defend the choice with evidence.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph naming the strategy and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students pick the strategy they judge best at stopping a sextortion attempt and defend it with two reasons from the sources, plus one limit of relying on it alone. Two students defend different strategies so the class can weigh the reasons. Each student turns in a one-paragraph defense.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate a sample digital-safety guide for completeness against several sources and justify additions.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student rates the guide and justifies two additions tied to sources.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students review a sample digital-safety guide, judge it against the source packet, and justify two additions that make it more complete or accurate. They mark where each goes. The Guide checks each addition is tied to a source.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm private things that should never be posted.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds three items to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks what should never go on social media. Students name items and the Guide records all on the board. The list grounds the safety setup built later.

create-2.objective: Generate a conjecture about which habit best protects privacy.
create-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes one we-think-this-habit-protects statement with a reason.
create-2.sampleTask: Pairs pick one habit and conjecture why it protects privacy best, naming what evidence would confirm it. They post the conjecture to revisit after the analyze task. The Guide reads two aloud.

create-3.objective: Develop a how-to card for setting up a safer account, from one source.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a card with at least four ordered setup steps.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs turn one source into a how-to card with at least four ordered steps for setting up a safer account. They test the steps on the sample profile and check a peer could follow them. The Guide posts the clearest cards.

create-4.objective: Design a digital-safety guide for younger students drawing on every source studied.
create-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide covering settings, habits, sextortion response, and where to get help.
create-4.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page digital-safety guide for incoming students covering privacy settings, daily habits, what to do about a sextortion threat, and where to get help, drawing on the sources and cards. Teams swap guides and check each step is accurate and doable. The Guide keeps a class set.

2.3.8.PS.7.standardStatement: Evaluate the impact of technology and social media on relationships (e.g., consent, communication, respect).
2.3.8.PS.7.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Recall the three lenses named in the standard and define each in a digital setting.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a short definition of consent, communication, and respect online.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide presents the three lenses for judging tech in relationships: consent, communication, and respect. Students write a short definition of each in a digital setting and give one example. The Guide reviews so the definitions are accurate for everyone.

understand-1.objective: Describe one way technology changes how people communicate in a relationship.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence description with an example.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students describe one way tech changes communication (faster replies, public posts, read receipts, group chats) in one sentence and give an example of how it could help or strain a relationship. Pairs compare descriptions. The Guide collects two clear examples to read aloud.

understand-2.objective: Explain how one digital habit can help or strain a relationship.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student explains one habit with both a help and a strain.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students pick one habit (constant texting, public posting, checking each other's status) and explain how it can build closeness and how it can cause strain. Pairs compare and agree on the clearest version. The Guide collects one strong example.

understand-3.objective: Explain why consent applies to sharing posts and photos about another person, citing an example.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student explains the consent rule for one sharing example.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students explain why a person should ask before posting a photo or detail about someone else, citing one example where skipping consent caused harm. They name the respectful move, which is to ask first. Pairs check the explanation uses the consent lens.

understand-4.objective: Generalize a respect rule and apply it to texting, posting, and group chats.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student applies one respect rule correctly to all three on one page.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students state one respect rule, treat the other person online as you would in person, and apply it to texting, posting, and a group chat. Three short applications on one page. Pairs check that the same rule drives all three.

apply-1.objective: Apply a respect check to one online exchange.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student marks whether the exchange passes a respect check and why.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide teaches a quick respect check: did each person get a say, was consent honored, was the tone respectful. Students apply it to one sample exchange and write what each part shows. Two students share their result.

apply-2.objective: Select the more respectful option for three digital-relationship scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student names the better option and one reason for each scenario.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios (a partner reads private messages, a group posts about a breakup, a friend shares a screenshot) and select the more respectful option with one reason tied to a lens. Pairs check the choice honors consent, communication, or respect. The Guide reviews one scenario with the class.

apply-3.objective: Apply the three lenses to a non-routine scenario where a partner posts without asking.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student names which lens was broken and the repair.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students get a scenario where one partner posts about the other without asking. They apply the three lenses, name which one was broken, and write a repair that restores it. Pairs check the repair fits the lens named.

apply-4.objective: Devise a set of group-chat or couple norms that protect consent, communication, and respect.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student produces at least three norms, one per lens.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students write at least three norms for a group chat or a couple, one for each lens: ask before sharing, say it directly not through others, keep it respectful even in conflict. They check each norm is specific enough to follow. Pairs trade and test the norms on one scenario.

analyze-1.objective: Identify in a sample chat which messages show respect and which do not.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student labels at least six messages.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a sample chat. Students label each message as respectful or not and note the word or move that decided it. The Guide reviews so every student leaves with the correct read.

analyze-2.objective: Categorize digital behaviors by their effect on a relationship.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts ten behavior cards into builds-trust or strains-trust.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students sort behavior cards into builds trust and strains trust, then pick two close calls and explain what decided each. Pairs compare their close calls with another pair. The Guide checks one sorted set per pair for fit with the lenses.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two relationships' tech habits and draw a conclusion about which supports respect, citing evidence.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the healthier set of habits and cites the evidence.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read two short profiles of how couples use tech and conclude which set of habits better supports respect and communication. They cite the detail in each profile that supports the conclusion. Pairs compare and settle differences with the text.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several short sources and find the shared finding about tech's impact on relationships.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each team names the shared finding and supports it with evidence from each source.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Teams read three short sources on tech and teen relationships and find the finding all of them share, such as that tech can speed up closeness and also pressure. They write it up with evidence from each source. Teams post their findings for a gallery read.

evaluate-3.objective: Argue whether a specific tech habit helps or harms a relationship, citing evidence.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a paragraph with a clear judgment and two reasons.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Guide names one common habit. Students judge whether it helps or harms a relationship, defend the call with two reasons from the sources, and name one case where the answer would flip. Two students who judged differently read their paragraphs so the class can weigh the reasons.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate the overall impact of social media on teen relationships using several sources and justify the judgment.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student rates the overall impact and justifies it with evidence from at least three sources.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Students weigh the helps and harms across the source packet and write a judgment about the overall impact of social media on teen relationships, justified with evidence from at least three sources. They name one thing that would strengthen the helps. The Guide checks the judgment rests on the sources.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm ways technology shows up in teen relationships.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds three ways to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Open brainstorm. Guide asks where tech shows up in relationships. Students name ways and the Guide records all on the board. The list grounds the healthy-habits agreement built later.

create-2.objective: Generate a conjecture about which habit most affects trust.
create-2.observableCheck: Each pair writes one we-think-this-habit-affects-trust statement with a reason.
create-2.sampleTask: Pairs pick one habit and conjecture how it most affects trust, naming what evidence would confirm it. They post the conjecture to revisit after the analyze task. The Guide reads two aloud.

create-3.objective: Develop a healthy-tech-habits agreement from one source.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces an agreement with at least four habits tied to the lenses.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs turn one source into a healthy-tech-habits agreement with at least four habits, each tied to consent, communication, or respect. They check a peer could follow it. The Guide posts the clearest agreements.

create-4.objective: Design a guide on respectful tech use in relationships for younger students, drawing on every source.
create-4.observableCheck: Each team produces a guide covering all three lenses with one habit each and where to get help.
create-4.sampleTask: Teams design a one-page guide for incoming students on respectful tech use in relationships, covering consent, communication, and respect with one clear habit each and where to get help, drawing on the sources and agreements. Teams swap guides and check each habit is doable. The Guide keeps a class set.

3-5-ETS1-1.standardStatement: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
3-5-ETS1-1.subject: science

remember-1.objective: Name the three parts of a defined design problem: the need or want, the criteria for success, and the constraints on materials, time, or cost.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student labels all three parts on a sample problem card without using notes.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide hands each student a card showing a worked design problem, such as designing a no-spill water bowl for a classroom pet. Students underline the need or want, circle the criteria for success, and box the constraints. Cards go up on the wall sorted by which part students found hardest to spot. Guide reviews two cards aloud and corrects any mislabeled part.

understand-1.objective: Restate a vague classroom complaint as a one-sentence need or want statement.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'someone needs ___ so that ___' sentence for one complaint.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide posts five vague complaints, such as 'the supply shelf is always a mess.' Each student picks one and rewrites it on a sentence strip using the frame 'someone needs ___ so that ___.' Strips post under the matching complaint. Pairs check that each statement names a person and a reason.

understand-2.objective: Explain how adding criteria and constraints turns a vague wish into a problem an engineer can solve, using one worked example.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences contrasting the vague wish with the defined version.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide shows 'make lunch better' beside a defined version that lists criteria (faster line, less waste) and constraints (no new staff, same budget). Students write two sentences explaining why the second version can be built and tested while the first cannot. Pairs trade papers and underline the sentence that best names the difference. Guide collects the slips as a check.

understand-3.objective: Explain, using two examples, why a design problem with no constraints produces solutions that cannot be fairly compared.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student gives a reason tied to a specific example in a short write-up.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students read two cases: one team told only 'build a bridge' and one told 'build a bridge from 30 straws that holds 10 coins.' Each student writes a short paragraph explaining why only the second team's solutions can be judged against each other. Students name the constraint that makes the comparison possible. Guide reads three paragraphs aloud.

understand-4.objective: Connect the criteria-and-constraints structure of a design problem to a non-engineering task such as planning a class party on a fixed budget.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student fills a two-column chart mapping party-planning choices to criteria and constraints.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide frames a class party with a forty dollar budget and a forty-five minute window. Students complete a two-column chart listing what counts as success (criteria) and what limits the plan (constraints). Each student writes one sentence saying how this matches the structure of an engineering design problem. Charts post for a gallery walk.

apply-1.objective: Fill in a template with the need, criteria, and constraints for a given playground scenario.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student completes all three template fields for the same scenario.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a scenario: kindergartners need shade at recess, the school can spend two hundred dollars, and the fix must go up in one weekend. Students complete a blank design-problem template with the need, the criteria, and the constraints pulled from the scenario. Pairs compare templates and fill any field left blank. Guide displays one completed template as the model.

apply-2.objective: Select fitting criteria and constraints from an option bank to assemble a complete problem statement for a stated need.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student builds a statement using at least two criteria and two constraints from the bank.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide states a need: students drop lunch trash on the way to the bin. Students pick from a bank of twelve cards (some criteria, some constraints, some neither) and assemble a complete problem statement on a worksheet. They reject the 'neither' cards and write why. Pairs check that no fake card slipped in.

apply-3.objective: Write a complete, original design problem statement for a classroom need the student chooses.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a statement naming the need, at least two criteria, and at least two constraints.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student picks a real classroom annoyance and drafts a full design problem statement: the need, the criteria for success, and the constraints on materials, time, or cost. Students keep the statement under four sentences. Partners read each draft and mark any part that is missing or untestable. Guide collects the drafts as the lesson artifact.

apply-4.objective: Define a design problem for a real school-wide issue after gathering input to set realistic constraints.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group records at least two pieces of input and uses them to set a constraint.
apply-4.sampleTask: Groups pick a school-wide issue, such as crowded hallways at dismissal. They interview two staff members or use posted facts to learn the real limits on time, space, and money. Each group writes a design problem statement whose constraints come from that input. Groups present the statement and name which constraint came from which source.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which part is missing from an incomplete design problem statement.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the missing part on each of four statements.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives four design problem statements, each missing one part: need, criteria, or constraint. Students label what each one is missing on a quick worksheet. Answers go on whiteboards for a fast show-and-check. Guide tallies which missing part tripped up the most students.

analyze-2.objective: Sort a mixed set of statements into needs, criteria, and constraints.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student places all twelve strips into the three correct piles.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Each pair gets an envelope of twelve statement strips drawn from one design problem. Students sort the strips into three labeled columns: need, criteria, constraints. They flag any strip they disagree on and resolve it. Guide reveals the answer key and pairs self-score.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two problem statements written for the same need and explain which is better defined, with evidence.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names two specific differences and cites them to back the choice.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives two statements for the same need, one tightly defined and one loose. Each student writes which is better defined and quotes two specific lines as evidence. Students must point to a constraint or criterion, not just say it is clearer. Pairs compare picks and settle any split.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several real product redesigns to infer the constraints the original engineers likely faced.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student lists at least two inferred constraints per product with a reason.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups examine before-and-after photos of three redesigned products, such as a spill-proof cup and a foldable scooter. For each, students infer the constraints the engineers worked under (cost, size, safety) and write the clue that points to each. Groups record inferences on a chart. The class compares charts to see which constraints showed up across all three products.

evaluate-3.objective: Critique a peer's design problem statement against a rubric and recommend specific fixes.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student gives a rubric score with one written fix for each weak row.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students swap design problem statements and score each other's against a three-row rubric: clear need, testable criteria, real constraints. For any row scored low, the reviewer writes one concrete fix. Authors read the feedback and revise one part on the spot. Guide collects both the original and the revision.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate competing problem definitions for a community issue and argue which one the class should pursue.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student takes a position and supports it with criteria-and-constraint reasoning.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide posts three different problem definitions for one community issue, such as litter in a local park. Students weigh each on how well it names a real need and sets workable constraints. Each student writes a short argument naming the definition the class should pursue and why the others fall short. Volunteers debate and the class votes.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm a list of classroom needs and wants that could each become a design problem.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds at least five items to a shared list.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide opens a five-minute brainstorm: what bugs you about this room or your day here? Each student writes at least five needs or wants on sticky notes, one per note. Notes go on a class chart with no judging allowed. The class clusters similar notes to spot the most common needs.

create-2.objective: Generate a draft problem statement with proposed criteria and constraints for one chosen need.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student drafts a statement that includes at least one criterion and one constraint.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student picks one need from the class brainstorm chart and drafts a problem statement, proposing at least one criterion for success and one constraint. Drafts stay rough; the goal is to get all parts on paper. Pairs read each other's drafts and suggest one more constraint. Students keep the draft for later revision.

create-3.objective: Develop a full design brief for one problem, naming the need, criteria, constraints, and a success measure.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a one-page brief with all four sections filled.
create-3.sampleTask: Students turn a draft statement into a one-page design brief with four sections: the need, the criteria for success, the constraints, and how the team will measure success. Each section must be specific enough that another team could build to it. Partners test the brief by trying to picture a solution from it alone. Guide collects the brief as the lesson product.

create-4.objective: Produce a design brief for a real client after gathering the client's requirements.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group records the client's stated needs and turns at least two into constraints.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups adopt a real client, such as a younger class or a staff member, and ask three set questions about what the client needs and what limits the solution. Groups write a design brief whose criteria and constraints come from the client's answers. Each brief notes which requirement came from the client. Groups present the brief and the client quote that drove the main constraint.

3-5-ETS1-2.standardStatement: Generate and compare multiple possible solutions to a problem based on how well each is likely to meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
3-5-ETS1-2.subject: science

remember-1.objective: List the two things every proposed solution must be judged against: the criteria for success and the constraints.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes both judging bases on a flashcard from memory.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide asks: when you have three possible solutions, what two things do you check each one against? Students write the criteria for success and the constraints on a flashcard. Cards are flashed to a partner who confirms both terms. Guide spot-checks five cards and clears up any mix-up between criteria and constraints.

understand-1.objective: Represent three given solutions to one problem as a quick labeled sketch each.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student produces three labeled sketches for the same problem.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide describes a problem, keeping a lunch cold until noon, and three solution ideas. Each student makes a quick labeled sketch of all three on a folded paper. Labels must name the key part of each idea. Partners check that the three sketches show genuinely different solutions.

understand-2.objective: Explain, for two solutions, how well each meets one named criterion and stays within one constraint.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a meets or does-not-meet note with a reason for each solution.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students take two sketched solutions, one criterion (stays cold to noon) and one constraint (costs under five dollars). For each solution they write whether it meets the criterion and the constraint, with a reason. Notes go in a two-by-two table. Pairs flag any solution rated without a reason.

understand-3.objective: Explain why comparing solutions requires applying the same criteria to each, using a worked case.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes why changing the test between solutions makes the comparison unfair.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide shows a comparison where one solution was judged on cost and another on speed. Students write a short explanation of why this is unfair and what would fix it. They name the rule: judge every solution against the same criteria. Guide reads two explanations aloud.

understand-4.objective: Relate comparing design solutions to choosing among options in daily life, such as picking a route to school.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student maps an everyday choice onto criteria and constraints in a short chart.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students pick an everyday choice with several options, such as routes to school or snacks to buy. They list the options, the criteria they use to choose, and the constraints. Each student writes one sentence noting that engineers compare solutions the same way. Charts post for a gallery walk.

apply-1.objective: Score three given solutions on a criteria checklist by marking yes or no for each row.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student completes the full checklist for all three solutions.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands a checklist with four criteria down the side and three solutions across the top. Students mark yes or no in each cell from a short description of each solution. They total the yes marks per solution. Pairs compare totals and resolve any cell they scored differently.

apply-2.objective: Select a comparison method, a scored table, and use it to rank three solutions for a stated problem.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a ranked order with the table that justifies it.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students build a scored table that rates three solutions on three criteria using a one-to-three scale. They add the scores and rank the solutions from best to worst. Each student writes the winning solution and its total. Partners check that the math and the ranking agree.

apply-3.objective: Compare student-generated solutions to a self-chosen problem and pick the strongest with criteria-based reasons.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student names the chosen solution and cites two criteria that drove the choice.
apply-3.sampleTask: Each student lists three of their own solutions to a problem from an earlier lesson and compares them on criteria they set. They pick the strongest and write two criteria-based reasons for the choice. Partners push back on the choice to test the reasoning. Students revise the reasons if the pushback lands.

apply-4.objective: Run a class trade-off study comparing several real solutions to a school problem and recommend one.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a recommendation backed by a completed trade-off table.
apply-4.sampleTask: Groups gather three real options for a school problem, such as ways to cut cafeteria noise. They build a trade-off table scoring each option on agreed criteria and noting each constraint. Each group recommends one option and presents the table that supports it. The class notes where trade-offs forced a hard call.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which of three solutions fails a given constraint.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the failing solution and names the constraint it breaks.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives three solutions and one constraint, must fit in a ten centimeter box. Students mark which solution breaks the constraint and write the constraint by name. Whiteboards show answers for a fast check. Guide confirms and explains the trap option.

analyze-2.objective: Organize the strengths and weaknesses of three solutions into a comparison chart.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student fills a strengths-and-weaknesses chart for all three.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Each student takes three described solutions and fills a chart with one strength and one weakness for each. Entries must tie to a criterion or constraint, not personal taste. Pairs swap charts and add one strength or weakness the partner missed. Guide collects the charts as a check.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two ranking outcomes for the same solutions and explain why the rankings differ, with evidence.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the criterion weighting that caused the difference.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide shows two teams that ranked the same three solutions differently. Students compare the two scoring tables and find what each team weighted most. Each student writes the reason the rankings split and quotes the rows that show it. Pairs confirm they found the same cause.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several real engineering trade-offs to infer which criteria mattered most to the designers.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student ranks the inferred priorities with evidence for the top one.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups study three real products that clearly traded one quality for another, such as a lighter bike that costs more. For each, students infer which criterion the designers ranked highest and cite the clue. Groups rank the inferred priorities across the products on a chart. The class debates any product where the priority is unclear.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a team's chosen solution truly fits its criteria and constraints, and defend the verdict.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student gives a fits-or-not verdict with two pieces of evidence.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students review a team's pick of a winning solution and its scoring table. Each student decides whether the pick really fits the criteria and constraints, then writes two pieces of evidence for the verdict. Students check whether any constraint was ignored. Guide collects verdicts and reveals the team's later result.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate competing recommendations for a school problem and argue which the school should fund.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student takes a position supported by trade-off evidence.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide presents three group recommendations for one school problem with their trade-off tables. Students weigh the recommendations on criteria and cost. Each student writes a short argument for which to fund and why the others lose out. The class holds a quick funding vote and tallies reasons.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm as many possible solutions as you can for one stated problem.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least six distinct solution ideas.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide states one problem, keeping markers from drying out. Students brainstorm at least six different solutions on a rapid list, no idea rejected. Lists go on the board and the class circles the most unusual ideas. The point is volume and variety, not quality yet.

create-2.objective: Generate two new solution variations by combining features of existing ideas.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces two combined solutions with the source ideas named.
create-2.sampleTask: Students take the class brainstorm list and combine features from two or more ideas into two new solutions. Each new solution names which ideas it borrows from. Pairs check that the combinations are actually new, not copies. Students sketch the stronger of their two.

create-3.objective: Develop a set of three distinct candidate solutions plus the criteria to compare them.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student submits three solutions and a matching criteria list.
create-3.sampleTask: Each student designs three genuinely different solutions to a chosen problem and writes the three criteria they will use to compare them. The criteria must be testable. Partners check that the three solutions are distinct and the criteria measurable. Students keep the set for a future comparison.

create-4.objective: Produce a comparison plan that generates solutions and lays out how to test them for a real need.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a plan listing solutions, criteria, and a test method.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups take a real need and produce a plan: at least three candidate solutions, the criteria for judging them, and how they will test each one fairly. The plan names the data they will collect. Groups present the plan and the class checks that the test would actually compare the solutions. Guide keeps plans for a build day.

3-5-ETS1-3.standardStatement: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.
3-5-ETS1-3.subject: science

remember-1.objective: Define fair test, variable, and failure point in one phrase each.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes a short definition of all three terms from memory.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the three terms: fair test, variable, failure point. Students write a one-phrase definition for each on a card. Cards are checked against a partner's. Guide gives the agreed class definition for any term students struggled to phrase.

understand-1.objective: Identify the one variable that should change in a described fair test.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student circles the changed variable and underlines one kept the same.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide describes a test comparing two paper-airplane wing shapes. Students read the setup, circle the one thing being changed, and underline two things kept the same. Answers go on whiteboards. Guide confirms which variable is the test variable.

understand-2.objective: Explain why keeping all but one variable the same makes a test fair, using the airplane example.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking controlled variables to fair results.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students take the paper-airplane setup and write why changing only the wing shape lets them trust the result. They name one thing that would ruin the test if it changed too. Pairs compare and pick the clearer explanation. Guide collects the slips as a check.

understand-3.objective: Explain how spotting failure points in a prototype tells you what to improve, using a worked case.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student names a failure point and the improvement it suggests.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide shows a paper tower that buckled at its base under a book. Students explain what the failure point reveals and what change it points to. Each student writes the failure-to-fix link in one or two sentences. Guide reads two and highlights cases where the fix matches the failure.

understand-4.objective: Relate fair testing to judging claims outside science, such as comparing two paper-towel brands.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student designs a fair comparison for an everyday claim in a short note.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students pick an everyday claim, such as one paper towel is more absorbent. They write how to test it fairly: what to change, what to keep the same. Each student notes the measurement that would mark the winner. Notes post for a gallery walk.

apply-1.objective: Follow a procedure to run one trial of a given fair test and record the result.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student records a measured result for one trial on the data sheet.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide provides a simple setup, ramps and toy cars, with a step list. Each student runs one trial following the steps and records the distance on a shared data sheet. Students must use the same release point each time. Guide checks that measurements use the same unit.

apply-2.objective: Set up a fair test by choosing which variable to change and which to control for a stated question.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student completes a plan naming the changed and controlled variables.
apply-2.sampleTask: Given the question 'which ramp height makes the car go farthest,' students complete a plan naming the one variable to change (height) and at least three to control (car, surface, release). They write the measurement they will take. Pairs check that nothing extra is changing. Guide approves plans before any testing.

apply-3.objective: Carry out a multi-trial fair test, identify a failure point, and propose a prototype improvement.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each group records three trials and writes one improvement tied to a failure point.
apply-3.sampleTask: Groups run three trials of a chosen test, keeping variables controlled, and record results. They spot where the design or the test broke down and write one improvement that targets that failure point. Each group revises the prototype or procedure on paper. Groups present the failure point and their fix.

apply-4.objective: Design and run a fair test across multiple variables to improve a prototype over two rounds.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group submits before-and-after data showing the effect of one change.
apply-4.sampleTask: Groups build a simple prototype, such as a parachute, and run a fair test, then change one variable and test again. They record both rounds and note which change improved performance and which failure point it fixed. Each group charts the before-and-after data. Groups present what they would change in a third round and why.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the uncontrolled variable that makes a described test unfair.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the variable that should have been held constant.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide describes a test where the tester changed both the ramp height and the car between trials. Students name the extra variable that ruins the comparison. Answers go on whiteboards. Guide explains why two changes at once hide the cause.

analyze-2.objective: Organize trial data into a table and identify the pattern across trials.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student records the trials and states the pattern in one sentence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students take a set of raw trial results and organize them into a clean table. They write one sentence naming the pattern, such as higher ramps gave longer rolls. Pairs check the table for misplaced numbers. Guide confirms the pattern with the class.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two test designs for the same question and explain which is more fair, with evidence.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names the controlled variables that make one design stronger.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives two test plans for the same question, one controlling more variables than the other. Students decide which is more fair and cite the controlled variables as evidence. Each student writes the weakness of the losing plan. Pairs confirm they chose the same plan for the same reason.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze multi-trial data to separate a real effect from random variation across several tests.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student marks which result is a true pattern and which is likely noise.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups receive data from several trials where one result swings oddly. Students decide which differences reflect the changed variable and which are likely random. Each student marks the outlier and explains why repeating trials matters. Groups compare calls and discuss how many trials make a result trustworthy.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a team's test was fair enough to trust its conclusion, and defend the call.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student gives a trust-or-not verdict with two pieces of evidence.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students review a team's test write-up and conclusion. Each student decides whether the test controlled variables well enough to trust the conclusion and writes two pieces of evidence. They check whether any failure point was ignored. Guide collects verdicts and shares the team's repeat result.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate competing improvement claims for a prototype and argue which change to keep.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student backs one change with test evidence and rejects another with a reason.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide presents three changes a class made to one prototype, each with its test data. Students weigh which change actually improved performance and which did not. Each student writes an argument to keep one change and drop another, citing the data. The class votes on the final design.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm ways a given test could go wrong or be unfair.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least five threats to a fair test.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide shows a test setup and asks: what could make this unfair? Students brainstorm at least five threats, such as a different starting push each time. Lists go on the board and the class groups similar threats. No threat is judged yet.

create-2.objective: Generate a fair-test plan for a question the student writes.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a plan with a question, one changed variable, and controls.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student writes a testable question and drafts a fair-test plan: the variable to change, the variables to control, and what to measure. Plans stay rough but complete. Partners check that only one variable changes. Students keep the plan for a build day.

create-3.objective: Develop a full test protocol that controls variables and defines how to read failure points.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student submits a protocol another team could follow without help.
create-3.sampleTask: Students turn a draft plan into a step-by-step protocol naming the controlled variables, the trials, and how a failure point will be recognized. Another student tries to follow the protocol on paper and flags any unclear step. The author fixes the gaps. Guide collects the protocol as the product.

create-4.objective: Produce a full test-and-improve cycle plan for a real prototype, including data collection and a redesign rule.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a plan covering test, failure analysis, and a redesign trigger.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups plan a complete test-and-improve cycle for a real prototype: how they will test fairly, what data they will collect, how they will spot failure points, and what result will trigger a redesign. The plan names the second-round change in advance. Groups present the plan and the class checks that the redesign rule is tied to data. Guide saves plans for build day.

5-ESS1-1.standardStatement: Support an argument that differences in the apparent brightness of the sun compared to other stars is due to their relative distances from Earth.
5-ESS1-1.subject: science

remember-1.objective: State that the sun is a star and that it looks brighter than other stars because it is much closer to Earth.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the closer-means-brighter fact in one sentence from memory.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide asks why the sun looks far brighter than the stars we see at night. Students write the key fact: the sun is a nearby star, and nearness makes it look brighter. Cards are checked with a partner. Guide corrects any answer that says the sun is hotter rather than closer.

understand-1.objective: Represent the closer-equals-brighter idea with a labeled diagram of Earth, the sun, and a distant star.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a diagram labeling which star is closer and which looks brighter.
understand-1.sampleTask: Each student draws Earth, the sun, and one distant star, labeling distances roughly and marking which appears brighter from Earth. Labels must show that the closer star looks brighter. Partners check that the diagram matches the rule. Guide displays one accurate diagram as the model.

understand-2.objective: Explain, using a flashlight demonstration, why the same light source looks dimmer from farther away.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes two sentences linking distance to apparent brightness.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide shines one flashlight while a student walks it farther from the class. Students write two sentences explaining why the same light looked dimmer as it moved away and tie this to stars. They name distance as the cause. Guide collects the explanations as a check.

understand-3.objective: Explain why a very bright distant star can look dim from Earth while the closer sun looks bright, using evidence.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student writes an explanation that separates true brightness from apparent brightness.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a fact card: some stars give off far more light than the sun yet look like faint dots. Students write why this happens, naming distance as the reason apparent brightness differs from true brightness. Each student uses the terms apparent and actual brightness. Guide reads two strong explanations aloud.

understand-4.objective: Relate the distance-and-brightness rule to an everyday case such as car headlights at different distances.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student maps the everyday case onto the star idea in a short note.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students pick an everyday light, such as headlights or streetlights seen near and far. They write how the near-and-far brightness change matches what happens with stars. Each student names distance as the shared cause. Notes post for a gallery walk.

apply-1.objective: Use a brightness-versus-distance data table to read off how bright a light appears at a stated distance.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student reads the correct apparent brightness for two given distances.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands a table of measured brightness at several distances from one bulb. Students read the apparent brightness at two stated distances and record them. They circle the trend direction. Pairs check that the readings match the table.

apply-2.objective: Use measured flashlight-brightness data to predict which of two stars at given distances looks brighter.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student makes a prediction and cites the data row that supports it.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students use a small data set linking distance to measured brightness to predict which of two equally bright stars, placed at different distances, looks brighter from Earth. Each student writes the prediction and the data that backs it. Partners check the reasoning. Guide confirms with the class.

apply-3.objective: Apply the distance rule to argue, with data, why the sun outshines a brighter but distant star.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim-evidence statement using the distance data.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students take data showing the sun's distance and a distant star's distance and true brightness. Each writes a claim that the sun looks brighter only because it is closer, backed by the distance numbers. The statement must use evidence, not just opinion. Partners check that the evidence supports the claim.

apply-4.objective: Build and defend a full argument, using gathered data, for why apparent brightness depends on distance.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a claim with at least two data sources and reasoning.
apply-4.sampleTask: Groups gather distance and brightness data for the sun and two named stars from provided cards. They build a claim, evidence, and reasoning argument that distance explains the brightness we see. Each group presents the argument with its data. The class checks that the reasoning links distance to apparent brightness.

analyze-1.objective: Identify, from a data table, which star is closest based on its apparent brightness.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the closest star and the brightness clue used.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a table of stars with equal true brightness and different apparent brightness. Students identify which is closest and name the clue. Answers go on whiteboards. Guide confirms that brightest-looking means closest when true brightness is equal.

analyze-2.objective: Organize star data into a chart and identify the pattern between distance and apparent brightness.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student states the distance-brightness pattern in one sentence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students take scrambled star data and organize it into a chart sorted by distance. They write one sentence naming the pattern between distance and apparent brightness. Pairs check the sort order. Guide confirms the inverse pattern with the class.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two stars with different true brightness and distances and explain which looks brighter, with evidence.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student weighs both factors and cites the data for the call.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives two stars: one brighter but far, one dimmer but near. Students decide which looks brighter from Earth and explain how distance and true brightness combine. Each student cites the data for the call. Pairs compare and resolve any split.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze a multi-star data set to separate the effect of distance from the effect of true brightness.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student sorts stars by which factor best explains their apparent brightness.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups receive data on several stars with varied distances and true brightness. Students sort the stars by whether distance or true brightness best explains how bright each looks. Each student flags the star where the two factors pull in opposite directions. Groups compare sorts and discuss the tricky cases.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a given argument about star brightness is supported by its evidence, and defend the verdict.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student gives a supported-or-not verdict with two pieces of evidence.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a peer argument claiming a star is bright because it is hot, not close. Each student judges whether the evidence supports the claim and writes two reasons. They check whether distance data was ignored. Guide collects verdicts and clarifies the misconception.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate competing explanations for one star's apparent brightness and argue which the data supports.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student backs one explanation with data and rejects another with a reason.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide posts three explanations for why a named star looks the way it does. Students weigh each against the distance and brightness data. Each student writes an argument for the best-supported explanation and against the weakest. The class votes and tallies the deciding evidence.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm reasons a star might look bright or dim from Earth.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least four possible reasons.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks what could make a star look bright or dim to us. Students brainstorm at least four reasons on a list, such as distance, size, or true brightness. Lists go on the board and the class circles distance for later testing. No reason is judged yet.

create-2.objective: Generate a model or sketch that shows how distance changes a star's apparent brightness.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a labeled model showing two stars at different distances.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student designs a simple model, drawing or using two bulbs, that shows the same light looking dimmer when farther away. Labels must mark distance and apparent brightness. Partners check that the model makes the distance effect clear. Students keep the model for the argument lesson.

create-3.objective: Develop a complete argument poster linking distance to the sun's apparent brightness, with evidence.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student submits a poster with a claim, evidence, and a diagram.
create-3.sampleTask: Students build a one-page poster arguing that the sun looks brightest because it is closest, including a claim, distance data, and a labeled diagram. The poster must let a reader follow the reasoning. Partners check that the evidence backs the claim. Guide collects posters as the product.

create-4.objective: Produce a full investigation plan that gathers brightness-and-distance data to support an argument about stars.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a plan naming the data, the model, and the argument it will support.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups plan an investigation using a flashlight to gather brightness-at-distance data and connect it to stars. The plan names the measurements, the model, and the claim the data will support. Groups present the plan and the class checks that the data would actually test the distance idea. Guide saves plans for a lab day.

5-ESS1-2.standardStatement: Represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of daily changes in length and direction of shadows, day and night, and the seasonal appearance of some stars in the night sky.
5-ESS1-2.subject: science

remember-1.objective: Name the three sky patterns to graph: daily shadow length and direction, the day-night cycle, and which stars appear in each season.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists all three patterns from memory on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide asks what sky patterns we can track over time. Students write the three on a card: shadow changes through the day, day and night, and seasonal star changes. Partners compare cards. Guide names any pattern a student missed.

understand-1.objective: Plot a small set of shadow-length readings on a labeled line graph.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student plots all given points with axes labeled.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a table of shadow lengths measured every hour. Each student plots the points with time on the x-axis and length on the y-axis. Axes must be labeled with units. Partners check that points land in the right spots.

understand-2.objective: Describe the daily shadow pattern shown on a finished graph in one or two sentences.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student names when shadows are longest and shortest from the graph.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students read a completed shadow-length graph and write when shadows are longest and shortest and how direction shifts. They name the midday low point. Pairs compare descriptions. Guide confirms the pattern.

understand-3.objective: Explain what a seasonal star chart reveals about why some stars appear only in certain months, using evidence from the display.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student cites a chart feature to back the explanation.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a chart of which constellations are visible each month. Students explain why some stars show up only in winter or summer, pointing to the chart. Each student cites one row as evidence. Guide reads two explanations aloud.

understand-4.objective: Relate graphing sky patterns to using a graph to track a daily-life pattern such as temperature over a week.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names a daily-life pattern a graph would reveal and sketches the axes.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students pick a daily-life pattern, such as outdoor temperature or bedtime over a week. They sketch the axes a graph would need and write what pattern it would reveal. Each student notes that sky data works the same way. Sketches post for a gallery walk.

apply-1.objective: Read a value off a shadow or day-length graph at a stated time.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student reads two correct values from the graph.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands a day-length graph across the year. Students read the day length for two stated months and record them. They circle the longest day. Pairs check the readings against the graph.

apply-2.objective: Choose the right graph type and plot a given sky data set to show its pattern.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student builds a graph whose type fits the data and shows the pattern.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students get a sky data set, such as monthly sunset times, and choose a line graph to plot it. They label axes and plot the points. Each student writes the pattern the graph reveals. Partners check that the graph type fits the data.

apply-3.objective: Use a self-made graph of collected shadow data to identify and describe the daily pattern.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student marks the pattern on their own graph and writes one sentence about it.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students plot shadow data they collected, or a provided proxy set, over a school day. Each student marks the longest and shortest shadows on the graph and writes the pattern in one sentence. Partners compare patterns. Guide checks that the marks match the data.

apply-4.objective: Build a multi-pattern display combining two sky data sets and use it to explain a connection.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a display linking two patterns with a written connection.
apply-4.sampleTask: Groups graph two related sky data sets, such as day length and shadow length across seasons. They use the paired display to explain how the patterns connect. Each group writes the connection in two sentences. Groups present the display and the link they found.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the high and low points on a sky-pattern graph.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the peak and trough on the graph.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a graph of star visibility or day length across the year. Students mark the highest and lowest points. Answers go on whiteboards. Guide confirms the peak and trough.

analyze-2.objective: Organize raw shadow readings into a graph and identify the trend.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student plots the data and states the trend in one sentence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students take scrambled hourly shadow readings and plot them in time order. They write one sentence naming the trend through the day. Pairs check the plot order. Guide confirms the U-shaped pattern.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two students' graphs of the same data and explain which displays the pattern more clearly, with evidence.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names a graphing choice that makes one display clearer.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives two graphs of the same shadow data, one with messy scaling. Students decide which shows the pattern more clearly and cite the graphing choice that helps, such as even spacing. Each student names the flaw in the weaker graph. Pairs confirm the call.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze several sky-pattern graphs to infer the cause they share, such as Earth's motion.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student links at least two graphs to one underlying cause.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups study graphs of shadows, day-night, and seasonal stars. Students infer the shared cause behind the patterns and write the clue from each graph. Each student links two graphs to Earth's motion. Groups compare inferences and discuss the connection.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a graph fairly represents its sky data, and defend the verdict.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student gives a fair-or-not verdict with two pieces of evidence.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students review a graph with a misleading axis scale. Each student decides whether it fairly shows the data and writes two reasons. They check the axis labels and spacing. Guide collects verdicts and shows the corrected graph.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate competing graphs made from the same data and argue which best reveals the pattern.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student backs one graph with evidence and rejects another with a reason.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide posts three graphs of one star-visibility data set. Students weigh which best reveals the seasonal pattern. Each student writes an argument for the clearest graph and against the weakest, citing graphing choices. The class votes.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm sky patterns that could be tracked and graphed over time.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least four trackable sky patterns.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks what in the sky changes in a pattern we could graph. Students brainstorm at least four, such as moon shape, sunset time, or shadow length. Lists go on the board. The class circles ones they could measure at school.

create-2.objective: Generate a data-collection plan and a blank graph for one sky pattern.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a plan with what to measure and a labeled blank graph.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student picks a sky pattern and plans how to collect the data: what to measure, how often, and over how long. They draw and label the blank graph they will fill. Partners check that the axes fit the plan. Students keep the plan for data days.

create-3.objective: Develop a finished graphical display from collected sky data that reveals a pattern.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student submits a labeled graph with the pattern marked and named.
create-3.sampleTask: Students plot a full sky data set into a clean, labeled display and mark the pattern it reveals. The graph must let a reader see the pattern without help. Partners check the labels and scale. Guide collects the display as the product.

create-4.objective: Produce a multi-display poster that combines sky data sets to argue for a pattern's cause.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a poster linking displays to a stated cause.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a poster with two or three sky-pattern graphs and a written claim about the cause behind them. The poster must connect the displays to the claim. Groups present the poster and the class checks that the graphs support the cause. Guide saves posters for the unit wall.

5-ESS2-1.standardStatement: Develop a model using an example to describe ways the geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and/or atmosphere interact.
5-ESS2-1.subject: science

remember-1.objective: Name the four Earth systems, geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere, and what each contains.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student matches all four spheres to their contents on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the four spheres. Students write what each holds: rock and soil, living things, water, air. Cards check against a partner. Guide corrects any sphere a student confused.

understand-1.objective: Label the four spheres on a picture of a natural scene.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student labels at least one example of each sphere in the scene.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a photo of a river valley. Students label parts that belong to each sphere: rocks, plants, river, air. Labels must cover all four. Partners check the labels.

understand-2.objective: Explain one interaction between two spheres shown in an example, naming cause and effect.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a cause-and-effect sentence for one sphere interaction.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide shows roots breaking a rock. Students write how the biosphere acts on the geosphere, naming cause and effect. Each student names both spheres. Pairs compare sentences. Guide collects as a check.

understand-3.objective: Explain how a single event, such as a rainstorm, links three or four spheres, using evidence from the example.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student traces the event through at least three spheres.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide describes a rainstorm over a forest. Students explain how it moves through atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, and biosphere, citing what happens in each. Each student names at least three spheres in order. Guide reads two strong traces aloud.

understand-4.objective: Relate sphere interactions to a built environment, such as how a city's roads and parks touch all four spheres.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student maps a city feature to each sphere it touches.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students pick a city feature, such as a storm drain or a park. They map how it interacts with each sphere on a quick chart. Each student names a give-and-take between two spheres. Charts post for a gallery walk.

apply-1.objective: Use a model diagram to identify which two spheres are interacting in a labeled arrow.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student names the two spheres for each of four arrows.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a four-sphere diagram with labeled arrows. Students name which two spheres each arrow connects. Answers go on a worksheet. Pairs check against the diagram.

apply-2.objective: Build a simple model showing one sphere interaction from a given example.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a labeled model of one interaction.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students pick one interaction, such as waves wearing a cliff, and build a labeled diagram model showing the two spheres and the effect. Arrows must show the direction of the action. Partners check that both spheres are named. Guide displays one as the model.

apply-3.objective: Develop a model of a real local example showing how several spheres interact.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student submits a model linking at least three spheres for one local example.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students choose a local example, such as a nearby pond or a tree-lined street, and build a model linking at least three spheres with labeled arrows. Each arrow names the action. Partners check that the links are real, not invented. Guide collects the models.

apply-4.objective: Construct a system model of a real event and use it to predict a downstream effect on another sphere.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a model with a predicted effect named and justified.
apply-4.sampleTask: Groups model a real event, such as a wildfire or a flood, showing how it moves through the spheres. They use the model to predict an effect on one more sphere and justify it. Each group marks the prediction on the model. Groups present the model and the prediction.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which sphere is missing from an incomplete interaction model.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the missing sphere on each of three models.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives three interaction models, each missing one sphere. Students name the missing sphere for each. Answers go on whiteboards. Guide confirms and explains the gap.

analyze-2.objective: Sort a set of example events by which spheres each one connects.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student sorts all events into the correct sphere-pair groups.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Each pair gets cards naming events, such as a landslide or photosynthesis. Students sort the cards by which spheres each connects. They flag any card that fits more than one group. Guide reveals the key and pairs self-score.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two models of the same event and explain which shows the sphere interactions more completely, with evidence.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names an interaction one model captures that the other misses.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives two student models of the same flood. Students decide which is more complete and cite an interaction the weaker model leaves out. Each student names the missing link. Pairs confirm the call.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze a complex scene to infer all the sphere interactions at work and how they chain together.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student maps at least four interactions and one chain across them.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups study a detailed scene, such as a coastal town in a storm. Students infer the sphere interactions and chain at least three into a sequence. Each student records four interactions and one chain. Groups compare maps and discuss the longest chain found.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a model accurately shows how the spheres interact in its example, and defend the verdict.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student gives an accurate-or-not verdict with two pieces of evidence.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students review a model claiming to show a rainstorm's path through the spheres. Each student decides whether it is accurate and writes two reasons. They check for a missing or wrong arrow. Guide collects verdicts and shows the corrected model.

evaluate-4.objective: Evaluate competing models of one event and argue which best describes the sphere interactions.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student backs one model with evidence and rejects another with a reason.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide posts three models of one event, such as a volcanic eruption. Students weigh which best shows the sphere interactions. Each student writes an argument for the best model and against the weakest, citing specific links. The class votes.

create-1.objective: Brainstorm examples of two Earth spheres affecting each other.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student lists at least five sphere-pair interactions.
create-1.sampleTask: Guide asks for ways two spheres push on each other. Students brainstorm at least five, such as wind shaping sand or plants cleaning air. Lists go on the board. The class groups them by which spheres are involved.

create-2.objective: Generate a rough model sketch of one chosen sphere interaction.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a labeled sketch naming both spheres and the effect.
create-2.sampleTask: Each student picks one interaction from the class brainstorm and sketches a rough model with both spheres labeled and an arrow for the effect. The sketch stays simple but complete. Partners check that the effect is clear. Students keep the sketch for refinement.

create-3.objective: Develop a full model with an example that describes how three or more spheres interact.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student submits a labeled model linking at least three spheres.
create-3.sampleTask: Students build a complete model around one example, linking at least three spheres with labeled, directed arrows. The model must let a reader follow the interactions. Partners test the model by explaining it back. Guide collects the model as the product.

create-4.objective: Produce a model of a real-world problem that shows sphere interactions and points to a solution.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a model that links spheres and names a solution it supports.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups model a real problem, such as beach erosion or urban flooding, showing the sphere interactions that drive it. They use the model to point to one solution and mark where it acts. Groups present the model and the solution. Guide saves models for the unit wall.

5-ESS2-2.standardStatement: Describe and graph the amounts of salt water and fresh water in various reservoirs to provide evidence about the distribution of water on Earth.
5-ESS2-2.subject: science

remember-1.objective: State that most of Earth's water is salt water in the oceans and only a small share is fresh water.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the salt-versus-fresh split in one sentence from memory.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide asks where most of Earth's water sits. Students write that oceans hold most of it as salt water and fresh water is a small share. Cards check with a partner. Guide corrects any answer that flips the proportion.

understand-1.objective: Plot the major water reservoirs and their amounts on a bar graph.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student plots all reservoir bars with axes labeled.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a table of water amounts by reservoir: oceans, ice caps, groundwater, lakes, rivers. Each student plots a bar graph with labeled axes. Bars must match the table. Partners check the heights.

understand-2.objective: Describe what a finished water-distribution graph shows about salt versus fresh water in one or two sentences.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student names the largest and smallest reservoir from the graph.
understand-2.sampleTask: Students read a completed water-distribution bar graph and write which reservoir is largest and which is smallest. They name the salt-to-fresh gap. Pairs compare descriptions. Guide confirms the pattern.

understand-3.objective: Explain why fresh water is scarce for people even though Earth looks covered in water, using the graph as evidence.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student cites a graph value to back the scarcity point.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide gives the water-distribution graph. Students explain why usable fresh water is scarce, pointing to how little sits in rivers and lakes versus oceans and ice. Each student cites a value. Guide reads two explanations aloud.

understand-4.objective: Relate Earth's water distribution to a local water source, naming where the school's water likely comes from.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names a likely local source and which reservoir it draws from.
understand-4.sampleTask: Students consider where their tap water comes from, such as a reservoir or groundwater. They write which Earth reservoir it draws from and whether that share is large or small. Each student ties the local source to the graph. Notes post for a gallery walk.

apply-1.objective: Read the amount of water in a stated reservoir off a distribution graph.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student reads two correct reservoir values from the graph.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide hands a water-distribution graph. Students read the amount for two stated reservoirs and record them. They circle the largest. Pairs check the readings.

apply-2.objective: Convert a table of water percentages into a graph that shows the distribution.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student builds a graph that matches the percentage table.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students take a table of water percentages by reservoir and build a bar or circle graph. They label each part with its share. Each student writes which two reservoirs hold almost all the water. Partners check the graph against the table.

apply-3.objective: Use a self-made water graph to identify and describe the salt-to-fresh distribution.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student marks the salt and fresh shares on their graph and writes a sentence.
apply-3.sampleTask: Students graph a water data set and mark the salt-water and fresh-water shares. Each student writes one sentence describing the split. Partners compare marks. Guide checks that the marks match the data.

apply-4.objective: Build a display comparing fresh-water reservoirs and use it to argue where usable water is found.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a display with a written claim about usable water.
apply-4.sampleTask: Groups graph only the fresh-water reservoirs, ice, groundwater, lakes, and rivers, and compare their sizes. They use the display to argue where usable fresh water actually sits. Each group writes the claim in two sentences. Groups present the display and the claim.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the largest and smallest reservoirs on a water-distribution graph.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the largest and smallest reservoir.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a water graph. Students mark the largest and smallest reservoirs. Answers go on whiteboards. Guide confirms.

analyze-2.objective: Organize scrambled reservoir data into a graph and identify the distribution pattern.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student plots the data and states the pattern in one sentence.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Students take scrambled reservoir amounts and plot them from largest to smallest. They write one sentence naming the pattern. Pairs check the order. Guide confirms that oceans dominate.

analyze-3.objective: Compare two graphs of the same water data and explain which represents the distribution more fairly, with evidence.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names a graphing choice that makes one display fairer.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Guide gives two graphs of the same water data, one with a broken scale. Students decide which is fairer and cite the graphing choice that helps. Each student names the flaw in the weaker graph. Pairs confirm.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze water and water-use data together to infer where fresh-water stress is likely.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student links a low fresh-water share to a stress conclusion with evidence.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups receive water-distribution data and a note on growing water use. Students infer where fresh-water stress is likely and cite the small fresh-water share as evidence. Each student writes the inference. Groups compare and discuss the reasoning.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge whether a water graph supports a stated claim about distribution, and defend the verdict.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student gives a supports-or-not verdict with two pieces of evidence.
